# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Factor of Safety. Explain briefly how the value of factor of safety is selected.

**[Model Answer — 3 Marks]**

**Factor of Safety (FoS / SF):** It is defined as the ratio of the material's ultimate strength (or yield strength) to the maximum allowable working stress.

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{\text{Ultimate Strength}}{\text{Working Stress}} = \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_w}$$

**Selection Criteria:** *(1 mark)*
- Reliability and consequences of failure (life-critical vs. non-critical)
- Type of loading (static, fluctuating, shock, fatigue)
- Material homogeneity and inspectability
- Manufacturing accuracy and quality control
- Accuracy of load estimation
- Codes and standards (IS, BIS, ASME)

**Typical values:**
- Aircraft structures: 1.2 – 1.5
- Machine components: 2 – 4
- Civil structures, bridges: 4 – 6

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between Ductile and Brittle materials with suitable examples.

**[Model Answer — 3 Marks]**

| Property | Ductile Material | Brittle Material |
|---|---|---|
| Plastic deformation before fracture | Large (≥ 5%) | Negligible (< 5%) |
| Yield point | Well defined | Not defined clearly |
| Failure nature | Cup-and-cone (shear) | Cleavage (sudden) |
| Energy absorption (toughness) | High | Low |
| Examples | Mild steel, copper, aluminium | Cast iron, glass, concrete |

**Engineering Implication:** *(1 mark)* Ductile materials give **visual warning** before failure (necking, deformation) — preferred for machinery. Brittle materials fail suddenly — used where compression-only loads occur (e.g., concrete in buildings).

---

## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two questions will be given. Answer **any one**. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**

A mild steel shaft of diameter **45 mm** is subjected to a **bending moment of 1.5 × 10⁶ N·mm** and a **torque of 1.0 × 10⁶ N·mm** at a critical section. The material has the following properties: Sut = 550 MPa, Sy = 320 MPa, S'e = 280 MPa. The shaft is subjected to **fluctuating bending** with σmax = 200 MPa and σmin = -200 MPa.

**OR**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the factor of safety based on:
- (i) Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)
- (ii) Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises)

##### Solution:

**Given:** d = 45 mm, M = 1.5 × 10⁶ N·mm, T = 1.0 × 10⁶ N·mm, Sy = 320 MPa.

**Step 1: Bending stress**
$$\sigma_b = \frac{32M}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 1.5 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (45)^3} = \frac{48 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 91125}$$

$$\sigma_b = \frac{48 \times 10^6}{286,289} = 167.66 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Torsional shear stress**
$$\tau = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 1.0 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 91125} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{286,289} = 55.89 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)**
$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{167.66}{2}\right)^2 + (55.89)^2}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{(83.83)^2 + (55.89)^2} = \sqrt{7027.6 + 3123.7} = \sqrt{10151.3} = 100.75 \text{ MPa}$$

The maximum shear stress criterion gives:
$$\tau_{max} \leq \frac{S_y}{2 \cdot \text{FoS}}$$

$$\text{FoS}_{Tresca} = \frac{S_y}{2 \cdot \tau_{max}} = \frac{320}{2 \times 100.75} = \frac{320}{201.5} = \boxed{1.588}$$

**Step 4: Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises)**
$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau^2} = \sqrt{(167.66)^2 + 3 \times (55.89)^2}$$

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{28110 + 9374.4} = \sqrt{37484.4} = 193.61 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\text{FoS}_{vonMises} = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_{VM}} = \frac{320}{193.61} = \boxed{1.653}$$

**Comparison:** von Mises gives a slightly higher (less conservative) FoS than Tresca — typical result.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Using the **Goodman criterion**, calculate the factor of safety of the shaft against fatigue failure. Assume Ka = 0.9, Kb = 0.85.

##### Solution:

**Step 1: Compute stress amplitude and mean stress**
$$\sigma_{max} = 200 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_{min} = -200 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{200 - (-200)}{2} = 200 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{200 + (-200)}{2} = 0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Modified endurance limit**
$$S_e = K_a \cdot K_b \cdot S_e' = 0.9 \times 0.85 \times 280 = 214.2 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Apply Goodman Criterion**
$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}$$

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{200}{214.2} + \frac{0}{550} = 0.9337 + 0 = 0.9337$$

$$\boxed{n = \frac{1}{0.9337} = 1.071}$$

**Interpretation:** Since σm = 0, the Goodman criterion gives the same result as using only Se. The shaft is unsafe in fatigue if FoS required is greater than 1.071.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Pattern]**

A steel component is subjected to a **static tensile load** of 50 kN applied suddenly. The cross-section is rectangular (40 mm × 20 mm) and the gauge length is 200 mm. Material properties: E = 2 × 10⁵ MPa, Sy = 350 MPa, Sut = 500 MPa.

**OR**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the maximum dynamic stress induced and the factor of safety against yielding.

##### Solution:

**Given:** W = 50 kN = 50,000 N, A = 40 × 20 = 800 mm², L = 200 mm, E = 2 × 10⁵ MPa

**Step 1: Static stress**
$$\sigma_s = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{50000}{800} = 62.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: For sudden load (h = 0)**
$$\sigma_d = 2 \times \sigma_s = 2 \times 62.5 = \boxed{125 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3: Factor of safety against yielding**
$$\text{FoS} = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_d} = \frac{350}{125} = \boxed{2.8}$$

**Conclusion:** The shaft is safe (FoS > 2 is generally acceptable).

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** If the load of 50 kN is **dropped from a height of 60 mm**, compute the new dynamic stress. Compare with the sudden-load case.

##### Solution:

**Step 1: Use the impact stress formula**
$$\sigma_d = \sigma_s \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2hE}{\sigma_s \cdot L}}\right)$$

$$\sigma_d = 62.5 \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 \times 60 \times 2 \times 10^5}{62.5 \times 200}}\right)$$

**Step 2: Simplify the term inside the square root**
$$\frac{2hE}{\sigma_s L} = \frac{2 \times 60 \times 200000}{62.5 \times 200} = \frac{24,000,000}{12,500} = 1920$$

$$\sqrt{1 + 1920} = \sqrt{1921} = 43.83$$

$$\sigma_d = 62.5 \times (1 + 43.83) = 62.5 \times 44.83$$

$$\sigma_d \approx \boxed{2802 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3: Comparison with sudden-load case**
- Sudden load (h = 0): σd = 125 MPa
- Impact load (h = 60 mm): σd ≈ 2802 MPa

The impact stress is over **22 times higher** than the sudden-load stress. This dramatic increase demonstrates why impact loads are extremely critical in design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common mistake:** Forgetting to convert W (load in N) into stress by dividing by area A. Many students directly compute σd = W·√(2hE/(W·L)) — this is dimensionally wrong.
> 2. **Sign convention error:** When calculating σmin, students often assume it's positive when the loading is "fluctuating" between 0 and σmax. Always re-read the problem.
> 3. **Forgetting shock factors in shaft design:** The Kb, Kt, Kts multipliers (shock factors) are frequently missed. Always re-read whether the problem specifies "with shock loading" or "without shock loading."
> 4. **Mean stress confusion:** σm is the *mean* of σmax and σmin — not the average of nominal stresses. Use σm = (σmax + σmin) / 2.
> 5. **Goodman vs Soderberg confusion:** Soderberg uses Sy in the denominator (more conservative); Goodman uses Sut (less conservative).

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

✅ **Stress-Strain Curve** — Memorize the 5 points: P, E, Y, UTS, F (Proportional, Elastic, Yield, Ultimate Tensile, Fracture).

✅ **Hooke's Law** — σ = Eε is valid ONLY in the proportional (linear elastic) region.

✅ **Young's Modulus E** is a **material constant**, not a design variable. Steel ≈ 2 × 10⁵ MPa.

✅ **Ductile** materials have a clearly defined **yield point**; **brittle** materials do not — for them, the **proportional limit** is used.

✅ **Bending Stress** for a circular shaft: σb = **32M / (πd³)**, Torsional Stress: τ = **16T / (πd³)**.

✅ **Factor of Safety = Strength / Working Stress** — always quote FoS after stating the reference strength (yield or ultimate).

✅ **Stress Concentration Factor (Kt)** = σmax / σnom — it is **always ≥ 1**.

✅ **Notch Sensitivity (q)** ∈ [0, 1] — Higher q means the material is more sensitive to notches in fatigue.

✅ **Fatigue Stress Concentration (Kf)** = 1 + q(Kt − 1) — use this in fatigue design (NOT Kt).

✅ **For sudden load (h = 0):** σd = 2σs. **For impact load:** σd = σs·√(2hE/(σs·L)).

✅ **Endurance Limit (S'e)** for steels ≈ **0.5 × Sut**. For non-ferrous metals, there is no true endurance limit.

✅ **Modifying Factors:** Ka (surface), Kb (size), Kc (load), Kd (temperature), Ke (reliability). Multiply them all: **Se = Ka · Kb · Kc · Kd · Ke · S'e**.

✅ **Mean Stress Correction (in order of increasing liberality):** Soderberg < Goodman < ASME-Elliptic < Gerber.

✅ **Equivalent Bending Moment:** **Me = (M + √(M² + T²)) / 2**, and Equivalent Torque **Te = √(M² + T²)**.

✅ **ASME Shaft Equation:** **d³ = (16/π) · (n/Se) · √((Kb·Me)² + (3/4)·(Kts·Te)²)** — solve and round up to next standard size.

✅ **For Goodman Criterion:** **(σa/Se) + (σm/Sut) = (1/n)** — the most-tested fatigue formula in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combined Steady and Variable Stress - Gerber, Goodman and Soderberg Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Combined Steady and Variable Stress - Gerber, Goodman and Soderberg Method ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In machine design, mechanical components in service rarely experience either a purely **steady (static) stress** or a purely **variable (fluctuating) stress**. The realistic loading scenario is a **combined steady and variable stress**, in which a constant mean stress $\sigma_m$ acts simultaneously with a variable (alternating) stress $\sigma_a$ of the same sign (typically tensile). > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Standard):** *Combined steady and variable stress* refers to a stress state in which the maximum stress $\sigma_{max}$ and minimum stress $\sigma_{min}$ are both of the **same sign** (both tensile or both compressive), and the mean stress is significantly greater than zero. The component fails either by yielding (due to mean stress) or by fatigue (due to variable stress), or by a combination of both. The stress state is fully characterized by: - **Mean stress** $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$ - **Alternating (variable) stress amplitude** $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$ - **Stress ratio** $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ For combined loading, $0 \le R < 1$ (when both are tensile) or $1 < R \le \infty$ (when both are compressive). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Engineering Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — The *Car Axle Under Static and Dynamic Load*:** > Imagine the rear axle of a loaded car. The weight of the car + passengers + cargo applies a **steady, never-changing bending stress** at the axle's critical cross-section. At the same time, every small bump in the road produces a **small, repeated, fluctuating bending stress** on top of the steady one. So the axle fibers at the top surface see: $\sigma_{max} = (\text{steady load stress}) + (\text{bump stress})$, and at the bottom surface: $\sigma_{min} = (\text{steady load stress}) - (\text{bump stress})$. Both are tensile (carrying the load), so this is a *combined steady + variable* situation. The axle will fail by **fatigue** if designed only for the static load — exactly the real-world problem that Gerber, Goodman, and Soderberg were trying to solve mathematically. ### 1.3 Why We Need Three Different Methods The 19th and 20th century engineers realized that the rotating-beam fatigue test only characterizes a material's behavior under **fully reversed loading** ($R = -1$, $\sigma_m = 0$, $\sigma_a = S_e$). But real shafts almost never operate at $R = -1$ — they have a static pre-load. So three different **failure loci** were proposed in the **Haigh Diagram** (mean-stress vs. alternating-stress space) to extrapolate the $R = -1$ fatigue point to other mean stresses: | Method | Failure Boundary Used | Conservatism | |---|---|---| | **Soderberg** | Yield strength $S_y$ | Most conservative (safe) | | **Goodman** | Ultimate strength $S_{ut}$ | Moderately conservative | | **Gerber** | Ultimate strength $S_{ut}$ (parabolic) | Least conservative (best fit to test data for ductile metals) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Stress Components of a Combined Cycle For a component experiencing combined steady and variable stress: $$\sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a$$ $$\sigma_{min} = \sigma_m - \sigma_a$$ $$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$$ $$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$$ $$R = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{\sigma_m - \sigma_a}{\sigma_m + \sigma_a}$$ ### 2.2 The Philosophy Behind Each Failure Line The three methods are all attempts to draw a line/curve in the $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ plane that separates the **safe region** (below) from the **failure region** (above). They all pass through two anchor points: 1. **Pure fatigue point:** $(\sigma_m = 0, \sigma_a = S_e)$ — from the rotating-beam test. 2. **Pure static point:** $(\sigma_m = S_y, \sigma_a = 0)$ for Soderberg, or $(\sigma_m = S_{ut}, \sigma_a = 0)$ for Goodman/Gerber. The shape connecting these two points differs: - **Soderberg** → straight line (most pessimistic) - **Goodman** → straight line (moderately pessimistic) - **Gerber** → parabola (best fit to actual ductile-metal test data) ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **master equation sheet** for Module 1 KTU questions on this topic. Memorize all three design equations and the difference between *static failure* and *fatigue failure* criteria. | # | Method | Failure Equation (No FoS) | Design Equation (with FoS = n) | Boundary Used | Curve Type | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Soderberg** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = 1$ | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | Yield $S_y$ | Straight line | | 2 | **Goodman** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$ | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | Ultimate $S_{ut}$ | Straight line | | 3 | **Gerber** | $\left(\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$ | $\left(\dfrac{n \sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^{2} + \dfrac{n \sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | Ultimate $S_{ut}$ | Parabola | | 4 | **ASME-Elliptic** | $\left(\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y}\right)^{2} = 1$ | $\left(\dfrac{n \sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{n \sigma_m}{S_y}\right)^{2} = 1$ | Yield $S_y$ | Quarter ellipse | ### 2.4 Closed-Form Factor-of-Safety Equations (Very High Yield for KTU) These are direct $n$ values without solving a quadratic: **Soderberg (linear):** $$n = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y}}$$ **Goodman (linear):** $$n = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}}$$ **Gerber (quadratic, requires quadratic formula):** $$n = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_m} \right)^{2} \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} \left[ -1 + \sqrt{1 + \left( \frac{2 \sigma_m S_e}{\sigma_a S_{ut}} \right)^{2}} \right]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These three methods are the *backbone* of all shaft design, pressure-vessel design under cyclic pressure, and connecting-rod design. KTU problems in **Module 1** and **Module 2 (Shafts)** almost always ask you to find the factor of safety using one or more of these methods. The Gerber $n$ is most accurate for ductile ferrous metals; the Goodman $n$ is the most common in design codes; the Soderberg $n$ is used when yielding must be absolutely avoided. ### 2.5 Haigh Diagram — The Master Picture The **Haigh Diagram** plots alternating stress $\sigma_a$ (y-axis) vs. mean stress $\sigma_m$ (x-axis). The three methods are simply three different curves on this single diagram. The line from origin to point $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ has slope $\sigma_a / \sigma_m$. The factor of safety $n$ is the ratio of the distance from origin to the failure curve, to the distance from origin to the actual operating point. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Soderberg Equation **Starting concept:** A ductile material fails either by **yielding** (when the mean stress alone is high) or by **fatigue** (when the cyclic stress is high). Soderberg assumed a **linear interpolation** between these two failure modes. **Step 1 — Identify the two boundary points in the Haigh diagram:** - *Fatigue limit only* (zero mean stress, fully reversed): $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a) = (0, S_e)$ - *Yield only* (zero alternating stress, pure static): $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a) = (S_y, 0)$ **Step 2 — Equation of the line passing through these two points** in the $\sigma_m$–$\sigma_a$ plane (intercepts form): $$\frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} + \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} = 1$$ **Step 3 — Incorporate the factor of safety $n$:** If the actual operating stresses are $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$, the component is safe only if the operating point lies inside the triangle. The factor of safety is the ratio of the failure boundary to the operating point. Since the equation is linear and homogeneous of degree 1, multiplying both stresses by $n$ moves the point to the failure line: $$\frac{n \, \sigma_m}{S_y} + \frac{n \, \sigma_a}{S_e} = 1$$ $$\boxed{\;\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \frac{1}{n}\;}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation of the Goodman Equation The derivation is **identical in form** to Soderberg, except the *static* anchor point is changed from yield strength $S_y$ to the **ultimate tensile strength $S_{ut}$**. The historical reasoning: in brittle materials, or when the question is *fracture* rather than *yielding*, the failure occurs at the ultimate strength under static load. **Step 1 — Boundary points:** - *Fatigue boundary:* $(0, S_e)$ - *Static fracture boundary:* $(S_{ut}, 0)$ **Step 2 — Line connecting them:** $$\frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} + \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} = 1$$ **Step 3 — With factor of safety:** $$\boxed{\;\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}\;}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation of the Gerber Equation (Most Important for KTU) > [!IMPORTANT] > The Gerber method uses a **parabola** fitted through the same two boundary points as Goodman. It is the **best empirical fit** to actual fatigue-test data for ductile metals like steel and aluminum, and is therefore the most commonly asked Gerber derivation in KTU exams. **Step 1 — Assume a general second-degree curve** in the $\sigma_m$–$\sigma_a$ plane passing through $(0, S_e)$ and $(S_{ut}, 0)$. The most general form is: $$\left( \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} \right)^{2} + k \left( \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} \right) = 1$$ **Step 2 — Apply the boundary conditions.** For any point on the curve, when $\sigma_m = 0$, we need $\sigma_a = S_e$ (already satisfied by the squared term). When $\sigma_a = 0$, we need $\sigma_m = S_{ut}$, which gives: $$k \cdot (1) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = 1$$ **Step 3 — Therefore the Gerber failure locus (no FoS) is:** $$\left( \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} \right)^{2} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$$ **Step 4 — Incorporate the factor of safety $n$** by replacing $\sigma_m \to n\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a \to n\sigma_a$ (because the equation is homogeneous of degree 2 in $\sigma_a$ and degree 1 in $\sigma_m$): $$\left( \frac{n \sigma_a}{S_e} \right)^{2} + \frac{n \sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$$ **Step 5 — Rearrange into standard quadratic form in $n$:** $$\frac{\sigma_a^{2}}{S_e^{2}} n^{2} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} n - 1 = 0$$ **Step 6 — Apply the quadratic formula** $n = \dfrac{-b + \sqrt{b^{2} - 4ac}}{2a}$ (taking the positive root since $n > 0$): $$n = \frac{-\dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} + \sqrt{\left( \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} \right)^{2} + 4 \dfrac{\sigma_a^{2}}{S_e^{2}}}}{2 \, \dfrac{\sigma_a^{2}}{S_e^{2}}}$$ **Step 7 — Rationalize** by multiplying numerator and denominator appropriately to obtain the standard KTU form: $$\boxed{\;n = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_m} \right)^{2} \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} \left[ -1 + \sqrt{ 1 + \left( \frac{2 \sigma_m S_e}{\sigma_a S_{ut}} \right)^{2} } \right]\;}$$ ### 3.4 Derivation of the ASME-Elliptic Equation (Bonus) The ASME Code for pressure vessels and rotating equipment uses a quarter-ellipse joining $(0, S_e)$ to $(S_y, 0)$: $$\left( \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} \right)^{2} + \left( \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} \right)^{2} = 1$$ This is **more conservative than Gerber** (because it uses $S_y$ instead of $S_{ut}$) but **less conservative than Soderberg** (because it allows the curve to bow outward from the straight line). With factor of safety: $$\left( \frac{n \sigma_a}{S_e} \right)^{2} + \left( \frac{n \sigma_m}{S_y} \right)^{2} = 1$$ $$n = \frac{1}{\sqrt{ \left( \dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} \right)^{2} + \left( \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} \right)^{2} }}$$ ### 3.5 Comparative Analysis (Decision Matrix for the KTU Student) > [!TIP] > **Rule of thumb for KTU problems:** > - *"Find the most conservative factor of safety"* → use **Soderberg**. > - *"Find the factor of safety for ductile material under combined loading"* (no specification) → use **Gerber**. > - *"Use the simple straight-line formula"* (if no method is specified but it is a typical KTU numerical) → use **Goodman**. | Aspect | Soderberg | Goodman | Gerber | |---|---|---|---| | Static anchor | $S_y$ | $S_{ut}$ | $S_{ut}$ | | Shape | Line | Line | Parabola | | $n$ for same load | Lowest | Middle | Highest | | Best suited for | Ductile, yielding-critical | Brittle, fracture-critical | Ductile, general design | | Difficulty of $n$ solution | Trivial | Trivial | Quadratic formula needed | ## 4.
