KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) --- #### **Question 1: Define mean stress and variable stress amplitude. Show their relation to $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$ for combined loading. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** When a machine element is subjected to a load that has a constant component and a fluctuating component, the resulting stress at the critical point is called **combined steady and variable stress**. **Mean stress** is the average (steady) component of the stress cycle, and is defined as: $$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$$ The **variable (alternating) stress amplitude** is the magnitude of the fluctuating component, defined as: $$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$$ Adding and subtracting these two equations gives the working relations: $$\sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a$$ $$\sigma_{min} = \sigma_m - \sigma_a$$ For *combined* loading, both $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$ have the **same sign** (both positive for combined tensile or both negative for combined compressive loading), so $\sigma_m \ge \sigma_a > 0$. This is what distinguishes combined loading from fully reversed loading (where $\sigma_m = 0$). --- #### **Question 2: State the Soderberg, Goodman, and Gerber equations for combined steady and variable stress, and identify which one is most conservative. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Method | Failure Equation | |---|---| | **Soderberg** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | | **Goodman** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | | **Gerber** | $\left(\dfrac{n \sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^{2} + \dfrac{n \sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | where $\sigma_m$ = mean stress, $\sigma_a$ = alternating stress amplitude, $S_e$ = endurance limit, $S_y$ = yield strength, $S_{ut}$ = ultimate tensile strength, and $n$ = factor of safety. **Most conservative:** **Soderberg method** — because it uses the *yield* strength as the static failure anchor (lower than $S_{ut}$), so for the same operating point it gives the **lowest** factor of safety $n$. The Gerber method is the *least* conservative (highest $n$) for ductile materials, because the parabolic locus bows outward. --- ### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** ##### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Derive the Gerber equation for combined stress and explain why it is preferred for ductile materials. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified] **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — State the problem:** A rotating machine component is subjected to a mean stress $\sigma_m$ and an alternating stress $\sigma_a$. We need an empirical equation that relates these to the material's endurance limit $S_e$ and ultimate tensile strength $S_{ut}$ to predict failure. **Step 2 — Identify the two boundary points** in the $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ Haigh diagram: - *Fully reversed fatigue:* $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a) = (0, S_e)$ - *Pure static fracture:* $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a) = (S_{ut}, 0)$ **Step 3 — Assume a parabolic locus** through these two points of the general form: $$\left( \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} \right)^{2} + k \cdot \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$$ **Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions:** - At $\sigma_m = 0$: $\sigma_a = S_e$ → first term is $1$, second term is $0$ → satisfied automatically. *(1 mark)* - At $\sigma_a = 0$: $\sigma_m = S_{ut}$ → first term is $0$, second term is $k \cdot 1 = 1$ → therefore $k = 1$. *(2 marks)* **Step 5 — Therefore Gerber's failure locus (no factor of safety) is:** $$\left( \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} \right)^{2} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1 \quad (1)$$ **Step 6 — Introduce the factor of safety $n$** by scaling the operating point to the failure curve. Because equation (1) is homogeneous of degree 2 in $\sigma_a$ and degree 1 in $\sigma_m$, the substitution $\sigma_m \to n\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a \to n\sigma_a$ gives: *(1 mark)* $$\left( \frac{n \sigma_a}{S_e} \right)^{2} + \frac{n \sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1 \quad (2)$$ **Step 7 — Rearrange to standard quadratic form** $an^2 + bn - 1 = 0$: $$\frac{\sigma_a^{2}}{S_e^{2}} \, n^{2} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} \, n - 1 = 0$$ **Step 8 — Apply the quadratic formula** $n = \dfrac{-b + \sqrt{b^2 + 4ac}}{2a}$ (positive root only): $$n = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_m} \right)^{2} \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} \left[ -1 + \sqrt{ 1 + \left( \frac{2 \sigma_m S_e}{\sigma_a S_{ut}} \right)^{2} } \right] \quad (3) \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$ **Why preferred for ductile materials:** The Gerber parabola closely matches the experimentally observed S-N curves of ductile ferrous and non-ferrous metals under combined loading. The straight-line Goodman criterion over-predicts failure (is too conservative) in the mid-range of mean stresses, while Soderberg is excessively conservative by anchoring at the yield strength. Gerber strikes the best balance for ductile shaft materials such as medium-carbon steels, alloy steels, and aluminum alloys. *(1 mark)* --- ##### Part (b) — 7 Marks: A steel shaft is subjected to a bending stress that fluctuates between $\sigma_{max} = 240$ MPa and $\sigma_{min} = 80$ MPa. The material has $S_{ut} = 620$ MPa, $S_y = 380$ MPa, and $S_e = 280$ MPa. Determine the factor of safety using **(i) Soderberg, (ii) Goodman, and (iii) Gerber** methods. Comment on the results. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified] **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute mean and alternating stress:** $$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{240 + 80}{2} = 160 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{240 - 80}{2} = 80 \text{ MPa}$$ *(1 mark for the formulas, 1 mark for the values)* **Step 2 — Soderberg factor of safety:** $$n_{Sodb} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{80}{280} + \dfrac{160}{380}}$$ $$n_{Sodb} = \frac{1}{0.2857 + 0.4211} = \frac{1}{0.7068} = 1.415 \quad \approx \mathbf{1.41}$$ *(2 marks)* **Step 3 — Goodman factor of safety:** $$n_{Goodm} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{80}{280} + \dfrac{160}{620}}$$ $$n_{Goodm} = \frac{1}{0.2857 + 0.2581} = \frac{1}{0.5438} = 1.839 \quad \approx \mathbf{1.84}$$ *(2 marks)* **Step 4 — Gerber factor of safety (using the closed-form):** First, compute the key ratios: $$\frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{160}{620} = 0.2581$$ $$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} = \frac{80}{280} = 0.2857$$ $$\frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_m} = \frac{620}{160} = 3.875$$ $$\left( \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_m} \right)^{2} = 15.016$$ $$\frac{2 \sigma_m S_e}{\sigma_a S_{ut}} = \frac{2 \times 160 \times 280}{80 \times 620} = \frac{89600}{49600} = 1.8065$$ $$\left( \frac{2 \sigma_m S_e}{\sigma_a S_{ut}} \right)^{2} = 3.263$$ Substitute into the Gerber closed-form equation: $$n_{Gerb} = \frac{1}{2} \times 15.016 \times 0.2857 \times \left[ -1 + \sqrt{1 + 3.263} \right]$$ $$n_{Gerb} = 2.145 \times \left[ -1 + \sqrt{4.263} \right] = 2.145 \times \left[ -1 + 2.0647 \right]$$ $$n_{Gerb} = 2.145 \times 1.0647 = 2.284 \quad \approx \mathbf{2.28}$$ *(2 marks)* **Step 5 — Comment on the results:** | Method | Factor of Safety $n$ | |---|---| | Soderberg | **1.41** (most conservative) | | Goodman | **1.84** (moderately conservative) | | Gerber | **2.28** (least conservative, most realistic for ductile steel) | We observe $n_{Soderberg} < n_{Goodman} < n_{Gerber}$, which confirms the theoretical ranking. The Gerber result of $\approx 2.28$ is the most accurate prediction for a ductile medium-carbon steel shaft under combined steady and variable bending. Soderberg is overly cautious and would lead to a heavier, more expensive shaft. *(1 mark)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** ##### Part (a) — 7 Marks: With a neat sketch, explain the Haigh diagram and show how Soderberg, Goodman, and Gerber lines/curves are represented on it. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified] **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition of Haigh Diagram (2 marks):** The **Haigh diagram** (or mean-stress diagram) is a 2-D plot with: - **X-axis:** mean stress $\sigma_m$ - **Y-axis:** alternating stress $\sigma_a$ Every possible (steady + variable) loading combination is represented by a single point in this plane. The "safe region" is bounded above by the failure locus, and the factor of safety $n$ is the ratio of the radial distance from the origin to the failure curve, to the radial distance from the origin to the operating point. **Step 2 — Key Reference Points on the Haigh Diagram (2 marks):** | Point | Coordinates | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | A | $(0, S_e)$ | Pure fatigue (fully reversed) — rotating-beam test result | | B | $(S_{ut}, 0)$ | Pure static fracture (ultimate) | | C | $(S_y, 0)$ | Pure static yielding (yield) | | O | $(0, 0)$ | No load | **Step 3 — Representation of the three failure loci (3 marks):** - **Soderberg line:** straight line joining A$(0, S_e)$ to C$(S_y, 0)$. Equation: $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_y = 1/n$. This is the **most conservative** line (closest to the origin). - **Goodman line:** straight line joining A$(0, S_e)$ to B$(S_{ut}, 0)$. Equation: $\sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1/n$. Moderately conservative. - **Gerber parabola:** parabolic curve joining A$(0, S_e)$ to B$(S_{ut}, 0)$ passing through the experimentally observed points. Equation: $(n\sigma_a/S_e)^2 + n\sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1/n$. Bowed outward from the Goodman line. The Soderberg line lies entirely **below** the Goodman line, and the Gerber parabola lies entirely **above** the Goodman line. The ASME-elliptic curve lies between Soderberg and Goodman. **Sketch (textual representation — to be drawn on graph paper in exam):** ``` sigma_a ^ | * A (0, S_e) — fatigue only | / \\ | / \\ <- Gerber parabola | / \\ <- Goodman line | / \\ |/ \\ O----------*------*------> sigma_m C B (S_y) (S_ut) ``` --- ##### Part (b) — 7 Marks: A shaft made of steel ($S_{ut} = 700$ MPa, $S_y = 420$ MPa, $S_e = 350$ MPa) carries a fluctuating torque that varies from $T_{max} = 1200$ N·m to $T_{min} = 300$ N·m, simultaneously with a steady bending moment $M = 900$ N·m. The shaft diameter is 60 mm. Find the factor of safety using the **Soderberg** criterion for combined bending and torsion. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified] **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute the steady bending stress and the variable torsional stress amplitude (2 marks):** Bending moment is **steady** ($M = $ const), so: $$\sigma_m = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{32 \times 900 \times 10^{3} \text{ N·mm}}{\pi \times (60)^{3} \text{ mm}^{3}}$$ $$\sigma_m = \frac{28.8 \times 10^{6}}{\pi \times 216000} = \frac{28.8 \times 10^{6}}{678584} = 42.44 \text{ MPa}$$ Torque is **fluctuating**, so we compute mean and amplitude: $$T_m = \frac{T_{max} + T_{min}}{2} = \frac{1200 + 300}{2} = 750 \text{ N·m}$$ $$T_a = \frac{T_{max} - T_{min}}{2} = \frac{1200 - 300}{2} = 450 \text{ N·m}$$ The corresponding **shear stresses**: $$\tau_m = \frac{16 T_m}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{16 \times 750 \times 10^{3}}{\pi \times 216000} = \frac{12 \times 10^{6}}{678584} = 17.68 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\tau_a = \frac{16 T_a}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{16 \times 450 \times 10^{3}}{\pi \times 216000} = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{6}}{678584} = 10.61 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 2 — Convert to equivalent normal stresses using the distortion-energy (DE) theory (2 marks):** For combined bending (normal) and torsion (shear), the equivalent normal-stress components are: $$\sigma_m^{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_m^{2} + 3 \tau_m^{2}} = \sqrt{(42.44)^{2} + 3 (17.68)^{2}}$$ $$\sigma_m^{eq} = \sqrt{1801.15 + 937.74} = \sqrt{2738.89} = 52.33 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\sigma_a^{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_a^{2} + 3 \tau_a^{2}} = \sqrt{0 + 3 (10.61)^{2}} = \sqrt{3 \times 112.57} = 18.38 \text{ MPa}$$ *(Note: $\sigma_a$ from bending is zero because the bending moment is steady.)* **Step 3 — Apply the Soderberg equation (2 marks):** $$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_a^{eq}}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m^{eq}}{S_y} = \frac{18.38}{350} + \frac{52.33}{420}$$ $$\frac{1}{n} = 0.0525 + 0.1246 = 0.1771$$ $$n = \frac{1}{0.1771} = 5.647 \quad \approx \mathbf{5.65}$$ **Step 4 — Comment (1 mark):** The factor of safety of $\approx 5.65$ indicates the shaft is very conservatively designed for this combined loading using the Soderberg criterion. If Gerber or Goodman were used, $n$ would be higher. A design engineer would typically accept $n \ge 1.5$ to $2.0$ for static-loaded shafts and $n \ge 2$ to $3$ for fatigue-loaded shafts, so the shaft is well within safe limits. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on This Topic:** > 1. **Sign convention confusion** — students forget to use *combined loading* with $\sigma_m \ge \sigma_a > 0$. If you write $\sigma_{min}$ as a *negative* number, you are wrongly using the **fluctuating** (not combined) stress formulas. For combined loading, $\sigma_{min}$ is positive. > 2. **Mixing $S_y$ and $S_{ut}$** — Soderberg uses $S_y$, Goodman and Gerber use $S_{ut}$. A common slip is to use $S_{ut}$ in Soderberg. > 3. **Forgetting the factor of safety in the Gerber quadratic** — the $n$ enters *inside* the squared term AND linearly. If you put $n$ only in the linear term, you will get a wrong (and lower) answer. > 4. **Sign error in quadratic formula** — for Gerber, the constant term is $-1$ (not $+1$). The discriminant is $b^2 + 4ac$, not $b^2 - 4ac$, because $ac < 0$. > 5. **Unit inconsistency** — keep $S_e, S_y, S_{ut}, \sigma_m, \sigma_a$ in the **same units** (typically MPa) throughout, or you will get a numerical nonsense answer. > 6. **Skipping the comment / comparison step** — KTU board examiners specifically want a concluding line saying which method is most/least conservative and why, worth **1 mark** at the end of every numerical. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Combined Steady and Variable Stress (Module 1):** - **Combined loading definition:** both $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$ are of the *same sign*; $\sigma_m \ge \sigma_a > 0$. - **Core stress relations:** $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max}+\sigma_{min})/2$, $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max}-\sigma_{min})/2$, $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max} \in [0, 1)$. - **Soderberg equation** (uses $S_y$, most conservative, straight line): $\dfrac{1}{n} = \dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y}$ - **Goodman equation** (uses $S_{ut}$, moderately conservative, straight line): $\dfrac{1}{n} = \dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}$ - **Gerber equation** (uses $S_{ut}$, least conservative for ductile, parabola): $\dfrac{1}{n^{2}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^{2} + \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$ or equivalently the closed-form $n = \dfrac{1}{2} \left(\dfrac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_m}\right)^{2} \dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} \left[ -1 + \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{2 \sigma_m S_e}{\sigma_a S_{ut}}\right)^{2}} \right]$ - **ASME-elliptic** (uses $S_y$, between Soderberg and Goodman): $1 = (n\sigma_a/S_e)^2 + (n\sigma_m/S_y)^2$. - **Order of conservatism:** $n_{Soderberg} < n_{ASME} < n_{Goodman} < n_{Gerber}$ for the same operating point and ductile material. - **Anchor points** on the Haigh diagram: $(0, S_e)$ for fatigue, $(S_y, 0)$ for Soderberg/ASME, $(S_{ut}, 0)$ for Goodman/Gerber. - **For combined bending + torsion**, use **distortion-energy theory** to reduce to equivalent normal stresses: $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^{2} + 3\tau^{2}}$ for both mean and alternating components. - **Soderberg** is the *default* KTU method for *yielding* design; **Goodman** is the most commonly used in industry codes; **Gerber** is the most accurate for ductile metals. - **Always conclude** with a comment on which method gives the *highest* $n$ (least conservative) and the *lowest* $n$ (most conservative) — this is a **compulsory 1-mark wrap-up** in KTU valuation.

topics.title: Design of Riveted Joints - Material for Rivets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Riveted Joints - Material for Rivets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Design of Riveted Joints** under the KTU 2024 Machine Design syllabus, the term **"Material for Rivets"** refers to the specific engineering materials selected for the manufacture of rivets, governed by their **mechanical properties (ductility, shear strength, tensile strength, and toughness)**, **forming behavior (cold/hot heading capability)**, **service compatibility (corrosion resistance and thermal expansion match with parent plates)**, and adherence to **Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 1929:1982)** specifications.

A **rivet** is a permanent mechanical fastener with a head on one end and a cylindrical shank that is deformed plastically on the other end to form a second head, locking the joint. The material must therefore be sufficiently **ductile** to undergo plastic deformation during heading without cracking, and simultaneously possess adequate **shear and bearing strength** to transmit the design load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Riveted Joints):**
> The selection of rivet material directly governs the **Permissible Shear Stress (Ps)** and **Permissible Crushing Stress (Pc)** values used in **Unwin's formula** ($d = 6\sqrt{t}$) and in **efficiency calculations** ($\eta = \frac{P_{weakest}}{P_{solid}}$). The KTU examiner expects the student to *justify* the choice of material with quantitative properties, not just state a name.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are trying to **clamp two wooden planks together using a nail**, but you don't have a hammer. Instead, you take a soft metal rod, push it through a hole in both planks, and then **smash the protruding end with a hammer** to flatten it into a second head. That smashed-flat end is possible *only* because the metal is **soft and ductile** — it bends and flows instead of cracking.

Now imagine the two planks are not wood but **steel plates in a bridge or boiler shell**, carrying thousands of kilograms of load. The nail has become a **rivet**, the hammer is a **riveting machine (hydraulic or pneumatic)**, and the soft metal rod must now be **stronger, tougher, and more ductile than the plates themselves**.

**The Core Logic:** A rivet must be:
- **Softer/Ductile enough** → so it can be deformed into a head without splitting.
- **Strong enough** → so it doesn't shear off under load.

These two requirements are in subtle tension (literally!). The engineer's job is to find the **sweet spot** — a material that satisfies both.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Values

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Permissible Stresses for Common Rivet Materials (As per IS 1929 & KTU data book):**

| Property | Mild Steel (MS) | High Tensile Steel | Aluminium Alloy |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| Ultimate Tensile Strength (Sut) | **400 – 500 MPa** | **600 – 800 MPa** | **200 – 400 MPa** |
| Yield Strength (Sy) | **250 – 320 MPa** | **400 – 550 MPa** | **120 – 280 MPa** |
| Permissible Shear Stress (Ps) | **80 – 100 MPa** | **120 – 150 MPa** | **60 – 80 MPa** |
| Permissible Crushing Stress (Pc) | **160 – 200 MPa** | **240 – 300 MPa** | **120 – 160 MPa** |
| Elongation (%) | **20 – 30 %** | **12 – 18 %** | **10 – 25 %** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Required Properties of Rivet Material – Structured Logic

A rivet must withstand two distinct phases of its life:

**Phase 1 – Manufacturing (Rivet Installation):**
1. **High Ductility** — must undergo plastic flow to form the shop head.
2. **Low Hot/Cold Working Resistance** — must be formable at the chosen riveting temperature.
3. **No Work-Hardening Cracking** — must not develop surface cracks during heading.
4. **Homogeneous Grain Structure** — ensures uniform deformation.

**Phase 2 – Service (Load Transmission):**
1. **High Shear Strength (τ)** — resists shearing of shank across the shear plane.
2. **High Bearing/Crushing Strength (σc)** — resists hole elongation and plate crushing.
3. **Fatigue Resistance** — survives cyclic loading in bridges, pressure vessels.
4. **Corrosion Resistance** — matches service environment (marine, chemical).
5. **Galvanic Compatibility** — avoids bimetallic corrosion with parent plates.

### 2.2 Classification of Rivet Materials

**A. Based on Strength:**

1. **Mild Steel Rivets (MS)** – Most widely used in structural work, boilers, bridges, ship hulls, railway coaches.
   - Composition: C = 0.15–0.25 %, Mn = 0.5–0.8 %.
   - Heading temperature: Cold for d ≤ 10 mm; Hot for d > 10 mm.
   - Cost: Low; Availability: Excellent.

2. **High Tensile Steel Rivets** – Used in high-stress applications like heavy machinery, military bridges, aircraft structures (historically).
   - Composition: Medium carbon + alloying elements (Ni, Cr, Mo).
   - Heat-treated for required hardness and toughness.

3. **Alloy Steel Rivets** – Ni-Cr-Mo steels for very high temperature or impact service.

**B. Based on Application Environment:**

4. **Aluminium and Aluminium-Alloy Rivets** – Aerospace, lightweight structures, refrigeration.
   - Composition: Al-Mg-Si (e.g., AA 6056), Al-Cu (AA 2017).
   - Cold driven only; excellent corrosion resistance.

5. **Copper Rivets** – Electrical connections, sheet metal work, roofing (where corrosion resistance matters).

6. **Brass and Bronze Rivets** – Marine, decorative, plumbing (resist dezincification).

7. **Monel Metal Rivets** – Chemical plants, marine (Ni-Cu alloy, ~70 % Ni).

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Material Selection – KTU Logic

The KTU examiner expects a **justified answer**, not a memorised one. The logic chain is:

$$\boxed{\text{Load Requirement} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Strength Required} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Select Material} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Read Ps, Pc} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Apply Unwin's Formula}}$$

- **If parent plate is Mild Steel** → use **Mild Steel rivets** (avoid galvanic corrosion; matched thermal expansion).
- **If parent plate is Aluminium** → use **Aluminium rivets** (otherwise bimetallic corrosion destroys the joint).
- **If parent plate is Copper/Brass** → use **Copper rivets**.
- **If load is very high** → use **High Tensile Steel** (with hot riveting).
- **If environment is corrosive** → use **Monel or Aluminium-bronze** rivets.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula & Data Table for Rivet Material – KTU 2024**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---------------------|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Unwin's Formula (MS rivets) | $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ | d, t in mm | Quick rivet diameter for MS |
| 2 | Unwin's Formula (HS rivets) | $d = 5.2\sqrt{t}$ | d, t in mm | Quick rivet diameter for HS |
| 3 | Unwin's Formula (Al rivets) | $d = 6.6\sqrt{t}$ | d, t in mm | Quick rivet diameter for Al |
| 4 | Permissible Shear Stress (MS) | $P_s$ | **80 – 100 MPa** | Shear failure check |
| 5 | Permissible Crushing Stress (MS) | $P_c$ | **160 – 200 MPa** | Bearing failure check |
| 6 | Ratio (Pc / Ps) for MS | $\frac{P_c}{P_s}$ | **2.0** | Joint efficiency analysis |
| 7 | Shear Resistance per pitch | $P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot n_s \cdot \tau_s$ | N | Strength of joint |
| 8 | Crushing Resistance per pitch | $P_c = d \cdot t \cdot n_c \cdot \sigma_c$ | N | Strength of joint |
| 9 | Tearing Resistance of plate | $P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | N | Plate tearing check |
| 10 | Joint Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{weakest}}{P_{solid\,plate}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Final answer metric |

**Material Property Summary (Quick Recall):**

| Material | UTS (MPa) | Ps (MPa) | Pc (MPa) | Heading | Application |
|----------|-----------|----------|----------|---------|-------------|
| Mild Steel | 400–500 | 80–100 | 160–200 | Hot/Cold | Boiler, Bridge, Structure |
| High Tensile Steel | 600–800 | 120–150 | 240–300 | Hot | Heavy Machinery |
| Aluminium Alloy | 200–400 | 60–80 | 120–160 | Cold | Aircraft, Refrigeration |
| Copper | 200–250 | 50–70 | 100–140 | Cold | Sheet Metal, Electrical |
| Monel (Ni-Cu) | 500–600 | 100–120 | 200–240 | Cold | Chemical, Marine |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Boiler Shell Construction:** MS rivets of diameter 25–32 mm hot-driven; the rivet material must withstand both internal steam pressure (causing shear) and tube-sheet expansion (causing bearing).
- **Aircraft Fuselage (Historical — replaced by welding & composites):** Aluminium-alloy rivets (AA 2117, AA 2017) cold-driven — light, corrosion-resistant, fatigue-tolerant.
- **Ship Hull Plates:** MS rivets, hot-driven, with caulking edges to ensure watertightness; rivet material must be galvanically compatible with steel plates.
- **Railway Bridges (Historical):** High Tensile Steel rivets for main girders, MS rivets for secondary members — material selection by stress level.
- **Chemical Storage Tanks:** Monel or stainless-steel rivets when tank contents are acidic/alkaline — material selection by environment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Unwin's Formula – Why the Constant "6"?

Unwin's empirical formula for the diameter of a **Mild Steel rivet** is derived from the empirical relationship between rivet diameter and plate thickness, calibrated against experimental shearing and crushing tests on thousands of riveted specimens.

**Starting Premise (Empirical Fit):**

Empirical observations by Unwin on standard MS riveted joints showed that the ratio of rivet diameter $d$ to plate thickness $t$ follows:

$$\frac{d}{t} \approx k$$

where $k$ is a constant dependent on material. For mild steel, fitting the experimental data to a power law:

$$\frac{d}{t} = 6 \cdot t^{-0.5}$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Empirical relationship observed:} \quad & \frac{d}{t} = \frac{k}{\sqrt{t}} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Rearranging for } d: \quad & d = \frac{k \cdot t}{\sqrt{t}} = k \cdot \sqrt{t} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Substituting } k = 6 \text{ (for MS):} \quad & d = 6\sqrt{t} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Units consistency check:} \quad & \sqrt{t} \;[\sqrt{mm}] \;\Rightarrow\; d \text{ in mm with } k \text{ in mm}^{0.5}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Transition Explained:**
> The constant $k$ is **not dimensionless**. It carries the unit of $\sqrt{mm}$ so that the product $k \cdot \sqrt{t}$ yields a length in mm. For Mild Steel, the value $k = 6$ corresponds to a rivet whose shear area ($\frac{\pi}{4}d^2$) and bearing area ($d \cdot t$) are approximately balanced — i.e., the joint is **equally strong in shear and crushing**. This balance gives the maximum joint efficiency.

**Verification for MS (k = 6):**

For $t = 10$ mm:
$$d = 6\sqrt{10} = 6 \times 3.162 = 18.97 \text{ mm} \approx 19 \text{ mm}$$

Check shear vs crushing balance:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Shear strength of one rivet:} \quad & P_s = \frac{\pi}{4}(19)^2 \cdot 90 = 25{,}524 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
\text{Crushing strength (single shear, single plate):} \quad & P_c = 19 \cdot 10 \cdot 180 = 34{,}200 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
\text{Ratio } \frac{P_s}{P_c}: \quad & \frac{25{,}524}{34{,}200} \approx 0.75
\end{aligned}$$

This confirms Unwin's constant of 6 strikes a reasonable strength balance for mild steel rivets in standard joints.

**For High Tensile Steel (k = 5.2):**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{HS} & = 5.2\sqrt{t} \\[4pt]
\text{For } t = 10 \text{ mm:} \quad & d = 5.2 \times 3.162 = 16.44 \text{ mm} \approx 16 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logical Transition:** Because high tensile steel has a **higher Ps and Pc** than mild steel, a *smaller diameter* is sufficient to develop the same strength. Hence $k$ is **smaller** for higher-strength materials.

**For Aluminium Alloy (k = 6.6):**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{Al} & = 6.6\sqrt{t} \\[4pt]
\text{For } t = 10 \text{ mm:} \quad & d = 6.6 \times 3.162 = 20.87 \text{ mm} \approx 21 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logical Transition:** Aluminium has **lower Ps** than MS, so the rivet must be **larger in diameter** to compensate. Hence $k$ is **larger** for weaker materials.

### 3.2 Derivation of Joint Efficiency Expression

The efficiency of a riveted joint is defined as:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Strength of the riveted joint at the weakest section}}{\text{Strength of the un-riveted (solid) plate}}$$

**Step-by-Step:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Solid plate strength:} \quad & P_{solid} = (p \cdot t) \cdot \sigma_t \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Weakest joint strength (e.g., tearing):} \quad & P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Substituting into } \eta \text{ formula:} \quad & \eta = \frac{(p-d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Cancelling } t \text{ and } \sigma_t: \quad & \eta = \frac{p - d}{p} = 1 - \frac{d}{p}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Material Selection Decision Matrix (Analytical)

The table below is the **engineering decision matrix** that the KTU examiner expects you to apply when justifying your rivet material choice:

| Selection Criterion | Best Material Choice | Justification (Quantitative / Physical) |
|---------------------|----------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Highest Strength-to-Weight Ratio | Aluminium Alloy (AA 7075) | $\rho \approx 2.8$ g/cc, Sut $\approx 570$ MPa → $S/\rho \approx 200$ kNm/kg |
| Cheapest with Adequate Strength | Mild Steel | Cost ₹60–80/kg, readily available, hot-formable |
| Maximum Corrosion Resistance | Monel (Ni-Cu 70/30) | Resists H₂SO₄, HF, seawater |
| Galvanic Match with Aluminium Plate | Aluminium Rivet | Same metal → no galvanic cell |
| Maximum Fatigue Resistance | High Tensile Steel (Ni-Cr-Mo) | Fine-grained, tempered martensite |
| High Temperature Service (>300 °C) | Alloy Steel (Cr-Mo) | Creep-resistant up to 500 °C |
| Electrical Conductivity | Copper (ETP grade) | Conductivity > 100 % IACS |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Rivet Material Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RIVET MATERIAL SELECTION]:::root --> B{Load Magnitude?}
    A --> C{Service Environment?}
    A --> D{Parent Plate Material?}

    B -->|Moderate| E[Mild Steel MS]
    B -->|High| F[High Tensile Steel HS]
    B -->|Very High| G[Alloy Steel Ni-Cr-Mo]

    C -->|Corrosive Marine| H[Monel Ni-Cu]
    C -->|Lightweight / Aerospace| I[Aluminium Alloy]
    C -->|General Atmosphere| E
    C -->|High Temp > 300C| G

    D -->|Steel Plates| E
    D -->|Aluminium Plates| I
    D -->|Copper Plates| J[Copper Rivet]
    D -->|Stainless Plates| K[Stainless Steel Rivet]

    E --> L{Heading Method?}
    L -->|d le 10 mm| M[Cold Heading]
    L -->|d gt 10 mm| N[Hot Heading at 900 to 1100 C]

    F --> N
    I --> O[Cold Heading Only]
    H --> O
    J --> O

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef mat fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **root node (A)** is the engineering decision: choosing a rivet material.
- Three **primary criteria branches** (B, C, D) represent the dominant selection drivers.
- Each criterion resolves to a **specific material class** (yellow nodes: E, F, G, H, I, J, K).
- The **green terminal nodes (L → M, N, O)** represent the **manufacturing constraint** — the heading method dictated by the chosen material and rivet diameter.

### 4.2 Material Property Comparison – Visual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MS[MILD STEEL]
        MS1[UTS 400 to 500 MPa]:::mild
        MS2[Ps 80 to 100 MPa]:::mild
        MS3[Pc 160 to 200 MPa]:::mild
        MS4[Cost LOW]:::mild
    end

    subgraph HS[HIGH TENSILE STEEL]
        HS1[UTS 600 to 800 MPa]:::high
        HS2[Ps 120 to 150 MPa]:::high
        HS3[Pc 240 to 300 MPa]:::high
        HS4[Cost MEDIUM]:::high
    end

    subgraph AL[ALUMINIUM ALLOY]
        AL1[UTS 200 to 400 MPa]:::alu
        AL2[Ps 60 to 80 MPa]:::alu
        AL3[Pc 120 to 160 MPa]:::alu
        AL4[Weight VERY LOW]:::alu
    end

    MS -->|Lower strength, hot driven| COMPARE{JOINT DESIGN DECISION}
    HS -->|Higher strength, hot driven| COMPARE
    AL -->|Lightweight, cold driven| COMPARE

    COMPARE --> FINAL[Select Material with Ps and Pc that balance shear and crushing failure modes]:::decision

    classDef mild fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef high fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef alu fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Reading:** The diagram visually emphasises the trade-off between **strength**, **cost**, **weight**, and **forming method**. KTU examiners frequently award marks for showing such comparative reasoning rather than listing material names in isolation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

*State any four desirable properties required for the material used in the manufacture of rivets. Also name two commonly used rivet materials.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The following four properties are essential for rivet material:

1. **High Ductility** – to permit plastic deformation during forming of the second (shop) head without cracking.

2. **Sufficient Shear Strength** – so that the rivet does not fail in shear under the design load of the joint.

3. **Adequate Bearing / Crushing Strength** – to prevent hole elongation and localised crushing of the plate.

4. **Galvanic Compatibility with the Parent Plate** – to avoid bimetallic (galvanic) corrosion in service environments.

**Two commonly used materials:**
- Mild Steel (for structural and boiler joints)
- Aluminium Alloy (for aircraft and lightweight structures)

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for listing 4 properties + 0.5 mark each for material names = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

*Write Unwin's formula for the diameter of a rivet when the rivet material is (i) Mild Steel, (ii) High Tensile Steel, and (iii) Aluminium. Comment on the variation of the constant.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Mild Steel:} \quad & d = 6\sqrt{t} \\[4pt]
\text{(ii) High Tensile Steel:} \quad & d = 5.2\sqrt{t} \\[4pt]
\text{(iii) Aluminium:} \quad & d = 6.6\sqrt{t}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** The constant **decreases** as the **strength of the rivet material increases** (HS = 5.2 < MS = 6.0 < Al = 6.6). This is because a stronger material requires a **smaller cross-section** to develop the same shear/crushing strength, hence the diameter reduces for a given plate thickness. Conversely, weaker materials (like Aluminium) require **larger diameters** to compensate for their lower Ps and Pc values, giving a higher Unwin's constant.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark each for the three formulae + 1 mark for the comment = 3 marks, but compressed to 3-mark short answer with each formula carrying ~0.75 mark and the comment carrying ~0.75 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various materials used for rivets, comparing their properties and typical applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A double-riveted lap joint is used to connect two mild steel plates, each 12 mm thick. The rivets are 20 mm diameter and are made of mild steel. Determine:
- (i) the shear strength of the joint per pitch length,
- (ii) the crushing strength of the joint per pitch length,
- (iii) the efficiency of the joint.

Take permissible stresses for the rivet material as: Ps = 90 MPa, Pc = 180 MPa, and for the plate as σt = 140 MPa.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Detailed Explanation of Rivet Materials [7 Marks]:**

**1. Mild Steel (MS) – Most Common:**
- Composition: 0.15–0.25 % C, 0.5–0.8 % Mn, balance Fe.
- Sut = 400–500 MPa; Ps = 80–100 MPa; Pc = 160–200 MPa.
- Heading: Hot-driven for d > 10 mm at 900–1100 °C; Cold for d ≤ 10 mm.
- Applications: Structural steel work, boiler shells, bridges, ship hulls, railway wagons, building frames.
- Advantages: Cheap, ductile, easily available, hot-formable.
- Disadvantages: Corrodes in marine atmosphere; lower strength-to-weight ratio.

**2. High Tensile Steel (HS):**
- Composition: Medium carbon steel + alloying elements (Ni, Cr, Mo).
- Sut = 600–800 MPa; Ps = 120–150 MPa; Pc = 240–300 MPa.
- Heading: Hot-driven; quenched and tempered after driving for high strength.
- Applications: Heavy machinery frames, military bridges, railway girders under high cyclic loads.
- Advantage: Higher strength allows fewer/smaller rivets → lighter joint.
- Disadvantage: More expensive, less ductile, requires controlled heat treatment.

**3. Aluminium and Aluminium Alloys:**
- Composition: AA 2117 (Al-Cu-Mg), AA 6056 (Al-Mg-Si), AA 7075 (Al-Zn-Mg-Cu).
- Sut = 200–570 MPa depending on alloy; Ps = 60–150 MPa.
- Heading: Always cold-driven (cannot be hot-driven due to low melting point ~660 °C).
- Applications: Aircraft fuselage and wing skins, refrigeration panels, ship superstructures.
- Advantage: Lightweight (ρ ≈ 2.7 g/cc), excellent corrosion resistance.
- Disadvantage: Lower absolute strength; galvanic corrosion when joined to steel without insulation.

**4. Copper Rivets:**
- Composition: Pure copper (ETP grade, 99.9 % Cu).
- Sut = 200–250 MPa.
- Heading: Cold-driven.
- Applications: Electrical connections, copper sheet metal work (roofing, gutters), non-ferrous plate joints.
- Advantage: Excellent electrical conductivity; corrosion-resistant in atmosphere.
- Disadvantage: Expensive relative to MS; soft → low bearing strength.

**5. Brass and Bronze Rivets:**
- Composition: Cu-Zn (brass) or Cu-Sn (bronze); sometimes Al-bronze.
- Applications: Marine (dezincification-resistant), decorative work, plumbing.
- Advantage: Corrosion-resistant in seawater; good aesthetic finish.
- Disadvantage: Expensive; requires special tools.

**6. Monel Metal Rivets:**
- Composition: Ni 70 % + Cu 30 % (with traces of Fe, Mn).
- Sut = 500–600 MPa; excellent corrosion resistance in acids and seawater.
- Heading: Cold-driven.
- Applications: Chemical plants, marine propellers, acid storage tanks.
- Disadvantage: Very expensive; limited availability.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:**
- Plate thickness t = 12 mm (mild steel)
- Rivet diameter d = 20 mm (mild steel)
- Type: Double-riveted **lap joint**
- Ps = 90 MPa, Pc = 180 MPa, σt = 140 MPa

**Step 1: Pitch of Rivets (p):**
For double-riveted lap joint, Unwin's formula gives:
$$d = 6\sqrt{t} = 6\sqrt{12} = 6 \times 3.464 = 20.78 \text{ mm}$$

Given d = 20 mm, this is consistent with Unwin's recommendation. ✓

Pitch p = 3d + 6 = 3(20) + 6 = **66 mm** (standard empirical pitch).

**Step 2: Shear Strength per Pitch Length:**
For a **lap joint**, the rivets are in **single shear**:
$$P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau_s \cdot n$$

where n = number of rivets per pitch = 2 (double riveted).

$$\begin{aligned}
P_s & = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (20)^2 \cdot 90 \cdot 2 \\[4pt]
& = \frac{3.1416}{4} \cdot 400 \cdot 90 \cdot 2 \\[4pt]
& = 0.7854 \cdot 400 \cdot 180 \\[4pt]
& = 0.7854 \cdot 72{,}000 \\[4pt]
& = 56{,}548.8 \text{ N} \approx \boxed{56.55 \text{ kN}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Crushing Strength per Pitch Length:**
For lap joint, each rivet crushes against one plate thickness:
$$P_c = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c \cdot n$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_c & = 20 \cdot 12 \cdot 180 \cdot 2 \\[4pt]
& = 240 \cdot 360 \\[4pt]
& = 86{,}400 \text{ N} = \boxed{86.4 \text{ kN}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Tearing Strength of Plate (per pitch length, worst case):**
$$P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = (66 - 20) \cdot 12 \cdot 140$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_t & = 46 \cdot 12 \cdot 140 \\[4pt]
& = 552 \cdot 140 \\[4pt]
& = 77{,}280 \text{ N} = 77.28 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Strength of Solid Plate (per pitch length):**
$$P_{solid} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 66 \cdot 12 \cdot 140 = 110{,}880 \text{ N} = 110.88 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Identify Weakest Section:**
Comparing the three failure loads:
$$P_s = 56.55 \text{ kN} \;\; < \;\; P_t = 77.28 \text{ kN} \;\; < \;\; P_c = 86.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Weakest = Shear Failure** → Governing strength $P_{joint} = 56.55$ kN.

**Step 7: Joint Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{P_{weakest}}{P_{solid}} \times 100\% = \frac{56.55}{110.88} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 51.0\%}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the factors governing the selection of rivet material. Why is mild steel the most common choice, and under what circumstances would you replace it with high tensile steel or aluminium alloy?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-riveted lap joint connects two aluminium plates, each 8 mm thick. The rivets are made of aluminium alloy with diameter 14 mm. Determine:
- (i) the joint efficiency,
- (ii) the safe load the joint can carry per pitch length.

Take permissible stresses for aluminium rivet: Ps = 70 MPa, Pc = 140 MPa; for aluminium plate: σt = 100 MPa.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Discussion of Rivet Material Selection Factors [7 Marks]:**

The selection of rivet material is governed by the following **five primary factors**:

**1. Strength of the Parent Plate:**
The rivet must have a strength **equal to or greater than** the plate to avoid being the weak link. For high-strength plates (e.g., HS steel, 600 MPa), MS rivets would fail first; hence HS rivets are used.

**2. Type of Loading:**
- **Static Load:** MS rivets are adequate.
- **Cyclic / Fatigue Load:** HS steel with fine grain (tempered martensite) is preferred.
- **Impact Load:** Tough alloy steels (Ni-Cr-Mo) are required.

**3. Service Environment:**
- **Atmospheric:** MS rivets acceptable with paint coating.
- **Marine / Salt-laden:** Monel or aluminium-bronze rivets.
- **Chemical / Acidic:** Stainless steel or Monel rivets.
- **High Temperature (>300 °C):** Alloy steel (Cr-Mo-V).

**4. Galvanic Compatibility:**
The rivet and plate must be **closely matched** in the **galvanic series** to avoid bimetallic corrosion. Hence:
- Aluminium plates → Aluminium rivets (NOT steel rivets — would cause rapid corrosion of aluminium plate due to galvanic cell).
- Copper plates → Copper or brass rivets.
- Steel plates → MS or HS rivets.

**5. Weight and Formability:**
- **Aerospace:** Aluminium rivets (cold-driven, ρ = 2.7 g/cc).
- **Heavy structural:** MS rivets (hot-driven for large d, ρ = 7.8 g/cc).

**Why Mild Steel is Most Common [2 Marks]:**
- **Lowest cost** among metallic fasteners.
- **Adequate strength** for 80 % of engineering applications (boilers, bridges, buildings, ships).
- **Easily hot-formed** into heads at 900–1100 °C.
- **Compatible** with the most widely used parent plate (steel).
- **Available in all standard sizes** with IS 1929 specifications.

**When to Replace Mild Steel [2 Marks]:**
- **Use HS Steel** when: (i) joint strength is inadequate with MS, (ii) weight saving is required by reducing number/size of rivets, (iii) fatigue resistance is critical.
- **Use Aluminium** when: (i) parent plate is aluminium (galvanic compatibility), (ii) weight minimisation is essential (aircraft, aerospace), (iii) cold-driving only is feasible.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution for Aluminium Joint [7 Marks]:**

**Given:**
- Plate: Aluminium, t = 8 mm
- Rivet: Aluminium alloy, d = 14 mm
- Joint: Single-riveted lap joint
- Ps = 70 MPa, Pc = 140 MPa, σt = 100 MPa

**Step 1: Pitch of Rivets:**
For single-riveted lap joint (aluminium), Unwin's formula gives:
$$d = 6.6\sqrt{t} = 6.6\sqrt{8} = 6.6 \times 2.828 = 18.67 \text{ mm}$$

Given d = 14 mm — this is **smaller** than Unwin's recommendation, suggesting a conservative design choice.

Pitch p = 3d + 6 = 3(14) + 6 = **48 mm**

**Step 2: Shear Strength per Pitch:**
Single rivet in single shear (lap joint):
$$P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (14)^2 \cdot 70$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_s & = 0.7854 \cdot 196 \cdot 70 \\[4pt]
& = 0.7854 \cdot 13{,}720 \\[4pt]
& = 10{,}776 \text{ N} \approx 10.78 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Crushing Strength per Pitch:**
$$P_c = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c = 14 \cdot 8 \cdot 140 = 15{,}680 \text{ N} = 15.68 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Tearing Strength of Plate:**
$$P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = (48 - 14) \cdot 8 \cdot 100 = 34 \cdot 800 = 27{,}200 \text{ N} = 27.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Strength of Solid Plate:**
$$P_{solid} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = 48 \cdot 8 \cdot 100 = 38{,}400 \text{ N} = 38.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Identify Weakest Mode:**
$$P_s = 10.78 \text{ kN} \;\; < \;\; P_c = 15.68 \text{ kN} \;\; < \;\; P_t = 27.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Weakest = Shear Failure** → $P_{joint} = 10.78$ kN.

**Step 7: Joint Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{P_{joint}}{P_{solid}} \times 100\% = \frac{10.78}{38.4} \times 100\% = \boxed{28.07\%}$$

**Step 8: Safe Load per Pitch Length:**
$$\boxed{P_{safe} = 10.78 \text{ kN}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Number of Rivets per Pitch (n):** In Part B numerical, students often write $\frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau_s$ for *double-riveted* joint and forget to multiply by **n = 2**. This costs **2 marks** instantly.
>
> 2. **Confusing Shear Planes in Lap vs Butt Joints:** Lap joint = **single shear** (n = 1 plane per rivet); Butt joint with double cover plates = **double shear** (n = 2 planes per rivet). Mixing these costs another **1–2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Mixing up Unwin's Constants:** Memorising only the MS formula and writing $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ for aluminium or HS steel loses **1 mark** and shows incomplete preparation.
>
> 4. **Using Plate's σt for Rivet Strength Check:** A frequent error — students substitute σt into the shear formula instead of Ps. The rivet has its **own** Ps and Pc, separate from the plate.
>
> 5. **Not Justifying Material Selection:** In Part A theory answers, merely naming "Mild Steel" without stating its UTS/Ps/Pc values and application context loses the **justification marks** which KTU examiners specifically look for.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Material for Rivets (KTU 2024 Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Rivet material must satisfy **ductility** (for heading) + **shear/bearing strength** (for load carrying).

- **Most Common Material:** **Mild Steel** — used in 80 %+ of riveted joints (boilers, bridges, structures).

- **Unwin's Formula – Three Variants to Memorise:**
  - MS: $d = 6\sqrt{t}$
  - HS: $d = 5.2\sqrt{t}$
  - Al: $d = 6.6\sqrt{t}$

- **Standard Permissible Stresses (Recall Values):**
  - MS: Ps = 90 MPa, Pc = 180 MPa (ratio Pc/Ps = 2.0).
  - HS: Ps = 130 MPa, Pc = 260 MPa.
  - Al: Ps = 70 MPa, Pc = 140 MPa.

- **Heading Methods:**
  - **Hot Heading** (at 900–1100 °C): Steel rivets with d > 10 mm.
  - **Cold Heading**: Aluminium, copper, brass, monel, and small-diameter MS rivets.

- **Galvanic Compatibility Rule:** Rivet metal ≈ Parent plate metal (same galvanic series position).

- **Special Applications:**
  - Aircraft → Aluminium alloy rivets.
  - Marine → Monel / Al-bronze rivets.
  - High-strength structures → High tensile steel rivets.

- **IS Code Reference:** **IS 1929:1982** — Specification for rivet materials.

- **Efficiency Benchmark:** A well-designed double-riveted MS joint achieves efficiency of **60–80 %**.

- **Quick Mnemonic for Rivet Material Properties:** **"DASH-C"** — **D**uctility, **A**dequate strength, **S**hear capacity, **H**ot/Cold formability, **C**orrosion resistance.

- **Numerical Pattern to Master:**
  1. Compute d (Unwin's formula).
  2. Compute pitch p (Unwin's rule: p = 3d + 6 for structural joints).
  3. Compute $P_s$, $P_c$, $P_t$.
  4. Identify weakest mode.
  5. Compute $\eta = \frac{P_{weakest}}{P_{solid}}$.

- **Common KTU Trick Question:** "Why is aluminium not used for hot riveting?" → **Answer:** Aluminium's melting point (~660 °C) is too close to typical hot-heading temperatures (900–1100 °C), so it would melt or lose temper. Hence **aluminium is always cold-driven.**

- **Key Distinction:** Rivet material ≠ Plate material in *strength*; they are *similar* in *chemical composition* to avoid galvanic corrosion. This subtle point is frequently tested.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modes of Failure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modes of Failure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Modes of Failure - Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

In Machine Design (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2), **Modes of Failure** are defined as the various mechanisms by which a machine component loses its functional capability or becomes unfit for its intended service under the influence of applied loads, environmental conditions, or time-dependent degradation processes. 

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCMET602, every machine component must be designed by first identifying the **probable modes of failure** it may experience during its service life, and then ensuring that the component has adequate resistance against each identified mode. The design process is incomplete until each mode is quantitatively addressed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The modes of failure form the foundational framework from which all design equations, factor-of-safety selections, and material selection decisions are derived. Every subsequent formula in Machine Design traces back to a specific failure mode.

## The Ten Principal Modes of Failure

The standard classification adopted in KTU-aligned Machine Design references recognizes the following principal modes:

1. **Yielding (Plastic Deformation)** — Permanent deformation when stress exceeds the yield strength.
2. **Fracture (Rupture)** — Complete separation of the material into two or more pieces, which may be ductile or brittle.
3. **Fatigue Failure** — Failure under the action of fluctuating/cyclic stresses below the static yield strength.
4. **Creep** — Time-dependent progressive deformation occurring at elevated temperatures (typically above 0.4 × melting temperature).
5. **Wear** — Progressive loss of material from contacting surfaces due to relative motion.
6. **Corrosion** — Chemical or electrochemical deterioration of the material due to environmental exposure.
7. **Buckling (Instability)** — Sudden lateral deflection of slender members under compressive loading.
8. **Distortion (Excessive Elastic Deformation)** — Failure of function due to elastic deflection beyond permissible limits.
9. **Impact Failure** — Failure due to sudden shock loading with high strain rates.
10. **Thermal Failure** — Failure induced by thermal stresses arising from temperature gradients or constrained thermal expansion.

## Conceptual Analogy - The Human Body Perspective

Imagine a machine component as a **human bone** under different stress conditions:

- A bone subjected to a single sudden heavy load may **fracture (break)** → this is *fracture failure*.
- A bone subjected to repeated running (cyclic load) may develop micro-cracks leading to a **stress fracture** → this is *fatigue failure*.
- A bone held under a constant moderate load for hours may slowly bend → this is *creep*.
- A joint subjected to constant rubbing may wear out at the cartilage → this is *wear failure*.
- A steel rod exposed to salty seawater may rust and weaken → this is *corrosion failure*.

Just as a doctor identifies the *type* of stress a patient is experiencing before prescribing treatment, a machine designer must first identify the *mode of failure* before selecting material, geometry, and factor of safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A single component may be vulnerable to *multiple modes of failure simultaneously*. For example, a turbine blade experiences creep, fatigue, and corrosion concurrently. The designer must satisfy design criteria for **all** applicable modes.

## Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard engineering constants and metrics are used universally in failure analysis:

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | g | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Density of structural steel | ρ | **7850** | kg/m³ |
| Modulus of Elasticity (Steel) | E | **2 × 10⁵** | MPa (N/mm²) |
| Modulus of Elasticity (Aluminum) | E | **0.7 × 10⁵** | MPa |
| Modulus of Rigidity (Steel) | G | **0.8 × 10⁵** | MPa |
| Poisson's Ratio (Steel) | ν | **0.30** | dimensionless |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Classification of Modes of Failure - Structured Logic

### A. Categorization by Nature of Loading

The KTU 2024 framework categorizes modes of failure based on the *type of load* applied:

**1. Static Loading Modes**
- **Yielding** — governed by the yield criterion (von Mises / Maximum Shear Stress).
- **Fracture (Brittle)** — governed by Maximum Normal Stress criterion.
- **Buckling** — governed by Euler's critical load formula.
- **Distortion** — governed by deflection equations and stiffness criteria.

**2. Dynamic Loading Modes**
- **Impact Failure** — governed by strain energy absorption capacity.
- **Fatigue Failure** — governed by endurance limit and S-N curves.
- **Cumulative Damage** — governed by Miner's rule for variable amplitude loading.

**3. Time-Dependent Modes**
- **Creep** — governed by stress-temperature-time relationships (e.g., Norton-Bailey).
- **Stress Relaxation** — reduction of stress at constant strain over time.
- **Wear** — governed by Archard's wear equation.
- **Corrosion** — governed by Faraday's laws of electrochemistry.

### B. The "Why" Behind Each Mode

- **Why does yielding occur?** Because the internal stress at a critical point exceeds the yield strength (Sy) of the material, causing dislocations to move freely and produce permanent deformation.

- **Why does fatigue occur?** Because cyclic stresses initiate micro-cracks at points of stress concentration (surface defects, inclusions, geometric discontinuities), which propagate with each cycle until catastrophic fracture occurs.

- **Why does creep occur?** Because at elevated temperatures (typically T > 0.4 Tm where Tm = melting point in Kelvin), atomic diffusion enables grain boundary sliding and dislocation climb, allowing time-dependent plastic flow even under stresses below the yield strength at room temperature.

- **Why does buckling occur?** Because slender compressive members possess an equilibrium configuration that becomes unstable when the applied load reaches the Euler critical load, after which any perturbation causes large lateral deflection.

- **Why does wear occur?** Because contacting surfaces under load experience asperity-level contact, micro-welding, and material removal through adhesion, abrasion, or fatigue of surface layers.

- **Why does corrosion occur?** Because metals in contact with electrolytes undergo electrochemical oxidation-reduction reactions, converting metallic atoms into ions and weakening the cross-section.

### C. The Design Equation for Each Mode

For every mode of failure, the fundamental design equation follows the universal pattern:

$$\text{Design Stress (induced)} \leq \frac{\text{Material Strength (resisting)}}{\text{Factor of Safety}}$$

This translates to specific equations such as:

- **Yielding:** σ ≤ Sy / n
- **Fracture:** σ ≤ Sut / n
- **Fatigue:** σa ≤ Se / n
- **Buckling:** P ≤ Pcr / n
- **Deflection:** δ ≤ δpermissible

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the **complete high-yield formula set** for Modes of Failure analysis. Memorize these derivations and boundary conditions for KTU University Examinations.

| Mode of Failure | Governing Formula | Variable Definitions | Boundary Condition / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Yielding (Static)** | σmax = Sy / n | σmax = max principal stress, Sy = yield strength, n = factor of safety | σmax must not exceed proportional limit |
| **Brittle Fracture** | σmax = Sut / n | Sut = ultimate tensile strength | Use for brittle materials like cast iron, ceramics |
| **Ductile Fracture** | σmax = Sut / n (after yielding) | Material yields first, then strain hardens | Use when ductility is sufficient |
| **Euler Buckling** | Pcr = π²EI / L²e | I = moment of inertia, Le = effective length | Valid for slenderness ratio (L/k) > critical slenderness ratio |
| **Johnson Buckling** | Pcr = A·[σy − (σy·L/k)²/(4π²E)] | A = cross-section area, k = radius of gyration | Valid for moderate slenderness ratios |
| **Critical Slenderness Ratio** | (L/k)c = √(2π²E/σy) | Transition between Euler and Johnson | Determines which formula applies |
| **Deflection Criterion** | ymax ≤ δpermitted | ymax = maximum deflection | Serviceability requirement, not strength |
| **Endurance Limit (Steel)** | S'e = 0.5·Sut | Empirical relationship | For Sut < 1400 MPa |
| **Endurance Limit (non-Steel)** | S'e = 0.4·Sut | For non-ferrous metals | Aluminum, copper alloys |
| **Endurance Limit (Brittle)** | S'e = 0.36·Sut | For brittle materials | Cast iron, concrete |
| **Modified Endurance Limit** | Se = ka·kb·kc·kd·ke·S'e | ka = surface factor, kb = size factor, kc = load factor, kd = temperature factor, ke = reliability factor | Marin equation for fatigue |
| **Stress Concentration Factor** | Kt = σmax / σnom | σmax = peak stress at discontinuity | σmax = Kt · σnom |
| **Fatigue Stress Concentration** | Kf = 1 + q·(Kt − 1) | q = notch sensitivity (0 to 1) | Kf = effective fatigue stress concentration factor |
| **Notch Sensitivity** | q = 1/(1 + √a/r) | a = Neuber's constant, r = notch radius | Neuber's equation |
| **Mean Stress (Fatigue)** | σm = (σmax + σmin)/2 | Used in Goodman, Soderberg, Gerber diagrams | Correction for non-zero mean stress |
| **Stress Amplitude (Fatigue)** | σa = (σmax − σmin)/2 | Used in fatigue criteria | Stress range / 2 |
| **Stress Ratio** | R = σmin / σmax | R = -1 for fully reversed | Defines loading type |
| **Goodman Criterion** | σa/Se + σm/Sut = 1/n | Linear mean stress correction | Most conservative, widely used in KTU |
| **Soderberg Criterion** | σa/Se + σm/Sy = 1/n | Conservative, based on yield | Most conservative mean stress correction |
| **Gerber Criterion** | σa/Se + (σm/Sut)² = 1/n | Parabolic, less conservative | Best fit for ductile materials |
| **Goodman with Yield** | 1/n = σa/Se + σm/Sut | Modified for yield prevention | ASME preferred |
| **Strain Energy (Impact)** | U = (1/2)·F·δ = (σ²·V)/(2E) | Energy absorbed by impact loading | Equate kinetic energy to strain energy |
| **Creep Rate (Norton-Bailey)** | ε̇ = A·σⁿ·exp(−Q/RT) | A, n = material constants, Q = activation energy, T = absolute temperature | Valid for steady-state creep |
| **Larson-Miller Parameter** | LMP = T·(C + log t) | C ≈ 20 typically, t = rupture time (hours) | Used for creep rupture prediction |
| **Archard Wear Equation** | V = k·(F·L)/(3·H) | k = wear coefficient, F = normal load, L = sliding distance, H = hardness | Volume of material removed |
| **Faraday's Law (Corrosion)** | m = (I·t·M)/(n·F) | I = current, t = time, M = molar mass, n = electrons transferred, F = Faraday constant = 96485 C/mol | Mass loss due to corrosion |

## Real-World Engineering Utility

| Mode of Failure | Industry Application | Design Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Yielding | Structural beams, pressure vessels | Use ductile materials, limit stress below Sy |
| Fatigue | Aircraft wings, crankshafts, bridges | Use high-cycle fatigue-resistant alloys, surface treatments |
| Creep | Gas turbine blades, boiler tubes | Use superalloys (Inconel, Nimonic), operating temperature limits |
| Wear | Bearings, gears, brake pads | Surface hardening, lubrication, material selection |
| Corrosion | Marine structures, chemical plants | Coatings, cathodic protection, stainless steels |
| Buckling | Columns, struts, thin-walled pressure vessels | Increase cross-section radius of gyration, add stiffeners |
| Impact | Hammers, rock drills, automotive crumple zones | Use high-toughness materials, energy-absorbing geometry |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Derivation of the Critical Slenderness Ratio for Column Buckling

The critical slenderness ratio marks the transition point between **long columns** (governed by Euler buckling) and **intermediate columns** (governed by the Johnson parabolic formula). At this transition point, both equations predict the same critical load.

### Step 1: Write the Euler Buckling Equation

The Euler critical load for a column is given by:

$$P_{cr,Euler} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{L_e^2}$$

where:
- E = Young's Modulus of the material
- I = Moment of inertia of the cross-section
- Le = Effective length of the column based on end conditions

### Step 2: Express the Euler Equation in Terms of Slenderness Ratio

The slenderness ratio is defined as:

$$\frac{L_e}{k} = \frac{L_e}{\sqrt{I/A}}$$

where k = radius of gyration and A = cross-sectional area.

Therefore, I = A · k², and substituting into the Euler equation:

$$P_{cr,Euler} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot A \cdot k^2}{L_e^2}$$

### Step 3: Convert to Average Critical Stress

Divide both sides by the cross-sectional area A:

$$\sigma_{cr,Euler} = \frac{P_{cr,Euler}}{A} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot k^2}{L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E}{(L_e/k)^2}$$

**Explanation of step:** This conversion gives the critical stress (load per unit area) instead of the total critical load, allowing us to compare with the material yield strength directly.

### Step 4: Write the Johnson Parabolic Formula

For intermediate columns, the Johnson formula is:

$$\sigma_{cr,Johnson} = \sigma_y - \frac{\sigma_y^2}{4\pi^2 E} \cdot \left(\frac{L_e}{k}\right)^2$$

where σy = yield strength of the material.

### Step 5: Equate the Two Expressions at the Transition Point

At the transition, σcr,Euler = σcr,Johnson = σy (the yield stress):

$$\sigma_y = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E}{(L_e/k)_c^2}$$

### Step 6: Solve for the Critical Slenderness Ratio (L/k)c

Rearranging the equation:

$$(L_e/k)_c^2 = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E}{\sigma_y}$$

Taking the square root of both sides:

$$\left(\frac{L_e}{k}\right)_c = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 \cdot E}{\sigma_y}} = \pi \cdot \sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_y}}$$

This is the final derived expression for the critical slenderness ratio.

**Interpretation:** For columns with slenderness ratio greater than this value, Euler's formula applies (elastic buckling). For columns with slenderness ratio below this value, the Johnson formula applies (inelastic buckling / yielding).

---

## Derivation 2: Derivation of Notch Sensitivity (q) Using Neuber's Equation

Neuber's equation is widely used in machine design to compute the fatigue stress concentration factor Kf from the geometric stress concentration factor Kt.

### Step 1: State the Definition of Notch Sensitivity

The notch sensitivity (q) quantifies how sensitive a material is to the presence of a notch. It is defined as:

$$q = \frac{K_f - 1}{K_t - 1}$$

where:
- Kt = theoretical (geometric) stress concentration factor
- Kf = fatigue stress concentration factor (effective)

The value of q ranges from 0 (material completely insensitive to notch) to 1 (material fully sensitive to notch).

### Step 2: Rearrange the Definition to Solve for Kf

$$K_f = 1 + q \cdot (K_t - 1)$$

### Step 3: Substitute Neuber's Empirical Formula for q

Neuber observed that for a given material and notch radius r, the value of q can be expressed as:

$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a/r}}$$

where a = Neuber's constant (a material property, in mm).

### Step 4: Final Combined Expression for Kf

Substituting Neuber's q into the Kf equation:

$$K_f = 1 + \frac{K_t - 1}{1 + \sqrt{a/r}}$$

This equation allows the designer to compute the **effective fatigue stress concentration factor** using only the geometric factor (Kt from charts), notch radius (r), and Neubert's constant (a from material tables).

**Limitation Note:** This equation is valid for r values up to approximately 2 to 4 times the Neuber constant, depending on the material's ductility.

---

## Derivation 3: Soderberg Line Derivation from Combined Loading

The Soderberg criterion provides the most conservative relationship between alternating stress (σa), mean stress (σm), endurance limit (Se), and yield strength (Sy).

### Step 1: Recognize the Two Failure Criteria Simultaneously

For a fluctuating stress, two failure modes must be prevented:
- **Fatigue failure:** σa must not exceed Se
- **Yield failure:** σmax (= σa + σm) must not exceed Sy

### Step 2: Write the Two Failure Inequalities

Fatigue: σa ≤ Se (for σm = 0)
Yielding: σa + σm ≤ Sy (for σa = 0)

### Step 3: Linear Interpolation Between the Two Failure Points

The two endpoints define a line in (σa, σm) space:
- Point 1: (σa = Se, σm = 0)
- Point 2: (σa = 0, σm = Sy)

Using two-point form of a line:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = 1$$

### Step 4: Introduce Factor of Safety n

For a design factor of safety n:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \frac{1}{n}$$

This is the Soderberg equation.

### Step 5: Solve for the Allowable Stress Amplitude

Rearranging to express σa in terms of σm:

$$\sigma_a = \frac{S_e}{n} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y}\right)$$

**Interpretation:** When σm is positive (tensile mean stress), the allowable σa decreases linearly. When σm is negative (compressive mean stress), the allowable σa increases (beneficial compressive residual stresses).

### Step 6: Compare with Goodman Criterion

The Goodman criterion uses Sut instead of Sy at the second endpoint:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

Since Sy < Sut, the Soderberg line lies **inside** the Goodman line in the (σa, σm) plane, making Soderberg more conservative.

---

## Derivations 4 and 5: Additional Derivations

### Derivation 4: Mean Stress and Amplitude Extraction from a Sinusoidal Loading

For a fluctuating load F(t) = Fm + Fa·sin(ωt):

$$F_{max} = F_m + F_a$$
$$F_{min} = F_m - F_a$$

Solving simultaneously:

$$F_m = \frac{F_{max} + F_{min}}{2}$$
$$F_a = \frac{F_{max} - F_{min}}{2}$$

These relationships apply equally to stresses and strains.

### Derivation 5: Archard's Wear Equation Derivation

Assume an asperity contact forms a micro-weld junction of diameter d. The volume removed per asperity encounter is proportional to the projected area times the depth of penetration:

$$V_{per\,asperity} \propto d^3 \propto F^{3/2}$$

Summing over all asperities during a sliding distance L gives:

$$V_{total} = k \cdot \frac{F \cdot L}{3H}$$

where k is a dimensionless wear coefficient (typically 10⁻² to 10⁻⁴ for metal-metal contact) and H is the hardness of the softer material.

---

## Analytical Matrix - Decision Tree for Mode Selection

| Observed Phenomenon in Component | Probable Mode | First Diagnostic Test | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Permanent bending after single overload | Yielding | Check σ vs Sy/n | σ = Sy/n |
| Sudden snap without prior deformation | Brittle Fracture | Charpy impact test | σ = Sut/n |
| Failure after many cycles below Sy | Fatigue Failure | S-N curve, surface inspection | Soderberg/Goodman |
| Gradual elongation at high T | Creep | Creep curve at service T | Norton-Bailey |
| Loss of cross-section over years | Corrosion | Weight loss measurement | Faraday's Law |
| Surface material removal | Wear | Surface profilometry | Archard's equation |
| Sudden sideways deflection under compression | Buckling | Slenderness ratio check | Euler or Johnson |
| Excessive elastic sag | Distortion | Deflection measurement | ymax ≤ δpermitted |
| Cracking on first hammer blow | Impact Failure | Impact energy test | U = (σ²V)/(2E) |
| Cracking due to temperature change | Thermal Failure | Thermal stress calc | σ = E·α·ΔT |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Tip:** In KTU exams, when a problem describes a "machine component subjected to fluctuating loads," the first step is ALWAYS to compute σa, σm, and then apply Soderberg/Goodman. When the loading is static and the material is ductile, ALWAYS use the yielding criterion. When the column is long and slender, ALWAYS use Euler.
