<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "efficiency of a riveted joint." What is the typical efficiency range for a double-riveted butt joint with double straps used in boiler construction?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **efficiency of a riveted joint** is defined as the ratio of the strength of the jointed plate (i.e., the minimum of tearing, shearing, and crushing strengths per pitch length) to the strength of an unperforated (solid) plate of the same dimensions.

$$
\eta = \frac{\min(P_t, P_s, P_c)}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}
$$

For a **double-riveted butt joint with double straps** used in boiler shells, the typical efficiency ranges between **70% and 80%**. This high efficiency is the reason such joints are preferred for high-pressure boiler circumferential seams where hoop stress is critical.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's tip: Always quote the formula in symbolic form before the numerical answer — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for the typical range.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State Unwin's formula for the diameter of a rivet in a boiler joint. Why is it used in preference to a purely theoretical calculation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Unwin's empirical formula for rivet diameter in terms of plate thickness is:

$$
\boxed{d = 6.05 \sqrt{t} \;\; \text{mm}}
$$

where $t$ is the plate thickness in mm.

**Why preferred over theoretical calculation:**
- The formula was developed from **extensive experimental data** on actual boiler plate failures in practice.
- It inherently accounts for **the difference in shear and crushing strengths** of typical boiler rivet materials, providing a balanced design where neither shear nor crushing dominates.
- It simplifies design calculations and gives **results consistent with workshop practice** for plate thicknesses between 6 mm and 25 mm.
- Purely theoretical formulas (e.g., equating $P_s = P_c$ with arbitrary allowable stresses) often yield rivet sizes that are either too small (unsafe) or unnecessarily large (uneconomical).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

*Design a **double-riveted lap joint with zig-zag riveting** for the circumferential seam of a cylindrical boiler shell having internal diameter $D = 1800$ mm and steam pressure $p = 1.4$ MPa. Use mild steel plates with the following allowable stresses:*
- *Tensile stress in plate, $\sigma_t = 80$ N/mm²*
- *Shear stress in rivet, $\tau_s = 60$ N/mm²*
- *Crushing stress in rivet, $\sigma_c = 120$ N/mm²*

*Also calculate the efficiency of the joint.*

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Plate Thickness and Rivet Diameter (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Calculate required plate thickness (hoop stress governs circumferential seam):**

The hoop stress in a thin cylindrical shell:

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot D}{2 t}
$$

For safe design with efficiency $\eta$:

$$
t_{\text{req}} = \frac{p \cdot D}{2 \eta \sigma_t}
$$

Assuming an initial efficiency of 70%:

$$
t_{\text{req}} = \frac{1.4 \times 1800}{2 \times 0.70 \times 80} = \frac{2520}{112} = 22.5 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt standard plate thickness $t = 24$ mm.

**Step 2 — Rivet diameter by Unwin's formula:**

$$
d = 6.05 \sqrt{24} = 6.05 \times 4.899 = 29.64 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt standard rivet diameter $d = 30$ mm.

**Step 3 — Number of rivets per pitch:** For double-riveted zig-zag, $n = 2$.

**Step 4 — Initial pitch assumption:** Start with $p = 90$ mm.

#### Part (b) — Strength Calculations and Efficiency (7 marks)

**Step 5 — Tearing strength per pitch:**

$$
P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t = (90 - 30) \times 24 \times 80
$$

$$
P_t = 60 \times 24 \times 80 = 115200 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Shearing strength (single shear in lap joint, 2 rivets):**

$$
P_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \tau_s = 2 \times \frac{\pi (30)^2}{4} \times 60
$$

$$
P_s = 2 \times 706.86 \times 60 = 84823 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 7 — Crushing strength (2 rivets):**

$$
P_c = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_c = 2 \times 30 \times 24 \times 120 = 172800 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 8 — Identify minimum strength:**

$$
P_{\min} = \min(115200, 84823, 172800) = 84823 \text{ N} \; \text{(Shearing governs)}
$$

**Step 9 — Efficiency of joint:**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{\min}}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} = \frac{84823}{90 \times 24 \times 80} = \frac{84823}{172800} = 0.491 = 49.1\%
$$

**Step 10 — Increase pitch to improve efficiency.** Since shearing governs, increase pitch so that $P_s = P_t$ (balance condition):

$$
(p - 30) \times 24 \times 80 = 2 \times \frac{\pi (30)^2}{4} \times 60 = 84823
$$

$$
(p - 30) = \frac{84823}{1920} = 44.18
$$

$$
p = 74.18 \text{ mm}, \text{ adopt } p = 75 \text{ mm}
$$

**Recompute efficiency at $p = 75$ mm:**

$$
P_t = (75 - 30) \times 24 \times 80 = 86400 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{84823}{75 \times 24 \times 80} = \frac{84823}{144000} = 0.589 = 58.9\%
$$

**Step 11 — Pitch limit check:**
- $p_{\max} = 32 t = 32 \times 24 = 768$ mm — **OK** ✓
- $p_{\min} = 2.67 d = 2.67 \times 30 = 80$ mm — 75 mm is slightly less. Adopt $p = 80$ mm.

**Final pitch $p = 80$ mm, efficiency = ~55%, which is acceptable for a double-riveted lap joint.**

#### Final Design Summary (for Question A)

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Plate thickness $t$ | 24 mm |
| Rivet diameter $d$ | 30 mm |
| Pitch $p$ | 80 mm |
| Joint type | Double-riveted zig-zag lap |
| Efficiency | ~55–60% |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to check the **minimum pitch limit** ($p_{\min} = 2.67 d$) and lose 1–2 marks. Also, do not assume the failure mode — always compute all three ($P_t$, $P_s$, $P_c$) and pick the minimum. Marks are explicitly distributed: 2 marks for $t$, 2 marks for $d$, 3 marks for strengths, 2 marks for efficiency, 1 mark for pitch limit verification.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative for Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

*The shell of a Lancashire boiler is 2.5 m in diameter and 7.5 m long, designed to withstand a steam pressure of 1.0 MPa. Design a **single-riveted butt joint with a single strap** for the longitudinal joint. The allowable stresses are:*
- *$\sigma_t = 78$ N/mm², $\tau_s = 56$ N/mm², $\sigma_c = 112$ N/mm²*

*Calculate the efficiency. State why the longitudinal joint requires less strong riveting than the circumferential joint for the same shell.*

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Plate Thickness and Joint Geometry (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Longitudinal joint resists longitudinal stress, which is half of hoop stress:**

$$
\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot D}{4 t} = \frac{1.0 \times 2500}{4 t} = \frac{625}{t} \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Required thickness (assume $\eta = 60\%$):**

$$
t_{\text{req}} = \frac{p \cdot D}{4 \eta \sigma_t} = \frac{1.0 \times 2500}{4 \times 0.60 \times 78} = \frac{2500}{187.2} = 13.35 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $t = 14$ mm.

**Step 3 — Rivet diameter (Unwin):**

$$
d = 6.05 \sqrt{14} = 6.05 \times 3.742 = 22.64 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $d = 24$ mm.

**Step 4 — Strap thickness:** Standard rule: $t_s = \dfrac{5}{8} t$ to $\dfrac{3}{4} t$

$$
t_s = 0.625 \times 14 = 8.75 \text{ mm}, \text{ adopt } t_s = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of rivets per pitch:** $n = 1$ (single-riveted).

**Step 6 — Initial pitch:** Try $p = 60$ mm.

#### Part (b) — Strengths, Efficiency, and Comparison with Circumferential Joint (7 marks)

**Step 7 — Strengths per pitch length:**

Tearing (main plate):

$$
P_t = (60 - 24) \times 14 \times 78 = 36 \times 14 \times 78 = 39312 \text{ N}
$$

Tearing (strap plate):

$$
P_{t,s} = (60 - 24) \times 10 \times 78 = 28116 \text{ N} \quad \text{(strap is weaker)}
$$

Shearing (1 rivet, double shear in butt joint):

$$
P_s = 2 \times \frac{\pi (24)^2}{4} \times 56 = 2 \times 452.39 \times 56 = 50667 \text{ N}
$$

Crushing (1 rivet):

$$
P_c = 24 \times 14 \times 112 = 37632 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 8 — Minimum strength:** $P_{\min} = P_c = 37632$ N (crushing of main plate governs).

**Step 9 — Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{37632}{60 \times 14 \times 78} = \frac{37632}{65520} = 0.574 = 57.4\%
$$

**Step 10 — Pitch adjustment to balance crushing and tearing:**

Set $P_c = P_t$:

$$
24 \times 14 \times 112 = (p - 24) \times 14 \times 78
$$

$$
37632 = (p - 24) \times 1092
$$

$$
p - 24 = 34.46 \Rightarrow p = 58.46 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $p = 60$ mm — original assumption is acceptable.

**Step 11 — Why longitudinal joint needs less strong riveting than circumferential joint:**

The longitudinal stress $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p D}{4 t}$ is **half** of the hoop stress $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p D}{2 t}$. Since the longitudinal joint resists only longitudinal stress, it carries **half the load per unit width** compared to the circumferential joint which resists hoop stress. Therefore:
- A **lighter (single-riveted) joint** suffices for the longitudinal seam.
- The **circumferential seam** (hoop stress) requires a **heavier (double or triple-riveted) joint**.

For this boiler:
- Circumferential joint: $p \cdot D = 1.0 \times 2500 = 2500$ N/mm of axial load per mm length.
- Longitudinal joint: half of this = 1250 N/mm per mm length.

#### Final Design Summary (for Question B)

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Plate thickness $t$ | 14 mm |
| Rivet diameter $d$ | 24 mm |
| Strap thickness $t_s$ | 10 mm |
| Pitch $p$ | 60 mm |
| Joint type | Single-riveted butt, single strap |
| Efficiency | ~57% |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall #1:** In butt joints, the **strap plate** is usually weaker in tearing than the main plate (because of lower $t_s$). Always compute tearing strength of **both** plates and take the minimum. Marks: 1 mark for hoop/longitudinal stress identification, 2 marks for thickness, 2 marks for rivet and strap, 4 marks for all three strengths, 2 marks for efficiency, 1 mark for the conceptual comparison, 2 marks for clarity and unit handling.
>
> **Pitfall #2:** In **double-shear** butt joints, $P_s$ uses factor 2 (two shear planes per rivet). Many students forget this and lose 2 marks. In **lap joints**, only single-shear exists.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Boiler and Tank Joints**

- ☐ **Unwin's formula** $d = 6.05\sqrt{t}$ (mm) — applies to boiler quality plates with $6 \le t \le 25$ mm.
- ☐ **Three failure modes**: tearing ($P_t$), shearing ($P_s$), crushing ($P_c$) — always compute all three and take minimum.
- ☐ **Joint efficiency** $\eta = P_{\min} / (p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t)$ — typically 50% to 80% depending on joint type.
- ☐ **Hoop stress** $\sigma_h = pD/2t$ is **twice** the longitudinal stress $\sigma_l = pD/4t$ in a thin cylindrical shell.
- ☐ **Circumferential joints** (resist hoop stress) need **stronger riveting** than longitudinal joints.
- ☐ **Butt joints** are in **double shear**; **lap joints** are in **single shear** — the $P_s$ formula differs by a factor of 2.
- ☐ **Strap thickness** in butt joints: $t_s = 5t/8$ to $3t/4$.
- ☐ **Pitch limits**: $p_{\min} = 2.67d$ (practical clearance); $p_{\max} = 32t$ or 300 mm for tension joints.
- ☐ **Caulking and fullering** are required to make the joint **leak-proof**, not just strong.
- ☐ **Standard allowable stresses** (mild steel, KTU conventional values): $\sigma_t = 80$ N/mm², $\tau_s = 60$ N/mm², $\sigma_c = 120$ N/mm².
- ☐ **Modern practice** favours **welding** over riveting; **efficiency of butt-welded joint** ≈ 80–90% with full penetration, comparable to **triple-riveted** joints.
- ☐ **IBR 1950** governs Indian boiler design; design must be certified by a **Chartered Engineer**.

### Quick-Reference Memory Map

```
Boiler Joint Design
├── Internal pressure p
│   ├── Hoop stress = pD/2t  → Circumferential joint (heavy)
│   └── Longitudinal stress = pD/4t → Longitudinal joint (light)
├── Plate thickness t (from pD/2t/eta/sigma_t)
├── Rivet diameter d = 6.05 sqrt(t)
├── Failure modes
│   ├── Tearing Pt = (p-d) t sigma_t
│   ├── Shearing Ps = n (pi d^2/4) tau_s  (×2 for butt joint)
│   └── Crushing Pc = n d t sigma_c
├── Joint strength = min(Pt, Ps, Pc)
├── Efficiency = Pmin / (p t sigma_t)
├── Pitch limits: 2.67d ≤ p ≤ 32t (or 300mm)
└── Leak-tightness: caulking + fullering
```

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU Exam Strategy:** For a 14-mark design problem, structure your answer in **four labelled sections** — (1) Given Data, (2) Thickness & Rivet Diameter, (3) Strength Calculations (with three sub-parts $P_t$, $P_s$, $P_c$), (4) Efficiency & Pitch Verification. Examiners allocate marks by section, and this layout scores maximum marks even with partial completion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Structural Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Machine Design (PCMET602)** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, **Structural Joints** (also called **Permanent and Semi-Permanent Fasteners**) are defined as engineered mechanical interfaces used to permanently or semi-permanently connect two or more machine/structural members so that they behave as a single integrated unit capable of transmitting the applied load (tensile, compressive, shear, or torsional) without failure or excessive deformation.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2), structural joints broadly cover the following families:

1. **Riveted Joints** – Permanent joints using ductile metal pins (rivets) deformed plastically to lock plates together.
2. **Welded Joints** – Permanent joints formed by localized fusion of parent metal with/without filler.
3. **Pin, Cotter, and Knuckle Joints** – Semi-permanent/articulated joints used for transmitting axial pull/torque in rods, levers, and links.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**  
> *"Design of Riveted, Welded, Pin, Cotter, and Knuckle Joints – selection, strength, efficiency, and failure analysis."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **steel bridge** with two giant plates that must be joined end-to-end so a train can roll over them smoothly. You cannot just glue them — the joint must resist **huge shear, tension, and vibration loads**. The engineer has a toolkit of "connectors":

- **Rivet** → Think of a tiny metal "nail" whose tail is hammered hot until it swells, locking the plates like a permanent button (similar to the button on your jeans — once fastened, it stays).
- **Weld** → Like melting two ice cubes together at the seam and refreezing them into one solid block.
- **Pin / Cotter / Knuckle Joint** → Like the **hinge pin of a door** — it holds two members but allows rotation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Metrics to Remember (in bold):**  
> - **Pitch (p)** = centre-to-centre distance of adjacent rivets.  
> - **Diameter of rivet (d)** is chosen as $d = 6.05 \sqrt{t}$ (Unwin's formula, in mm) where $t$ is the plate thickness.  
> - **Efficiency ($\eta$)** of a joint = $\dfrac{\text{Strength of joint}}{\text{Strength of solid plate}} \times 100\%$.  
> - Standard **pitch values (p)**: 1.5d, 2d, 2.5d, 3d depending on the row type.

## 1.3 Why Are Structural Joints a Core KTU Topic?

Every machine — from a **two-wheeler's chain link** to a **100-storey building's beam-column connection** — is only as strong as its weakest joint. KTU examiners frequently test this topic because it bridges **strength of materials (PCCET305)** and **practical design**, and it offers many short, calculation-based Part A and Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Structural Joints

Structural joints are classified based on **load transfer mechanism** and **reversibility**:

| **S.No** | **Joint Type** | **Permanence** | **Load Transfer Mode** | **Typical Application** |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Riveted Joint | Permanent | Shear & Bearing | Bridge girders, boilers, pressure vessels |
| 2 | Welded Joint | Permanent | Fillet/butt fusion | Ship hulls, structural frames |
| 3 | Pin Joint | Semi-permanent | Shear on pin | Clevis linkages, suspension links |
| 4 | Cotter Joint | Semi-permanent | Shear on cotter | Piston rod–crosshead, valve rods |
| 5 | Knuckle Joint | Semi-permanent | Tension + shear | Tie rods, tension links |

## 2.2 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

### A. Riveted Joints

A riveted joint transfers load through three possible failure modes:

1. **Shearing of rivet** — Rivet is sheared across its cross-section.
2. **Bearing / Crushing of rivet (or plate hole)** — Contact pressure between rivet and plate hole.
3. **Tearing of plate between rivet holes (Tensile failure)** — Plate fails in tension along the weakest section.

The **joint strength** is the **minimum** of these three strengths, following the **Weakest-Link Principle** (analogous to a chain breaking at its weakest link).

**Modes of failure (KTU pictorial convention):**

- **Single-shear** — Two plates pulled in opposite directions, rivet sheared once.
- **Double-shear** — Three plates with middle plate pulled opposite to outer two; rivet sheared twice.

### B. Welded Joints

A welded joint transfers load via a **fillet weld** (triangular cross-section) or a **butt weld** (full penetration). KTU focuses on **parallel (longitudinal) fillet welds** and **transverse fillet welds**.

- **Throat thickness (t)** = $0.707 \times s$ where $s$ is the leg size.
- **Shear strength of weld** = $0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \tau$ where $L$ is weld length, $\tau$ is allowable shear stress.

### C. Knuckle Joint

A **knuckle joint** transmits axial tensile load between two rods that may have a small angular misalignment. It consists of a **single eye**, **fork**, a **pin**, and a **taper pin / split pin** for locking.

Failure occurs by:
1. Tension in the body of the eye/fork.
2. Shearing of the pin.
3. Crushing of the pin in the eye/fork.

### D. Cotter Joint

A **cotter joint** connects two coaxial rods (e.g., piston rod and crosshead). The **cotter** (a flat wedge-shaped piece) transmits load via **shear and crushing**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table for last-minute revision. All KTU questions are solved using only these formulas.**

| **S.No** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Unwin's formula (rivet dia) | $d = 6.05\sqrt{t}$ | $d, t$ in **mm**; $t$ = plate thickness |
| 2 | Shear strength of rivet (single) | $P_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} \cdot \tau$ | $N$ (Newtons) |
| 3 | Shear strength (double shear) | $P_s = \dfrac{\pi}{2} d^{2} \cdot \tau$ | — |
| 4 | Bearing / crushing strength | $P_b = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$ | $N$ |
| 5 | Tearing strength of plate | $P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | $N$; $p$ = pitch |
| 6 | Efficiency of joint | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\min}}{P_{solid}} \times 100$ | percentage |
| 7 | Strength of solid plate | $P_{solid} = (p \cdot t) \cdot \sigma_t$ | $N$ |
| 8 | Throat thickness (fillet weld) | $t = 0.707\,s$ | $s$ = leg size |
| 9 | Shear strength of fillet weld | $P_w = 0.707\,s \cdot L \cdot \tau$ | $N$ |
| 10 | Pin shear strength (knuckle) | $P_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} \cdot \tau$ | $N$ |
| 11 | Pin crushing (knuckle) | $P_c = d \cdot t_1 \cdot \sigma_c$ | $N$; $t_1$ = eye/fork thickness |
| 12 | Tensile stress in eye body | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{1.5\,W}{(a - d)\,t_1} \le \sigma_{all}$ | $a$ = eye outer diameter |
| 13 | Cotter shear strength | $P_s = 2\,b\,c \cdot \tau$ | $N$; $b$ = cotter width, $c$ = thickness |
| 14 | Cotter crushing strength | $P_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,(d_1^{2} - d^{2}) \cdot \sigma_c$ | $N$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $W$ = axial load on rod; $d$ = pin/rod diameter; $t$ = plate thickness; $p$ = pitch; $a$ = outer eye diameter; $b$ = cotter width; $c$ = cotter thickness at the middle.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Riveted joints** dominated boiler and bridge construction before 1950; even today, **high-strength structural rivets** are used in heavy structural steelwork.
- **Welded joints** are the workhorse of modern fabrication (skyscrapers, ship hulls, automotive chassis) because they are **leak-proof** and **lightweight**.
- **Knuckle joints** are essential wherever two rods must be connected end-to-end with a hinge — e.g., **tie-rod in a railway coupling**, **connecting rod in a punching press**.
- **Cotter joints** are the classic answer for connecting a **piston rod to the crosshead** of a steam engine, where axial alignment is critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Unwin's Formula for Rivet Diameter

**Given:** Empirical observation that the shear strength of a rivet should approximately equal its **bearing strength** against the plate (this is the "balanced design" concept KTU loves to test).

**Starting condition:**

$$
\dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} \cdot \tau \;=\; d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b
$$

**Step 1 — Divide both sides by $d$:**

$$
\dfrac{\pi}{4} d \cdot \tau \;=\; t \cdot \sigma_b
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $d$:**

$$
d \;=\; \dfrac{4 \, t \, \sigma_b}{\pi \, \tau}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute empirical ratios used in structural steelwork:**

For mild steel, $\dfrac{\sigma_b}{\tau} \approx 1.875$ and $4/\pi \approx 1.273$. Hence:

$$
d \;=\; 1.273 \times 1.875 \times t \;\approx\; 2.387\,t
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to the "Unwin's constant form":**

Rearranging to obtain $d$ in terms of $\sqrt{t}$ (so the formula is dimensionless-correct for $t$ in mm):

$$
d \;=\; 6.05\,\sqrt{t} \quad \text{(in mm)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** Unwin's formula gives a rivet diameter that **balances shear and bearing**, so neither failure mode dominates — a hallmark of an efficient, economical design.

## 3.2 Derivation: Efficiency of a Single-Row Riveted Lap Joint

A **single-row lap joint** has one row of $n$ rivets, each of diameter $d$, pitch $p$, and plate thickness $t$.

**Step 1 — Shear strength of $n$ rivets (single shear):**

$$
P_s \;=\; n \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} \cdot \tau
$$

**Step 2 — Bearing / crushing strength of $n$ rivets:**

$$
P_b \;=\; n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b
$$

**Step 3 — Tearing strength of plate along the rivet pitch line:**

The plate section that remains after drilling a hole is $(p - d)$, so:

$$
P_t \;=\; (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t
$$

**Step 4 — Strength of the (un-joined) solid plate over the same pitch length:**

$$
P_{solid} \;=\; p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t
$$

**Step 5 — Apply weakest-link principle:**

$$
P_{joint} \;=\; \min(P_s, \, P_b, \, P_t)
$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency formula:**

$$
\eta \;=\; \dfrac{P_{joint}}{P_{solid}} \times 100 \;=\; \min\!\left( \dfrac{n\,\pi d^{2}\,\tau}{4 p\,t\,\sigma_t}, \; \dfrac{n\,d}{p} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_b}{\sigma_t}, \; \dfrac{p-d}{p} \right) \times 100
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Knuckle Joint — Pin Shear & Eye Tension

**Given:** Axial load $W$ on the rod, pin diameter $d$, eye thickness $t_1$, outer eye diameter $a$, fork thickness $t_2 = 2 t_1$ (standard KTU assumption).

**Step 1 — Pin shear strength (single shear between eye and fork):**

$$
P_s \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,d^{2} \cdot \tau
$$

**Step 2 — Pin crushing strength against the eye:**

$$
P_{c,eye} \;=\; d \cdot t_1 \cdot \sigma_c
$$

**Step 3 — Pin crushing strength against the fork (two arms combined):**

$$
P_{c,fork} \;=\; d \cdot 2t_1 \cdot \sigma_c
$$

**Step 4 — Tensile stress in the eye body section (rectangular section through the eye hole):**

$$
\sigma_t \;=\; \dfrac{W}{(a - d) \, t_1}
$$

**Step 5 — Modified KTU design condition (with stress concentration factor 1.5):**

$$
\dfrac{1.5\,W}{(a - d)\,t_1} \;\le\; \sigma_{t,allow}
$$

**Step 6 — Rearranging to solve for outer eye diameter $a$:**

$$
a \;\ge\; d \;+\; \dfrac{1.5\,W}{t_1 \cdot \sigma_{t,allow}}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Fillet Weld Strength (Transverse vs Parallel)

**Given:** Two plates joined by fillet welds of leg size $s$ and length $L$, subjected to tensile load $W$.

**Step 1 — Throat thickness:**

$$
t \;=\; 0.707\,s
$$

**Step 2 — Shear area of the weld (parallel fillet, where load is parallel to weld):**

$$
A_{w} \;=\; 0.707\,s \cdot L
$$

**Step 3 — Shear strength of the weld:**

$$
P_w \;=\; 0.707\,s \cdot L \cdot \tau_{allow}
$$

**Step 4 — Set weld strength equal to applied load:**

$$
W \;=\; 0.707\,s \cdot L \cdot \tau_{allow}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for required weld length:**

$$
L \;=\; \dfrac{W}{0.707\,s \cdot \tau_{allow}}
$$

**Step 6 — Transverse fillet weld has higher strength (≈ 1.414 × parallel):**

$$
P_{w,transverse} \;\approx\; 0.707\,s \cdot L \cdot \tau \cdot \sqrt{2}
$$

$$
P_{w,transverse} \;\approx\; s \cdot L \cdot \tau
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** A **transverse fillet weld** is about **30% stronger** than a parallel one for the same leg size, because the throat is oriented to resist load directly.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Riveted Joint** | **Welded Joint** | **Knuckle Joint** | **Cotter Joint** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Permanence | Permanent | Permanent | Semi-permanent | Semi-permanent |
| Stress concentration | High (hole) | Low–Medium | High (eye) | Medium |
| Weight | Heavy | Light | Medium | Medium |
| Skill required | Riveting gang | Welder + cert. | Machinist | Machinist |
| Inspection ease | Easy (visual) | Difficult (NDT) | Easy | Easy |
| KTU exam weightage | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Structural Joints (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["STRUCTURAL JOINTS"]
    nodeB["PERMANENT JOINTS"]
    nodeC["SEMI PERMANENT JOINTS"]
    nodeD["RIVETED JOINTS"]
    nodeE["WELDED JOINTS"]
    nodeF["PIN JOINT"]
    nodeG["COTTER JOINT"]
    nodeH["KNUCKLE JOINT"]
    nodeI["LAP / BUTT TYPES"]
    nodeJ["FILLET / BUTT TYPES"]
    nodeK["SHEAR AND BEARING FAILURE"]
    nodeL["THROAT SHEAR FAILURE"]
    nodeM["PIN SHEAR AND EYE TENSION"]
    nodeN["COTTER SHEAR AND CRUSHING"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeD --> nodeK
    nodeE --> nodeJ
    nodeE --> nodeL
    nodeF --> nodeM
    nodeG --> nodeN
    nodeH --> nodeM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Permanent joints branch into **riveted** and **welded** sub-families, each with characteristic failure modes. Semi-permanent joints all hinge around a **pin** or **cotter** that is loaded in shear and bearing.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Design Procedure for a Knuckle Joint (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["START: Given axial load W and material"]
    stepB["Choose pin diameter d from shear criterion"]
    stepC["Compute pin diameter d"]
    stepD["Choose eye outer diameter a"]
    stepE["Compute a from tensile stress criterion"]
    stepF["Choose eye thickness t1"]
    stepG["Check pin crushing against eye"]
    stepH["Check pin crushing against fork"]
    stepI["Check pin shear strength"]
    stepJ["ALL CHECKS PASS"]
    stepK["REVISE DIMENSIONS"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepI --> stepK
    stepK --> stepD
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Failure Modes in a Riveted Joint

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeP["APPLIED LOAD P"]
    nodeQ["MODE 1: RIVET SHEAR"]
    nodeR["MODE 2: BEARING CRUSH"]
    nodeS["MODE 3: PLATE TEARING"]
    nodeT["P sub s equals pi by 4 d squared tau"]
    nodeU["P sub b equals d into t into sigma sub b"]
    nodeV["P sub t equals p minus d into t into sigma sub t"]
    nodeW["P JOINT equals MIN of P sub s, P sub b, P sub t"]

    nodeP --> nodeQ --> nodeT
    nodeP --> nodeR --> nodeU
    nodeP --> nodeS --> nodeV
    nodeT --> nodeW
    nodeU --> nodeW
    nodeV --> nodeW
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block 4.1** – Classification tree shows the three families of joints covered in KTU Module 2.  
> **Block 4.2** – Design procedure illustrates the iterative "check–revise" loop examiners expect you to demonstrate.  
> **Block 4.3** – Failure-mode diagram enforces the **weakest-link principle** that defines joint strength.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the term "efficiency of a riveted joint" and write the formula. State the typical efficiency range for a properly designed joint.

**Model Answer:**

> The **efficiency of a riveted joint ($\eta$)** is defined as the ratio of the **strength of the weakest section of the riveted joint** to the **strength of an un-joined (solid) plate of the same dimensions**, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\eta \;=\; \dfrac{P_{joint}}{P_{solid}} \times 100 \;=\; \min\!\left( \dfrac{P_s}{P_{solid}}, \; \dfrac{P_b}{P_{solid}}, \; \dfrac{P_t}{P_{solid}} \right) \times 100
$$

For a **properly designed structural riveted joint**, efficiency typically lies in the range of **60% – 80%**. KTU examiners accept "between 60% and 80%" as the correct answer.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between a **knuckle joint** and a **cotter joint**. State one typical application of each.

**Model Answer:**

| **Feature** | **Knuckle Joint** | **Cotter Joint** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Geometry | Eye + Fork connected by a pin | Two coaxial rods joined by a wedge-shaped cotter |
| Load direction | Mostly **tensile/axial** with possible hinge | **Axial tension or compression** |
| Pin/cotter role | Pin is sheared and crushed | Cotter is sheared (in two planes) and crushed |
| Typical application | Tie-rod of a **railway carriage coupling**, connecting rod in a press | **Piston rod–crosshead** of a steam engine, valve rod |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 + 7**. Students must answer **ONE full question** per module.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** A lap joint is formed between two **mild steel plates of thickness 12 mm** using **single-row rivets of diameter 20 mm**. The pitch is **60 mm**, and the rivets are in **single shear**. Permissible stresses are $\tau = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_b = 160$ MPa, $\sigma_t = 120$ MPa. Calculate the **shear, bearing, and tearing strengths per pitch length** and hence the **efficiency** of the joint.

**(b)** List any **three failure modes** of a riveted joint with neat sketches (describe verbally since sketches are not possible in text).

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $t = 12$ mm, $d = 20$ mm, $p = 60$ mm, $n = 1$ (single row), single shear, $\tau = 80$ N/mm², $\sigma_b = 160$ N/mm², $\sigma_t = 120$ N/mm².

**Step 1 — Shear strength of one rivet:**

$$
P_s \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,d^{2}\,\tau \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 20^{2} \times 80
$$

$$
P_s \;=\; 0.7854 \times 400 \times 80 \;=\; 25{,}133 \text{ N} \;\approx\; 25.13 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Bearing strength of one rivet:**

$$
P_b \;=\; d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b \;=\; 20 \times 12 \times 160 \;=\; 38{,}400 \text{ N} \;=\; 38.40 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Tearing strength of plate per pitch:**

$$
P_t \;=\; (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t \;=\; (60 - 20) \times 12 \times 120
$$

$$
P_t \;=\; 40 \times 12 \times 120 \;=\; 57{,}600 \text{ N} \;=\; 57.60 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Weakest-link principle:**

$$
P_{joint} \;=\; \min(25.13, \; 38.40, \; 57.60) \;=\; 25.13 \text{ kN} \quad \text{(governed by shear)}
$$

**Step 5 — Strength of solid plate over same pitch:**

$$
P_{solid} \;=\; p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t \;=\; 60 \times 12 \times 120 \;=\; 86{,}400 \text{ N} \;=\; 86.40 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency:**

$$
\eta \;=\; \dfrac{25.13}{86.40} \times 100 \;=\; 29.08\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The joint is **shear-governed** and has very low efficiency (≈29%) because only a single rivet per pitch is used. A practical joint would have **multiple rivets per row** to raise efficiency to 60–80%.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

The **three primary failure modes** of a riveted joint are:

1. **Shearing of the rivet** — The rivet shank shears across its cross-section due to tangential force between the plates.
2. **Bearing / Crushing failure** — The rivet shank (or the plate hole wall) yields under high contact pressure, leading to ovalization of the hole and plate separation.
3. **Tearing of the plate** — The plate fails in tension along the **pitch line** because the drilled hole reduces the net cross-sectional area.

**Secondary failure mode (bonus point):** **Rivet head popping / shearing of rivet head** under excessive tensile load.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** A **knuckle joint** is used to connect two mild steel rods carrying an **axial tensile load of 50 kN**. The permissible stresses are $\tau = 60$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_t = 140$ MPa. Design the joint and determine the **pin diameter $d$, outer eye diameter $a$, and eye thickness $t_1$**.

**(b)** Explain the **derivation of Unwin's formula** for rivet diameter. Why is the constant **6.05** used?

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $W = 50{,}000$ N, $\tau = 60$ N/mm², $\sigma_c = 120$ N/mm², $\sigma_t = 140$ N/mm².

**Step 1 — Pin diameter from shear criterion:**

$$
d \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4 W}{\pi \tau}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 50{,}000}{\pi \times 60}}
$$

$$
d \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{200{,}000}{188.5}} \;=\; \sqrt{1061.0} \;\approx\; 32.6 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt **standard diameter $d = 35$ mm** (next higher standard value).

**Step 2 — Outer eye diameter from tensile criterion:**

Using $\sigma_t = \dfrac{1.5\,W}{(a - d)\,t_1}$ with $t_1 = 1.5\,d$ (initial assumption):

$$
t_1 \;=\; 1.5 \times 35 \;=\; 52.5 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{adopt } t_1 = 55 \text{ mm})
$$

Rearranging:

$$
a \;=\; d \;+\; \dfrac{1.5\,W}{t_1 \cdot \sigma_t} \;=\; 35 \;+\; \dfrac{1.5 \times 50{,}000}{55 \times 140}
$$

$$
a \;=\; 35 \;+\; \dfrac{75{,}000}{7700} \;=\; 35 \;+\; 9.74 \;\approx\; 45 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt **$a = 50$ mm** (next standard size for clearance).

**Step 3 — Verify pin crushing on the eye:**

$$
P_{c,eye} \;=\; d \cdot t_1 \cdot \sigma_c \;=\; 35 \times 55 \times 120 \;=\; 231{,}000 \text{ N}
$$

Since $P_{c,eye} = 231$ kN $> W = 50$ kN, the crushing check **passes** comfortably.

**Step 4 — Verify pin shear (single shear):**

$$
P_s \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,d^{2}\,\tau \;=\; 0.7854 \times 35^{2} \times 60 \;=\; 57{,}715 \text{ N} \;\approx\; 57.7 \text{ kN}
$$

Since $P_s = 57.7$ kN $> W = 50$ kN, the shear check **passes**.

**Final design:**

- Pin diameter: **$d = 35$ mm**
- Outer eye diameter: **$a = 50$ mm**
- Eye thickness: **$t_1 = 55$ mm** (fork thickness $t_2 = 110$ mm)

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Derivation of Unwin's formula (recap):**

Unwin's formula arises from the **balanced design condition** that the **shear strength of a rivet** equals its **bearing (crushing) strength against the plate**:

$$
\dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} \cdot \tau \;=\; d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b
$$

Solving:

$$
d \;=\; \dfrac{4\,\sigma_b}{\pi\,\tau} \cdot t
$$

For **mild steel structural rivets**, the empirical ratio is:

$$
\dfrac{\sigma_b}{\tau} \;\approx\; 1.875 \quad \text{and} \quad \dfrac{4}{\pi} \;\approx\; 1.273
$$

Therefore:

$$
d \;\approx\; 1.273 \times 1.875 \times t \;\approx\; 2.387\,t
$$

To express $d$ in **mm** with $t$ in **mm**, the constant is rewritten in the form $6.05$ (this is the empirical constant calibrated against typical structural steel plate thicknesses in mm):

$$
\boxed{\,d \;=\; 6.05\,\sqrt{t}\,} \quad \text{(d and t in mm)}
$$

**Why constant 6.05?**  
The constant 6.05 is **empirically derived** from experimental data on **mild steel rivet-plate combinations**. It bundles together:

- The numerical value of $4/\pi$ (= 1.273)
- The typical ratio $\sigma_b/\tau$ (= 1.875)
- A dimensional scaling factor so that, when $t$ is in **mm**, $d$ comes out directly in **mm** under the square-root law.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.5 stress concentration factor** in knuckle-joint eye tension. Always use $1.5 W / [(a-d)\,t_1] \le \sigma_t$.
> 2. **Using $p$ instead of $(p-d)$** for tearing strength. The drilled hole subtracts area — a costly 1–2 mark error.
> 3. **Forgetting to choose the minimum** among the three riveted-joint strengths. KTU strictly tests the "weakest link" understanding.
> 4. **Mixing up single vs double shear** — double shear doubles the shear strength.
> 5. **In weld problems**, students often use $s \cdot L$ (leg × length) instead of $0.707\,s \cdot L$ (throat × length). The **throat** is what resists shear.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Unwin's formula:** $d = 6.05\,\sqrt{t}$ mm — your starting point for **every** riveted-joint design problem.
- **Three failure modes of riveted joints** = Shear of rivet, Bearing on hole, Tearing of plate → take **minimum**.
- **Efficiency** typically 60–80% for a well-designed joint; very low (≈30%) for a single-rivet lap joint.
- **Fillet weld throat** = $0.707 \times$ leg size; **transverse welds are 1.414× stronger** than parallel welds.
- **Knuckle joint** uses the **1.5 stress-concentration factor** in the eye-tensile check.
- **Cotter joint** is **two-rod coaxial** connection; cotter fails in **double shear** ($2bc\tau$).
- **Pitch $p$** for chain/zygomatic row uses **2.5d**; for diamond row **2d** (advanced).
- **Mild steel safe stresses** (KTU convention): $\tau = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_b = 160$ MPa, $\sigma_t = 120$ MPa — memorize for quick problem solving.
- **Always draw FBD / sketches** in the exam — 2 to 3 marks are reserved for figures in Part B.
- **Sign convention:** Tensile loads on rivets = shear on shank + bearing on hole wall.
- **Section modulus & bending** in welded joints are out of Module 2 scope (Module 3 covers eccentric loading).
- **Check list for any joint problem:** (1) Identify joint type, (2) List permissible stresses, (3) Compute all failure-mode strengths, (4) Take minimum, (5) Compare with applied load, (6) State efficiency or safety factor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Welded Joints - Welding Symbols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Welded Joints - Welding Symbols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Welding Symbol** is a standardized graphical notation used on engineering drawings to convey complete information about the type, size, extent, location, and finishing requirements of a welded joint, without the need for descriptive notes. As per **AWS A2.4** (American Welding Society) and **IS 813** / **ISO 2553** standards adopted by KTU, the symbol acts as the universal "language of the welder" — an abbreviated pictorial instruction that precisely specifies how a particular joint must be fabricated.

Mathematically, the welding symbol is **not a single drawing**, but a **system of eight logical elements** (arrow, reference line, weld symbol, dimensions, supplementary symbols, finish symbol, tail, and specifications) arranged in a fixed geometric grammar that any certified welder across the world can interpret.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:**
> "A welding symbol is a graphical representation of the welded joint requirements, comprising an arrow, a reference line, and a weld symbol, with optional supplementary symbols, dimensions, finish marks, and a tail for specifications."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Road Sign for Welders"

Imagine you hire a **welder in Kochi** to fabricate a steel bracket that must later be repaired by a welder in **Kozhikode**. If the design engineer writes a long paragraph describing the joint ("make a triangular weld of 6 mm leg on the left side, grind it flush, do it in the field"), there is enormous scope for mis-interpretation, language barriers, and errors.

A **welding symbol is like a traffic road sign** — a small, compact, internationally agreed-upon pictogram that instantly communicates:
- **WHAT** type of weld to make (triangle = fillet, V-shape = groove),
- **HOW BIG** it should be (dimension),
- **WHERE** to put it (arrow side or other side),
- **WHICH SIDE** of the joint (above or below reference line),
- **HOW** the surface should be finished (flush, convex, concave),
- and **HOW OFTEN** the weld is made around the joint (all-around symbol).

Just as a stop sign means the same thing in every country that signed the Vienna Convention on Road Signs, a fillet weld symbol means the same thing to every welder who follows **AWS A2.4 / IS 813**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU loves this topic:** Questions on welding symbols appear almost every semester in PCMET602 because it tests the student's ability to *read* and *interpret* engineering drawings — a core Machine Design skill. Expect 3-mark short questions asking to draw/interpret symbols and 7-mark sub-questions asking to design a fillet welded joint.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Minimum fillet weld leg size (h)** | **4 mm** | As per IS 9595 for plates up to 10 mm thick |
| **Maximum effective throat** | Limited by plate thickness | Prevents burn-through |
| **Standard symbol scale (AWS A2.4)** | Length = **2 × height** | Visual legibility norm |
| **Reference line length** | Not less than **25 mm** | Readable on A4/A3 drawings |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight Elements of a Welding Symbol (Logical Breakdown)

The **AWS A2.4 standard** specifies that a complete welding symbol consists of the following elements arranged in a precise geometric hierarchy:

1. **Reference Line** — The horizontal baseline. Always drawn parallel to the bottom of the drawing sheet. Acts as the "anchor" on which all other elements are placed.
2. **Arrow** — A leader line that points to the joint being welded. The arrow carries a critical meaning: the side of the joint to which the arrow points is the **"Arrow Side"** (weld on that side), and the opposite side is the **"Other Side"**.
3. **Weld Symbol** — The actual pictorial glyph (triangle for fillet, V for groove, etc.) placed *on* the reference line. Placement above the line = other side; placement below the line = arrow side.
4. **Dimensions** — Size (leg, depth, pitch), length, and number/center-to-center spacing of welds.
5. **Supplementary Symbols** — Weld-all-around (circle), field weld (flag), contour (flush, convex, concave).
6. **Finish Symbol** — Letter codes C (chipping), G (grinding), M (machining), R (rolling), P (peening), or a contour symbol.
7. **Tail** — The right-side extension of the reference line, used for **welding procedure specifications (WPS)**, method of inspection, or other notes.
8. **Specification, Process, or Other Reference** — Written inside or attached to the tail.

### 2.2 Classification of Weld Types

**A. Groove Welds (Butt Joints)** — Used where full penetration is required.
- **Square Groove** (||) — for thin plates < 6 mm
- **V-Groove** (V) — single bevel for plates 6–20 mm
- **Bevel Groove** (V with one straight side) — for unequal thickness plates
- **U-Groove** (U) — for thicker plates, less filler metal
- **J-Groove** (J) — for unequal access

**B. Fillet Welds** (most common in KTU problems) — Triangular cross-section.
- **Transverse Fillet** — weld axis perpendicular to the line of force (stronger)
- **Parallel Fillet** — weld axis parallel to the line of force
- **Combined/Inclined Fillet** — at an angle to the force

**C. Plug & Slot Welds** — Made by filling holes/slots in one member.
- **Plug Weld** (rectangle) — circular hole
- **Slot Weld** (rectangle) — elongated slot

**D. Spot & Projection Welds** — Resistance welding symbols.
- **Spot Weld** (circle)
- **Seam Weld** (oval)
- **Projection Weld** (circle inside rectangle)

**E. Stud Welds** — Stud welded to a base plate.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** you need to solve any KTU Module 2 welding problem. Memorize the symbol conventions, the throat thickness relation, and the two strength equations for parallel vs. transverse fillets.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Effective Throat Thickness (fillet)** | $t_{e} = 0.707 \, h$ | $h$ = leg size | AWS D1.1 / mm |
| 2 | **Convexity Correction** | $t_{e} = 0.707 \, h + 3\,\text{mm}$ | For convex fillets | mm |
| 3 | **Strength of Fillet Weld (shear)** | $P = 0.707 \, h \, L \, \tau_{all}$ | $L$ = total weld length, $\tau_{all}$ = allowable shear | N |
| 4 | **Strength Parallel to Force** | $P_{\parallel} = 0.707 \, h \, L \, \tau_{all}$ | Same as #3 (loaded in shear) | N |
| 5 | **Strength Transverse to Force** | $P_{\perp} = 0.707 \, h \, L \, (0.707\,\sigma_{t})$ | Higher strength, factor 0.707 | N |
| 6 | **Minimum Leg Size (IS 9595)** | $h_{\min} = \sqrt{2 \, t_{\max}}$ | Empirical, $t_{\max}$ = thicker plate | mm |
| 7 | **Maximum Weld Length (parallel)** | $L_{\max} \leq 100 \, h$ | Prevents uneven stress | mm |
| 8 | **Effective Length** | $L_{eff} = L - 2 \, h$ | Subtract 2 × leg for crater/starting | mm |
| 9 | **Combined Load Fillet Weld** | $P = 0.707 \, h \sum L_i \, \tau_{all}$ | Sum of all individual weld lengths | N |
| 10 | **Weld Pitch / Spacing** | $p = L / n$ | $n$ = number of weld segments | mm |
| 11 | **Shear Stress in Fillet** | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{0.707 \, h \, L}$ | Direct shear formula | MPa |
| 12 | **Butt Weld Strength** | $P = t \, L \, \sigma_{t}$ | $t$ = plate thickness, full penetration | N |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Welding symbols are mandatory in:
- **Structural Steel Fabrication** (bridges, transmission towers, industrial sheds) — IS 800:2007 mandates symbol-based drawings.
- **Pressure Vessel Design** (boilers, LPG tanks) — ASME Section VIII.
- **Automotive Chassis** (car frames, truck cabins) — robotic welds use symbols for CAM programming.
- **Shipbuilding** — modular construction across global yards relies on AWS A2.4.
- **Aerospace & Railways** — KTU graduates typically handle drawings in these sectors.

The **ability to read welding symbols is therefore not academic** — it is the legal, contractual, and safety-critical language of the metal-fabrication industry.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Throat Thickness of a Fillet Weld

**Statement:** A fillet weld has a right-triangular cross-section with leg size $h$. The plane of maximum shear is at **45°** to both legs, and the shortest distance from the root of the triangle to the hypotenuse (which is the *effective throat thickness*) is derived geometrically.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**
The fillet weld is a right isosceles triangle with both legs equal to $h$.

$$
\text{Area of triangle} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot h \cdot h = \frac{h^2}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the throat plane.**
The throat is the line drawn from the root perpendicular to the hypotenuse. In a right isosceles triangle, this is the altitude to the hypotenuse.

**Step 3 — Apply the altitude-to-hypotenuse theorem.**
For a right triangle with legs $a$ and $b$, the altitude to the hypotenuse is

$$
h_{alt} = \frac{a \, b}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $a = b = h$.**

$$
h_{alt} = \frac{h \cdot h}{\sqrt{h^2 + h^2}} = \frac{h^2}{\sqrt{2 \, h^2}} = \frac{h^2}{h\sqrt{2}} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Rationalise.**

$$
t_e = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{h\sqrt{2}}{2} = 0.707 \, h
$$

> **Result:** The effective throat thickness of an equal-leg fillet weld is $t_e = 0.707 \, h$. This is the **minimum cross-section** that resists the load, and therefore it is the **design section** for all strength calculations. **This is the single most important derivation in KTU Module 2.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Strength of a Transverse Fillet Weld

**Statement:** When a transverse fillet weld is loaded by a tensile force $P$ perpendicular to the weld axis, the load is resisted by **direct tension across the throat plane**.

**Step 1 — Identify the failure plane.**
The throat plane is inclined at **45°** to the direction of the applied force. The force $P$ can therefore be resolved into:
- A component **normal** to the throat: $P \cos 45° = 0.707 \, P$ (tensile)
- A component **shear** along the throat: $P \sin 45° = 0.707 \, P$ (shear)

**Step 2 — Write the area of the throat.**
The throat area for a weld of length $L$ is

$$
A_t = t_e \cdot L = 0.707 \, h \cdot L
$$

**Step 3 — Set up the strength equations.**
Tensile stress on throat:

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{0.707 \, P}{0.707 \, h \, L} = \frac{P}{h \, L}
$$

Shear stress on throat:

$$
\tau = \frac{0.707 \, P}{0.707 \, h \, L} = \frac{P}{h \, L}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the maximum-shear-stress theory (Tresca).**
For combined tension and shear on the same plane, the **equivalent tensile stress** is conservatively taken as

$$
\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 4\tau^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{P}{hL}\right)^2 + 4\left(\frac{P}{hL}\right)^2} = \sqrt{5} \cdot \frac{P}{hL} \approx 2.236 \cdot \frac{P}{hL}
$$

**Step 5 — KTU-simplified empirical form.**
For design simplicity, KTU problems (following V.B. Bhandari / Shigley) use a **direct-shear-on-throat** assumption with an effective factor:

$$
P_{\perp} = 0.707 \, h \, L \, (0.707 \, \sigma_{t,all})
$$

This yields roughly **70.7% higher strength** compared to a parallel fillet for the same $h$ and $L$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Leg Size (IS 9595 / AWS D1.1)

**Statement:** Empirical relation to prevent the heat-affected zone (HAZ) from weakening the base plate.

**Step 1 — Reason qualitatively.**
If $h$ is too small relative to plate thickness $t$, the heat of welding dissipates quickly into the large plate, producing a brittle HAZ.

**Step 2 — Empirical formula (for $t \leq 25$ mm):**

$$
h_{\min} = \sqrt{2 \, t_{\max}}
$$

where $t_{\max}$ is the thickness of the **thicker** of the two plates being joined.

**Step 3 — Worked numerical example.**
If $t_{\max} = 10$ mm,

$$
h_{\min} = \sqrt{2 \times 10} = \sqrt{20} = 4.47 \approx 5 \text{ mm}
$$

> Adopt the next standard size: **$h = 6$ mm**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Weld Type vs. Failure Mode

| Weld Type | Failure Plane | Governing Stress | Strength Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fillet — Parallel** | Throat (45°) | Pure shear | $P = 0.707 \, h \, L \, \tau_{all}$ |
| **Fillet — Transverse** | Throat (45°) | Combined $\sigma + \tau$ | $P = 0.707 \, h \, L \, (0.707 \, \sigma_{t,all})$ |
| **Fillet — Inclined at angle $\theta$** | Throat (45°) | Mixed | $P = \dfrac{0.707 \, h \, L \, \sigma_{t,all}}{1 + 0.5 \sin^2\theta}$ (Shigley) |
| **Butt (Square Groove)** | Plate cross-section | Pure tension | $P = t \, L \, \sigma_{t,all}$ |
| **Plug / Slot** | Shear on cylindrical surface | Shear | $P = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \tau_{all}$ (for plug) |
| **Spot** | Shear on nugget diameter | Shear | $P = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \tau_{all}$ per spot |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Welding Symbol

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete architecture** of an AWS A2.4 welding symbol — showing how each of the eight elements interacts with the reference line as the central anchor. Use this as your **mental model** while reading or drawing a welded joint on a KTU answer sheet.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ref1[REFERENCE LINE - Horizontal anchor baseline]
    arr1[ARROW - Leader pointing to joint location]
    tail1[TAIL - Right side extension for specifications]
    ws1[WELD SYMBOL - Triangle V U etc on reference line]
    dim1[DIMENSIONS - Leg size length pitch number]
    sup1[SUPPLEMENTARY SYMBOLS - All-around contour field weld]
    fin1[FINISH SYMBOL - C G M R P or contour shape]

    ref1 --> arr1
    ref1 --> ws1
    ref1 --> tail1
    ws1 --> dim1
    ws1 --> sup1
    ws1 --> fin1

    style ref1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style arr1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style tail1 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ws1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style dim1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style sup1 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style fin1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Reading order for a KTU exam:** Start at the **arrow** → identify the **weld symbol on the reference line** → note its position (above = other side, below = arrow side) → read **dimensions** left-to-right → observe any **supplementary symbols** (flag = field, circle = all-around) → finally check the **tail** for WPS.

---

### 4.2 Fillet Weld Symbol Placement Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Joint Cross Section] --> B{Which side of joint?}
    B -->|Same side as arrow points| C[Symbol BELOW reference line]
    B -->|Opposite side of arrow| D[Symbol ABOVE reference line]
    C --> E[Weld placed on ARROW SIDE]
    D --> F[Weld placed on OTHER SIDE]

    style F fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,color:#000

```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to draw a welding symbol for a *given* joint, always (1) draw the reference line first, (2) draw the arrow pointing to the joint, (3) decide which side you want the weld, and (4) place the weld symbol on the appropriate side of the reference line. Marks are awarded for this **placement logic**, not just the symbol shape.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **List and explain any THREE supplementary symbols used in welding symbols with neat sketches.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per symbol):**

**1. Field Weld Symbol — Flag (1 mark)**
A small filled triangular flag attached at the junction of the reference line and the arrow. It indicates that the welding is to be performed in the field (at the site of erection) and not in the fabrication shop. Example: site-welded joints in bridges and transmission towers.

**2. Weld-All-Around Symbol — Circle (1 mark)**
A small open circle placed at the junction of the reference line and the arrow. It indicates that the weld is to be made all around the joint, i.e., continuously along the entire perimeter. Example: pipe flange welding.

**3. Contour Symbol — Flush / Convex / Concave (1 mark)**
A horizontal straight line indicates a **flush** contour, a convex curve indicates a **convex** weld face, and a concave curve indicates a **concave** weld face. These specify the required surface profile of the finished weld.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
> **Differentiate between parallel fillet weld and transverse fillet weld.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Parallel Fillet Weld | Transverse Fillet Weld |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of Load** | Load acts *parallel* to the weld axis | Load acts *perpendicular* to the weld axis |
| **Failure Plane** | Throat plane experiences **pure shear** | Throat plane experiences **combined tension and shear** |
| **Strength** | $P = 0.707 \, h \, L \, \tau_{all}$ (lower) | $P = 0.707 \, h \, L \, (0.707 \, \sigma_{t,all})$ (higher by ~70.7%) |
| **Stress Distribution** | Non-uniform, peaks at the ends | More uniform along the weld length |
| **Typical Application** | Lap joints in side beams | Transverse stiffeners in plate girders |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Question A (14 marks)**
> `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / June 2023 model]`

**Q.A (a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **different types of groove welds** used in butt joints. Draw the standard AWS A2.4 symbols for each.

**Q.A (b) [7 marks]** Two mild steel plates of thickness **12 mm** and **10 mm** are joined by a **parallel fillet weld** of leg size **8 mm** on both sides of a lap joint. The weld length is **100 mm**. The plate has an ultimate tensile strength of **410 MPa** and yield strength of **250 MPa**. A factor of safety of **3** is recommended. Determine the **safe load** the joint can carry. Use $\tau_{all} = 0.5 \, \sigma_{t,allowable}$ as per IS 800.

---

**Model Solution — Q.A (a) [7 marks]**

**Groove welds** are used in **butt joints** where two plates lie in the same plane and full penetration is required.

| # | Type | AWS Symbol | Description | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Square Groove** | $\vert\vert$ | Two vertical lines | Plates $\leq 6$ mm |
| 2 | **V-Groove** | V | Letter V | Plates $6$–$20$ mm |
| 3 | **Bevel Groove** | V-shape with one vertical side | Plates of unequal thickness | T-joint or corner |
| 4 | **U-Groove** | U-shape | Thick plates, less filler metal | Plates $> 20$ mm |
| 5 | **J-Groove** | J-shape | One curved, one straight side | Unequal access |

**Sketch (description for answer sheet):** Draw the reference line; place the symbol on the lower side of the reference line for arrow-side placement. Add dimensions — root gap $b$, groove depth $d$, groove angle $\alpha$, included angle.

(1 mark per type × 5 = 5 marks; 1 mark for standard reference to AWS A2.4; 1 mark for sketch)

---

**Model Solution — Q.A (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- Plate thicknesses: $t_1 = 12$ mm, $t_2 = 10$ mm
- Fillet leg: $h = 8$ mm (parallel fillet on **both sides** of lap)
- Weld length per side: $L = 100$ mm
- $\sigma_u = 410$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa
- FOS $= 3$, $\tau_{all} = 0.5 \, \sigma_{t,all}$

**Step 1 — Compute allowable tensile stress.**

$$
\sigma_{t,all} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\text{FOS}} = \frac{250}{3} = 83.33 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute allowable shear stress.**

$$
\tau_{all} = 0.5 \times 83.33 = 41.67 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective throat thickness.**

$$
t_e = 0.707 \times h = 0.707 \times 8 = 5.656 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Total effective weld area (both sides).**

$$
A_t = 2 \times t_e \times L = 2 \times 5.656 \times 100 = 1131.2 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Safe load on the parallel fillet weld.**

$$
P = \tau_{all} \times A_t = 41.67 \times 1131.2
$$

$$
P = 47137 \text{ N} \approx 47.14 \text{ kN}
$$

**Answer: Safe load = 47.14 kN** (1 mark for final answer + 1 mark for unit; full marks for step-by-step)

---

> **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B (a) [7 marks]** With the help of neat sketches, explain the **eight elements of a complete welding symbol** as per **AWS A2.4 standard**.

**Q.B (b) [7 marks]** A tie-rod of a structural frame carries an axial tensile load of **80 kN**. It is to be connected to a gusset plate (12 mm thick) using **transverse fillet welds** on both sides. Take $\sigma_{t,all} = 110$ MPa. Design the weld — i.e., determine the **leg size $h$** and the **weld length $L$** for a balanced design.

---

**Model Solution — Q.B (a) [7 marks]**

The eight elements of an AWS A2.4 welding symbol are:

1. **Arrow (0.5 mark)** — Leader line pointing to the joint. Defines the *arrow side* and *other side*.
2. **Reference Line (0.5 mark)** — Horizontal line, drawn parallel to the bottom of the drawing sheet. Anchor of the symbol.
3. **Weld Symbol (1 mark)** — Triangle (fillet), V, U, etc. Placed below the line = arrow side; above = other side.
4. **Dimensions (1 mark)** — Weld size (leg), length, pitch, number, spacing.
5. **Supplementary Symbols (1 mark)** — All-around (circle), field weld (flag), melt-through, backing.
6. **Finish Symbol (1 mark)** — C (chipping), G (grinding), M (machining), R (rolling), P (peening), or contour symbols.
7. **Tail (0.5 mark)** — Right side extension of reference line for WPS notes.
8. **Specification (0.5 mark)** — Process (SMAW, GTAW), electrode, inspection method written near the tail.

**Sketch (mandatory for full marks):** Draw a horizontal reference line; attach an arrow to its left end pointing to a sketched joint; place a fillet-weld triangle on the reference line; add leg dimension "8" to the left of the triangle, length "100" to the right; add a small circle at the arrow junction (all-around); add a flag (field weld); add a small triangle to the tail indicating "Shop weld, GMAW process".

(7 marks = 1 mark per element × 6 + 1 mark for the labelled sketch)

---

**Model Solution — Q.B (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- Axial tensile load: $P = 80$ kN $= 80{,}000$ N
- Transverse fillet welds on **both sides** of gusset plate
- $\sigma_{t,all} = 110$ MPa

**Step 1 — Strength formula for transverse fillet weld (both sides).**

$$
P = 2 \times \left[ 0.707 \, h \, L \, (0.707 \, \sigma_{t,all}) \right]
$$

$$
P = 2 \times 0.5 \, h \, L \, \sigma_{t,all} = h \, L \, \sigma_{t,all}
$$

**Step 2 — Check minimum leg size from IS 9595.**

$$
h_{\min} = \sqrt{2 \, t_{\max}} = \sqrt{2 \times 12} = \sqrt{24} = 4.9 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt standard size $h = 6$ mm.

**Step 3 — Solve for weld length $L$.**

$$
L = \frac{P}{h \times \sigma_{t,all}} = \frac{80{,}000}{6 \times 110} = \frac{80{,}000}{660} = 121.2 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Add 2 × $h$ effective length correction.**

$$
L_{total} = L + 2h = 121.2 + 12 = 133.2 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt standard length $L = 135$ mm (round to nearest 5 mm).

**Step 5 — Verify maximum length rule (parallel rule for length ≤ 100 h).**

$$
L_{\max} = 100 \, h = 100 \times 6 = 600 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark \, (\text{our } L = 135 \text{ mm is well within})
$$

**Answer:** Leg size $h = 6$ mm; Weld length per side $L = 135$ mm (or 140 mm for safety margin).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–3 Marks Each in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Wrong placement of the weld symbol** on the reference line. *Examiner's note:* Marks are deducted if the symbol is placed above the line for an arrow-side weld.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by 2** for double-sided welds (both sides of lap joint, both sides of gusset plate). Always re-read the problem statement for "both sides" or "on either side".
> 3. **Using $h$ instead of $t_e = 0.707h$** as the effective section. The leg $h$ is **NOT** the load-resisting section — the throat is.
> 4. **Mixing up parallel and transverse formulas.** Parallel = $0.707 \, hL\tau_{all}$; transverse = $0.707 \, hL \times 0.707\sigma_{t,all}$.
> 5. **Not adding effective length correction** $L_{eff} = L - 2h$ for weld crater losses (loses 1 mark consistently).
> 6. **Skipping the sketch** in 7-mark questions. Even a rough block diagram earns the 1 mark reserved for "neat sketch".
> 7. **Wrong units** — confusing MPa with N/mm² (they are equal, but write MPa consistently), or mixing kN and N.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] Welding symbols follow **AWS A2.4** (US) / **IS 813** / **ISO 2553** (international) — KTU accepts either.
- [x] A complete symbol = **Arrow + Reference Line + Weld Symbol + Dimensions + Supplementary Symbols + Finish Symbol + Tail + Specification**.
- [x] **Below reference line = arrow side; above = other side.** Placement is the most frequently tested concept.
- [x] **Effective throat of fillet weld** is the most important derived quantity: $t_e = 0.707 \, h$.
- [x] **Parallel fillet strength:** $P = 0.707 \, h \, L \, \tau_{all}$ — pure shear.
- [x] **Transverse fillet strength:** $P = 0.707 \, h \, L \, (0.707 \, \sigma_{t,all})$ — combined loading, ~70.7% stronger than parallel.
- [x] **Butt (groove) weld strength:** $P = t \, L \, \sigma_{t,all}$ — full plate thickness, full penetration.
- [x] **Minimum leg size (IS 9595):** $h_{\min} = \sqrt{2 \, t_{\max}}$ for $t \leq 25$ mm.
- [x] **Maximum weld length rule:** $L \leq 100 h$ for parallel fillets to avoid uneven stress distribution.
- [x] **Effective length correction:** $L_{eff} = L - 2h$ (or $L + 2h$ for total layout).
- [x] **Supplementary symbols to remember:** Flag (field weld), Circle (all-around), Straight line (flush contour), Convex/concave arcs (surface profile).
- [x] **Finish codes:** C = chip, G = grind, M = machine, R = roll, P = peen.
- [x] **Common groove welds:** Square, V, Bevel, U, J — V is the most asked in KTU.
- [x] **Always add a sketch** in 7-mark derivation questions — a labelled diagram fetches easy 1 mark.
- [x] **Always state standards** (AWS A2.4 / IS 813 / IS 800 / IS 9595) in your first line of the answer to show examiner that you know the codal references.
- [x] **Units discipline:** use **MPa** for stress, **mm** for dimensions, **N or kN** for load. Never mix.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stresses in Fillet and Butt Welds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stresses in Fillet and Butt Welds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **welded joint** is a permanent joint formed by localized coalescence (fusion) of metallic surfaces, produced by heating the materials to a suitable temperature with or without the application of pressure and with or without the use of filler metal. In machine design, welded joints are broadly classified into two principal categories based on the geometry of the fused region:

1. **Fillet Weld** – A weld of approximately triangular cross-section joining two surfaces that meet at an angle (typically between 60° and 120°, most commonly 90°).
2. **Butt Weld** – A weld joining two plates or members lying in the same plane, where the weld metal is deposited between the prepared edges of the joint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under static loading, the design of a welded joint is governed by **shear failure** (in fillet welds) and **normal/tensile failure** (in butt welds). Permissible stresses are derived from the ultimate tensile strength of the electrode / parent metal.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine two pieces of metal that you want to glue together at a right angle:

- **Butt weld** is like placing the two ends face-to-face (like two book pages meeting at a flat seam) and melting them together so they become one continuous piece.
- **Fillet weld** is like putting a triangular dab of molten metal into the inside corner of an "L"-shape — the metal fills the corner gap like icing on the corner of a cake.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does a fillet weld fail in shear, not tension?**
> Because the weakest section of the fillet weld is not the leg but the **throat** — the shortest distance from the root to the hypotenuse of the triangular cross-section. Failure occurs by shearing along this throat plane, similar to how a paper clip shears when you twist it.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| **Leg size** | $s$ | The size of the fillet weld leg measured along the fusion faces |
| **Throat thickness** | $t$ | The shortest distance from the root of the weld to the face (hypotenuse) |
| **Effective length** | $L_w$ | Total length minus 2 × leg size (to account for crater at ends) |
| **Fusion zone** | — | The interface between weld metal and parent metal |
| **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** | — | The parent metal zone adjacent to the weld whose microstructure is altered |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** For an **equal-leg right-angled fillet weld**, throat thickness $t = 0.707 \, s = s / \sqrt{2}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stress Analysis of Fillet Welds (Longitudinal / Parallel)

When a fillet weld is loaded **parallel to its axis** (longitudinal fillet weld), the load creates a **shear stress** on the throat cross-section.

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

1. Load $F$ is applied parallel to the weld axis.
2. The weakest plane is the throat section, oriented at 45° to the load.
3. Area resisting shear = throat thickness × effective length.
4. The throat acts like a rivet in shear — failure is by tearing along the throat.

For a single fillet weld:

$$\tau = \dfrac{F}{0.707 \, s \, L_w}$$

For **two parallel fillet welds** (one on each side):

$$\tau = \dfrac{F}{2 \times 0.707 \, s \, L_w}$$

---

### 2.2 Stress Analysis of Fillet Welds (Transverse)

When a fillet weld is loaded **perpendicular to its axis** (transverse fillet weld), the throat plane is again inclined at 45° to the load, producing a **combination** of:

- **Direct shear** on the throat (acting parallel to the load)
- **Pure normal (tensile/compressive) stress** on the throat (acting normal to the throat)

Both stresses occur on the same throat plane.

**Normal stress on throat:**

$$\sigma_n = \dfrac{F \sin 45°}{s \cos 45° \times L_w} = \dfrac{F}{s \, L_w}$$

Wait — let me re-derive carefully:

Area of throat plane per unit length (inclined) $= s \cos 45° = s / \sqrt{2}$

$$A_{throat} = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{2}} \times L_w = 0.707 \, s \, L_w$$

**Shear stress on throat (parallel component):**

$$\tau = \dfrac{F \cos 45°}{0.707 \, s \, L_w} = \dfrac{F}{2 \times 0.707 \, s \, L_w} = \dfrac{F}{s \, L_w \sqrt{2}}$$

Actually, the standard textbook derivation simplifies as:

$$\tau_{throat} = \dfrac{F}{0.707 \, s \, L_w}$$

This is the **average shear stress** acting along the throat plane, with the assumption that the weld has a uniform strength factor of 0.707.

---

### 2.3 Strength of Fillet Weld Under Combined Loading

For fillet welds subjected to **combined shear and tension** (e.g., a transverse fillet weld carrying an eccentric or direct load):

The combined **principal (maximum) shear stress** is given by:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\tau^2 + \sigma^2}$$

where $\tau$ is the shear stress on the throat plane and $\sigma$ is the normal (tensile) stress on the throat plane.

The **equivalent shear stress** (using maximum shear stress theory):

$$\tau_{eq} = \sqrt{\tau^2 + \sigma^2} \leq \tau_{permissible}$$

Some texts use:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2} \leq \sigma_{permissible}$$

(using von Mises / equivalent stress). The KTU standard practice uses:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Criterion for Fillet Welds:**
> $$\sigma' = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2} \leq S_{yt} / (FoS)$$
> where $S_{yt}$ is the yield strength of the electrode / weld metal.

---

### 2.4 Strength of Butt Welds

A butt weld has the **same cross-sectional area** as the parent plate (assuming full penetration).

**Tensile / Compressive stress in butt weld:**

$$\sigma_t = \dfrac{F}{L \times t}$$

where:
- $L$ = length of the weld (≈ plate width)
- $t$ = thickness of the thinner plate

**Shear stress in butt weld (if loaded transversely to cause shear):**

$$\tau = \dfrac{F}{L \times t}$$

The design strength of a butt weld is generally equal to the **strength of the parent metal**, provided:
- Full penetration is achieved
- The weld is free from defects
- Effective stress concentration factors are properly accounted for

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Joint Type | Loading | Governing Stress Formula | Design Criterion |
|---|------------|---------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Fillet weld (parallel) | Axial parallel load $F$ | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{0.707 \, s \, L_w}$ | $\tau \leq \tau_{perm}$ |
| 2 | Fillet weld (transverse) | Axial transverse load $F$ | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{0.707 \, s \, L_w}$ | $\tau \leq \tau_{perm}$ |
| 3 | Fillet weld (combined) | $\sigma$ + $\tau$ on throat | $\sigma' = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | $\sigma' \leq \sigma_{perm}$ |
| 4 | Butt weld (tension) | Axial tensile load $F$ | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{F}{L \times t}$ | $\sigma_t \leq \sigma_{perm}$ |
| 5 | Butt weld (compression) | Axial compressive load $F$ | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{F}{L \times t}$ | $\sigma_c \leq \sigma_{perm}$ |
| 6 | Throat thickness (equal leg) | Geometry | $t = 0.707 \, s$ | — |
| 7 | Effective weld length | End crater | $L_w = L_{total} - 2s$ | — |

**Permissible Stress Values (KTU Standard):**

| Material | $\sigma_{perm}$ (MPa) | $\tau_{perm}$ (MPa) |
|----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Mild Steel (IS 2062) | 100 – 140 | 70 – 100 |
| Structural Steel | 120 – 165 | 80 – 110 |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure vessels, boilers, penstocks**: Use butt welds for cylindrical shell joints to ensure uniform strength.
- **Structural steel frames**: Use fillet welds for connecting gusset plates, brackets, and stiffeners — economical and easy to inspect.
- **Machine tool frames**: Use fillet welds to attach base plates, ribs, and webs.
- **Ship hulls and bridges**: Combine both welds for cost-effective, high-strength joints.

> [!NOTE]
> The **fillet weld throat** is the critical design plane because it has the minimum cross-section AND the maximum stress concentration. Every fillet weld design must satisfy: **actual stress ≤ permissible stress of the weld metal**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Throat Thickness of an Equal-Leg Fillet Weld

Consider an equal-leg right-angled fillet weld with leg size $s$.

The cross-section of the weld is an **isosceles right triangle** with both legs of length $s$. The throat is the **hypotenuse** of this triangle (the minimum distance from the root to the face of the weld).

**Step 1:** By the Pythagorean theorem:

$$t^2 = s^2 + s^2 = 2s^2$$

**Step 2:** Take the square root:

$$t = \sqrt{2s^2} = s\sqrt{2}$$

**Step 3:** Rationalize the denominator / simplify:

$$t = \dfrac{s\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{2s}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \sqrt{2}$$

Re-evaluating cleanly:

$$t = \dfrac{s \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{2s}{2} = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Express as a decimal:

$$t = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{s \cdot \sqrt{2}}{2} = 0.707 \, s$$

$$\boxed{t = 0.707 \, s}$$

> This is the **fundamental geometric relationship** used in every fillet weld calculation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Shear Stress in a Longitudinal Fillet Weld

A lap joint is connected by two parallel fillet welds, each of leg size $s$ and length $L$, subjected to an axial load $F$.

**Step 1:** Identify the failure plane. The throat plane, inclined at 45°, is the weakest section.

**Step 2:** Compute the throat area of ONE weld:

$$A_1 = t \times L = 0.707 \, s \, L$$

**Step 3:** For TWO welds (both sides of the lap joint):

$$A_{total} = 2 \times 0.707 \, s \, L = 1.414 \, s \, L$$

**Step 4:** Apply the shear stress formula. Since the load tends to shear off the weld along the throat plane:

$$\tau = \dfrac{F}{A_{total}} = \dfrac{F}{1.414 \, s \, L}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = \dfrac{F}{1.414 \, s \, L}}$$

This can also be written equivalently as:

$$\tau = \dfrac{F}{0.707 \, s \, (2L)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Stress in a Transverse Fillet Weld

Consider a butt-lap joint with **transverse fillet welds** loaded by an axial force $F$.

The throat plane is inclined at 45° to the load direction.

**Step 1:** Resolve the force $F$ into components **along** and **perpendicular** to the throat plane:

- Component along the throat (shear): $F_{\parallel} = F \cos 45° = F / \sqrt{2}$
- Component normal to the throat (tension): $F_{\perp} = F \sin 45° = F / \sqrt{2}$

**Step 2:** Area of the throat plane (per weld):

$$A_{throat} = 0.707 \, s \, L$$

**Step 3:** Compute the stresses on the throat:

**Normal (tensile) stress:**

$$\sigma_n = \dfrac{F_{\perp}}{A_{throat}} = \dfrac{F / \sqrt{2}}{0.707 \, s \, L} = \dfrac{F}{0.707 \cdot \sqrt{2} \, s \, L} = \dfrac{F}{s \, L}$$

(since $0.707 \times \sqrt{2} = 1$)

$$\sigma_n = \dfrac{F}{s \, L}$$

**Shear stress:**

$$\tau = \dfrac{F_{\parallel}}{A_{throat}} = \dfrac{F / \sqrt{2}}{0.707 \, s \, L} = \dfrac{F}{s \, L}$$

$$\tau = \dfrac{F}{s \, L}$$

**Step 4:** Using the maximum shear stress theory (Tresca), the equivalent stress is:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_n^2 + 4\tau^2}$$

Substituting equal values:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_n^2 + 4\sigma_n^2} = \sqrt{5 \, \sigma_n^2} = \sqrt{5} \, \sigma_n$$

$$\sigma_{eq} = 2.236 \times \dfrac{F}{s \, L}$$

**Step 5:** Compare with the longitudinal fillet weld (which has only pure shear):

$$\tau_{long} = \dfrac{F}{1.414 \, s \, L} = 0.707 \, \dfrac{F}{s \, L}$$

**Ratio:**

$$\dfrac{\sigma_{eq,trans}}{\tau_{long}} = \dfrac{2.236}{0.707} \approx 3.16$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A transverse fillet weld is theoretically about **3.16 times stronger** than a longitudinal fillet weld of the same size and length — but in practice, due to stress concentration, this ratio is taken as approximately **1.5 to 2**. Always use permissible stress values from the design code rather than relying on this theoretical ratio.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Stress in a Butt Weld (Axial Tension)

Two plates of thickness $t$ and width $b$ are joined by a single-V butt weld and subjected to axial tension $F$.

**Step 1:** The cross-section of the weld equals the cross-section of the plate (full penetration assumed).

$$A_{weld} = b \times t$$

**Step 2:** The tensile stress in the weld:

$$\sigma_t = \dfrac{F}{A_{weld}} = \dfrac{F}{b \, t}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_t = \dfrac{F}{b \, t}}$$

**Step 3:** The strength of the butt weld equals the strength of the parent plate (provided the weld metal has at least equal strength):

$$F_{max} = \sigma_{perm} \times b \times t$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Insight:** For butt welds, the **weld efficiency** is normally 100% when using a matching electrode. The efficiency reduces only if partial penetration, V-groove angle issues, or mismatched electrode strength are present.

---

### 3.5 Comprehensive Worked Example

**Problem:**
A tie bar of width $b = 80$ mm and thickness $t = 12$ mm carries an axial tensile load of $F = 96$ kN. It is connected to a gusset plate using:
- (i) A double parallel (longitudinal) fillet weld with leg size $s = 8$ mm
- (ii) A double transverse fillet weld with leg size $s = 8$ mm

Determine the **length of weld required** in each case. Take permissible shear stress $\tau_{perm} = 90$ MPa.

---

**Case (i): Longitudinal Fillet Weld**

Given:
- $F = 96,000$ N
- $s = 8$ mm
- $\tau_{perm} = 90$ MPa
- Number of welds = 2

Using:

$$\tau = \dfrac{F}{2 \times 0.707 \, s \, L}$$

Rearranging:

$$L = \dfrac{F}{2 \times 0.707 \, s \, \tau_{perm}}$$

Substituting:

$$L = \dfrac{96{,}000}{2 \times 0.707 \times 8 \times 90}$$

$$L = \dfrac{96{,}000}{1018.08}$$

$$L \approx 94.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Effective length** (excluding end craters): $L_w = L_{total} - 2s$

If $L$ is total length including craters:

$$L_{total} = L_w + 2s = L_w + 16 \text{ mm}$$

If $L$ above is effective length, use directly.

Assuming the calculated $L$ is the **effective** length:

$$\boxed{L \approx 94.3 \text{ mm per side}}$$

---

**Case (ii): Transverse Fillet Weld**
