For transverse fillet welds, the failure is by combined shear and tension. Using the equivalent stress criterion:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_n^2 + 4\tau^2} = \sqrt{5} \, \sigma_n$$

Or, equivalently, the shear stress formula is applied with a **stress concentration / orientation factor**.

Using the simplified transverse weld strength approach (treating as shear on throat with transverse weld strength factor ~1.5):

$$\tau_{eq} = \dfrac{F}{2 \times 0.707 \, s \, L \times \text{strength factor}}$$

Or directly using the maximum stress:

The maximum combined stress on the throat plane:

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{F}{s \, L}$$

(taking $0.707 \times \sqrt{2} = 1$ for combined stress component)

But conservatively, treat transverse fillet weld as shear:

$$\tau = \dfrac{F}{2 \times 0.707 \, s \, L} \leq \tau_{perm}$$

$$L = \dfrac{F}{2 \times 0.707 \, s \, \tau_{perm}} = \dfrac{96{,}000}{2 \times 0.707 \times 8 \times 90} \approx 94.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Comparison:**

| Joint Type | Length Required |
|------------|-----------------|
| Longitudinal fillet weld | ≈ 94.3 mm |
| Transverse fillet weld (conservative) | ≈ 94.3 mm |
| Transverse fillet weld (using 1.5 strength factor) | ≈ 63 mm |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Weld Geometry and Stress Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["LOAD F APPLIED AXIALLY"] --> B{"TYPE OF WELD"}
    B --> C["FILLET WELD"]
    B --> D["BUTT WELD"]
    C --> E{"LOAD DIRECTION"}
    E --> F["PARALLEL LONGITUDINAL"]
    E --> G["TRANSVERSE"]
    F --> H["SHEAR ON THROAT PLANE"]
    G --> I["COMBINED SHEAR AND TENSION ON THROAT"]
    H --> J["TAU EQUALS F BY 1.414 S L"]
    I --> K["SIGMA PRIME EQUALS ROOT SIGMA SQ PLUS 3 TAU SQ"]
    D --> L["BUTT WELD TENSION"]
    L --> M["SIGMA EQUALS F BY L T"]
    J --> N["CHECK TAU LESS THAN TAU PERM"]
    K --> O["CHECK SIGMA PRIME LESS THAN SIGMA PERM"]
    M --> P["CHECK SIGMA LESS THAN SIGMA PERM"]
    N --> Q["DESIGN SAFE"]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Weld Failure Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        P1["AXIAL LOAD F"]
        P2["LEG SIZE S"]
        P3["WELD LENGTH L"]
        P4["PLATE THICKNESS T"]
        P5["PERMISSIBLE STRESS"]
    end
    subgraph GEOMETRY["GEOMETRY MODULE"]
        G1["THROAT T EQUALS 0.707 S"]
        G2["EFFECTIVE LENGTH LW EQUALS L MINUS 2S"]
    end
    subgraph STRESS["STRESS COMPUTATION"]
        S1["SHEAR STRESS TAU"]
        S2["NORMAL STRESS SIGMA"]
        S3["COMBINED STRESS SIGMA PRIME"]
    end
    subgraph VERIFY["VERIFICATION MODULE"]
        V1["COMPUTED LESS THAN PERMISSIBLE"]
        V2["SAFE OR UNSAFE FLAG"]
    end
    P1 --> S1
    P1 --> S2
    P2 --> G1
    P3 --> G2
    G1 --> S1
    G2 --> S1
    P4 --> S2
    P1 --> S3
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> S3
    S1 --> V1
    S2 --> V1
    S3 --> V1
    P5 --> V1
    V1 --> V2
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic: Fillet Weld vs. Butt Weld (Textual Cross-Section)

| Feature | Fillet Weld | Butt Weld |
|---------|-------------|-----------|
| Cross-section shape | Triangular (45°-45°-90°) | Rectangular (matches plate) |
| Failure plane | Throat (inclined 45°) | Cross-section perpendicular to load |
| Governing stress | Shear (primary) | Normal tension/compression |
| Typical load | Lap joints, T-joints | Plates in same plane |
| Strength relative to plate | 60–80% (varies) | 100% (full penetration) |
| Leg / throat relation | $t = 0.707 s$ | $t = t_{plate}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual cue for students:** When you look at a fillet weld cross-section, the throat is the **hypotenuse** of the triangle. The force "tries to slide" the upper plate along this hypotenuse — that's why we use **shear stress** for fillet welds.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**What is a fillet weld? State the relationship between leg size and throat thickness for an equal-leg fillet weld.**

**Model Answer:**

A fillet weld is a weld of approximately triangular cross-section used to join two surfaces that meet at an angle (typically 60° to 120°). It is the most common type of weld used in machine structures.

For an equal-leg right-angled fillet weld with leg size $s$:

$$t = 0.707 \, s$$

where $t$ is the throat thickness (shortest distance from root to hypotenuse). This relationship is derived from Pythagoras' theorem applied to the isosceles right triangle cross-section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3-mark framing:** Definition (1 mark) + Diagram/drawing cue (1 mark) + Relationship derivation (1 mark). Always mention $0.707$ explicitly.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Why is a butt weld considered stronger than a fillet weld of the same size?**

**Model Answer:**

A butt weld is considered stronger than a fillet weld because:

1. The cross-section of a butt weld is **rectangular** and equal to the parent plate cross-section (in case of full penetration), so it carries load by **direct tension/compression**.

2. A fillet weld fails by **shear along the throat plane**, which is inclined at 45° and has area only $0.707 \, s \, L_w$ — significantly less than the full plate area.

3. The presence of **stress concentration** at the weld toe in fillet welds further reduces its effective strength.

4. Butt welds achieve **100% weld efficiency** (with full penetration and matching electrode), whereas fillet welds typically achieve **60–80%** efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3-mark framing:** Two distinct technical reasons (2 marks) + Efficiency comparison (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A tie bar $80$ mm wide and $12$ mm thick is welded to a gusset plate by **two parallel fillet welds**. The bar carries an axial tensile load of $120$ kN. Calculate the **size of the weld** required. Take permissible shear stress $\tau_{perm} = 100$ MPa.

**(b)** If the same tie bar is welded using a **transverse fillet weld** (two welds), determine the **length of weld** required. Discuss the relative strength of longitudinal vs transverse fillet welds.

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Weld Size Calculation**

Given:
- $b = 80$ mm (width of tie bar)
- $t = 12$ mm (thickness)
- $F = 120,000$ N
- $\tau_{perm} = 100$ MPa
- Number of welds = 2

**Step 1:** Weld size limits. As per IS 816 / AWS standards:

$$s_{min} \approx 5 \text{ mm}, \quad s_{max} \leq t_{min} - 1.5 \text{ mm} = 12 - 1.5 = 10.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$5 \leq s \leq 10.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the shear stress formula for parallel fillet weld:

$$\tau = \dfrac{F}{2 \times 0.707 \, s \, L}$$

But $L$ is not given — so we solve for $s$ by assuming the bar width as effective length (if weld length equals bar width 80 mm):

$$\tau = \dfrac{120{,}000}{2 \times 0.707 \times s \times 80} = \dfrac{120{,}000}{113.12 \, s}$$

Setting $\tau = \tau_{perm}$:

$$100 = \dfrac{120{,}000}{113.12 \, s}$$

$$s = \dfrac{120{,}000}{113.12 \times 100} = \dfrac{120{,}000}{11{,}312} \approx 10.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Check against limits. $s = 10.6$ mm > $s_{max} = 10.5$ mm, so we use $s_{max} = 10$ mm and increase weld length.

**Final Answer (a):**

$$\boxed{s = 10 \text{ mm (with adequate length)}}$$

---

**(b) Transverse Weld Length**

For transverse fillet weld (2 welds), the failure occurs by combined stress:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_n^2 + 4\tau^2} = \sqrt{5} \times \dfrac{F}{s \, L}$$

Or, conservatively using the same shear formula:

$$\tau = \dfrac{F}{2 \times 0.707 \, s \, L} \leq \tau_{perm}$$

With $s = 10$ mm:

$$L = \dfrac{120{,}000}{2 \times 0.707 \times 10 \times 100} = \dfrac{120{,}000}{1414} \approx 84.9 \text{ mm}$$

Using the equivalent stress approach with factor $\sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$:

$$L = \dfrac{F}{s \times \tau_{perm} / 1.5} \approx \dfrac{120{,}000}{10 \times 100 / 1.5} = 180 \text{ mm (approx, conservative)}$$

**Relative Strength Discussion:**

The theoretical strength of a transverse fillet weld is approximately **$\sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$ times** that of a longitudinal fillet weld of the same dimensions, because the throat plane in a transverse weld resists load by combined shear and tension, whereas in a longitudinal weld, only shear acts.

In practice, the **stress concentration** at the toe of transverse welds reduces this theoretical advantage, and the practical strength ratio is taken as **1.5 to 2.0**.

**Final Answer (b):**

$$\boxed{L \approx 85 \text{ mm (using simple shear criterion)}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **stress distribution in a transverse fillet weld** with a neat sketch. Derive expressions for normal and shear stresses on the throat plane.

**(b)** A circular bar of diameter $40$ mm is welded end-to-end to another bar by a **butt weld**. The joint is subjected to an axial tensile load of $80$ kN. Determine:
- (i) The maximum tensile stress in the weld.
- (ii) The safety factor of the joint, if the ultimate tensile strength of the weld metal is $400$ MPa.
- (iii) The maximum load the joint can carry if the permissible stress is $120$ MPa.

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Stress Distribution in Transverse Fillet Weld**

A transverse fillet weld is loaded perpendicular to its length. The throat plane is inclined at 45° to the direction of load.

**Derivation:**

Let $F$ be the axial load, $s$ the leg size, and $L$ the weld length.

**Throat plane area:**

$$A_t = 0.707 \, s \, L = \dfrac{s \, L}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Resolving $F$ along and perpendicular to throat:**

$$F_{\parallel} = F \cos 45° = \dfrac{F}{\sqrt{2}}$$

$$F_{\perp} = F \sin 45° = \dfrac{F}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Normal (tensile) stress on throat:**

$$\sigma_n = \dfrac{F_{\perp}}{A_t} = \dfrac{F / \sqrt{2}}{s \, L / \sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{F}{s \, L}$$

**Shear stress on throat:**

$$\tau = \dfrac{F_{\parallel}}{A_t} = \dfrac{F}{s \, L}$$

**Combined stress (using Tresca):**

$$\sigma_{max} = \sqrt{\sigma_n^2 + 4\tau^2} = \sqrt{5} \, \dfrac{F}{s \, L}$$

---

**(b) Butt Weld Analysis**

Given:
- Bar diameter $d = 40$ mm
- Axial tensile load $F = 80$ kN
- Ultimate tensile strength $S_{ut} = 400$ MPa
- Permissible stress $\sigma_{perm} = 120$ MPa

**Step 1:** Cross-sectional area:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 40^2 = 1256.64 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 (i): Maximum tensile stress:**

$$\sigma_t = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{1256.64} = 63.66 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_t \approx 63.66 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3 (ii): Factor of Safety:**

$$FoS = \dfrac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_t} = \dfrac{400}{63.66} \approx 6.28$$

$$\boxed{FoS \approx 6.28}$$

**Step 4 (iii): Maximum load:**

$$F_{max} = \sigma_{perm} \times A = 120 \times 1256.64 = 150{,}797 \text{ N} \approx 150.8 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{F_{max} \approx 150.8 \text{ kN}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 0.707**: Many students compute weld area as $s \times L$ instead of $0.707 \, s \, L$. This makes the weld appear stronger than it actually is, leading to an unsafe design — and **3 marks deduction** in KTU valuation.
>
> 2. **Using throat thickness instead of leg size for the weld "size"**: When asked for "size of weld", always return $s$ (leg size), not $t$ (throat).
>
> 3. **Ignoring effective length**: $L_{eff} = L_{total} - 2s$ for fillet welds (to account for craters). Forgetting this in long welds (>500 mm) leads to small errors, but for short welds (< 100 mm), it's a **major mistake**.
>
> 4. **Confusing transverse and longitudinal weld formulas**: Transverse welds have combined stress; longitudinal welds have pure shear. Do NOT use the same formula blindly.
>
> 5. **Mixing up units**: $F$ in N, $s$ in mm, $\tau$ in MPa (N/mm²) — keep consistent!
>
> 6. **Not checking weld size limits**: Weld leg size must be $\geq 5$ mm and $\leq t_{min} - 1.5$ mm.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **fillet weld** has triangular cross-section; **throat thickness** $t = 0.707 \, s$.
- A **butt weld** has rectangular cross-section equal to the parent plate (in full penetration).
- Fillet welds fail by **shear on the throat plane**; butt welds fail by **normal stress**.
- **Longitudinal fillet welds** carry pure shear: $\tau = F / (1.414 \, s \, L)$.
- **Transverse fillet welds** carry combined shear and normal stress: $\sigma' = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$.
- **Effective weld length** = total length − 2 × leg size (to exclude crater ends).
- **Weld size limits**: $s_{min} \approx 5$ mm; $s_{max} \leq t_{min} - 1.5$ mm.
- **Butt weld efficiency** ≈ 100% with full penetration + matching electrode.
- **Fillet weld efficiency** ≈ 60–80% (lower due to shear failure mode and stress concentration).
- **Transverse fillet weld is theoretically stronger** than longitudinal fillet weld by a factor of $\sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$ (practically 1.5 to 2).
- **Design criterion**: Always ensure **computed stress ≤ permissible stress** of the electrode / weld metal.
- Standard permissible stresses: mild steel → $\sigma_{perm} = 100$–$140$ MPa, $\tau_{perm} = 70$–$100$ MPa.
- When combining stresses, use the **maximum shear stress theory (Tresca)** or **von Mises** for accurate design.
- **Inspection note**: Fillet welds are easier to inspect visually; butt welds often require NDT (radiography, ultrasonic) for defect detection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Butt Joint in Tension
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Butt Joint in Tension

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

A **Butt Joint** is a type of welded joint formed by placing two plates edge-to-edge in the same plane and joining them by depositing weld metal along the interface. When the joint is subjected to a **tensile load**, the configuration is termed a **Butt Joint in Tension**. The weld transmits the axial tensile force directly across the fusion zone, making the throat thickness equal to the plate thickness (for **full penetration welds**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMET602 - Module 2):**
> Butt joints are classified under *Welded Joints* (Permanent Joints). Per IS 800:2007 (General Construction in Steel — Code of Practice), butt welds are treated as the parent metal itself when full penetration is achieved, and the design strength is governed by the **throat area** and **permissible tensile stress of the parent metal**.

### Types of Butt Joints (Edge Preparations)

| Type | Edge Preparation | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Square Butt** | No bevel; edges square | Plates ≤ 6 mm thick |
| **Single V-Butt** | One side bevelled to a V (≈ 60°) | Plates 6 – 20 mm thick |
| **Double V-Butt** | Both sides bevelled to a V | Plates > 20 mm thick (economy of weld metal) |
| **Single U-Butt** | One side grooved to U-shape | Thick plates; less weld metal than V |
| **Double U-Butt** | Both sides U-shaped | Very thick plates (> 25 mm) |
| **Single J-Butt** | One side J-shaped | Different thickness plate joining |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two slices of bread held together with butter (the **weld metal**) smeared along the cut edges. If you pull the slices apart in opposite directions (tension), the butter film must resist being pulled apart. The thicker the butter film (= **throat thickness**) and the longer the smeared region (= **weld length**), the harder it is to separate the slices. If the butter only coats one side of the cut, it's a **single-V butt**; if both sides are coated, it's a **double-V butt** (stronger but uses more butter).

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Permissible tensile stress in weld** (IS 800:2007): $\sigma_{at} = 0.6 \, f_y$ where $f_y$ = yield stress of parent metal.
- **Partial safety factor for weld metal** $\gamma_{mw}$: **1.25** (shop welding), **1.50** (field welding).
- **Partial safety factor for material** $\gamma_{m1}$: **1.10**.
- **Standard V-angle**: **60° ± 5°** (root gap ≈ 2 mm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Philosophy:** A *full penetration* butt weld is considered as strong as the parent plate itself, provided weld metal strength ≥ parent metal strength. This is why butt welds are preferred for high-stress, fatigue-loaded applications like pressure vessels, bridges, and crane girders.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept: How a Butt Joint Carries Tension

When two plates are welded along their edges and pulled in tension, the load path is:

1. **Plate 1** → **Fusion Zone (HAZ)** → **Weld Metal** → **Fusion Zone (HAZ)** → **Plate 2**
2. The weld acts as a **continuous connector** with a cross-section equal to *throat thickness × weld length*.
3. The **failure plane** is the weakest section — either through the parent plate or through the weld throat.

The butt weld can be loaded in two orientations:
- **Parallel Loading:** Load acts **along the length** of the weld.
- **Transverse Loading:** Load acts **perpendicular** to the weld axis.

### 2.2 Strength of Butt Joint — Governing Equations

#### Case A: Full Penetration Butt Weld (Most Common in KTU Problems)

For a **full penetration** butt weld, the effective throat thickness equals the plate thickness:

$$t_t = t_p$$

The **design tensile strength** of the butt weld is:

$$P_{dw} = \frac{f_y}{\gamma_{m1} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} \times l_w \times t_t$$

Where:
- $f_y$ = yield stress of parent metal (MPa)
- $\gamma_{m1}$ = partial safety factor for material = **1.10**
- $\gamma_{mw}$ = partial safety factor for weld metal = **1.25** (shop), **1.50** (field)
- $l_w$ = effective length of weld (= actual length for full penetration)
- $t_t$ = effective throat thickness

#### Case B: Permissible Stress Approach (Simpler KTU Method)

$$P_{allow} = \sigma_{at} \times l_w \times t_t$$

Where permissible tensile stress:

$$\sigma_{at} = 0.6 \, f_y$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-ready** and aligned with **IS 800:2007 / KTU 2024 Scheme** treatment. Use `\vert` notation to avoid table corruption.

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plate thickness | $t_p$ | Given input | mm |
| 2 | Effective throat thickness (full penetration) | $t_t$ | $t_t = t_p$ | mm |
| 3 | Effective weld length | $l_w$ | $l$ (no deduction for full penetration butt weld) | mm |
| 4 | Permissible tensile stress | $\sigma_{at}$ | $0.6 \, f_y$ | MPa |
| 5 | Permissible shear stress | $\tau_{av}$ | $0.4 \, f_y$ | MPa |
| 6 | Design strength (LRFD) | $P_{dw}$ | $\dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m1} \, \gamma_{mw}} \, l_w \, t_t$ | N |
| 7 | Allowable load (Working Stress) | $P_{all}$ | $\sigma_{at} \cdot l_w \cdot t_t$ | N |
| 8 | Transverse vs Parallel strength ratio | — | Transverse ≈ 1.0 to 1.3 × Parallel | — |
| 9 | Weld metal volume (V-butt single) | $V$ | $\dfrac{w^2 \cdot l}{2} + w \cdot l \cdot g$ | mm³ |
| 10 | Partial safety factor (shop weld) | $\gamma_{mw}$ | **1.25** | — |
| 11 | Partial safety factor (field weld) | $\gamma_{mw}$ | **1.50** | — |

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering

Butt joints in tension are the **backbone of welded structural engineering**. Real-world applications include:
- **Pressure vessels & boilers** (cylindrical shells joined longitudinally)
- **Bridge girders** (flange-to-flange splices)
- **Crane booms and jibs** (high fatigue loading)
- **Railway wagon chassis** and **ship hulls**
- **Transmission towers** and **offshore platforms**

The key engineering insight is that **a properly executed butt weld restores the parent metal strength**, allowing the joint to behave as a continuous member. This is why butt welds are always preferred over fillet welds for primary load paths.

### 2.5 Stress Concentration & Transverse Loading Effect

For **transverse loading**, experimental studies show the butt weld carries about **30% higher** load compared to parallel loading of the same throat area. This is because:
- The stress lines in transverse loading must "funnel" through the weld — creating a **2D stress state** (biaxial tension).
- In parallel loading, the stress is **uniaxial** and uniform.
- Codes often permit a **lower factor of safety** for transverse butt welds.

However, in **permissible stress methods (KTU standard)**, both cases use the same $\sigma_{at} = 0.6 f_y$, simplifying calculations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Strength of Butt Joint in Tension (Full Penetration)

**Given:**
- Two plates of thickness $t_p$ joined by a full penetration butt weld
- Weld length $l_w$
- Applied tensile load $P$

**Step 1 — Identify the failure plane**

The weld metal cross-section resisting tension = throat area:

$$A_t = l_w \times t_t$$

**Step 2 — Apply stress definition**

By definition of normal stress:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A_t} = \frac{P}{l_w \cdot t_t}$$

**Step 3 — Set up failure criterion**

The joint fails when the tensile stress in the throat exceeds the permissible stress:

$$\frac{P}{l_w \cdot t_t} \leq \sigma_{at}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for allowable load (Working Stress Method)**

$$P_{allow} = \sigma_{at} \cdot l_w \cdot t_t$$

**Step 5 — Substitute permissible stress**

Since $\sigma_{at} = 0.6 \, f_y$ (per IS 800 WSM):

$$P_{allow} = 0.6 \, f_y \cdot l_w \cdot t_t$$

**Step 6 — Express in LRFD form (Limit State Method)**

Using design strength approach with safety factors:

$$P_{dw} = \frac{f_y}{\gamma_{m1} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} \cdot l_w \cdot t_t$$

For shop welding with $\gamma_{m1} = 1.10$ and $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$:

$$P_{dw} = \frac{f_y}{1.375} \cdot l_w \cdot t_t \approx 0.727 \, f_y \cdot l_w \cdot t_t$$

### 3.2 Numerical Example — KTU Solved Problem Pattern

**Problem:** Two mild steel plates of thickness **12 mm** are joined by a **single V-butt weld** to transmit a tensile load of **180 kN**. The yield stress of the plate is **250 MPa**. Find the **required weld length** for:
(a) Working stress method (WSM)
(b) Limit state method (LSM) for shop welding

**Given Data:**
- $t_p = 12$ mm, $P = 180 \times 10^3$ N, $f_y = 250$ MPa
- For full penetration V-butt: $t_t = 12$ mm

---

**Solution (a) — Working Stress Method:**

Step 1: Permissible tensile stress:

$$\sigma_{at} = 0.6 \times f_y = 0.6 \times 250 = 150 \text{ MPa}$$

Step 2: Effective throat area required:

$$A_t = \frac{P}{\sigma_{at}} = \frac{180 \times 10^3}{150} = 1200 \text{ mm}^2$$

Step 3: Required weld length:

$$l_w = \frac{A_t}{t_t} = \frac{1200}{12} = 100 \text{ mm}$$

---

**Solution (b) — Limit State Method (Shop Welding):**

Step 1: Design strength formula:

$$P_{dw} = \frac{f_y}{\gamma_{m1} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} \cdot l_w \cdot t_t$$

Step 2: Substitute $\gamma_{m1} = 1.10$, $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$:

$$P_{dw} = \frac{250}{1.10 \times 1.25} \cdot l_w \cdot 12 = \frac{250}{1.375} \cdot l_w \cdot 12$$

Step 3: Simplify:

$$P_{dw} = 2181.82 \cdot l_w \text{ (in N, when } l_w \text{ in mm)}$$

Step 4: Equate to applied load $P = 180{,}000$ N:

$$l_w = \frac{180{,}000}{2181.82} = 82.5 \text{ mm}$$

Step 5: Adopt next higher standard length (KTU practice):

$$l_w = 85 \text{ mm} \approx 90 \text{ mm (preferred)}$$

---

**Analytical Comparison Table:**

| Method | Permissible / Design Stress | Required Length $l_w$ |
|---|---|---|
| WSM ($\sigma_{at} = 0.6 f_y$) | **150 MPa** | **100 mm** |
| LSM Shop (combined $\gamma = 1.375$) | ≈ 181.8 MPa | **82.5 mm** |
| LSM Field ($\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$) | ≈ 166.7 MPa | **90 mm** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** WSM gives a *more conservative* (longer) weld length because it uses a single lumped safety factor. LSM distinguishes material vs. fabrication uncertainty, yielding slightly more economical designs for shop welds.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Butt Joint?

| Criterion | Square Butt | Single V-Butt | Double V-Butt |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thickness range** | ≤ 6 mm | 6 – 20 mm | > 20 mm |
| **Weld metal volume** | Minimum | Moderate | ~50% less than single V |
| **Distortion** | Low | High | Moderate |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Higher prep, less weld |
| **Accessibility** | Both sides not required | One side only | Both sides required |
| **Strength efficiency** | 100% if full pen. | 100% if full pen. | 100% if full pen. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Butt Joint Geometry & Stress Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plate 1\nLeft Edge] --> B[Fusion Zone\nHAZ Left]
    B --> C[Weld Metal\nThroat Region\nThickness equals t_p]
    C --> D[Fusion Zone\nHAZ Right]
    D --> E[Plate 2\nRight Edge]
    F[Tensile Load P\nApplied at Left] -->|Tension| A
    E -->|Tension Reaction| G[Tensile Load P\nApplied at Right]
    H[Cross Section AA\nWidth equals l_w] -.-> C
    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFE699,stroke:#BF8F00,stroke-width:3px
    style E fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Edge Preparations Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Butt Joint Selection] --> Q1{Plate Thickness?}
    Q1 -->|Up to 6 mm| S1[Square Butt Joint\nNo Edge Prep]
    Q1 -->|6 to 20 mm| S2[Single V Butt Joint\n60 deg Bevel One Side]
    Q1 -->|Above 20 mm| S3[Double V Butt Joint\n60 deg Bevel Both Sides]
    Q2{Quality Requirement?} --> S4[Use Double U or J for Heavy Plates]
    S2 --> Calc1[Throat Thickness equals Plate Thickness]
    S3 --> Calc1
    S1 --> Calc1
    Calc1 --> Str[Design Strength Pd = fy x lw x tt / gamma]
    style Root fill:#D9E1F2,stroke:#2F5496,stroke-width:2px
    style Calc1 fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#BF8F00,stroke-width:2px
    style Str fill:#E2EFDA,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — KTU Problem-Solving Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start: Given P, t_p, f_y] --> P1[Assume Full Penetration Weld]
    P1 --> P2[Set Throat Thickness tt = tp]
    P2 --> P3{Choose Design Method}
    P3 -->|WSM| W1[Compute sigma_at = 0.6 x fy]
    P3 -->|LSM| L1[Compute fd = fy / gamma_m1 x gamma_mw]
    W1 --> W2[Required lw = P divided by sigma_at x tt]
    L1 --> L2[Required lw = P divided by fd x tt]
    W2 --> P4[Adopt Standard Length]
    L2 --> P4
    P4 --> P5[Check Transverse or Parallel Loading]
    P5 --> P6[Report Final Weld Length]
    style P0 fill:#DEEBF7,stroke:#2F5496
    style P6 fill:#C6E0B4,stroke:#548235,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Stress Flow in Transverse vs Parallel Butt Joint

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PARALLEL_CASE [PARALLEL LOADING]
        PL1[Load P] -->|Axial| PL2[Weld Throat\nUniaxial Stress]
        PL2 -->|Axial| PL3[Reaction P]
    end
    subgraph TRANSVERSE_CASE [TRANSVERSE LOADING]
        TL1[Load P] -->|Biaxial Funnel| TL2[Weld Throat\nBiaxial Stress]
        TL2 -->|Biaxial| TL3[Reaction P]
    end
    PARALLEL_CASE -->|Strength Ratio 1.0| COMP[Compare]
    TRANSVERSE_CASE -->|Strength Ratio 1.3| COMP
    style TL2 fill:#F8CBAD,stroke:#C00000
    style PL2 fill:#FFE699,stroke:#BF8F00
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define a butt joint. With a neat sketch, differentiate between single-V and double-V butt joints.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **butt joint** is a permanent joint formed by placing two members in the same plane and joining their edges by depositing weld metal, such that the tensile or compressive load is transmitted through the weld perpendicular or parallel to the line of the joint.

- **Single V-Butt:** Only one edge is bevelled to form a V-groove (≈ 60°) with a root gap of about 2 mm. Used for plates **6 – 20 mm** thick. Requires welding from **one side only**.
- **Double V-Butt:** Both edges are bevelled to form a V-groove on either side. Used for plates **> 20 mm** thick. Requires welding from **both sides**. Weld metal volume is **approximately half** that of a single V-butt, reducing distortion and cost.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State the permissible stresses in a butt weld as per IS 800.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

As per **IS 800:2007**, for a full-penetration butt weld treated as parent metal:
- **Permissible tensile stress** $\sigma_{at} = 0.6 \, f_y$
- **Permissible compressive stress** $\sigma_{ac} = 0.6 \, f_y$
- **Permissible shear stress** $\tau_{av} = 0.4 \, f_y$

Where $f_y$ is the **yield stress** of the parent metal. In limit state method, partial safety factors are $\gamma_{m1} = 1.10$ (material) and $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (shop weld) / 1.50 (field weld).

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting to convert kN to N** when computing areas — loses 1–2 marks.
> - Using $\sigma_{at} = 0.5 f_y$ (old code value) instead of $0.6 f_y$ (IS 800:2007) — **strictly outdated, lose 1 mark.**
> - Not specifying shop vs. field welding when using LSM — **partial safety factor mismatch loses 1 mark.**
> - For partial penetration welds, forgetting to compute effective throat from groove angle — **lose 2 marks.**
> - Writing $l_w = l - 2s$ for **butt welds** (this deduction applies to **fillet welds only**). Butt welds with full penetration use $l_w = l$.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

Two mild steel plates each **16 mm thick** are joined by a **single V-butt weld** to transmit a **tensile load of 250 kN**. The yield stress of the plate material is **320 MPa** and the ultimate tensile strength is **450 MPa**.

**(a)** Determine the **effective length of the weld** required by:
   (i) Working Stress Method (WSM)
   (ii) Limit State Method (LSM) for **shop welding** [7 marks]

**(b)** If the same plates were joined by a **double V-butt weld**, calculate the **percentage saving in weld metal volume** and comment on distortion. The V-groove angle is 60° with root gap 2 mm. [7 marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — (i) Working Stress Method:**

Given: $P = 250 \times 10^3$ N, $t_p = 16$ mm, $f_y = 320$ MPa.

Permissible tensile stress:

$$\sigma_{at} = 0.6 \, f_y = 0.6 \times 320 = 192 \text{ MPa}$$

For full penetration single V-butt: $t_t = t_p = 16$ mm.

Required length:

$$l_w = \frac{P}{\sigma_{at} \times t_t} = \frac{250 \times 10^3}{192 \times 16} = \frac{250000}{3072} = 81.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt $l_w = 85$ mm.**

**Part (a) — (ii) Limit State Method (Shop):**

$$P_{dw} = \frac{f_y}{\gamma_{m1} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} \cdot l_w \cdot t_t$$

$$\frac{f_y}{\gamma_{m1} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} = \frac{320}{1.10 \times 1.25} = \frac{320}{1.375} = 232.7 \text{ MPa}$$

$$l_w = \frac{250 \times 10^3}{232.7 \times 16} = \frac{250000}{3723.6} = 67.1 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt $l_w = 70$ mm.**

**Part (b) — Weld Metal Volume Comparison:**

For a single V-butt with groove angle $60°$, root gap $g = 2$ mm, depth of bevel $h = 16 - 2 = 14$ mm:

Cross-sectional area of weld (single V):

$$A_{SV} = g \cdot t_p + \frac{(t_p - g)^2 \tan(30°)}{2}$$

$$A_{SV} = 2 \times 16 + \frac{(16-2)^2 \times 0.5774}{2} = 32 + \frac{196 \times 0.5774}{2} = 32 + 56.6 = 88.6 \text{ mm}^2$$

For double V-butt, each side bevel depth ≈ $h/2 = 7$ mm:

$$A_{DV} = 2 \left[ g \cdot \frac{t_p}{2} + \frac{(t_p/2 - g)^2 \tan(30°)}{2} \right] = 2\left[16 + \frac{(8-2)^2 \times 0.5774}{2}\right]$$

$$A_{DV} = 2 \times [16 + 10.4] = 2 \times 26.4 = 52.8 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Percentage saving:**

$$\% \text{ saving} = \frac{88.6 - 52.8}{88.6} \times 100 = 40.4\%$$

**Distortion:** Double V-butt produces **symmetrical heat input** from both sides, hence **angular distortion is significantly reduced** compared to single V-butt. However, **flame-cut preparation cost is higher** on both sides.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** A tie member in a roof truss consists of two mild steel flats **100 mm × 12 mm** joined end-to-end by a **butt weld**. The joint is loaded in tension parallel to the weld. Determine the **tensile strength of the joint** if the permissible stress is 150 MPa. [7 marks]

**(b)** Explain with neat sketches the **types of butt joints** based on edge preparation. State **two advantages** of double-V butt joint over single-V butt joint. [7 marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Given: Plate cross-section = $100 \times 12 = 1200 \text{ mm}^2$, $\sigma_{at} = 150$ MPa.

For a butt weld in tension parallel to load, full penetration is assumed. Throat thickness $t_t = t_p = 12$ mm.

Tensile strength of the joint:

$$P_{allow} = \sigma_{at} \times l_w \times t_t$$

But the **weld length = width of plate = 100 mm** (since plates are joined end-to-end across the full width):

$$P_{allow} = 150 \times 100 \times 12 = 180{,}000 \text{ N} = 180 \text{ kN}$$

**Efficiency check:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{allow}}{P_{plate}} = \frac{\sigma_{at}}{\sigma_{at,plate}} = \frac{150}{150} = 100\%$$

A full penetration butt weld achieves **100% joint efficiency**.

**Part (b):** Types of butt joints and sketch (already covered in Q1 of Part A). **Two advantages of double-V over single-V:**

1. **Weld metal economy**: Approximately **40% less weld metal** is required (as computed in Q-A above), reducing cost and heat input.
2. **Reduced distortion**: Symmetrical heat input from both sides minimizes **angular (out-of-plane) distortion** and residual stresses.
3. *(Bonus)* Better mechanical properties in the weld root due to **refined grain structure** from re-welding the second pass.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Butt Joint in Tension**

- A **butt joint** joins plates edge-to-edge in the same plane; load is transmitted via the **weld throat**.
- For **full penetration** butt welds, **throat thickness $t_t$ = plate thickness $t_p$**.
- **Effective length** of full-penetration butt weld = **actual length** (no end deduction, unlike fillet welds).
- **Permissible tensile stress** (IS 800:2007 WSM): $\sigma_{at} = 0.6 \, f_y$.
- **LSM partial safety factors**: $\gamma_{m1} = 1.10$ (material), $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (shop) / $1.50$ (field).
- **Design strength formula**: $P_{dw} = \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m1} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} \cdot l_w \cdot t_t$.
- **Square butt** ≤ 6 mm; **Single V-butt** 6–20 mm; **Double V-butt** > 20 mm thickness.
- **Double V-butt** saves ~40% weld metal and reduces distortion compared to single V.
- **Transverse loading** gives ~30% higher strength than parallel loading (experimental observation), but codes use the same $\sigma_{at}$ for simplicity.
- **Joint efficiency** of a properly executed full-penetration butt weld = **100%**.
- **Root gap** for V-grooves = **2 mm** standard; **groove angle** = **60° ± 5°**.
- **Always state** whether welding is **shop or field** — it changes $\gamma_{mw}$ and hence $l_w$.
- **Convert units carefully**: $P$ in Newtons, lengths in mm, stress in MPa (= N/mm²).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fillet Weld in Tension
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fillet Weld in Tension

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Fillet Weld

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fillet Weld:** A **fillet weld** is a roughly triangular (right-angled or inclined) weld cross-section deposited in the corner formed between two intersecting members. It is the most commonly used type of weld for joining plates, sheets, and structural sections that meet at an angle (typically 90°).

**Key Geometric Parameters of a Fillet Weld:**

- **Leg Size ($s$ or $h$):** The length of the inclined side of the right triangle (i.e., the shorter leg if not symmetrical, but for a standard fillet weld, both legs are equal to $s$).
- **Throat Thickness ($t$ or $t_t$):** The perpendicular distance from the root (corner) to the hypotenuse (face) of the fillet weld.
- **Root:** The point where the two parent members intersect (the corner).
- **Face:** The outer exposed surface of the weld.
- **Toe:** The junction between the weld face and the parent metal on either side.

> [!NOTE]
> For an **equal-leg fillet weld** of leg size $s$, the theoretical throat thickness is $t = 0.707 \, s$. This is because $t = s \cdot \sin(45°) = s / \sqrt{2} \approx 0.707s$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine two steel plates placed at right angles forming an 'L' shape on a table. To "glue" them together permanently, you melt a triangular bead of molten metal into the corner along the edge. As the molten metal solidifies, it forms a strong triangular bridge (the **fillet**) that locks the two plates together.

Think of the **throat** as the "narrow bridge" that bears all the load. Even though the weld bead looks chunky from outside, the actual load-carrying cross-section is the thin diagonal line through it. **This is the most critical concept: design stress calculations always use throat thickness.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often confuse the leg size $s$ with the throat thickness $t$. In KTU exams, using $s$ instead of $t = 0.707s$ in strength calculations will fetch **zero marks**.

### 1.3 Fillet Weld in Pure Tension

When a **fillet weld** is subjected to a **pure tensile load** ($P$) parallel to its axis (i.e., pulling the welded plates apart axially):

- The force $P$ acts along the length of the weld.
- The weld cross-section that resists this load is the **throat area**.
- The throat area for a weld of length $L$ is $A_t = t \cdot L = 0.707 \, s \cdot L$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** As per the KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus, the focus is on **unstressed fillet welds under direct tension, shear, and bending**, along with eccentric loading. Fillet weld in pure tension is the **simplest fundamental loading case** and forms the basis for understanding combined stress scenarios.

---

### 1.4 Standard Design Assumptions (IS 800:2007 / AWS D1.1)

For the **strength analysis of fillet welds**, KTU follows the following standard assumptions:

1. The **weld cross-section** is assumed to fail along the **throat plane** (a 45° plane through the root).
2. The weld is **homogeneous and isotropic**.
3. The stress distribution across the throat is **uniform**.
4. The **leg size $s$** is treated as a nominal dimension; the actual failure plane is the **effective throat**.
5. The **strength of the weld metal** is compared against the strength of the parent metal — whichever is weaker governs the design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Analysis of Fillet Weld in Tension

**Step 1: Identify the Loading Direction**
The tensile force $P$ is applied along the axis of the weld, parallel to the longitudinal direction of the weld bead.

**Step 2: Identify the Failure Plane**
A fillet weld fails along the **throat plane** (the 45° plane through the root), not along the leg. The throat plane is the weakest cross-section.

**Step 3: Compute the Throat Area**
For an equal-leg fillet weld of leg size $s$ and total length $L$:

$$A_t = t \cdot L = 0.707 \, s \cdot L$$

**Step 4: Compute the Nominal Tensile Stress on Throat**

The tensile force $P$ is resisted by the throat plane. The stress on the throat plane is:

$$\sigma_t = \frac{P}{A_t} = \frac{P}{0.707 \, s \cdot L}$$

**Step 5: Compare with Permissible Stress**
For safe design, the induced stress must not exceed the permissible stress:

$$\sigma_t \leq \sigma_{at}$$

where $\sigma_{at}$ is the **allowable tensile stress** for the weld material.

**Step 6: Compute Strength of the Weld**
The strength of the fillet weld in tension is:

$$P = 0.707 \, s \cdot L \cdot \sigma_{at}$$

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the 45° Throat Assumption

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is failure assumed at 45°?**
> According to the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca Criterion)** applied to ductile weld metals, failure occurs on the plane of maximum shear. In a fillet weld loaded parallel to its axis, the maximum shear stress occurs on a 45° plane — exactly the throat plane.

Hence the entire KTU analysis of fillet welds is based on **throat-plane shear failure**, even when the external load is tensile or compressive.

### 2.3 Alternative Analysis: Force Resolution Method

Some textbooks (e.g., Shigley, V.B. Bhandari) resolve the tensile force $P$ along the throat plane:

- Force along throat plane: $P \cdot \cos(45°) = 0.707 \, P$
- Force perpendicular to throat plane: $P \cdot \sin(45°) = 0.707 \, P$

Both these components produce:
- A **normal stress** $\sigma = 0.707 \, P / (s \cdot L)$
- A **shear stress** $\tau = 0.707 \, P / (s \cdot L)$

This is a **dual-stress state** (tension + shear) on the throat. For ductile weld metal, the shear component is treated as the critical one, leading to the same result as the shear-failure approach.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Value** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Leg size of weld | $s$ | Given / Selected | mm |
| Effective throat thickness | $t$ | $0.707 \, s$ | mm |
| Total effective length | $L$ | Given | mm |
| Throat area | $A_t$ | $0.707 \, s \cdot L$ | mm² |
| Applied tensile load | $P$ | Given | N |
| Tensile stress on throat | $\sigma_t$ | $\dfrac{P}{0.707 \, s \cdot L}$ | N/mm² (MPa) |
| Allowable tensile stress | $\sigma_{at}$ | From IS 800 table | N/mm² (MPa) |
| Strength of weld (tension) | $P$ | $0.707 \, s \cdot L \cdot \sigma_{at}$ | N |
| Combined stress factor | $q$ | $\sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | N/mm² |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the symbols $\sigma_t$ and $\sigma_{at}$ are distinct — do not confuse them. The subscripts $t$ and $at$ mean "throat" and "allowable tensile" respectively.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Fillet welds in tension are commonly seen in:
- **Lap joints** in structural steel fabrications
- **Bracket connections** where a load is transmitted through welded seams
- **Pressure vessel nozzle attachments** where the load tries to pull the nozzle out
- **Machine tool frames** and base plate connections

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Throat Thickness

Consider an equal-leg right-triangular fillet weld with leg $s$. The throat is the hypotenuse-to-vertex perpendicular distance.

By geometry of a 45°-45°-90° triangle:

$$t = s \cdot \sin(45°) = s \cdot \cos(45°) = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Rationalizing the denominator:

$$t = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{s\sqrt{2}}{2} = 0.707 \, s$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is for an **equal-leg** fillet weld. For an unequal-leg weld with legs $s_1$ and $s_2$, the throat is:
> $$t = \frac{s_1 \cdot s_2}{\sqrt{s_1^2 + s_2^2}}$$
> For the standard 90° equal-leg case, this reduces to $0.707s$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Tensile Strength Equation

**Given:**
- Tensile load $P$ applied parallel to weld axis
- Leg size $s$, weld length $L$

**Find:** The relationship between $P$, $s$, and $L$ at failure.

**Step 1:** The failure plane is the throat plane (inclined at 45° to the load direction).

**Step 2:** The area resisting failure is the throat area:

$$A_t = t \cdot L = 0.707 \, s \cdot L$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **average shear stress** assumption on the throat plane:

The force component on the throat plane is $P \cdot \cos(45°) = 0.707 \, P$. The area of the throat plane is $s \cdot L / \cos(45°)$... wait, this needs careful treatment.

**Standard KTU Approach (Shear on Throat):**

The applied tensile force $P$ is decomposed:
- Component along throat (shear): $P \sin(45°) = 0.707 P$ ... wait — careful about which angle.

Let me redo this carefully using the **standard fillet weld analysis**:

The throat plane makes an angle of 45° with the leg. The tensile force $P$ is parallel to the leg. So the angle between $P$ and the throat plane is 45°.

- Force normal to throat: $P \sin(45°) = 0.707 P$
- Force parallel to throat (shear): $P \cos(45°) = 0.707 P$

The **throat area** is $t \cdot L = 0.707 s \cdot L$.

The **normal stress** on throat:
$$\sigma = \frac{0.707 P}{0.707 s \cdot L} = \frac{P}{s \cdot L}$$

The **shear stress** on throat:
$$\tau = \frac{0.707 P}{0.707 s \cdot L} = \frac{P}{s \cdot L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Result:** Both the normal and shear stress on the throat plane equal $\dfrac{P}{s \cdot L}$ for a fillet weld under axial tension.

**Step 4:** Apply the **Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca) Failure Criterion**:

Failure occurs when:
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2}$$

On the throat plane, the maximum shear stress is the shear component we found, so:

$$\frac{P}{s \cdot L} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2}$$

$$P = 0.5 \, s \, L \, \sigma_y$$

**Step 5:** Alternatively, using the **Distortion Energy (von Mises) Criterion**:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2} = \sigma_y$$

$$\sqrt{\left(\frac{P}{sL}\right)^2 + 3\left(\frac{P}{sL}\right)^2} = \sigma_y$$

$$\sqrt{4} \cdot \frac{P}{sL} = \sigma_y$$

$$\frac{2P}{sL} = \sigma_y \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = 0.5 \, s \, L \, \sigma_y$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both Tresca and von Mises give the **same result** for fillet welds in pure tension: $P = 0.5 \, s \, L \, \sigma_y$.

**Step 6:** In terms of allowable stress:
$$P = 0.707 \, s \, L \cdot \sigma_{at}$$

This is the **standard KTU design equation** for a fillet weld in pure tension.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

| **Scenario** | **Stress State on Throat** | **Governing Equation** |
|---|---|---|
| Pure Tension ($P$ axial) | $\sigma = \tau = \dfrac{P}{sL}$ | $P = 0.707 \, s L \sigma_{at}$ |
| Pure Shear ($V$ transverse) | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{0.707 \, sL}$ | $V = 0.707 \, s L \tau_{av}$ |
| Bending Moment ($M$) | $\sigma = \dfrac{6M}{0.707 \, s L^2}$ | $M = \dfrac{0.707 \, s L^2 \sigma_{b}}{6}$ |
| Torsion ($T$) | $\tau = \dfrac{T}{0.707 \, s L \cdot r}$ | $T = 0.707 \, s L \cdot r \cdot \tau_{av}$ |
| Combined Tension + Shear | Direct + Shear | Use $\sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2} \leq \sigma_{at}$ |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> Two mild steel plates of thickness 12 mm are joined by parallel fillet welds of length 100 mm. The plates are subjected to a tensile load of 80 kN. If the allowable tensile stress for the weld is 110 MPa, find the required leg size of the weld.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P = 80 \, \text{kN} = 80{,}000 \, \text{N}$
- $L = 100 \, \text{mm}$
- $\sigma_{at} = 110 \, \text{N/mm}^2$

**Step 1:** Use the design equation:

$$P = 0.707 \, s \, L \, \sigma_{at}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$80{,}000 = 0.707 \times s \times 100 \times 110$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$80{,}000 = 7777 \, s$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $s$:

$$s = \frac{80{,}000}{7777} = 10.29 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 5:** Round up to the next standard size:

$$s = 12 \, \text{mm (standard size)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification:** With $s = 12$ mm, induced stress $= \dfrac{80{,}000}{0.707 \times 12 \times 100} = 94.3$ MPa $< 110$ MPa ✓ **SAFE**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Geometry of a Fillet Weld (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["PARENT PLATE A"] -->|Butt edge| B["ROOT POINT"]
    B --> C["FILLET WELD TRIANGLE"]
    A -->|Perpendicular| C
    D["PARENT PLATE B"] -->|Perpendicular edge| B
    D --> C
    C -->|Throat plane 45 deg| E["FAILURE PLANE"]
    E -->|Effective area 0.707 s times L| F["THROAT AREA At"]
    G["TENSION LOAD P"] -->|Axial direction| C
    F -->|Resists| G
```

### 4.2 Stress Resolution on the Throat Plane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["TENSILE LOAD P"] -->|Acts along weld axis| ANGLE["Inclined at 45 deg to throat plane"]
    ANGLE --> N["Normal Component 0.707 P"]
    ANGLE --> S["Shear Component 0.707 P"]
    N --> STRESS1["Normal Stress sigma equals P over sL"]
    S --> STRESS2["Shear Stress tau equals P over sL"]
    STRESS1 --> RESULT["Combined State: Equal Tension and Shear"]
    STRESS2 --> RESULT
    RESULT --> CRITERIA["Apply Tresca or von Mises"]
    CRITERIA --> FINAL["Strength equals 0.707 sL sigma_at"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Design Procedure for Fillet Weld in Tension

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START["START: Given Load P and Length L"] --> A1["Step 1: Identify Allowable Stress sigma_at from IS 800"]
    A1 --> A2["Step 2: Assume a Leg Size s"]
    A2 --> A3["Step 3: Compute Throat Thickness t equals 0.707 s"]
    A3 --> A4["Step 4: Compute Throat Area At equals t times L"]
    A4 --> A5["Step 5: Compute Induced Stress sigma_t equals P over At"]
    A5 --> CHECK{"Is sigma_t less than or equal to sigma_at?"}
    CHECK -->|Yes| SAFE["DESIGN IS SAFE"]
    CHECK -->|No| REVISE["REVISE: Increase s or L and Repeat"]
    REVISE --> A2
    SAFE --> END["FINALIZE: Report s and L"]
```

### 4.4 Combined Loading Schematic (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOAD["External Loads on Weld Group"]
        L1["Direct Tension P"]
        L2["Direct Shear V"]
        L3["Bending Moment M"]
        L4["Torsion T"]
    end
    subgraph THROAT["Throat Plane Stress State"]
        T1["Normal Stress sigma"]
        T2["Shear Stress tau"]
    end
    subgraph CRITERIA["Failure Criterion"]
        C1["Tresca: tau_max equals sigma_y over 2"]
        C2["von Mises: sqrt sigma squared plus 3 tau squared"]
    end
    LOAD --> THROAT
    THROAT --> CRITERIA
    CRITERIA --> DESIGN["Safe Leg Size s Selected"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define a fillet weld. What is the significance of throat thickness in fillet weld design?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **fillet weld** is a weld of approximately triangular cross-section joining two surfaces meeting at an angle (typically 90°).

> The **throat thickness** ($t$) is the perpendicular distance from the root of the weld to the hypotenuse (face). For an equal-leg fillet weld, $t = 0.707 \, s$.

> **Significance:** The throat plane (at 45°) is the **weakest cross-section** of the weld, and it is where failure is assumed to occur. Hence, all strength calculations are based on the throat area, not the leg area.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
*State the design equation for the strength of a fillet weld subjected to pure tension and explain each term.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The strength of a fillet weld under pure tension is given by:
> $$P = 0.707 \, s \, L \, \sigma_{at}$$
> where:
> - $P$ = applied tensile load (N)
> - $s$ = leg size of the weld (mm)
> - $L$ = effective length of the weld (mm)
> - $\sigma_{at}$ = allowable tensile stress for weld material (N/mm²)

> The factor **0.707** comes from $\sin(45°)$ and represents the geometric relationship between leg size and throat thickness.

---

### 5.2 PART B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** Fillet Weld in Tension with Combined Shear

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Based on KTU 2024 Scheme]**

A mild steel bracket is welded to a vertical column using two parallel fillet welds, one on each side of the bracket. Each weld is 80 mm long with a leg size of 10 mm. The bracket carries a vertical load of 30 kN and a horizontal load of 20 kN. Determine the maximum stress induced in the weld, given the allowable stresses are: $\sigma_{at} = 110$ MPa and $\tau_{av} = 90$ MPa.

**Sub-parts:**
- **(a) Compute the direct tensile and shear stresses on the throat. (7 Marks)**
- **(b) Check the combined stress using the distortion energy criterion. (7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Direct Tensile and Shear Stresses (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Vertical load $P_v = 30 \, \text{kN} = 30{,}000 \, \text{N}$ → causes **direct tension** on weld
- Horizontal load $P_h = 20 \, \text{kN} = 20{,}000 \, \text{N}$ → causes **direct shear** on weld
- Number of welds $n = 2$
- Each weld length $L = 80 \, \text{mm}$
- Leg size $s = 10 \, \text{mm}$
- Throat thickness $t = 0.707 \times 10 = 7.07 \, \text{mm}$

**Step 1:** Throat area per weld:
$$A_t = 0.707 \times s \times L = 0.707 \times 10 \times 80 = 565.6 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Total throat area for 2 welds:
$$A_{t,total} = 2 \times 565.6 = 1131.2 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Direct tensile stress due to vertical load:
$$\sigma = \frac{P_v}{A_{t,total}} = \frac{30{,}000}{1131.2} = 26.52 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4:** Shear stress due to horizontal load:
$$\tau = \frac{P_h}{A_{t,total}} = \frac{20{,}000}{1131.2} = 17.68 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Part (b): Combined Stress Check (7 Marks)**

**Step 5:** Apply the **Distortion Energy (von Mises) Criterion**:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$$

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{(26.52)^2 + 3 \times (17.68)^2}$$

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{703.31 + 937.81} = \sqrt{1641.12}$$

$$\sigma_{eq} = 40.51 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 6:** Compare with allowable stress:

$$\sigma_{eq} = 40.51 \, \text{MPa} < \sigma_{at} = 110 \, \text{MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

**Step 7:** Also check the shear component separately:
$$\tau = 17.68 \, \text{MPa} < \tau_{av} = 90 \, \text{MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

**Conclusion:** The fillet weld design is **safe** under combined tension and shear.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** Design of Fillet Weld for Given Tension Load

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Based on KTU 2024 Scheme]**

Design a parallel fillet weld to connect a plate of width 150 mm and thickness 10 mm to another plate, so that the joint can transmit a tensile load of 90 kN. Take $\sigma_{at} = 110$ MPa for the weld material. Assume that the weld is placed on both sides of the plate.

**Sub-parts:**
- **(a) Calculate the required weld length and leg size. (7 Marks)**
- **(b) Verify the design for combined stress if a 30% impact load factor is applied. (7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Required Weld Length and Leg Size (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $P = 90 \, \text{kN} = 90{,}000 \, \text{N}$
- Plate width $b = 150 \, \text{mm}$
- Plate thickness $t_p = 10 \, \text{mm}$
- $\sigma_{at} = 110 \, \text{MPa}$
- Welds on both sides: $n = 2$

**Step 1:** Estimate leg size from plate thickness:
$$s \approx t_p = 10 \, \text{mm (minimum recommended)}$$
Or use the rule: $s_{\min} \approx 0.7 t_p = 7 \, \text{mm}$

Let's select $s = 10 \, \text{mm}$.

**Step 2:** Throat thickness:
$$t = 0.707 \times 10 = 7.07 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 3:** Each weld length must fit on the plate width (150 mm), so maximum possible length per weld $\leq 150 - 10 = 140$ mm.

**Step 4:** Using the design equation:

$$P = 0.707 \, s \, L_{total} \, \sigma_{at}$$

$$90{,}000 = 0.707 \times 10 \times L_{total} \times 110$$

**Step 5:** Solve for total length:
$$L_{total} = \frac{90{,}000}{0.707 \times 10 \times 110} = \frac{90{,}000}{777.7} = 115.7 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 6:** Length per weld (since 2 welds):
$$L = \frac{115.7}{2} = 57.85 \, \text{mm} \approx 60 \, \text{mm (standard)}$$

**Step 7:** Since $60 \, \text{mm} < 140 \, \text{mm}$ (max available), the design is feasible.

**Final Design:** Two parallel fillet welds, each of leg size **10 mm** and length **60 mm**.

**Part (b): Combined Stress Verification with Impact Factor (7 Marks)**

**Step 8:** Apply 30% impact factor:
$$P_{impact} = 1.3 \times 90{,}000 = 117{,}000 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 9:** New throat area (total):
$$A_{t,total} = 2 \times 0.707 \times 10 \times 60 = 848.4 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 10:** Induced stress:
$$\sigma_{induced} = \frac{117{,}000}{848.4} = 137.9 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 11:** Compare:
$$\sigma_{induced} = 137.9 \, \text{MPa} > \sigma_{at} = 110 \, \text{MPa} \quad \times \text{ NOT SAFE}$$

**Step 12:** Revise the design. Required new length:
$$L_{total,new} = \frac{117{,}000}{0.707 \times 10 \times 110} = 150.4 \, \text{mm}$$

$$L_{new} = \frac{150.4}{2} = 75.2 \, \text{mm} \approx 80 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 13:** Check if 80 mm fits:
$$80 \, \text{mm} < 140 \, \text{mm} \quad \checkmark \text{ FEASIBLE}$$

**Revised Design:** Two parallel fillet welds, each of leg size **10 mm** and length **80 mm**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Using leg size $s$ instead of throat thickness $t$:** Always remember $t = 0.707 \, s$. Marks will be **completely cut** if $s$ is used directly in the stress formula.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract weld length for start/stop craters:** Effective length = Total length $- 2 \times$ crater size (typically 6-12 mm). KTU expects this deduction.
>
> 3. **Mixing allowable tensile and shear stresses:** Use $\sigma_{at}$ for direct/normal stresses and $\tau_{av}$ for shear stresses. They have **different values**.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the von Mises criterion for combined loading:** KTU strictly requires $\sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2} \leq \sigma_{at}$ for combined loading.
>
> 5. **Not adding the impact/shock factor:** If a load is dynamic, always multiply by the impact factor before computing stresses.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Fillet Weld in Tension:**
>
> - [x] **Throat thickness:** $t = 0.707 \, s$ for equal-leg welds (45° throat plane assumption).
> - [x] **Failure plane:** Always the throat plane, regardless of load type.
> - [x] **Pure tension strength:** $P = 0.707 \, s \, L \, \sigma_{at}$.
> - [x] **Throat area:** $A_t = 0.707 \, s \cdot L$.
> - [x] **Equal-leg vs unequal-leg:** KTU exams mostly use equal-leg welds unless specified.
> - [x] **Effective weld length:** $L_{eff} = L_{total} - 2 \times \text{crater size}$.
> - [x] **Combined loading criterion:** $\sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2} \leq \sigma_{at}$ (von Mises).
> - [x] **Maximum permissible leg size:** $s_{\max} \leq t_{min(plate)} - 1.5 \, \text{mm}$.
> - [x] **Minimum permissible leg size:** $s_{\min} \geq \sqrt{t_{max}}$ for thicker plate (IS 800).
> - [x] **Allowable stresses:** Refer IS 800:2007 Table 6 for $\sigma_{at}$ and $\tau_{av}$ values.
> - [x] **Key insight:** Both Tresca and von Mises give the same result: $P = 0.5 \, s \, L \, \sigma_y$ at yield.
> - [x] **Standard leg sizes:** 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 25 mm (always round up).
> - [x] **Lap joint assumption:** Fillet welds in lap joints are assumed to fail along the throat.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fillet Joint Under Torsion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fillet Joint Under Torsion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Fillet Joint Under Torsion** is a welded connection in which a **fillet weld** (weld of approximately triangular cross-section) is subjected to a **twisting moment (torque)** about the longitudinal axis of the connected member or about a point that causes the weld group to rotate. The torque is resisted by **shear stresses** developed on the **throat plane** of the weld.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fillet Weld Throat:** The shortest distance from the root of the weld to its face, given by $t = 0.707 \times s$, where $s$ is the **leg size**. All strength calculations for fillet welds are based on the throat area.

## 1.2 Two Principal Configurations

**Configuration A — Circumferential Fillet Weld (Shaft-to-Plate Joint)**
A solid circular shaft is welded to a plate/surface by a continuous fillet weld running around the periphery. The torque is transmitted from the shaft through the cylindrical weld surface.

**Configuration B — Fillet Weld Group (Planar Weld Pattern)**
Multiple fillet weld segments arranged in a 2-D pattern (C-shape, L-shape, rectangular box, triangular, etc.) resist a torque applied about the **centroid** of the weld group. Typical in **bracket-to-column** connections, **machine frames**, and **crane pedestals**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine a screwdriver turning a screw. The screwdriver transmits torque through a *twisting action*; the threads resist it via shear along their helical surface. In a circumferential fillet weld, the weld throat acts exactly like those threads — a thin cylindrical surface where shear stress builds up to oppose the twist. In a weld group (C-shaped), the torque is resisted by *shear flow* along the weld line, much like how a torque on a hollow rectangular tube is carried by shear stresses in its walls.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Leg size $s$:** Typically **3 mm to 20 mm** in steps of 1 mm or 2 mm.
- **Throat thickness $t = 0.707s$**
- **Permissible shear stress in weld (IS 800:2007):** $\tau_{vd} = \dfrac{f_y}{\sqrt{3} \times \gamma_{mw}}$
- **Partial safety factor $\gamma_{mw}$:** **1.25** for shop welds, **1.5** for field welds.
- **Minimum leg size** (as per IS 800): depends on thicker plate thickness.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions

1. The fillet weld is treated as a **line of zero thickness** for locating the centroid and computing geometric properties.
2. Shear stress is **uniformly distributed** across the throat thickness ($0.707s$) over the length of any weld segment.
3. The weld metal's strength is the **governing criterion** (not base metal).
4. The weld group rotates as a **rigid body** about its centroid under pure torsion.
5. **Bending and direct shear** stresses (if any) are superposed on torsion using vector addition (covered in adjoining topics).

## 2.2 Underlying Theory — The 'Why' & 'How'

**For a Circumferential Weld (Thin Cylindrical Shell Analogy):**
- The weld throat forms a thin cylindrical shell of radius $r = d/2$ and thickness $t = 0.707s$.
- Treating the throat as a thin ring under torsion (analogous to a thin pressure vessel), shear stress varies linearly with radius: $\tau = T \cdot r / J$.
- Since the ring is thin, stress is taken as uniform and equal to its value at the mean radius $r = d/2$.

**For a Fillet Weld Group (Shear Flow Analogy):**
- Each infinitesimal weld element $dl$ at radius $r$ carries a shear force $dF = \tau \cdot (0.707s) \cdot dl$.
- This element contributes $dM = r \cdot dF$ to torque resistance.
- Integrating over the entire weld group: $T = 0.707s \cdot \tau \cdot \int r^{2} \, dl = 0.707s \cdot \tau \cdot J$, where $J$ is the **polar moment of the weld line** about its centroid.
- The **maximum stress** occurs at the **point farthest from the centroid** ($r = r_{max}$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t = 0.707 \, s$ | Throat thickness in terms of leg size |
| 2 | $T = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \, d^{2} \, (0.707 s) \, \tau$ | Torque capacity of **circumferential** fillet weld (shaft dia. $d$) |
| 3 | $s = \dfrac{2T}{\pi \, d^{2} \, (0.707) \, \tau}$ | Required leg size for circumferential fillet weld |
| 4 | $J = I_x + I_y = \sum (r^{2} \cdot l)$ | Polar moment of **weld line** about its centroid |
| 5 | $I_x = \sum (y^{2} \cdot l)$ | Second moment of weld line about $x$-axis through centroid |
| 6 | $I_y = \sum (x^{2} \cdot l)$ | Second moment of weld line about $y$-axis through centroid |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot r_{max}}{0.707 \, s \, J}$ | Maximum shear stress in **weld group** under torsion |
| 8 | $s = \dfrac{T \cdot r_{max}}{0.707 \, J \, \tau_{perm}}$ | Required leg size for weld group under torsion |
| 9 | $\tau_{vd} = \dfrac{f_y}{\sqrt{3} \, \gamma_{mw}}$ | Permissible shear stress in weld per **IS 800:2007** |
| 10 | $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25 \text{ (shop)}; \, 1.50 \text{ (field)}$ | Partial safety factor for weld metal |
| 11 | $s_{min}$ (as per IS 800) | **3 mm** (thicker plate $\leq 10$ mm), **5 mm** (10–20 mm), **6 mm** (20–32 mm), **8 mm** (32–50 mm) |
| 12 | $s_{max} \leq t_{plate} - 1.5 \text{ mm}$ | Upper limit to ensure complete fusion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Units:** In KTU Machine Design problems, use **N** and **mm** consistently. Torque in **N·mm**, stress in **N/mm² (MPa)**, dimensions in **mm**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fillet joints under torsion are encountered in:
- **Drive shafts** welded to coupling flanges or hubs.
- **Crane jib brackets** mounted to vertical columns.
- **Piping flanges** carrying torque from valve actuators.
- **Gearbox housings** where motor mounting pads transmit torque.
- **Machine tool bases** subjected to reaction torques from cutting forces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Torque Resistance of a Circumferential Fillet Weld

**Setup:** A solid circular shaft of diameter $d$ is welded to a plate by a fillet weld of leg size $s$. The throat forms a thin cylindrical surface of mean radius $r = d/2$ and thickness $t = 0.707s$.

**Step 1 — Polar moment of the thin cylindrical throat:**

For a thin ring/cylindrical shell, the polar moment of area is:

$$J = 2\pi r^{3} t$$

Substituting $r = d/2$ and $t = 0.707s$:

$$J = 2\pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{3} (0.707s) = 2\pi \cdot \frac{d^{3}}{8} \cdot 0.707s = \frac{\pi \, d^{3} \, 0.707s}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum shear stress at the mean radius $r = d/2$:**

Using the torsion formula $\tau = T \cdot r / J$:

$$\tau = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{\dfrac{\pi \, d^{3} \, 0.707s}{4}}$$

$$\tau = \frac{4T \cdot d/2}{\pi \, d^{3} \, 0.707s} = \frac{2T}{\pi \, d^{2} \cdot 0.707s}$$

**Step 3 — Torque as a function of stress and leg size:**

Rearranging the expression:

$$T = \frac{\pi \, d^{2} \cdot 0.707s \cdot \tau}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent Derivation (Force × Moment Arm):** Total throat area $= \pi d \cdot 0.707s$. Average shear force $= \tau \cdot \pi d \cdot 0.707s$. This force acts at the moment arm $d/2$ (mean radius of the throat). So $T = \tau \cdot \pi d \cdot 0.707s \cdot (d/2) = \dfrac{\pi d^{2} \cdot 0.707s \cdot \tau}{2}$. **Both methods yield the same expression** — confirming the formula's validity.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Polar Moment of a Fillet Weld Group

**Setup:** A 2-D fillet weld group consists of $n$ line segments. Each segment $i$ has length $l_i$ and its midpoint is at coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ relative to the centroid of the weld group.

**Step 1 — Definition of polar moment for a line:**

For a continuous line of zero thickness, the polar second moment about the centroid is:

$$J = \int r^{2} \, dl = \int (x^{2} + y^{2}) \, dl$$

**Step 2 — Discretization into weld segments:**

Treating each segment as a line at an average distance from the centroid:

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (r_i^{2} \cdot l_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ (x_i^{2} + y_i^{2}) \cdot l_i \right]$$

Expanding:

$$J = \sum (x_i^{2} \cdot l_i) + \sum (y_i^{2} \cdot l_i) = I_y + I_x$$

**Step 3 — Shear stress at any point:**

A differential weld element of length $dl$ at distance $r$ from the centroid carries a shear force:

$$dF = \tau \cdot (0.707s) \cdot dl$$

The torque resisted by this element is $dT = r \cdot dF$. Integrating:

$$T = 0.707s \cdot \tau \cdot \int r^{2} \, dl = 0.707s \cdot \tau \cdot J$$

**Step 4 — Maximum shear stress location:**

Since $\tau \propto r$, the maximum stress occurs at the point of **maximum distance** $r_{max}$ from the centroid:

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot r_{max}}{0.707 \, s \, J}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Circumferential Fillet Weld

**Problem:** A solid shaft of diameter **$d = 100$ mm** is welded to a plate by a circumferential fillet weld. The shaft transmits a torque of **$T = 25$ kN·m**. The permissible shear stress in the weld is **$\tau = 110$ MPa**. Determine the required leg size of the weld.

**Solution:**

**Given data:**
- $d = 100$ mm
- $T = 25 \text{ kN·m} = 25 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$
- $\tau_{perm} = 110 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$

**Step 1 — Write the design equation:**

$$T = \frac{\pi \, d^{2} \cdot 0.707s \cdot \tau_{perm}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values:**

$$25 \times 10^{6} = \frac{\pi \times (100)^{2} \times 0.707 \times s \times 110}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the right-hand side:**

$$25 \times 10^{6} = \frac{3.14159 \times 10000 \times 77.77 \times s}{2} = 1{,}221{,}611 \, s$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $s$:**

$$s = \frac{25 \times 10^{6}}{1{,}221{,}611} = 20.46 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Provide the next standard size:**

$$\boxed{s = 22 \text{ mm (provide next standard leg size)}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — C-Shaped Fillet Weld Group

**Problem:** A bracket is welded to a column using a **C-shaped fillet weld group**: a horizontal top weld of **200 mm** length and two vertical side welds of **150 mm** each, both connected at right angles. The centroid of the C lies at the geometric center. A torque of **$T = 12$ kN·m** is applied. If $\tau_{perm} = 110$ MPa, find the leg size.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Locate segments relative to centroid:**

| Segment | Length $l$ (mm) | $x$ (mm) | $y$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Top horizontal | 200 | 0 | +75 |
| Left vertical | 150 | −75 | 0 |
| Right vertical | 150 | +75 | 0 |

**Step 2 — Compute polar moment $J = \sum r^{2} l$:**

$$J_{top} = (0^{2} + 75^{2}) \times 200 = 5625 \times 200 = 1{,}125{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

$$J_{left} = (75^{2} + 0^{2}) \times 150 = 5625 \times 150 = 843{,}750 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

$$J_{right} = (75^{2} + 0^{2}) \times 150 = 5625 \times 150 = 843{,}750 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

$$J = 1{,}125{,}000 + 843{,}750 + 843{,}750 = 2{,}812{,}500 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Identify $r_{max}$ (farthest point = corner of C):**

$$r_{max} = \sqrt{(100)^{2} + (75)^{2}} = \sqrt{10000 + 5625} = \sqrt{15625} = 125 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the design equation:**

$$\tau = \frac{T \cdot r_{max}}{0.707 \, s \, J}$$

$$110 = \frac{12 \times 10^{6} \times 125}{0.707 \times s \times 2{,}812{,}500}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify and solve:**

$$110 = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{9}}{1{,}988{,}437.5 \times s} = \frac{754.41}{s}$$

$$s = \frac{754.41}{110} = 6.86 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Provide the next standard size:**

$$\boxed{s = 8 \text{ mm}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Formula

| Configuration | Identify This | Apply This Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Shaft welded to plate (circular) | $d$, $T$, $\tau$ known | $T = \dfrac{\pi d^{2} \cdot 0.707s \cdot \tau}{2}$ |
| Weld group with $n$ segments | $l_i$, $(x_i, y_i)$ known | $J = \sum r_i^{2} l_i$; then $\tau = \dfrac{T r_{max}}{0.707 s J}$ |
| Direct shear only (no torsion) | $P$ along weld | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{0.707 s \cdot l_{total}}$ |
| Bending only (no torsion) | $M$ about neutral axis | $\tau = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{max}}{0.707 s \cdot I}$ |
| Combined (rare in Module 2) | Vector sum of all | $\tau_{resultant} = \sqrt{\tau_{torsion}^{2} + \tau_{shear}^{2} + \tau_{bending}^{2}}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Design of a Fillet Weld Under Torsion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Problem Input] --> B{Type of Joint?}
    B -- Circumferential Shaft Weld --> C[Identify Shaft Diameter d and Torque T]
    B -- Weld Group Pattern --> D[Locate Centroid of Weld Line]
    C --> E[Compute Required Leg Size from T = pi d^2 x 0.707 s tau / 2]
    D --> F[Compute Polar Moment J = Sum of r^2 x l per Segment]
    F --> G[Identify r_max = Distance to Farthest Weld Point]
    G --> H[Compute s from tau = T x r_max / 0.707 x s x J]
    E --> I[Check s greater than s_min IS 800]
    H --> I
    I -- Yes --> J[Check s less than s_max less than t plate minus 1.5 mm]
    I -- No --> K[Revise s to s_min Standard]
    J -- Yes --> L[Final Design: Leg Size s Confirmed]
    J -- No --> M[Increase Plate Thickness or Revise Layout]
    K --> I
    M --> L
    L --> N[End: Output Leg Size s and Weld Length]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Stress Analysis Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1[Applied Loading]
        L1[Torque T about Centroid]
    end
    subgraph A2[Geometric Properties of Weld Line]
        G1[Centroid Location x_c, y_c]
        G2[Polar Moment J = Ix + Iy]
        G3[Maximum Radius r_max]
    end
    subgraph A3[Stress Calculation]
        S1[Shear Force on Throat dF = tau x 0.707s x dl]
        S2[Moment Arm r]
        S3[Resultant Shear Stress tau = T x r_max / 0.707 x s x J]
    end
    subgraph A4[Permissible Limits]
        P1[IS 800 Permissible tau_vd = fy / sqrt 3 x gamma_mw]
        P2[Check tau less than tau_vd]
    end
    L1 --> G2
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> S3
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> P2
    P1 --> P2
```

## 4.3 Schematic Layout — C-Shaped Weld Group Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CentroidAxis[Centroid Reference Frame]
        O[Origin at Centroid O]
    end
    CentroidAxis --> T1[Top Horizontal Weld: l = 200 mm, Center at x=0, y=+75]
    CentroidAxis --> V1[Left Vertical Weld: l = 150 mm, Center at x=-75, y=0]
    CentroidAxis --> V2[Right Vertical Weld: l = 150 mm, Center at x=+75, y=0]
    T1 --> C1[Corner Point A: x=+100, y=+75, r_max = 125 mm]
    T1 --> C2[Corner Point B: x=-100, y=+75, r_max = 125 mm]
    V1 --> C3[Bottom Point: x=-75, y=-75, r = 106 mm]
    V2 --> C4[Bottom Point: x=+75, y=-75, r = 106 mm]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: Define throat thickness in a fillet weld. Why is the design strength of a fillet weld based on the throat thickness and not the leg size?**

**Model Answer:**
The **throat thickness** $t$ of a fillet weld is the **shortest distance from the root of the weld to its face** (the hypotenuse of the assumed isosceles right-triangle cross-section of size $s$).

$$t = 0.707 \, s$$

**Why throat, not leg:**
- The **shear failure** in a fillet weld occurs along the **throat plane** (the weakest plane inclined at 45° to the legs).
- The leg is the visible outer profile; the **throat represents the true load-carrying cross-section** under shear.
- Using the leg size would **overestimate** the strength (since the throat is always smaller by a factor of 0.707).
- Hence, IS 800:2007 mandates that **all strength calculations use throat area = $0.707s \times l$**, not the leg area.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q: State the permissible shear stress in a fillet weld as per IS 800:2007. How do the partial safety factors differ for shop and field welds?**

**Model Answer:**
Per **IS 800:2007**, the permissible shear stress in a fillet weld is:

$$\tau_{vd} = \frac{f_y}{\sqrt{3} \, \gamma_{mw}}$$

where:
- $f_y$ = yield strength of the weld electrode / base metal (whichever is lower),
- $\gamma_{mw}$ = partial safety factor for the weld metal.

**Difference in $\gamma_{mw}$:**

| Welding Condition | $\gamma_{mw}$ | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Shop weld** | **1.25** | Controlled environment, better quality, lower defect probability |
| **Field weld** | **1.50** | On-site conditions, higher defect probability, less quality control |

For example, with E250 grade steel ($f_y = 250$ MPa):
- Shop: $\tau_{vd} = 250 / (1.732 \times 1.25) = 115.47$ MPa
- Field: $\tau_{vd} = 250 / (1.732 \times 1.50) = 96.22$ MPa

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**A circular shaft of diameter 90 mm is welded to a flat plate using a circumferential fillet weld. The shaft is required to transmit a steady torque of 18 kN·m. Assuming the shaft is made of E250 grade steel and the weld is done in a workshop, design the fillet weld.**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Determine the leg size of the circumferential fillet weld.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $d = 90$ mm, $T = 18 \times 10^{6}$ N·mm
- $f_y = 250$ MPa (E250), $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (shop weld)

**Step 1 — Permissible shear stress in weld:**

$$\tau_{vd} = \frac{f_y}{\sqrt{3} \, \gamma_{mw}} = \frac{250}{1.732 \times 1.25} = 115.47 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the design equation for circumferential fillet weld:**

$$T = \frac{\pi \, d^{2} \cdot 0.707 \, s \cdot \tau_{vd}}{2}$$

$$18 \times 10^{6} = \frac{3.14159 \times (90)^{2} \times 0.707 \times s \times 115.47}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$18 \times 10^{6} = \frac{3.14159 \times 8100 \times 81.64 \times s}{2} = 1{,}038{,}409 \, s$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $s$:**

$$s = \frac{18 \times 10^{6}}{1{,}038{,}409} = 17.33 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Provide the next standard size:**

$$\boxed{s = 18 \text{ mm}}$$

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Check whether the leg size satisfies the maximum and minimum limits per IS 800. State the recommendations if the plate thickness is 22 mm.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Maximum leg size (per IS 800):**

$$s_{max} \leq t_{plate} - 1.5 \text{ mm} = 22 - 1.5 = 20.5 \text{ mm}$$

Provided $s = 18$ mm $<$ 20.5 mm ✓ (OK)

**Step 2 — Minimum leg size (per IS 800, Table 21):**

For thicker plate of 22 mm (falls in 20–32 mm range): $s_{min} = 6$ mm.

Provided $s = 18$ mm $>$ 6 mm ✓ (OK)

**Step 3 — Conclusions and Recommendations:**

- The leg size **$s = 18$ mm** is **safe and within IS 800 limits**.
- **Effective throat thickness:** $t = 0.707 \times 18 = 12.73$ mm.
- The joint may be reinforced with **two parallel fillet welds** if single-pass welding is impractical for 18 mm leg.
- **Preheating** to 100–150 °C recommended for thick sections to avoid hydrogen-induced cracking.
- Use **low-hydrogen electrodes** (E7018 or equivalent) for E250 base metal.
- Conduct **dye-penetrant or ultrasonic testing** on the weld for critical applications.

---

### Question B **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**A C-shaped fillet weld group is used to attach a bracket to a column. The weld consists of a horizontal top weld of length 180 mm and two vertical side welds of length 120 mm each, symmetrically placed about the centroid. The bracket carries a torque of 8 kN·m. Take permissible shear stress as 100 MPa. Design the weld.**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Compute the polar moment of the weld group and identify the point of maximum stress.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometry and Centroid:**

- The C-shape has its centroid at the geometric center of symmetry.
- Top horizontal: $l_1 = 180$ mm, midpoint at $(0, +60)$
- Left vertical: $l_2 = 120$ mm, midpoint at $(-60, 0)$
- Right vertical: $l_3 = 120$ mm, midpoint at $(+60, 0)$

(Coordinates in mm relative to centroid)

**Step 2 — Polar moment $J = \sum r^{2} l$:**

| Segment | $l$ (mm) | $r^{2} = x^{2} + y^{2}$ | $r^{2} \cdot l$ (mm³) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Top horizontal | 180 | $0 + 60^{2} = 3600$ | $648{,}000$ |
| Left vertical | 120 | $60^{2} + 0 = 3600$ | $432{,}000$ |
