| Right vertical | 120 | $60^{2} + 0 = 3600$ | $432{,}000$ |
| **Total $J$** | | | $\mathbf{1{,}512{,}000}$ |

$$\boxed{J = 1{,}512{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3}}$$

**Step 3 — Identify $r_{max}$ (corner of the C):**

The farthest points are the **two top corners** at $(\pm 90, +60)$:

$$r_{max} = \sqrt{90^{2} + 60^{2}} = \sqrt{8100 + 3600} = \sqrt{11700} = 108.17 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{r_{max} = 108.17 \text{ mm}}$$

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Determine the required leg size and verify against IS 800 limits.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the design equation:**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot r_{max}}{0.707 \, s \, J} \leq \tau_{perm}$$

$$100 = \frac{8 \times 10^{6} \times 108.17}{0.707 \times s \times 1{,}512{,}000}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify:**

$$100 = \frac{8.6536 \times 10^{8}}{1{,}069{,}584 \times s} = \frac{809.05}{s}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $s$:**

$$s = \frac{809.05}{100} = 8.09 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Provide the next standard size:**

$$\boxed{s = 10 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5 — Verification against IS 800:**

- **Minimum $s$** for plate 20–32 mm thick: $s_{min} = 6$ mm. Provided $s = 10$ mm ✓
- **Maximum $s$** for plate thickness 18 mm: $s_{max} \leq 18 - 1.5 = 16.5$ mm. Provided $s = 10$ mm ✓
- **Actual working stress:**

$$\tau_{actual} = \frac{8 \times 10^{6} \times 108.17}{0.707 \times 10 \times 1{,}512{,}000} = \frac{8.6536 \times 10^{8}}{1.0696 \times 10^{7}} = 80.9 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $80.9 < 100$ MPa, the design is **safe with a factor of safety of $100/80.9 = 1.236$** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.707 factor:** Students often compute the throat area as $s \times l$ instead of $0.707s \times l$. This overestimates capacity by 41% — a major error.
> 2. **Confusing $r$ for entire segment vs. center of segment:** The standard textbook approach uses the distance from the centroid to the **midpoint** of each segment, not the distance to either end. Using endpoint distance over- or under-estimates $J$.
> 3. **Using field-weld permissible stress by mistake:** A shop weld gets $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$; field gets 1.5. Mixing these up gives wrong sizes.
> 4. **Not checking $s_{min}$:** Providing a very small $s$ (e.g., 3 mm) may satisfy stress but violate IS 800 minimum leg size for the given plate thickness.
> 5. **Skipping unit conversion:** $T$ in kN·m must be converted to N·mm (multiply by $10^{6}$). Forgetting this yields an answer 1000× too small.
> 6. **Forgetting $r_{max}$ for weld group:** The maximum stress depends on the **farthest point**, not the average or any other radius.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fillet Joint Under Torsion — High-Density Recap:**

- ✅ **Fillet weld throat:** $t = 0.707s$ — always the **failure plane** under load.
- ✅ **Two configurations:** **Circumferential weld** (shaft) and **Weld group** (planar).
- ✅ **Circumferential formula:** $T = \dfrac{\pi d^{2} \cdot 0.707s \cdot \tau}{2}$ — derived from thin cylindrical shell analogy.
- ✅ **Weld group formula:** $\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot r_{max}}{0.707 \, s \, J}$ — derived from shear flow on a 2-D weld line.
- ✅ **Polar moment of weld line:** $J = \sum r^{2} l = I_x + I_y$ — use **midpoint distances** of each segment.
- ✅ **Permissible stress (IS 800):** $\tau_{vd} = \dfrac{f_y}{\sqrt{3} \, \gamma_{mw}}$ with $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (shop) or 1.5 (field).
- ✅ **IS 800 size limits:** $s_{min}$ from table (3, 5, 6, 8 mm by plate thickness); $s_{max} \leq t_{plate} - 1.5$ mm.
- ✅ **Always provide next standard size** — never give the calculated value directly.
- ✅ **Units check:** $T$ in N·mm, $d, l, r$ in mm, $\tau$ in N/mm² (MPa).
- ✅ **For circumferential weld, $J$ is implicit** — the formula already integrates it; do not compute $J$ separately.
- ✅ **For weld group, find centroid first** (symmetry helps), then $J$, then $r_{max}$, then $s$.
- ✅ **Failure mode assumed:** Shear on the **throat plane** at 45° — this is the IS 800 convention.
- ✅ **Combined loading** (torsion + shear + bending) uses vector sum; not in Module 2 but related.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fillet Weld Under Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fillet Weld Under Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **fillet weld under bending** refers to a triangular-section weld connecting two members where the applied load generates a **bending moment** about the weld group's centroidal axis. The weld experiences a **linearly varying normal stress** along its length, superimposed on any direct shear stress from concurrent loading. Design assumes the **throat of the weld (t = 0.707s)** is the critical load-carrying dimension, and the **neutral axis passes through the centroid of the weld group**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** A fillet weld of leg size `s` has an effective **throat thickness t = 0.707·s**. Under pure bending moment `M`, the **maximum bending stress** at the extreme fiber is given by `τ_b = M·c / I`, where `I` is the second moment of area of the throat section about the neutral axis, and `c` is the distance to the farthest weld point.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have **two flat steel plates** joined by a triangular weld bead along their edges. Now, if you grab one plate and try to **twist or bend** the joint:

- The weld points **closest to the center** of the joint barely feel any stress — like the center of a seesaw that doesn't move.
- The weld points **farthest from the center** feel the most stress — like the ends of a seesaw moving up and down dramatically.
- This is exactly how a **beam in bending** behaves: stress is zero at the neutral axis and maximum at the outer fibers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:** Think of the weld as a **chain of tiny load-bearing links** holding two plates together. The links at the center don't need to work hard (they're at the neutral axis), but the links at the outer edges are stretched or compressed the most. This is why designers place **longer welds further from the load line** — to engage more weld material exactly where the stress is highest.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Throat thickness factor:** **0.707** (derived from `sin 45°`)
- **Allowable shear stress for shop welds (mild steel):** typically **110 N/mm²** (varies with electrode)
- **Allowable shear stress for field welds:** typically **95 N/mm²**
- **Standard leg sizes (s):** **3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20 mm** (per IS 814 / AWS standards)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Fillet welds under combined bending and direct shear are **HIGH-YIELD** for ESE questions. Always combine stresses using the vector (Pythagorean) formula: `τ_max = √(τ_b² + τ_d²)`.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — Why and How

When a fillet weld group is subjected to a bending moment, the following **assumptions** govern the analysis (as per IS 800:2007 and standard machine design texts):

1. **The weld is treated as a line** of thickness `t = 0.707s` lying along the weld's effective length.
2. **Stress distribution is linear** along the length of the weld (analogous to beam bending).
3. **The neutral axis passes through the centroid** of the weld group.
4. **Failure occurs at the throat** because it is the smallest cross-sectional dimension.
5. **Combined loading** (bending + direct shear) is handled by vector summation of the stresses.

#### Operational Logic Step-by-Step:

- **Step 1 — Identify the weld geometry:** A bracket welded to a column has welds on two sides (vertical) plus possibly top/bottom welds. The weld group forms a "C" or rectangular shape.
- **Step 2 — Locate the centroid:** The neutral axis (NA) passes through the centroid of the weld group. For symmetrical welds, NA is at the geometric center.
- **Step 3 — Calculate moment of inertia (I):** Using the throat dimension `t = 0.707s` as the "thickness" and the weld's projected length as the "width":
  - For a **single vertical weld of length L**: `I = t·L³/12 = 0.707·s·L³/12`
  - For a **C-shaped weld group** with two vertical welds at distance `b` apart: `I = 2·[t·L³/12 + t·L·(b/2)²]`
- **Step 4 — Apply bending equation:** `τ_b = M·c / I`, where `c` is the distance from NA to the farthest weld point.
- **Step 5 — Combine with direct shear (if any):** `τ_max = √(τ_b² + τ_d²)`
- **Step 6 — Check against allowable stress:** `τ_max ≤ τ_allowable`

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — these are the 12 formulas you MUST know for KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $t = 0.707 \cdot s$ | Throat thickness (leg size s) | mm |
| 2 | $A_{throat} = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L$ | Throat area (single weld length L) | mm² |
| 3 | $\tau_d = \dfrac{P}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L}$ | Direct shear stress due to axial load P | N/mm² |
| 4 | $I = \dfrac{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L^{3}}{12}$ | Moment of inertia of a single vertical weld | mm⁴ |
| 5 | $I = 2 \cdot \left[\dfrac{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L^{3}}{12} + 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \left(\dfrac{b}{2}\right)^{2}\right]$ | I for C-shaped weld group (two parallel welds, spacing b) | mm⁴ |
| 6 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{c}$ | Section modulus of weld group | mm³ |
| 7 | $\tau_b = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Bending stress at distance c from NA | N/mm² |
| 8 | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\tau_b^{2} + \tau_d^{2}}$ | Combined maximum stress (vector sum) | N/mm² |
| 9 | $P = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \tau_{allow}$ | Strength of a single fillet weld under direct shear | N |
| 10 | $M = \dfrac{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L^{2}}{6} \cdot \tau_{allow}$ | Moment capacity of a single vertical weld | N·mm |
| 11 | $I_{xx} = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot \left[\dfrac{d^{3}}{12}\right]$ (top+bottom) + vertical contributions | I for rectangular weld group | mm⁴ |
| 12 | $\tau_{allow} = 0.4 \cdot f_{y}$ (shop) | Allowable shear stress (per IS 800 for shop welds) | N/mm² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** For **unsymmetrical weld groups**, the neutral axis shifts toward the heavier weld side. Always recalculate the centroid using `ȳ = Σ(Aᵢ·yᵢ) / Σ(Aᵢ)` before computing stresses. This is a **frequently tested pitfall** in KTU 2024 scheme questions.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fillet welds under bending appear in:

- **Cantilever brackets** welded to columns (e.g., crane supports, pipe racks)
- **Seat angles** connecting beams to columns in steel structures
- **Machine tool bases** with welded stiffeners
- **Vehicle chassis** where brackets transfer bending loads
- **Pressure vessel saddles** supporting vessel weight
- **Robotic arm joints** transmitting bending moments

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Throat Thickness (t = 0.707s)

**Starting Point:** A fillet weld has an isosceles right-triangular cross-section with leg size `s`. The hypotenuse forms the face of the weld, and the perpendicular distance from the root to the hypotenuse is the **throat thickness**.

**Geometric Setup:**
- The two legs of the right triangle are both equal to `s`
- The hypotenuse length = `s·√2`
- The throat `t` is the altitude from the right-angle vertex to the hypotenuse

**Derivation Steps:**

For a right triangle with legs `a` and `b`, the altitude to the hypotenuse is:

$$t = \frac{a \cdot b}{\sqrt{a^{2} + b^{2}}}$$

Substituting `a = b = s`:

$$t = \frac{s \cdot s}{\sqrt{s^{2} + s^{2}}} = \frac{s^{2}}{s \cdot \sqrt{2}} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Rationalizing the denominator:

$$t = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{s \cdot \sqrt{2}}{2} = 0.707 \cdot s$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The throat thickness carries **70.7%** of the nominal leg size. This is why the factor 0.707 is **non-negotiable** in every KTU weld design calculation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Bending Stress in a Single Vertical Fillet Weld

**Setup:** A fillet weld of leg size `s` and length `L` is subjected to a bending moment `M` about an axis perpendicular to its length.

**Assumption:** The weld behaves like a rectangular beam of width `t = 0.707s` and depth `L`.

**Moment of Inertia:**

For a rectangular section of width `b = 0.707s` and depth `d = L`, the moment of inertia about the centroidal axis (perpendicular to length) is:

$$I = \frac{b \cdot d^{3}}{12} = \frac{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L^{3}}{12}$$

**Bending Stress:**

Using the flexure formula, the stress at distance `y` from the neutral axis is:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

The maximum stress occurs at the extreme fiber where `y = c = L/2`:

$$\tau_b = \frac{M \cdot (L/2)}{\frac{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L^{3}}{12}}$$

Simplifying:

$$\tau_b = \frac{6 \cdot M}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L^{2}} = \frac{8.485 \cdot M}{s \cdot L^{2}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Bending Stress in a C-Shaped Weld Group

**Setup:** Two parallel vertical welds, each of length `L` and leg size `s`, separated by a horizontal distance `b`.

**Using Parallel Axis Theorem:**

For each vertical weld:
- Moment of inertia about its own centroidal axis: `I₀ = (0.707·s·L³)/12`
- Distance from NA to each weld's centroid: `b/2`
- Area of each weld's throat: `A = 0.707·s·L`

**Total I for the group:**

$$I = 2 \cdot \left[ I_{0} + A \cdot \left(\frac{b}{2}\right)^{2} \right]$$

Substituting:

$$I = 2 \cdot \left[ \frac{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L^{3}}{12} + 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \frac{b^{2}}{4} \right]$$

Factoring out `0.707·s·L`:

$$I = 2 \cdot 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \left[ \frac{L^{2}}{12} + \frac{b^{2}}{4} \right]$$

$$I = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \left[ \frac{L^{2}}{6} + \frac{b^{2}}{2} \right]$$

**Bending Stress at Extreme Fiber** (c = b/2):

$$\tau_b = \frac{M \cdot c}{I} = \frac{M \cdot (b/2)}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \left[ \frac{L^{2}}{6} + \frac{b^{2}}{2} \right]}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Combined Bending and Direct Shear

**Setup:** A weld group carries both a transverse load `P` (causing direct shear) and a bending moment `M`.

**Direct Shear Stress:**

The direct shear is assumed uniform across the entire throat area:

$$\tau_d = \frac{P}{A_{total}} = \frac{P}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L_{total}}$$

For a C-shaped weld group with total length `L_total = 2L + 2b` (including top and bottom welds):

$$\tau_d = \frac{P}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot (2L + 2b)}$$

**Bending Stress** (as derived above).

**Combined Maximum Shear Stress (Vector Sum):**

Since direct shear acts vertically and bending stress is also vertical (at the extreme fiber), they are perpendicular **at the critical point** (corner of the weld). Using the Pythagorean theorem:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\tau_b^{2} + \tau_d^{2}}$$

**Design Condition:**

$$\tau_{max} \leq \tau_{allow}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Stress Components by Location

> [!NOTE]
> **This matrix is critical for KTU 2024 — examiners often test the student's ability to identify which stresses act where.**

| Weld Location | Direct Shear (τ_d) | Bending Stress (τ_b) | Total Stress (τ_max) |
|---------------|--------------------|-----------------------|----------------------|
| Centroid (NA) | Present (uniform) | **Zero** | τ_d |
| Mid-length of vertical weld | Present | Small (≈ 25% of max) | √(small² + τ_d²) |
| Top/Bottom of vertical weld | Present | **Maximum** | √(τ_b² + τ_d²) |
| Corner of weld group | Present | **Maximum** | **Critical point** |
| Far end of horizontal weld | Present | Smaller | Moderate |

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Master Pattern)

**Problem:** A bracket welded to a vertical column carries an eccentric load. Two vertical fillet welds of length `L = 100 mm` and leg size `s = 8 mm` are placed `b = 150 mm` apart. The applied load is `P = 20 kN` acting at an eccentricity `e = 200 mm` from the weld group's centroid. Calculate the maximum stress in the weld. Take `τ_allow = 110 N/mm²`.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the loads:**
- Direct shear: `P = 20,000 N`
- Bending moment: `M = P × e = 20,000 × 200 = 4,000,000 N·mm`

**Step 2 — Calculate total throat area:**
For two vertical welds: `A_total = 2 × (0.707 × 8 × 100) = 1,131.2 mm²`

**Step 3 — Direct shear stress:**

$$\tau_d = \frac{P}{A_{total}} = \frac{20{,}000}{1{,}131.2} = 17.68 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate moment of inertia:**

$$I = 2 \times \left[ \frac{0.707 \times 8 \times 100^{3}}{12} + 0.707 \times 8 \times 100 \times \left(\frac{150}{2}\right)^{2} \right]$$

$$I = 2 \times \left[ 471{,}333 + 2{,}651{,}250 \right] = 6{,}245{,}167 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 5 — Bending stress at extreme fiber (c = b/2 = 75 mm):**

$$\tau_b = \frac{M \cdot c}{I} = \frac{4{,}000{,}000 \times 75}{6{,}245{,}167} = 48.04 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Combined maximum stress:**

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\tau_b^{2} + \tau_d^{2}} = \sqrt{48.04^{2} + 17.68^{2}} = \sqrt{2307.8 + 312.6} = \sqrt{2620.4}$$

$$\tau_{max} = 51.19 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 7 — Check against allowable:**
`51.19 N/mm² < 110 N/mm²` ✓ **Design is SAFE.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fillet Weld Under Bending

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["External Load P Applied with Eccentricity e"] --> B["Decomposition at Weld Group Centroid"]
    B --> C["Direct Vertical Force P"]
    B --> D["Bending Moment M = P x e"]
    C --> E["Uniform Shear Stress td = P / Athroat"]
    D --> F["Linear Bending Stress Distribution"]
    E --> G["Stress Summation at Critical Point"]
    F --> G
    G --> H{"Identify Critical Weld Location"}
    H --> I["Corner Point: Maximum tb + td"]
    H --> J["Centroid Point: Only td, tb = 0"]
    I --> K["Compute Combined Stress tmax = sqrt tb^2 + td^2"]
    J --> K
    K --> L{"tmax less than or equal tallow"}
    L -- Yes --> M["DESIGN ACCEPTABLE"]
    L -- No --> N["REVISE: Increase s or L or add welds"]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style M fill:#51CF66,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style N fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

### 4.2 Weld Group Geometry and Stress Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["C-SHAPED WELD GROUP (Front View)"]
        A1["Top Weld: Length b, Throat t"]
        A2["Left Vertical Weld: Length L, Throat t"]
        A3["Right Vertical Weld: Length L, Throat t"]
        A4["Bottom Weld: Length b, Throat t"]
    end
    subgraph B["STRESS DISTRIBUTION ALONG VERTICAL WELD (Linear)"]
        B1["Top: tb = MAX, td = MAX, tmax = CRITICAL"]
        B2["Middle: tb = 0, td = uniform, tmax = td"]
        B3["Bottom: tb = MAX, td = MAX, tmax = CRITICAL"]
    end
    subgraph C["WELD SECTION (Cross-Section, 45-Degree Triangle)"]
        C1["Leg s, Leg s"]
        C2["Throat t = 0.707 x s"]
        C3["Face of Weld: s x sqrt 2"]
    end
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    A --> C
    style A fill:#E7F5FF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFE7E7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFF3BF,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Critical Point Identification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start: Weld Group Subjected to M and P"] --> S2["Step 1: Locate Centroid of Weld Group"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 2: Calculate I about Centroidal Axis"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 3: Identify c = Max Distance from NA"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 4: Calculate tb = M x c / I"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 5: Calculate td = P / Athroat"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 6: Locate Weld Point Farthest from NA"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 7: At That Point, Combine tb and td"]
    S8 --> S9["Step 8: tmax = sqrt tb^2 + td^2"]
    S9 --> S10["Step 9: Compare with tallow"]
    S10 --> S11{"Is tmax within limit"}
    S11 -- Yes --> S12["SAFE - Report Final s and L"]
    S11 -- No --> S13["UNSAFE - Iterate Design"]
    style S1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S9 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style S12 fill:#51CF66,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S13 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** The **critical point** is always the corner of the weld group **farthest from the neutral axis** because both `τ_b` (maximum) and `τ_d` (full magnitude) coincide there. This is a **guaranteed 2-mark question** in KTU 2024 — students who misidentify the critical point lose marks immediately.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define throat thickness of a fillet weld. Why is it considered the critical dimension in design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **throat thickness** of a fillet weld is the **shortest distance from the root of the weld to its face (hypotenuse)**. For a fillet weld with equal leg size `s`, the throat thickness is:

$$t = 0.707 \cdot s$$

It is the critical dimension because:
1. **It is the smallest cross-section** of the weld, and failure initiates at the weakest section.
2. **Loads are transferred through the throat** — the leg sizes alone do not directly carry load.
3. **The factor 0.707 (≈ sin 45°)** accounts for the inclined failure plane, ensuring the design strength is based on actual fracture resistance rather than nominal weld size. (1 mark each point = 3 marks)

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is the stress distribution in a fillet weld under bending assumed to be linear? State the formula for maximum bending stress.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The stress distribution in a fillet weld under bending is assumed to be **linear** based on the following reasoning (1 mark):

The weld is treated as a **line of material** (of thickness `t = 0.707s`) that behaves analogously to a **beam in flexure**. Just as in beam theory, the strain varies linearly with distance from the neutral axis, and since the material is elastic, the stress also varies linearly. This is consistent with the **flexure formula** (1 mark):

$$\tau_b = \frac{M \cdot c}{I}$$

where `I` is the moment of inertia of the **throat section** about the centroidal axis, and `c` is the distance from the neutral axis to the extreme fiber (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two alternative questions are provided below for internal choice.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

A cantilever bracket is welded to a vertical column using **two parallel vertical fillet welds**, each of length `L = 120 mm`, with a horizontal spacing of `b = 200 mm` between them. The bracket carries a vertical load of `P = 30 kN` at a horizontal distance of `e = 300 mm` from the column face. Take allowable shear stress in the weld as `τ_allow = 110 N/mm²`. Design the weld leg size.

##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Calculate the stresses in the weld group.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- `P = 30,000 N`
- `e = 300 mm`
- `M = P × e = 30,000 × 300 = 9 × 10⁶ N·mm`
- `L = 120 mm` (each vertical weld)
- `b = 200 mm` (spacing)
- `τ_allow = 110 N/mm²`

**Assume leg size `s`** (to be determined).

**Step 1 — Throat thickness:**
`t = 0.707·s`

**Step 2 — Direct shear stress (for two welds, total throat area):**

$$A_{total} = 2 \times 0.707 \times s \times 120 = 169.68 \cdot s \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$\tau_d = \frac{P}{A_{total}} = \frac{30{,}000}{169.68 \cdot s} = \frac{176.8}{s} \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Moment of inertia of weld group (C-shaped, two vertical welds):**

$$I = 2 \times \left[ \frac{0.707 \cdot s \cdot 120^{3}}{12} + 0.707 \cdot s \cdot 120 \cdot \left(\frac{200}{2}\right)^{2} \right]$$

$$I = 2 \cdot s \cdot \left[ 101{,}808 + 848{,}400 \right] = 1{,}900{,}416 \cdot s \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 4 — Bending stress at extreme fiber (c = 100 mm):**

$$\tau_b = \frac{M \cdot c}{I} = \frac{9 \times 10^{6} \times 100}{1{,}900{,}416 \cdot s} = \frac{473.6}{s} \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Combined stress (at the top/bottom corner of vertical welds):**

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\tau_b^{2} + \tau_d^{2}} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{473.6}{s}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{176.8}{s}\right)^{2}}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sqrt{473.6^{2} + 176.8^{2}}}{s} = \frac{\sqrt{224{,}297 + 31{,}258}}{s} = \frac{505.4}{s} \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Determine the required leg size `s`.**

**Setting up the design equation:**

$$\tau_{max} \leq \tau_{allow}$$

$$\frac{505.4}{s} \leq 110$$

$$s \geq \frac{505.4}{110} = 4.59 \text{ mm}$$

**Selecting standard leg size:**
Adopt the next higher standard size: **s = 6 mm** (per IS 814 standard sizes: 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10...).

**Verification with s = 6 mm:**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{505.4}{6} = 84.23 \text{ N/mm}^{2} < 110 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

**Final Design:** Use fillet welds of **leg size s = 6 mm** with length `L = 120 mm` and spacing `b = 200 mm`. **(7 marks: 3 for equation, 2 for computation, 2 for selection and verification)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A steel plate bracket is welded to a column using **C-shaped fillet welds** (two vertical welds + one top horizontal weld, no bottom weld). Each vertical weld has length `L = 100 mm` and the horizontal top weld has length `b = 180 mm`. The bracket carries a vertical load `P = 25 kN` with eccentricity `e = 250 mm` from the centroid of the weld group. If the leg size of the weld is `s = 8 mm`, determine the maximum stress in the weld. Take `τ_allow = 110 N/mm²`. Check if the design is safe.

##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Identify the centroid and calculate moment of inertia.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- `P = 25,000 N`
- `e = 250 mm`
- `M = 25,000 × 250 = 6.25 × 10⁶ N·mm`
- Two vertical welds, each `L = 100 mm`, leg `s = 8 mm`
- One horizontal top weld, `b = 180 mm`
- `τ_allow = 110 N/mm²`

**Step 1 — Centroid location:**
Take the **top horizontal weld as reference axis** (y = 0). 

Areas (in terms of throat dimension):
- Each vertical weld: `A_v = 0.707 × 8 × 100 = 565.6 mm²`
- Top horizontal weld: `A_h = 0.707 × 8 × 180 = 1018.1 mm²`

**Vertical centroid (ȳ from top):**

$$\bar{y} = \frac{2 \cdot A_v \cdot (L/2) + A_h \cdot 0}{2 A_v + A_h} = \frac{2 \times 565.6 \times 50 + 0}{2 \times 565.6 + 1018.1} = \frac{56{,}560}{2149.3} = 26.32 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Moment of inertia about horizontal centroidal axis:**

For each vertical weld (using parallel axis theorem):

$$I_{v,total} = 2 \times \left[ \frac{0.707 \times 8 \times 100^{3}}{12} + 565.6 \times (50 - 26.32)^{2} \right]$$

$$I_{v,total} = 2 \times \left[ 471{,}333 + 565.6 \times 561.4 \right] = 2 \times \left[ 471{,}333 + 317{,}540 \right] = 1{,}577{,}746 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

For top horizontal weld:

$$I_{h} = \frac{1018.1 \times 180^{2}}{12} + 1018.1 \times (26.32)^{2} = 2{,}748{,}870 + 705{,}290 = 3{,}454{,}160 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Total:**

$$I = 1{,}577{,}746 + 3{,}454{,}160 = 5{,}031{,}906 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Calculate stresses and verify safety.**

**Step 3 — Maximum distance from NA:**

For the **bottom of the vertical welds**: `c_bottom = L - ȳ = 100 - 26.32 = 73.68 mm`
For the **top horizontal weld**: `c_top = ȳ = 26.32 mm`

The **critical point** is at the **bottom of the vertical weld** (larger `c`).

**Step 4 — Bending stress:**

$$\tau_b = \frac{M \cdot c}{I} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{6} \times 73.68}{5{,}031{,}906} = 91.51 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Direct shear stress:**
Total throat area: `A_total = 2 × 565.6 + 1018.1 = 2149.3 mm²`

$$\tau_d = \frac{25{,}000}{2149.3} = 11.63 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Combined stress at critical point:**

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{91.51^{2} + 11.63^{2}} = \sqrt{8374.1 + 135.3} = \sqrt{8509.4}$$

$$\tau_{max} = 92.25 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 7 — Safety check:**

`92.25 N/mm² < 110 N/mm²` ✓ **Design is SAFE.**

**Efficiency:** `(92.25 / 110) × 100% = 83.9%` — adequate design utilization.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Key):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.707 factor:** Using `s` directly instead of `0.707·s` for throat thickness. **Penalty: 2-3 marks deducted immediately.**
> 2. **Wrong centroid for unsymmetrical welds:** Assuming the NA is at the geometric center for C-shaped welds with only top horizontal weld. **Penalty: 1-2 marks for incorrect I calculation cascading into wrong stress.**
> 3. **Vector sum vs. arithmetic sum confusion:** Using `τ_b + τ_d` (arithmetic) instead of `√(τ_b² + τ_d²)` (vector). **Penalty: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Wrong critical point identification:** Evaluating stress at the centroid instead of the extreme fiber. **Penalty: 3 marks — entire stress calculation may be marked wrong.**
> 5. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing N and kN, or mm and m. **Penalty: 1 mark; may cause numerical errors.**
> 6. **Missing the eccentricity term:** Forgetting to convert eccentric load into both `P` AND `M`. **Penalty: 2-3 marks.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ✅ **Throat thickness:** `t = 0.707·s` — universal constant for fillet welds.
- ✅ **Bending stress formula:** `τ_b = M·c / I` — analogous to beam bending.
- ✅ **Direct shear formula:** `τ_d = P / (0.707·s·L_total)`.
- ✅ **Combined stress (KEY):** `τ_max = √(τ_b² + τ_d²)` — vector (Pythagorean) sum, NOT arithmetic.
- ✅ **Moment of inertia for C-shaped weld:** Use parallel axis theorem; `I = 2·[I₀ + A·(b/2)²]`.
- ✅ **Critical point:** Always the **corner farthest from the neutral axis** (top/bottom of vertical welds).
- ✅ **Allowable stresses:** Shop weld = 110 N/mm²; Field weld = 95 N/mm² (typical for mild steel).
- ✅ **Standard leg sizes:** 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20 mm — always round UP to next standard size.
- ✅ **Centroid shift:** For unsymmetrical welds, NA shifts toward the heavier weld side.
- ✅ **Eccentric load splits into:** `P` (direct shear) + `M = P·e` (bending) — both must be considered.
- ✅ **Assumption:** Weld is treated as a line; stress distribution is linear (elastic beam theory).
- ✅ **Failure plane:** Always at the throat (45° plane through the weld root).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always present your solution in the structured format: **(i) Given data, (ii) Assumed leg size, (iii) Centroid & I calculation, (iv) Stress components, (v) Combined stress, (vi) Verification, (vii) Standard size selection.** Examiners reward **methodical presentation** with full marks even if a minor arithmetic slip occurs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eccentrically Loaded Welds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Eccentrically Loaded Welds --- # 1. Eccentrically Loaded Welds — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Eccentrically Loaded Welds** are fillet weld groups subjected to a load whose **line of action does not pass through the centroid** of the weld group. The eccentricity $e$ between the load line of action and the centroid of the weld group generates an additional **bending moment** ($M = P \times e$) which must be combined with the **direct shear load** $P$ for a safe, KTU-compliant design. In KTU's Machine Design (PCMET602) syllabus, eccentrically loaded welds are categorised into two distinct geometric configurations, each requiring a different analytical model: | Configuration | Load Plane | Primary Stresses Induced | |---|---|---| | **Case 1: Eccentricity in the Plane of the Weld** | Load lies parallel to the weld plane | Direct (Primary) Shear + Torsional Shear | | **Case 2: Eccentricity Normal to the Plane of the Weld** | Load is perpendicular to the weld plane | Direct (Primary) Shear + Bending (Tensile/Compressive) Stress | The welds in *both* cases are assumed to be **fillet welds** loaded through their **throat** $t = 0.707\,s$, where $s$ is the leg size. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bracket & the See-Saw" Imagine a heavy steel bracket bolted (or welded) to a vertical column. Instead of pulling it **straight down along its centre line**, you pull it with a hook located some distance away from the column face. - The **straight-down pull** is the *primary shear* $P$, trying to slice the weld sideways. - Because your hook is offset by a distance $e$, the bracket also wants to **rotate** about the centroid of the weld group — like a see-saw pivoting at its centre. This twisting action creates the **torsional moment** $T = P \times e$. - Every tiny piece of weld is therefore fighting **two loads at once**: the straight pull *and* the twist. The weld fails when the **vector sum** of these two shear stresses at the *farthest point* from the centroid exceeds the allowable shear stress of the weld metal. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** In 90% of KTU board problems on this topic, the examiner expects you to: > 1. Locate the **weld-group centroid** (in $x$ and $y$). > 2. Compute the **polar moment of inertia** $J = I_x + I_y$ of the weld line. > 3. Identify the **critical (farthest) point** on the weld from the centroid. > 4. Apply the **vector addition formula** for maximum resultant stress. > Skipping any one of these four steps guarantees mark deduction. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Geometric Setup & Weld Group Properties For any fillet weld group composed of straight segments (vertical, horizontal, or both), the following geometric properties of the **weld line** (not the weld metal) must be established: 1. **Total effective throat area** of the weld group: $$A_w = 0.707\,s \sum L$$ where $s$ = leg size of the fillet weld, and $\sum L$ = total length of the weld including any return welds (returns are usually neglected in KTU problems unless explicitly mentioned). 2. **Centroid location** $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ of the weld line measured from a convenient reference axis (typically the column face or one corner of the weld group): $$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum L_i \cdot x_i}{\sum L_i} \qquad \bar{y} = \frac{\sum L_i \cdot y_i}{\sum L_i}$$ 3. **Moments of Inertia of the weld line** about its own centroidal axes: $$I_x = \sum \left[ \frac{L_i \cdot d_i^2}{12} + L_i \cdot y_i'^2 \right]$$ $$I_y = \sum \left[ \frac{L_i \cdot d_i^3}{12} + L_i \cdot x_i'^2 \right]$$ where $d_i$ is the segment dimension perpendicular to the axis, and $(x_i', y_i')$ is the centroid of each segment measured from the **weld-group centroid**. 4. **Polar moment of inertia** of the weld group: $$J = I_x + I_y$$ ## 2.2 Case 1: Eccentric Load in the Plane of the Weld (Torsion Case) This is the most frequently tested scenario. A vertical weld group is subjected to a horizontal force $P$ whose line of action is at an eccentricity $e$ from the centroid of the weld. ### 2.2.1 Primary (Direct) Shear Stress The direct shear is uniformly distributed across the throat area: $$\tau_d = \frac{P}{A_w} = \frac{P}{0.707\,s\sum L}$$ Direction: parallel to $P$ at every point of the weld. ### 2.2.2 Secondary (Torsional) Shear Stress The eccentric load creates a moment about the weld centroid: $$T = P \times e$$ This moment is resisted by the weld group acting like a thin-walled tube. At any point located at radial distance $r = \sqrt{x'^2 + y'^2}$ from the centroid: $$\tau_t = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$$ Direction: perpendicular to the radial line $r$ at that point (tangential to a circle centred on the centroid). ### 2.2.3 Resultant Maximum Shear Stress At any point, the direct and torsional shear stresses can be added **vectorially**. The angle $\theta$ between $\tau_d$ (always parallel to $P$) and $\tau_t$ (always perpendicular to $r$) must be determined geometrically. The **general vector-addition** (exact) formula: $$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\tau_d^2 + \tau_t^2 + 2\,\tau_d\,\tau_t\,\cos\theta}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Shortcut (Most Board Problems):** The maximum always occurs at the point where $\tau_d$ and $\tau_t$ are **parallel and in the same direction** (i.e., $\theta = 0°$, $\cos\theta = 1$). This point is typically the **upper-right or lower-left corner** (farthest from centroid). In such case: $$\boxed{\;\tau_{max} = \tau_d + \tau_t = \frac{P}{A_w} + \frac{P \cdot e \cdot r_{max}}{J}\;}$$ The conservative (worst-case) summation $\tau_d + \tau_t$ is accepted by KTU examiners for vertical weld groups with corner-aligned loads. ## 2.3 Case 2: Eccentric Load Normal to the Plane of the Weld (Bending Case) Here, a horizontal weld group (or a C-shaped weld) is subjected to a vertical load $P$ at an eccentricity $e$, creating a **bending moment** $M = P \times e$ in a plane containing the weld. ### 2.3.1 Direct Shear $$\tau_d = \frac{P}{A_w}$$ ### 2.3.2 Bending (Tensile/Compressive) Stress The moment is resisted as a bending stress across the weld throat: $$\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot c}{I}$$ where $c$ is the distance from the neutral axis (weld-group centroidal axis) to the **extreme fibre**, and $I$ is the moment of inertia of the weld line about that axis. ### 2.3.3 Maximum Combined Stress Since $\tau_d$ acts parallel to the load and $\sigma_b$ acts perpendicular to the weld line, they are orthogonal at the critical point: $$\boxed{\;\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\tau_d^2 + \sigma_b^2}\;}$$ For welds where the throat allows both shear and normal stress, the design criterion is: $$\tau_{max} \leq \tau_{allow} = 0.4\,S_{yt} \quad \text{(for ductile welds)}$$ where $S_{yt}$ is the **yield strength of the weld electrode** (typically E60 → $S_{yt} = 330$ MPa, E70 → $S_{yt} = 370$ MPa, etc.). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield KTU Formula Table — Print This Sheet Before Every Exam** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Effective throat | $t = 0.707\,s$ | mm | | 2 | Weld area | $A_w = 0.707\,s \sum L$ | mm² | | 3 | Centroid | $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum L_i x_i}{\sum L_i}$ | mm | | 4 | Moment of inertia (vertical segment) | $\dfrac{L\,d^3}{12} + L\,\bar{y}^2$ | mm³ | | 5 | Moment of inertia (horizontal segment) | $\dfrac{L\,d^3}{12} + L\,\bar{x}^2$ | mm³ | | 6 | Polar moment of inertia | $J = I_x + I_y$ | mm³ | | 7 | Torsional moment | $T = P \cdot e$ | N·mm | | 8 | Direct shear stress | $\tau_d = \dfrac{P}{A_w}$ | MPa | | 9 | Torsional shear stress | $\tau_t = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$ | MPa | | 10 | Combined (Case 1) — vector form | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\tau_d^2 + \tau_t^2 + 2\tau_d\tau_t\cos\theta}$ | MPa | | 11 | Combined (Case 1) — worst-case | $\tau_{max} = \tau_d + \tau_t$ | MPa | | 12 | Bending stress (Case 2) | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | MPa | | 13 | Combined (Case 2) | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\tau_d^2 + \sigma_b^2}$ | MPa | | 14 | Allowable shear in weld | $\tau_{allow} = 0.4\,S_{yt}$ | MPa | | 15 | Strength of weld group | $P = 0.707\,s\,L \cdot \tau_{allow}$ | N | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Eccentrically loaded weld analysis is the **backbone of all bracket-to-column connections** in: - **Structural steel buildings** (gusseted base plates, crane brackets) - **Pressure vessel saddles** (horizontal vessel support lugs) - **Transmission towers** (cantilevered cross-arm connections) - **Automotive chassis** (engine mount brackets to frame rails) - **Machine tool frames** (tool post brackets) An error in centroid location of just 5 mm on a 200 mm weld group can shift the maximum stress by **15–25%**, often determining whether a connection survives fatigue loading. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — Full KTU Board Style > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A steel bracket is welded to a vertical column using three vertical fillet welds as shown below. The welds are each of leg size $s = 10$ mm. The welds are located at horizontal distances $x_1 = 0$, $x_2 = 75$ mm, $x_3 = 150$ mm from the left edge of the column, and each weld is 100 mm long. An eccentric vertical load $P = 50$ kN is applied at a horizontal distance $e = 250$ mm from the **column face** (i.e., from the leftmost weld). The yield strength of the weld electrode is $S_{yt} = 370$ MPa (E70 series). Check whether the weld is safe. ### Step 1 — Sketch & Identify Geometry ``` P = 50 kN ↓ | e = 250 mm | =======|========|========|==== ← Column face (x = 0) Weld 1 (x=0) Weld 2 (x=75) Weld 3 (x=150) L = 100 mm L = 100 mm L = 100 mm ``` ### Step 2 — Compute Weld Group Centroid $$\bar{x} = \frac{L_1 x_1 + L_2 x_2 + L_3 x_3}{L_1 + L_2 + L_3}$$ $$\bar{x} = \frac{(100)(0) + (100)(75) + (100)(150)}{100 + 100 + 100}$$ $$\bar{x} = \frac{0 + 7500 + 15000}{300} = \frac{22500}{300} = 75 \text{ mm}$$ The centroid lies **between Weld 2 and Weld 3**, exactly at the centre of the middle weld. ### Step 3 — Compute Eccentricity about the Weld-Group Centroid The load $P$ acts at $x = 250$ mm from the column face. The centroid is at $\bar{x} = 75$ mm. So: $$e = 250 - \bar{x} = 250 - 75 = 175 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 4 — Compute Total Weld Throat Area $$A_w = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot \sum L = 0.707 \times 10 \times 300 = 2121 \text{ mm}^2$$ ### Step 5 — Compute Primary (Direct) Shear Stress The vertical load $P$ creates a vertical shear distributed uniformly: $$\tau_d = \frac{P}{A_w} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{2121} = 23.57 \text{ MPa}$$ Direction: **vertical (downward)** at every point of the weld. ### Step 6 — Compute Polar Moment of Inertia of the Weld Group For each weld segment of length $L = 100$ mm, its own moment of inertia about its centroidal horizontal axis is: $$I_{x,i}^{\text{self}} = \frac{L \cdot t^3}{12} \approx 0 \quad \text{(negligible, since } t \text{ is treated as a line)}$$ By symmetry of segment lengths, all segments lie at their own $y$-centroid $= 0$, so the parallel-axis term in $I_x$ vanishes: $$I_x = 0$$ For $I_y$, we apply the parallel-axis theorem to each weld's line: $$I_{y,i} = L_i \cdot x_i'^2$$ where $x_i' = x_i - \bar{x}$ is the horizontal distance from the weld centroid. - Weld 1: $x_1' = 0 - 75 = -75$ mm → $x_1'^2 = 5625$ mm² - Weld 2: $x_2' = 75 - 75 = 0$ mm → $x_2'^2 = 0$ - Weld 3: $x_3' = 150 - 75 = 75$ mm → $x_3'^2 = 5625$ mm² $$I_y = 100 \times 5625 + 100 \times 0 + 100 \times 5625 = 1{,}125{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$ $$J = I_x + I_y = 0 + 1{,}125{,}000 = 1{,}125{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$ ### Step 7 — Identify the Critical Point The farthest weld from the centroid is either Weld 1 or Weld 3 (both at $r_{max} = 75$ mm). Since Weld 1 is on the side **opposite** to the load, and Weld 3 is on the **same side** as the load, the torsional shear at Weld 3 **adds directly** to the direct shear (same direction). Hence the critical point is the **top/bottom of Weld 3**. $$r_{max} = 75 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 8 — Compute Torsional Shear Stress at Critical Point $$T = P \times e = 50{,}000 \times 175 = 8{,}750{,}000 \text{ N·mm}$$ $$\tau_t = \frac{T \cdot r_{max}}{J} = \frac{8{,}750{,}000 \times 75}{1{,}125{,}000} = 583.33 \text{ MPa}$$ Wait — this number looks impossibly high. Let us verify the units: $P$ in N, $e$ in mm, $J$ in mm³, so $\tau_t$ comes out in **N/mm² = MPa** ✓. However, the value is clearly unrealistic — it means our weld is grossly undersized. KTU examiners typically require re-sizing in such problems. Let us **re-design the weld size** $s$. ### Step 9 — Resize the Weld Leg (Standard KTU Follow-up) Design criterion: $$\tau_{max} = \tau_d + \tau_t \leq \tau_{allow} = 0.4 S_{yt} = 0.4 \times 370 = 148 \text{ MPa}$$ Substituting $\tau_d = P / (0.707\,s \sum L)$ and $\tau_t = (P \cdot e \cdot r_{max}) / (0.707\,s \cdot J)$ (note: throat factor $0.707s$ cancels in $J$ only if we treat $J$ as line inertia; for full throat-area inertia, we multiply both numerator and denominator by $0.707\,s$ which cancels): $$\frac{P}{0.707\,s \sum L} + \frac{P \cdot e \cdot r_{max}}{0.707\,s \cdot J} = \tau_{allow}$$ Factor out $\dfrac{1}{0.707\,s}$: $$\frac{1}{0.707\,s}\left[ \frac{P}{\sum L} + \frac{P \cdot e \cdot r_{max}}{J} \right] = 148$$ $$\frac{1}{0.707\,s}\left[ \frac{50{,}000}{300} + \frac{50{,}000 \times 175 \times 75}{1{,}125{,}000} \right] = 148$$ $$\frac{1}{0.707\,s}\left[ 166.67 + 583.33 \right] = 148$$ $$\frac{750.0}{0.707\,s} = 148 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \frac{750.0}{0.707 \times 148} = \frac{750.0}{104.64} = 7.17 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 10 — Select Standard Leg Size Round up to the next standard fillet weld leg size: $$\boxed{s = 8 \text{ mm}}$$ ### Step 11 — Re-verify with $s = 8$ mm $$A_w = 0.707 \times 8 \times 300 = 1696.8 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$\tau_d = \frac{50{,}000}{1696.8} = 29.46 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\tau_t = \frac{8{,}750{,}000 \times 75}{0.707 \times 8 \times 1{,}125{,}000} = \frac{8{,}750{,}000 \times 75}{6{,}363{,}000} = 103.1 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\tau_{max} = 29.46 + 103.1 = 132.56 \text{ MPa} \leq 148 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$ **The weld with leg size 8 mm is SAFE.** ## 3.2 Summary of the Analytical Matrix | Step | What to Compute | Common Student Mistake | |---|---|---| | 1 | Centroid $\bar{x}, \bar{y}$ | Forgetting to add **return weld** contributions | | 2 | Eccentricity $e$ from centroid (not column face) | Taking $e$ from the wrong reference axis | | 3 | Weld throat area $A_w$ | Using $\sum L$ only (ignoring the $0.707\,s$ factor) | | 4 | Polar moment of inertia $J$ | Forgetting the **self-inertia** $\frac{L d^3}{12}$ of each segment | | 5 | Identify critical point | Picking the wrong corner (always farthest from centroid in the load's direction) | | 6 | Vector add $\tau_d$ and $\tau_t$ | Using vector addition at the wrong point — must be the **same physical point** | | 7 | Compare with $\tau_{allow}$ | Forgetting the **0.4** safety factor on yield strength | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Eccentric Weld Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TD A["START: Eccentric Weld Problem"] --> B["Identify Weld Geometry
Segments, Lengths, Layout"] B --> C["Compute Weld Centroid
x-bar and y-bar"] C --> D{"Load in Plane or
Normal to Weld Plane?"} D -->|In Plane| E["CASE 1: Torsion Dominates
Compute T = P x e"] D -->|Normal to Plane| F["CASE 2: Bending Dominates
Compute M = P x e"] E --> G["Compute Direct Shear
tau_d = P / A_w"] F --> H["Compute Direct Shear
tau_d = P / A_w"] G --> I["Compute Torsional Shear
tau_t = T x r / J"] H --> J["Compute Bending Stress
sigma_b = M x c / I"] I --> K{"Identify Critical Point
Farthest from Centroid"} J --> K2{"Identify Extreme Fibre
Farthest from NA"} K --> L["Vector Sum
tau_max = tau_d + tau_t"] K2 --> M["Orthogonal Sum
tau_max = sqrt tau_d squared + sigma_b squared"] L --> N["Compare with tau_allow
tau_allow = 0.4 x S_yt"] M --> N N --> O{"tau_max <= tau_allow?"} O -->|Yes| P["DESIGN IS SAFE"] O -->|No| Q["REVISE: Increase s or L
Iterate until Satisfied"] Q --> B ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Weld Group Stress Components ```mermaid block-beta columns 3 block:Load["Applied Load P"]:3 LoadA["Direct Force P"] LoadB["Moment T = P x e"] block:Stress["Resultant Stresses"]:3 StrA["Primary Shear tau_d"] StrB["Torsional Shear tau_t"] StrC["Resultant tau_max"] block:Check["Design Check"]:3 ChkA["Allowable Stress"] ChkB["Compare and Verify"] ChkC["Safety Factor >= 1"] Load --> Stress --> Check ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Bracket-to-Column Weld Group (L-Shaped) ```mermaid graph LR COL["Column Face
Vertical Reference"]:::col WV["Vertical Weld
Length L1"]:::weld WH["Horizontal Weld
Length L2"]:::weld P["P at distance e
from centroid"]:::load CG["Weld Centroid
G(x-bar, y-bar)"]:::centroid RMAX["Critical Point
r = r_max"]:::critical COL --- WV WV --- WH P --- CG CG --- RMAX classDef col fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef weld fill:#e67e22,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef load fill:#c0392b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef centroid fill:#27ae60,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef critical fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]** ### Q1. Define *eccentrically loaded weld*. How does the eccentricity affect the design of a fillet weld group? **Model Answer:** An eccentrically loaded weld is a fillet weld group subjected to a load whose line of action does **not pass through the centroid** of the weld group. The eccentricity $e$ creates an additional moment $T = P \times e$, which superimposes torsional shear stresses $\tau_t$ on the direct shear $\tau_d$. The weld design must satisfy: $$\tau_{max} = \tau_d + \tau_t \leq 0.4\,S_{yt}$$ The eccentricity increases the **critical-point stress** linearly with $e$, so even a small offset can significantly enlarge the required weld size. --- ### Q2. What is the difference between *direct shear stress* and *torsional shear stress* in a fillet weld group subjected to eccentric loading? **Model Answer:** | Feature | Direct Shear Stress $\tau_d$ | Torsional Shear Stress $\tau_t$ | |---|---|---| | **Cause** | Applied load $P$ directly | Moment $T = P \cdot e$ due to eccentricity | | **Magnitude** | Constant across weld group | Varies linearly with $r$ (farthest from centroid) | | **Direction** | Parallel to load $P$ | Tangential to circle around centroid | | **Formula** | $\tau_d = P/A_w$ | $\tau_t = T \cdot r / J$ | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** A bracket is welded to a column by means of **two vertical fillet welds of length 120 mm each**, located at **horizontal distances of 60 mm and 240 mm** from the left edge of the column. An **eccentric vertical load $P = 40$ kN** is applied at a horizontal distance of **300 mm** from the leftmost weld. The yield strength of the weld electrode is $S_{yt} = 330$ MPa. Design the weld leg size $s$. **(a) Determine the weld-group centroid and the eccentricity from the centroid.** **[7 Marks]** **(b) Compute the polar moment of inertia, maximum combined shear stress, and the required leg size $s$.** **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Centroid Location** $$\bar{x} = \frac{L_1 x_1 + L_2 x_2}{L_1 + L_2} = \frac{(120)(0) + (120)(180)}{120 + 120}$$ $$\bar{x} = \frac{21{,}600}{240} = 90 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Eccentricity** The load is applied at 300 mm from the **leftmost weld**, i.e., at $x = 300$ mm from the column face. $$e = 300 - \bar{x} = 300 - 90 = 210 \text{ mm}$$ --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 3 — Total Weld Length and Throat Area** $$\sum L = 240 \text{ mm}, \quad A_w = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot 240 = 169.68\,s \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 4 — Polar Moment of Inertia** Using $I_y = \sum L_i x_i'^2$ (self-inertia negligible for line welds): - $x_1' = 0 - 90 = -90$ mm → $x_1'^2 = 8100$ mm² - $x_2' = 180 - 90 = +90$ mm → $x_2'^2 = 8100$ mm² $$I_y = 120 \times 8100 + 120 \times 8100 = 1{,}944{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$ $$I_x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad J = I_x + I_y = 1{,}944{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$$ **Step 5 — Direct and Torsional Shear** $$\tau_d = \frac{P}{A_w} = \frac{40{,}000}{169.68\,s} = \frac{235.7}{s} \text{ MPa}$$ $$T = P \cdot e = 40{,}000 \times 210 = 8.4 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$ $$r_{max} = 90 \text{ mm}$$ $$\tau_t = \frac{T \cdot r_{max}}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot J} = \frac{8.4 \times 10^6 \times 90}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot 1{,}944{,}000} = \frac{549.6}{s} \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 6 — Combined Maximum Stress (Worst Case)** $$\tau_{max} = \tau_d + \tau_t = \frac{235.7 + 549.6}{s} = \frac{785.3}{s} \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 7 — Design Equation** $$\tau_{max} \leq \tau_{allow} = 0.4 \times 330 = 132 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\frac{785.3}{s} \leq 132 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s \geq \frac{785.3}{132} = 5.95 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 8 — Standard Leg Size** $$\boxed{s = 6 \text{ mm (standard fillet weld leg)}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative* A **horizontal cantilever bracket** carrying a vertical load is welded along its top edge to a **horizontal C-shaped weld group**. The weld consists of a **top horizontal segment of 200 mm**, and **two vertical side segments of 80 mm each** (forming a channel). A load $P = 60$ kN acts vertically at a distance of 400 mm from the column face. Compute the maximum bending stress in the weld, and verify safety against $\tau_{allow} = 145$ MPa. **(a) Compute the polar moment of inertia and the bending stress at the extreme fibre.** **[7 Marks]** **(b) Add the direct shear, find the resultant combined stress, and check safety.** **[7 Marks]** *(Detailed solution follows the same methodology as Question A; the centroid lies at mid-width by symmetry, $I_x$ and $I_y$ are computed with parallel-axis theorem, and $\sigma_b = M c / I$ is evaluated at the extreme fibre. The final combined stress is $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\tau_d^2 + \sigma_b^2}$.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Wrong Reference for Eccentricity (–2 marks):** Students frequently take $e$ from the **column face** instead of from the **weld-group centroid**. The moment $T = P \cdot e$ must use $e$ measured from the centroid. > > 2. **Forgetting Self-Inertia of Segments (–1 to –2 marks):** When weld segments are long *and* parallel to the axis of interest (e.g., horizontal welds computing $I_y$), the term $\frac{L d^3}{12}$ must be included. For *line* welds treated as 2D, this is often neglected — but for **welds with non-zero thickness**, KTU may require it. > > 3. **Mixing Up Torsion and Bending Cases (–3 marks):** Always check whether the load is *in* the plane of the weld or *normal* to it. Wrong case = wrong formula = zero credit. > > 4. **Forgetting the 0.707 Throat Factor (–2 marks):** Weld **area** uses $0.707\,s$, but the **polar moment of inertia** uses the *line* properties. Don't double-apply the factor. > > 5. **Not Standardising the Leg Size (–1 mark):** Always round up to standard sizes (6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 22 mm). A non-standard $s = 7.17$ mm is **not** a valid KTU answer. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Eccentrically Loaded Welds** - ✅ Eccentric load $P$ at offset $e$ produces **moment** $T = P \cdot e$ about weld centroid. - ✅ **Case 1 (Torsion):** $\tau_d = P/A_w$, $\tau_t = T r / J$, combined $\tau_{max} = \tau_d + \tau_t$ (worst case). - ✅ **Case 2 (Bending):** $\tau_d = P/A_w$, $\sigma_b = M c / I$, combined $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\tau_d^2 + \sigma_b^2}$. - ✅ Weld centroid: $\bar{x} = \sum L_i x_i / \sum L_i$, $\bar{y} = \sum L_i y_i / \sum L_i$. - ✅ Polar moment of inertia: $J = I_x + I_y$, with parallel-axis theorem applied to each segment. - ✅ Throat thickness: $t = 0.707\,s$ where $s$ = leg size. - ✅ Allowable shear in weld: $\tau_{allow} = 0.4\,S_{yt}$ for static loading in ductile electrodes. - ✅ Critical point = farthest weld from centroid, on the **same side** as the load. - ✅ Always **round up** the calculated leg size to the nearest standard value. - ✅ For symmetric weld groups, the centroid lies on the axis of symmetry. - ✅ If the calculated stress exceeds allowable, **increase leg size $s$** first (cheapest); only enlarge $\sum L$ if $s$ exceeds practical limits. - ✅ Vector-addition (general) vs worst-case summation — KTU accepts the latter for symmetric corner loading. - ✅ Return welds (at bracket ends) are typically neglected unless the problem specifies them. ---

topics.title: Springs - Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Springs - Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Spring (KTU Standard Definition):**
> A **spring** is a *resilient* elastic machine element capable of storing **mechanical potential energy** when subjected to a load (axial, torsional, or bending), and of releasing this energy when the load is removed, without undergoing permanent (plastic) deformation. Springs are classified as **critical machine components** in the KTU Machine Design framework because their failure directly compromises the operational safety of the host assembly.

The term **resilient** is the key KTU-board-exam differentiator — it means the spring returns to its original dimensions upon unloading, with the energy-storing capability being a function of the **strain energy per unit volume** stored in the elastic deformation of the spring material.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Why Springs Exist"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Trampoline / Bow:**
> Imagine pulling a bow-string. The bow *deforms*, *stores energy*, and when released, *returns to shape*, propelling the arrow. A spring does exactly the same — it is a *mechanical energy reservoir*.
> - **Stretched rubber band** = a tension spring in miniature
> - **Car suspension coil** = a compression spring
> - **Clothes-pin wire** = a torsion spring
> - **Truck leaf-spring pack** = a flat/bending spring

In engineering systems, springs are used to: (i) absorb shock (vehicle suspensions), (ii) store energy (clock mainsprings), (iii) maintain contact force (valve springs in IC engines), (iv) balance forces (weighing scales), and (v) control vibration (engine valve springs).

### 1.3 Primary Functions of Springs (KTU 2024 CO-Aligned)

| Function | Engineering Example |
|----------|---------------------|
| Energy Storage | Mainspring of a watch, recoil mechanism of a gun |
| Load Balancing | Weighing machines, spring balances |
| Shock Absorption | Railway buffers, vehicle suspension |
| Vibration Damping | IC engine valve springs |
| Maintaining Contact Force | Clutch springs, brake return springs |
| Flexibility / Compliance | Couplings, sealing elements |

### 1.4 Physical & Material Properties Governing Spring Action

> [!NOTE]
> **Hooke's Law (the soul of spring design):**
> $$F = k \cdot \delta$$
> where $F$ is the applied force, $\delta$ is the deflection, and **k** is the *spring stiffness / spring rate* measured in **N/mm**. For helical springs, $k$ is derived from $G$ (Shear Modulus) and geometric parameters — covered in detail in the subsequent modules of KTU PCMET602.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Material Property — Modulus of Rigidity (G):**
> For **steel**: $G \approx 79.3 \text{ GPa}$ (or $80 \text{ GPa}$); for **phosphor bronze**: $G \approx 42 \text{ GPa}$; for **stainless steel (austenitic)**: $G \approx 77 \text{ GPa}$. These values must be committed to memory for KTU numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Philosophy (Hierarchical Logic)

KTU's classification of springs is typically based on **three orthogonal criteria**:

1. **Shape / Geometry** (the most common KTU classification axis)
2. **Type of load / stress** induced
3. **Direction of deformation**

> [!NOTE]
> A single spring can belong to multiple classes simultaneously. For example, a *helical compression spring* is (i) helical in shape, (ii) loaded in axial compression, and (iii) subjected to **torsional shear stress** in the wire cross-section.

### 2.2 Master Classification (KTU Board-Exam Format)

**A. Helical Springs** (wire wound in helix form)
- *Compression / Helical Compression Spring*
- *Extension / Tension / Draw Spring*
- *Torsion Spring*

**B. Spiral Springs** (flat strip wound in spiral)
- *Clock / Mainspring*

**C. Conical and Volute Springs**
- *Conical Compression Spring*
- *Volute Spring*

**D. Disc / Belleville Springs**
- *Frustum-shaped disc washers*

**E. Leaf / Laminated Springs**
- *Semi-elliptic leaf spring*
- *Elliptic leaf spring*
- *Cantilever leaf spring*

**F. Torsion Bar**
- *Straight cylindrical bar loaded in torsion*

**G. Rubber Springs**
- *Elastomeric blocks loaded in compression/shear*

### 2.3 Detailed Sub-Classification of Helical Springs

| Sub-Type | Loading | Wire Stress | Free-End Configuration | Typical Use |
|----------|---------|-------------|------------------------|-------------|
| Helical Compression | Axial Push | Torsion (pure shear) | Open / Closed / Ground ends | Suspension, valve trains |
| Helical Extension | Axial Pull | Torsion (pure shear) | Hook / Loop / Coned ends | Garage doors, trampolines |
| Helical Torsion | Moment (couple) | Bending | Tangential tangs / arms | Clothespins, mouse-traps |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Spring Geometry Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric parameters MUST be memorized as they appear in nearly every KTU board numerical.**

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Range / Standard |
|---|-----------|--------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Wire diameter | $d$ | — | 0.5 mm to 25 mm |
| 2 | Mean coil diameter | $D$ | Measured from wire-centerline axis | — |
| 3 | Outer coil diameter | $OD$ | $OD = D + d$ | — |
| 4 | Inner coil diameter | $ID$ | $ID = D - d$ | — |
| 5 | Spring index | $C$ | $C = D / d$ | 4 to 12 (ideal = 6 to 10) |
| 6 | Total number of coils | $N_t$ | $N_t = N_a + N_i$ (end coils) | — |
| 7 | Active coils | $N_a$ | Coils free to deflect | — |
| 8 | Solid length | $L_s$ | $L_s = N_t \cdot d$ | — |
| 9 | Free length | $L_f$ | $L_s + \delta_{max} + (N_i - 1) \cdot d$ | — |
| 10 | Pitch | $p$ | $p = (L_f - L_s) / N_a$ | — |
| 11 | Spring rate | $k$ | $k = F / \delta$ | N/mm |
| 12 | Shear modulus | $G$ | Material property | **80 GPa for steel** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $C = D/d$ called "Spring Index"?**
> It is the *geometric ratio that indexes the relative "slenderness"* of the coil. A low C (thick wire, small mean dia) → high stresses, hard to manufacture, prone to buckling. A high C (thin wire, large mean dia) → "tangly", hard to handle, creeps. Hence the KTU standard of $C = 6$ to $10$ as a *design sweet-spot*.

### 2.5 End-Type Classification (Compression Springs) — Critical for KTU Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$N_t$ depends on the end type. KTU problems ALWAYS give this data:**

| End Type | Active Coils $N_a$ | Total Coils $N_t$ | Solid Length $L_s$ |
|----------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Plain Ends | $N_a$ | $N_a$ | $L_s = N_a \cdot d$ |
| Plain Ends Ground | $N_a$ | $N_a$ | $L_s = (N_a + 1)d$ |
| Closed Ends | $N_a$ | $N_a + 2$ | $L_s = (N_a + 2)d$ |
| Closed & Ground Ends | $N_a$ | $N_a + 2$ | $L_s = (N_a + 2)d$ |

### 2.6 Spring Materials — KTU Board-Exam Knowledge

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU viva/practical, remember these materials with their key applications:**

| Material | Key Property | Typical KTU Use Case |
|----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **High-Carbon Steel (e.g., EN 42, EN 47)** | High fatigue strength, low cost | Automotive leaf springs |
| **Alloy Steels (Silicon-Manganese, Chrome-Vanadium)** | Excellent resilience, heat resistance | IC engine valve springs |
| **Stainless Steel (AISI 302, 304, 316)** | Corrosion resistance | Medical, food, marine |
| **Phosphor Bronze** | High conductivity, non-magnetic | Electrical contacts, precision instruments |
| **Beryllium Copper** | Non-sparking, high fatigue | Hazardous-environment springs |
| **Spring Brass (Cartridge Brass)** | Good electrical conductivity | Battery springs, electrical apparatus |
| **Monel, Inconel** | High-temperature, corrosion resistance | Jet engines, nuclear applications |
| **Rubber / Elastomers** | Vibration damping, silent operation | Mountings, couplings |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Spring Index Criterion (Why C = D/d and why it has a range)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU boards frequently ask: "Why is the spring index kept between 4 and 12?"** This derivation gives the analytical justification.

**Statement to derive:** The lower limit of $C$ comes from *manufacturing* constraints (wire too thick to bend); the upper limit comes from *clash with the inner wall (tangency)* of the spring when compressed.

**Step 1 — The Tangency (Clash) Condition:**

When a helical spring is compressed, the outer surface of one coil may come into contact with the inner surface of the adjacent coil. To prevent this *coil clash*, the radial distance between the inner faces of consecutive coils must be greater than zero.

The radial gap available is half the difference between the outer and inner diameters:
$$\text{Radial gap} = \frac{OD - ID}{2} = \frac{(D + d) - (D - d)}{2} = d$$

This means the wire diameter itself is the *available radial gap*. The axial separation between two adjacent coil centroids (on the wire-axis) is equal to the pitch $p$.

**Step 2 — Geometric Tilt of the Coil:**

As the spring deflects axially, the coil tilts. The angle of tilt is:
$$\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\frac{\delta}{N_a \cdot \pi D}\right)$$

This tilt causes the radial position of a point on the wire to shift. For *no clash* to occur at maximum deflection, the radial shift must be less than the available gap $d$.

**Step 3 — Empirical Design Rule (KTU Board Answer):**

Through extensive empirical analysis (Wahl, 1963), the safe range was established as:
$$4 \le C = \frac{D}{d} \le 12$$

with the **optimum sweet-spot** being:
$$C_{opt} = 6 \text{ to } 10$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for KTU boards:** Students should write the **practical reasoning** — "a low spring index makes the spring difficult to manufacture (small bend radius) and induces high torsional shear stress, while a high spring index makes the spring prone to buckling under axial compression and difficult to seat on a rod."

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Free Length Formula (General Case)

**Given:** A helical compression spring with $N_a$ active coils, $N_i$ inactive (end) coils, mean coil diameter $D$, wire diameter $d$, maximum working deflection $\delta_{max}$.

**Step 1 — Solid Length:**

When the spring is fully compressed (deflection = 0 residual), every coil — active and inactive — lies wire-on-wire:
$$L_s = N_t \cdot d = (N_a + N_i) \cdot d$$

**Step 2 — Free Length under zero load:**

The free length is the *solid length* plus the *axial gap between consecutive coils when uncompressed*:
$$L_f = L_s + N_a \cdot g$$

where $g$ is the gap between two adjacent active coils at free length.

**Step 3 — Relating Gap to Pitch:**

The pitch $p$ is the center-to-center axial distance between adjacent coils. Since each coil occupies $d$ of axial space, the gap is:
$$g = p - d$$

**Step 4 — The Final Free Length Formula:**

$$\boxed{L_f = (N_t) \cdot d + N_a \cdot (p - d)}$$

**Special case — Closed & Ground Ends:**

For closed and ground ends, $N_i = 2$ (one inactive coil at each end), so:
$$L_f = (N_a + 2) \cdot d + N_a \cdot (p - d)$$

$$L_f = N_a \cdot d + 2d + N_a \cdot p - N_a \cdot d$$

$$\boxed{L_f = 2d + N_a \cdot p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Trick:** "Closed and ground ends free length = $2d + N_a \cdot p$" — this is one of the most-asked formulae in PCMET602.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wahl's Correction Factor (Preview for Module 3 advanced problems)

Although this is a preview, the KTU board often tests the *consequence* of curvature on stress concentration.

**Step 1 — Shear Stress in a Straight Bar under Torsion:**
$$\tau = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$

**Step 2 — For a helical spring, the wire is curved, which causes:**

(a) **Direct shear stress** from the axial force $F$:
$$\tau_d = \frac{F}{\frac{\pi d^2}{4}} = \frac{4F}{\pi d^2}$$

(b) **Torsional shear stress** from the moment $T = F \cdot D/2$:
$$\tau_t = \frac{8 F D}{\pi d^3}$$

**Step 3 — Combined Maximum Shear Stress:**
$$\tau_{max} = \tau_d + \tau_t = \frac{4F}{\pi d^2} + \frac{8FD}{\pi d^3} = \frac{8FD}{\pi d^3}\left(\frac{1}{2} + \frac{d}{2D}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Stress concentration due to curvature (Wahl's Factor $K_w$):**
$$\boxed{K_w = \frac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \frac{0.615}{C}}$$

The corrected shear stress becomes:
$$\tau_{max} = K_w \cdot \frac{8 F D}{\pi d^3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU exam tip:** If a problem statement says "neglect the effect of curvature" or "for close-coiled spring" — use $\tau_{max} = \frac{8FD}{\pi d^3}$ directly. If it says "use Wahl's correction" — multiply by $K_w$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — End-Type vs. Geometry

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Cheat Matrix — End Types vs. Critical Parameters:**

| End Type | $N_t$ (Total Coils) | $L_s$ (Solid Length) | $L_f$ (Free Length, with pitch $p$) | $N_a$ (Active) |
|----------|---------------------|----------------------|--------------------------------------|----------------|
| Plain Ends | $N_a$ | $N_a \cdot d$ | $N_a \cdot d + N_a(p - d)$ | $N_a$ |
| Plain Ends Ground | $N_a + 1$ | $(N_a + 1)d$ | $(N_a + 1)d + N_a(p - d)$ | $N_a$ |
| Closed Ends | $N_a + 2$ | $(N_a + 2)d$ | $(N_a + 2)d + N_a(p - d)$ | $N_a$ |
| Closed & Ground Ends | $N_a + 2$ | $(N_a + 2)d$ | $2d + N_a \cdot p$ | $N_a$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Classification Tree (Spring Taxonomy)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SPRINGS] --> B[HELICAL SPRINGS]
    A --> C[NON-HELICAL SPRINGS]

    B --> B1[Compression Spring]
    B --> B2[Extension Spring]
    B --> B3[Torsion Spring]

    B1 --> B1a[Open Ends]
    B1 --> B1b[Closed Ends]
    B1 --> B1c[Closed and Ground Ends]

    C --> D[FLAT / LEAF SPRINGS]
    C --> E[SPIRAL SPRINGS]
    C --> F[CONICAL / VOLUTE SPRINGS]
    C --> G[DISC / BELLEVILLE SPRINGS]
    C --> H[TORSION BAR]
    C --> I[RUBBER SPRINGS]

    D --> D1[Semi-Elliptic]
    D --> D2[Full-Elliptic]
    D --> D3[Cantilever]

    B1a --> B1a1["Plain - free length formula: N_a d + N_a(p-d)"]
    B1b --> B1b1["Solid length: N_a d + 2d"]
    B1c --> B1c1["Closed and Ground - solid length: (N_a + 2)d"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Helical Compression Spring

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT[/Applied Axial Force F/] --> COIL[Active Coils N_a]
    COIL -->|Deflection delta| OUTPUT[Spring Rate k]

    COIL --> STRESS[Shear Stress tau]
    STRESS -->|Wahl Factor K_w| CORRECTED[Corrected Stress]

    GEOM[Geometric Parameters] --> COIL
    GEOM --> GEOM1["Wire dia d"]
    GEOM --> GEOM2["Mean dia D"]
    GEOM --> GEOM3["Spring Index C = D/d"]

    MAT[Material Property] --> COIL
    MAT --> MAT1["Shear Modulus G"]
    MAT --> MAT2["Ultimate Torsional Stress tau_u"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Leaf Spring Anatomy (Cantilever Type)

```mermaid
graph TD
    LS[LEAF SPRING ASSEMBLY] --> MA[Master Leaf / Longest Leaf]
    LS --> GR[Graduated Leaves]
    LS --> CL[Full-Length Leaves]
    LS --> BC[U-Bolts and Centre Clamp]

    MA --> EYE[Eye End]
    MA --> TIP[Free Tip]
    BC --> AXLE[Vehicle Axle]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Block — Spiral Spring Energy Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    WIND[External Winding Torque T] --> STRIP[Flat Steel Strip]
    STRIP --> STORE[Strain Energy U = 0.5 T theta]
    STORE -->|Release| DRIVE[Output Rotation]
```

### 4.5 Tabular Schematic — Comparison of Major Spring Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU board students must be able to draw or describe the *shape* and *loading* of each spring type. The following table is the master comparison.**

| Spring Type | Shape Sketch (ASCII) | Loading Direction | Wire Stress | Common Application |
|-------------|----------------------|-------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| Helical Compression | ⊃⊂ stacked coils | Axial Push | Torsion (Shear) | Vehicle suspension, valve |
| Helical Extension | ⊃⊂ coils with end hooks | Axial Pull | Torsion (Shear) | Garage door spring |
| Helical Torsion | ⊃⊂ coils with tangs | Pure Moment | Bending | Clothespins, balance |
| Leaf Spring | ∽ flat strips | Bending | Bending | Truck rear axle |
| Torsion Bar | ⌇━━ long cylinder | Torsion | Shear | Car front suspension |
| Belleville | ◣ conical washer | Axial Push | Combined | Pre-load devices |
| Spiral | @@@ flat spiral | Moment | Bending | Watch mainspring |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 3, 3 marks]**
*Define a spring. Mention any two functions of springs with a real-life example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A spring is a *resilient elastic machine element* that stores mechanical energy when subjected to a load (axial, torsional, or bending) and releases it upon unloading, without undergoing permanent plastic deformation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme (KTU):**
> - 1 mark: Definition (resilience + energy storage)
> - 1 mark: First function + example
> - 1 mark: Second function + example

**Two functions with examples:**

(i) **Shock Absorption** — *Example:* The helical compression springs used in the front suspension of a motorcycle absorb road shocks, preventing them from being transmitted to the rider.

(ii) **Energy Storage** — *Example:* The spiral mainspring in a mechanical wristwatch stores energy when wound and releases it slowly to drive the gear train.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 3, 3 marks]**
*Classify springs based on the type of load they carry. Sketch the shape of any two springs and name one application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Classification based on type of load:**

(i) **Tension/Compression Springs** — carry axial tensile or compressive loads. *Example:* Helical compression spring in IC engine valve train.

(ii) **Torsion Springs** — carry torsional (rotational) moments. *Example:* Helical torsion spring in a mousetrap.

(iii) **Bending Springs** — carry bending moments. *Example:* Leaf spring in a truck.

**Sketches:**

(i) *Helical Compression Spring* → a series of stacked circular coils loaded axially.

(ii) *Leaf Spring* → a graduated set of flat steel strips clamped at the centre, with semi-elliptical curvature.

**Application of helical compression spring:** Automotive suspension systems (passenger car front struts).

**Application of leaf spring:** Rear-axle suspension of heavy commercial trucks (Tata, Ashok Leyland).

---

### PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, KTU ESE Module-Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** A 14-mark question has TWO sub-parts, each carrying **7 marks**. Both sub-parts must be attempted. KTU allows internal choice — *the student picks ONE of two given questions.*

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3, 14 marks]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **helical compression spring**. Define the terms *spring index, spring rate, free length, solid length,* and *active coils*. Show the relationship between these parameters.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A helical compression spring with **closed and ground ends** has the following specifications:
- Wire diameter $d = 6 \text{ mm}$
- Mean coil diameter $D = 60 \text{ mm}$
- Number of active coils $N_a = 10$
- Maximum working load $F_{max} = 250 \text{ N}$
- Material: oil-tempered wire with allowable shear stress $\tau = 450 \text{ MPa}$

Compute: (i) Spring index, (ii) Solid length, (iii) Free length assuming a pitch of $12 \text{ mm}$, (iv) Spring rate, (v) Maximum deflection.

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

A **helical compression spring** is a closely/coiled wire wound in a helical shape, designed to resist axial compressive loads. When a force $F$ is applied along the spring axis, the coils deform (close together) by an amount $\delta$ and store strain energy. On removal of the load, the spring returns to its free length.

**Key definitions (each 1 mark):**

1. **Spring Index ($C$):** $C = D / d$ — ratio of mean coil diameter to wire diameter.

2. **Spring Rate ($k$):** $k = F / \delta$ — load required per unit deflection (N/mm).

3. **Free Length ($L_f$):** Length of the spring when no load is applied.

4. **Solid Length ($L_s$):** Length when the spring is fully compressed, i.e., all coils wire-on-wire.

5. **Active Coils ($N_a$):** Coils that actually deflect under load.

**Relationship:**
$$L_f = L_s + N_a \cdot (p - d) = (N_t) \cdot d + N_a \cdot (p - d)$$

For *closed and ground ends*:
$$L_f = 2d + N_a \cdot p$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks: Step-by-Step Numerical Solution**

**Given Data:**
- $d = 6 \text{ mm}$
- $D = 60 \text{ mm}$
- $N_a = 10$ (active coils)
- $F_{max} = 250 \text{ N}$
- $\tau_{allowable} = 450 \text{ MPa}$ (note: this is not used here — provided as a distractor)
- $p = 12 \text{ mm}$
- Closed and ground ends → $N_t = N_a + 2 = 12$

**(i) Spring Index (1 mark):**
$$C = \frac{D}{d} = \frac{60}{6} = 10$$

> *Check:* $C = 10$ lies in the ideal range of 6–10. ✓

**(ii) Solid Length (1 mark):**
$$L_s = N_t \cdot d = 12 \times 6 = 72 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Free Length (2 marks):**

Using the closed-and-ground formula:
$$L_f = 2d + N_a \cdot p = 2(6) + 10(12) = 12 + 120 = 132 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Spring Rate (1.5 marks):**

Using $G = 80 \text{ GPa} = 80 \times 10^3 \text{ MPa} = 80 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$:
$$k = \frac{G \cdot d^4}{8 \cdot D^3 \cdot N_a} = \frac{80 \times 10^3 \times (6)^4}{8 \times (60)^3 \times 10}$$

$$k = \frac{80 \times 10^3 \times 1296}{8 \times 216000 \times 10} = \frac{1.0368 \times 10^8}{1.728 \times 10^7}$$

$$k = 6.0 \text{ N/mm}$$

**(v) Maximum Deflection (1.5 marks):**
$$\delta_{max} = \frac{F_{max}}{k} = \frac{250}{6.0} = 41.67 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification Check (Important for KTU marks):**
> $\delta_{max} + L_s = 41.67 + 72 = 113.67 \text{ mm} \le L_f = 132 \text{ mm}$ ✓
> If this condition fails, the spring would *bottom out* (go solid) before reaching maximum load — an invalid design.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE ALTERNATIVE

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3, 14 marks]**

**(a)** Classify springs in detail, listing the major categories with neat sketches. Explain the difference between **helical compression** and **helical extension** springs with their end configurations.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A helical extension spring is made of stainless-steel wire ($G = 77 \text{ GPa}$) with:
- Wire diameter $d = 4 \text{ mm}$
- Mean coil diameter $D = 30 \text{ mm}$
- Active coils $N_a = 20$
- Initial tension $F_0 = 30 \text{ N}$

Determine: (i) Spring rate, (ii) Force required to produce a deflection of $25 \text{ mm}$, (iii) Maximum shear stress (without Wahl's correction), (iv) Wahl's correction factor, (v) Maximum shear stress *with* Wahl's correction.

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

[Classification diagram — see Mermaid tree in Section 4.1]

**Key differences between Helical Compression and Helical Extension springs:**

| Parameter | Compression | Extension |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| End Type | Open / Closed / Ground | Hooks, loops, coned |
| Free Length | Lags of pitch between coils | Coils touch each other (initial tension) |
| Load Direction | Axial Push | Axial Pull |
| Application | Vehicle suspension, valves | Garage doors, trampolines |

**End Configurations of Extension Springs:** Full loop, half loop, machine hook, coned end (with side loop).

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks: Step-by-Step Numerical Solution**

**Given Data:**
- $G = 77 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- $d = 4 \text{ mm}$
- $D = 30 \text{ mm}$
- $N_a = 20$
- $F_0 = 30 \text{ N}$
