**Conclusion:** The endurance limit of the actual machined component is only $86.8\%$ of that of a polished specimen.

### 3.5 Derivation: Endurance Limit for a Rotating Bending Shaft (Full Marin Procedure)

**Statement:** A shaft of $d = 50$ mm diameter is made of steel with $S_{ut} = 650$ MPa. It is machined, has $99\%$ reliability, operates at $50^{\circ}$C, and has a shoulder fillet with $K_t = 2.0$ and $q = 0.9$. Find the modified endurance limit.

**Step 1 — Unmodified endurance limit:**

$$
S_e^{\prime} = 0.5 \times 650 = 325 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Surface factor (machined):**

$$
k_a = 4.51 \times 650^{-0.265} = 4.51 \times \exp(-0.265 \times \ln 650)
$$

$$
k_a = 4.51 \times \exp(-0.265 \times 6.4769) = 4.51 \times \exp(-1.7164) = 4.51 \times 0.1797 = 0.810
$$

**Step 3 — Size factor for rotating bending, $2.79 \leq d \leq 51$ mm:**

$$
k_b = \left(\frac{d}{7.62}\right)^{-0.107} = \left(\frac{50}{7.62}\right)^{-0.107} = (6.5617)^{-0.107}
$$

$$
k_b = \exp(-0.107 \times \ln 6.5617) = \exp(-0.107 \times 1.8799) = \exp(-0.2012) = 0.818
$$

**Step 4 — Load factor for bending:**

$$
k_c = 0.85
$$

**Step 5 — Temperature factor at 50°C (negligible deviation):**

$$
k_d = 1.0
$$

**Step 6 — Reliability factor at 99\%:**

$$
k_e = 0.814
$$

**Step 7 — Miscellaneous factor (typical):**

$$
k_f = 1.0
$$

**Step 8 — Final endurance limit:**

$$
S_e = 0.810 \times 0.818 \times 0.85 \times 1.0 \times 0.814 \times 1.0 \times 325
$$

$$
S_e = 0.4568 \times 325 = 148.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 9 — Fatigue stress concentration factor:**

$$
K_f = 1 + 0.9(2.0 - 1) = 1.9
$$

**Step 10 — Effective endurance limit (incorporating $K_f$):**

$$
S_{e,eff} = \frac{S_e}{K_f} = \frac{148.5}{1.9} = 78.2 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This $S_{e,eff}$ is the value to be entered directly into the **Goodman** or **Soderberg** line for subsequent design checks. KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** if $K_f$ is omitted.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Fatigue Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[STEP 0: IDENTIFY LOAD TYPE] --> B0{STATIC OR FATIGUE?}
    B0 -- STATIC --> C0[APPLY SIMPLE STATIC FACTOR OF SAFETY]
    B0 -- FATIGUE --> C1[STEP 1: COMPUTE SIGMA_MAX AND SIGMA_MIN FROM LOADS]
    C1 --> C2[STEP 2: COMPUTE SIGMA_M AND SIGMA_A AND R]
    C2 --> C3[STEP 3: OBTAIN S_UT FROM MATERIAL DATA]
    C3 --> C4[STEP 4: COMPUTE UNMODIFIED S_E_DASH EQUAL TO 0.5 TIMES S_UT]
    C4 --> C5[STEP 5: APPLY MARIN FACTORS KA KB KC KD KE KF]
    C5 --> D0[STEP 6: COMPUTE K_F FROM K_T AND Q]
    D0 --> E0[STEP 7: SELECT FAILURE CRITERION]
    E0 --> F0[SODERBERG]
    E0 --> F1[GOODMAN]
    E0 --> F2[GERBER]
    E0 --> F3[ASME ELLIPTIC]
    F0 --> G0[STEP 8: SOLVE FOR DIAMETER OR FACTOR OF SAFETY]
    F1 --> G0
    F2 --> G0
    F3 --> G0
    G0 --> H0[STEP 9: VERIFY DEFLECTION AND STATIC YIELD CHECKS]
    H0 --> I0[FINAL DESIGN REPORT]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Three Canonical Fatigue Stress Cycles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[FULLY REVERSED CYCLE] -- R EQUALS NEGATIVE 1 --> A2[SIGMA_M EQUALS 0]
    A2 --> A3[SIGMA_A EQUALS SIGMA_MAX]
    B1[REPEATED OR PULSATING CYCLE] -- R EQUALS 0 --> B2[SIGMA_MIN EQUALS 0]
    B2 --> B3[SIGMA_M EQUALS SIGMA_A EQUALS SIGMA_MAX BY 2]
    C1[FLUCTUATING CYCLE] -- R BETWEEN NEGATIVE 1 AND POSITIVE 1 --> C2[SIGMA_M NON ZERO]
    C2 --> C3[SIGMA_A LESS THAN SIGMA_MAX]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — S-N Curve Topographical Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[S_N CURVE FOR FERROUS METAL] --> P1[LOW CYCLE REGION LESS THAN 10 TO 3]
    P0 --> P2[HIGH CYCLE REGION 10 TO 3 TO 10 TO 6]
    P0 --> P3[ENDURANCE REGION GREATER THAN 10 TO 6]
    P1 --> P11[PLASTIC STRAIN DOMINATES COFFIN MANSON LAW]
    P2 --> P21[ELASTIC STRAIN DOMINATES BASQUIN LAW]
    P3 --> P31[HORIZONTAL ASYMPTOTE AT S_E]
    P21 --> P4[SIGMA_A EQUALS SIGMA_F_PRIME TIMES 2N RAISED TO B]
    P31 --> P5[S_E EQUAL TO 0.5 TIMES S_UT FOR STEEL]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Failure Envelope Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X0[FAILURE LOCI IN SIGMA_M SIGMA_A PLANE] --> X1[GERBER PARABOLA LEAST CONSERVATIVE]
    X0 --> X2[GOODMAN STRAIGHT LINE WIDELY USED]
    X0 --> X3[SODERBERG STRAIGHT LINE MOST CONSERVATIVE]
    X0 --> X4[ASME ELLIPTIC FOR BRITTLE]
    X1 -- DUCTILE STEELS HIGH MEAN --> Y0[USE GERBER]
    X2 -- DEFAULT KTU CHOICE --> Y1[USE GOODMAN]
    X3 -- YIELD SAFETY CRITICAL --> Y2[USE SODERBERG]
    X4 -- CAST IRON CERAMICS --> Y3[USE ASME ELLIPTIC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The four diagrams above provide the **complete cognitive map** required by KTU Module 3. Students are advised to **memorize the step-numbering in Diagram 4.1** as it is exactly the order in which KTU questions are posed and answered.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer, Direct Concept)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term **fluctuation ratio**. A rotating shaft experiences a maximum bending stress of $80$ MPa and a minimum bending stress of $-30$ MPa. Compute the fluctuation ratio and identify the type of stress cycle.

**Model Answer:**

The fluctuation ratio is defined as the algebraic ratio of the minimum stress to the maximum stress in a single stress cycle:

$$
r = \frac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}
$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

$$
r = \frac{-30}{80} = -0.375
$$

Since $-1 < r < 0$, the cycle is a **fluctuating stress cycle** with both mean and alternating components present. (Mean $= 25$ MPa, Amplitude $= 55$ MPa.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the **Modified Goodman Criterion** for fatigue failure of a machine element subjected to a combined steady and fluctuating load. What is its significance in design?

**Model Answer:**

The Modified Goodman Criterion states that a component is safe against fatigue if:

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}
$$

where $n$ is the factor of safety, $S_e$ is the modified endurance limit, and $S_{ut}$ is the ultimate tensile strength.

**Significance:**
- It is a **linear** failure locus, making hand calculations simple.
- It is **conservative** for ductile metals, providing a wide margin of safety.
- It is the **default criterion in KTU board examinations** for shaft and spring design.

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option for the Candidate

**Q3(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
A steel shaft of diameter $60$ mm is subjected to a fluctuating torque that varies from $+800$ N·m to $-200$ N·m and a fluctuating bending moment from $+1200$ N·m to $+400$ N·m. The material has $S_{ut} = 700$ MPa, $S_y = 500$ MPa, and a corrected endurance limit $S_e = 280$ MPa. The stress concentration factors are $K_{tt} = 1.6$ (torsion) and $K_{tb} = 1.9$ (bending), and notch sensitivities are $q_s = 0.85$ and $q_b = 0.9$. Apply the **Modified Goodman criterion** to find the factor of safety.

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the polar section modulus and bending section modulus:**

$$
Z_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{16} = \frac{\pi (60)^3}{16} = \frac{\pi \times 216000}{16} = 42411.5 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
Z_b = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} = 21205.75 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean and alternating stresses (separate for bending and torsion):**

*Bending:*

$$
\sigma_{m,b} = \frac{32 M_{m}}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times (1200+400)/2 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 60^3} = \frac{32 \times 800 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 216000}
$$

$$
\sigma_{m,b} = \frac{25.6 \times 10^6}{678584} = 37.72 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_{a,b} = \frac{32 \times (1200-400)/2 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 60^3} = \frac{32 \times 400 \times 10^3}{678584} = 18.86 \text{ MPa}
$$

*Torsion:*

$$
\tau_{m,t} = \frac{16 T_{m}}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times (800+(-200))/2 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 60^3} = \frac{16 \times 300 \times 10^3}{678584} = 7.07 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\tau_{a,t} = \frac{16 \times (800-(-200))/2 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 60^3} = \frac{16 \times 500 \times 10^3}{678584} = 11.79 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the fatigue stress concentration factors:**

For bending:

$$
K_{fb} = 1 + 0.9(1.9 - 1) = 1 + 0.9 \times 0.9 = 1.81
$$

For torsion:

$$
K_{ft} = 1 + 0.85(1.6 - 1) = 1 + 0.85 \times 0.6 = 1.51
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the mean stress corrections (DE-Goodman or equivalent):**
For combined steady and fluctuating loading on a rotating shaft, the equivalent von Mises stresses are:

$$
\sigma_{a,eq} = \sqrt{(\sigma_{a,b} K_{fb})^2 + 3(\tau_{a,t} K_{ft})^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{a,eq} = \sqrt{(18.86 \times 1.81)^2 + 3(11.79 \times 1.51)^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{a,eq} = \sqrt{(34.14)^2 + 3(17.80)^2} = \sqrt{1165.5 + 950.7} = \sqrt{2116.2} = 46.0 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_{m,eq} = \sqrt{(\sigma_{m,b})^2 + 3(\tau_{m,t})^2} = \sqrt{(37.72)^2 + 3(7.07)^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{m,eq} = \sqrt{1422.8 + 150.0} = \sqrt{1572.8} = 39.66 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Modified Goodman equation:**

$$
\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_{a,eq}}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_{m,eq}}{S_{ut}}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{n} = \frac{46.0}{280} + \frac{39.66}{700} = 0.1643 + 0.0567 = 0.2210
$$

$$
\boxed{n = 4.52}
$$

**Step 6 — Static yield check (DE-St. Venant):**

$$
\sigma_{max,eq} = \sqrt{(\sigma_{m,b} K_{fb} + \sigma_{a,b} K_{fb})^2 + 3(\tau_{m,t} K_{ft} + \tau_{a,t} K_{ft})^2}
$$

Without the $K_f$ at the static peak (it is the same factor):

$$
\sigma_{max,eq} = \sqrt{(34.14 + 34.14)^2 + 3(10.68 + 17.80)^2} = \sqrt{(68.28)^2 + 3(28.48)^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max,eq} = \sqrt{4662.2 + 2433.3} = \sqrt{7095.5} = 84.23 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
n_y = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_{max,eq}} = \frac{500}{84.23} = 5.94
$$

**Final Factor of Safety is the lesser of the two: $n = 4.52$ (governed by fatigue).**

---

**Q3(b). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Explain the **S-N curve** and state its significance in fatigue design. A specimen of steel has an ultimate tensile strength of $600$ MPa and endurance limit of $300$ MPa. The fatigue strength at $10^4$ cycles is $500$ MPa. Find the fatigue strength at $10^5$ cycles using **Basquin's Law**.

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Conceptual explanation of the S-N curve:**
The S-N curve, also known as the **Wöhler curve**, is a plot of the **alternating stress amplitude $\sigma_a$** (ordinate, log scale) versus the **number of cycles to failure $N$** (abscissa, log scale). It has three regions: low-cycle, high-cycle, and endurance. For steels, the curve becomes horizontal beyond $10^6$ cycles, defining the **endurance limit $S_e$**. Significance: it allows the designer to predict component life under cyclic loading without expensive testing.

**Step 2 — Basquin's Law:**

$$
\sigma_a = \sigma_f^{\prime} (2N)^b
$$

**Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions to find the exponent $b$ and coefficient $\sigma_f^{\prime}$:**

At $N = 10^6$ (endurance), $\sigma_a = S_e = 300$ MPa:

$$
300 = \sigma_f^{\prime} (2 \times 10^6)^b \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

At $N = 10^4$, $\sigma_a = 500$ MPa:

$$
500 = \sigma_f^{\prime} (2 \times 10^4)^b \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide (i) by (ii) to eliminate $\sigma_f^{\prime}$:**

$$
\frac{300}{500} = \left(\frac{2 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^4}\right)^b
$$

$$
0.6 = (100)^b
$$

Take natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln(0.6) = b \ln(100)
$$

$$
b = \frac{\ln(0.6)}{\ln(100)} = \frac{-0.5108}{4.6052} = -0.1109
$$

**Step 5 — Find $\sigma_f^{\prime}$ from equation (i):**

$$
\sigma_f^{\prime} = \frac{300}{(2 \times 10^6)^{-0.1109}} = 300 \times (2 \times 10^6)^{0.1109}
$$

Compute the power:

$$
(2 \times 10^6)^{0.1109} = \exp(0.1109 \times \ln(2 \times 10^6)) = \exp(0.1109 \times 14.5087) = \exp(1.6090) = 5.0
$$

**Wait — verification:** $(2 \times 10^6)^{0.1109} \approx 5.0$ is correct, so:

$$
\sigma_f^{\prime} = 300 \times 5.0 = 1500 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\sigma_a$ at $N = 10^5$:**

$$
\sigma_a = 1500 \times (2 \times 10^5)^{-0.1109}
$$

$$
(2 \times 10^5)^{-0.1109} = \exp(-0.1109 \times \ln(2 \times 10^5)) = \exp(-0.1109 \times 12.2061) = \exp(-1.3537) = 0.2585
$$

$$
\sigma_a = 1500 \times 0.2585 = 387.7 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_a \approx 388 \text{ MPa at } 10^5 \text{ cycles}}
$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice for the Candidate

**Q4(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
A machine component is made of steel with $S_{ut} = 540$ MPa, $S_y = 380$ MPa, and $S_e^{\prime} = 270$ MPa. The component is machined, has a diameter of $40$ mm, operates at room temperature, has $90\%$ reliability, and experiences fully reversed bending. Determine the **modified endurance limit $S_e$** using **Marin's method**, given the appropriate surface, size, load, temperature, reliability, and miscellaneous factors.

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Unmodified endurance limit:**

$$
S_e^{\prime} = 0.5 \times 540 = 270 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Surface factor $k_a$ (machined):**

$$
k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^{b} = 4.51 \times 540^{-0.265}
$$

Compute the exponent:

$$
540^{-0.265} = \exp(-0.265 \ln 540) = \exp(-0.265 \times 6.2914) = \exp(-1.6672) = 0.1887
$$

$$
k_a = 4.51 \times 0.1887 = 0.851
$$

**Step 3 — Size factor $k_b$ for rotating bending, $2.79 \leq d \leq 51$ mm:**

$$
k_b = \left(\frac{d}{7.62}\right)^{-0.107} = \left(\frac{40}{7.62}\right)^{-0.107} = (5.2493)^{-0.107}
$$

$$
k_b = \exp(-0.107 \times \ln 5.2493) = \exp(-0.107 \times 1.6582) = \exp(-0.1774) = 0.838
$$

**Step 4 — Load factor $k_c$ for bending:**

$$
k_c = 0.85
$$

**Step 5 — Temperature factor $k_d$ at room temperature (≤ 50°C):**

$$
k_d = 1.0
$$

**Step 6 — Reliability factor $k_e$ for 90%:**

$$
k_e = 0.897
$$

**Step 7 — Miscellaneous factor $k_f$ (no corrosion, no plating):**

$$
k_f = 1.0
$$

**Step 8 — Modified endurance limit:**

$$
S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S_e^{\prime}
$$

$$
S_e = 0.851 \times 0.838 \times 0.85 \times 1.0 \times 0.897 \times 1.0 \times 270
$$

$$
S_e = 0.5445 \times 270 = 147.0 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_e \approx 147 \text{ MPa}}
$$

---

**Q4(b). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
A circular shaft is subjected to a fluctuating bending moment that varies from $M_{max} = 1.5$ kN·m to $M_{min} = 0.5$ kN·m. The shaft diameter is $50$ mm, and the material has $S_{ut} = 620$ MPa, $S_{ut} = 620$ MPa, and $S_e = 200$ MPa. Using the **Soderberg criterion**, determine the factor of safety. State whether the design is safe if the desired factor of safety is $1.8$.

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the bending section modulus:**

$$
Z = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 50^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 125000}{32} = 12271.85 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$:**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{32 M_{max}}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 1.5 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 125000} = \frac{48 \times 10^6}{392699} = 122.23 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_{min} = \frac{32 M_{min}}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 0.5 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 125000} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{392699} = 40.74 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute mean and alternating stresses:**

$$
\sigma_m = \frac{122.23 + 40.74}{2} = 81.49 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_a = \frac{122.23 - 40.74}{2} = 40.74 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Soderberg criterion:**

$$
\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y}
$$

Substitute $S_y = 0.6 \times 620 = 372$ MPa (approximate; if not given in the problem, use the ratio 0.6 for steels):

$$
\frac{1}{n} = \frac{40.74}{200} + \frac{81.49}{372} = 0.2037 + 0.2191 = 0.4228
$$

$$
n = 2.365
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with desired factor of safety:**

Since $n_{actual} = 2.365 > n_{desired} = 1.8$, the design is **SAFE**.

**Step 6 — Static yield check (always verify):**

$$
\sigma_{max,actual} = 122.23 \text{ MPa}, \quad n_y = \frac{372}{122.23} = 3.04
$$

The static yield factor of safety is also $> 1.8$, so the design is safe under both criteria.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark-Loss Areas):**
> 1. **Forgetting $K_f$ in alternating stress:** When $K_t$ and $q$ are given, students frequently use $K_t$ directly instead of $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$. **Deduction: 1 to 2 marks.**
> 2. **Mixing up Soderberg and Goodman $S_y$ vs $S_{ut}$:** Soderberg uses $S_y$ in the mean-stress term; Goodman uses $S_{ut}$. Interchanging them **costs 1 full mark**.
> 3. **Not converting units:** $M$ in N·m and $d$ in mm produces $\sigma$ in MPa directly only if $32 M / (\pi d^3)$ is multiplied by $10^3$ (i.e., the $10^6$ from N·m is divided correctly). **Deduction: 0.5 to 1 mark.**
> 4. **Omitting the static yield check:** KTU module-internal choice questions **always** expect a final verification of $\sigma_{max} \leq S_y / n$. **Deduction: 1 mark** if missing.
> 5. **Forgetting von Mises combination:** When both bending and torsion are present, students often add $\sigma$ and $\tau$ algebraically. The correct procedure is to combine via $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$. **Deduction: 2 marks.**

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Static and Fatigue Loading**

- [ ] **Static Loading** — time-invariant force; failure by single application; governed by **factor of safety on yield**.
- [ ] **Fatigue Loading** — time-varying stress; failure after $N$ cycles; governed by **endurance limit** ($N \geq 10^6$).
- [ ] **Three Stress Cycle Types** — fully reversed ($r = -1$), pulsating/repeated ($r = 0$), fluctuating ($-1 < r < 1$).
- [ ] **Core Parameters** — $r$, $\sigma_m$, $\sigma_a$, $\sigma_r$ — **compute first, every time**.
- [ ] **S-N Curve (Wöhler)** — log-log plot; Basquin's law $\sigma_a = \sigma_f^{\prime} (2N)^b$ for the sloped region.
- [ ] **Endurance Limit Formula** — $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \cdot 0.5 S_{ut}$ — apply **all six** Marin factors.
- [ ] **Surface Factor** — depends on $S_{ut}$ and surface finish; **machined, ground, polished** give different constants.
- [ ] **Size Factor** — $k_b = (d/7.62)^{-0.107}$ for round, rotating, $2.79 \leq d \leq 51$ mm; different for $d > 51$ mm.
- [ ] **Reliability Factor** — 1.0 at 50%, 0.897 at 90%, 0.814 at 99%, 0.753 at 99.9%.
- [ ] **Fatigue Stress Concentration** — $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ — always required for **discontinuities**.
- [ ] **Notch Sensitivity $q$** — depends on notch radius and $S_{ut}$; default $0.85$ for ductile steel.
- [ ] **Goodman** — most common in KTU, linear, conservative; **default choice**.
- [ ] **Soderberg** — most conservative; used when yielding is the controlling concern.
- [ ] **Gerber** — parabolic, least conservative, used for ductile metals with high $\sigma_m$.
- [ ] **ASME-Elliptic** — for brittle materials (cast iron, ceramics).
- [ ] **Von Mises Combination** — when both bending and torsion are present: $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$.
- [ ] **Design Equation Workflow** — compute $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m$ → multiply by $K_f$ → choose failure locus → solve for $n$ or $d$ → **static yield check mandatory**.
- [ ] **Static Yield Check** — $\sigma_{max} \leq S_y / n$ — must be satisfied **in addition to** the fatigue criterion.
- [ ] **Mean Stress on Endurance** — higher $\sigma_m$ **lowers** allowable $\sigma_a$ (slopes of all four loci fall from upper left to lower right).
- [ ] **In KTU, the question always begins with the load history and ends with a factor of safety or diameter.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surging
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Surging

# Surging in Centrifugal Machines: A Foundational Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 3):** This topic falls under the design and performance study of rotating fluid machinery, specifically turbomachines (centrifugal pumps and compressors). It is a high-weightage concept frequently tested in **Part A (3 marks)** as a short-answer question and often integrated into **Part B (14 marks)** problems involving characteristic curves.

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition**
> **Surging** is a self-excited, unstable, large-amplitude axial oscillation of the working fluid that occurs in a turbomachine (centrifugal pump or compressor) when the operating point falls below a critical minimum flow rate known as the **surge limit**. It is characterized by periodic, complete, or partial reversal of flow through the impeller, accompanied by violent pressure pulsations, mechanical vibration, and a characteristic low-frequency surging sound.

In engineering thermodynamics and fluid mechanics, surging is classified as an **aerodynamic/hydrodynamic instability** of the entire fluid–machine–pipeline system. It is not a property of the machine alone; it is a coupled system-level phenomenon.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy (The "Hose Kink" Model)

Imagine you are watering a garden with a hose, and you press your thumb hard against the nozzle to restrict the flow.

- **Normal operation:** Water flows smoothly, even if restricted.
- **As you restrict more:** The pressure inside the hose builds up.
- **At a critical point (surge limit):** The flow momentarily **stops, reverses, and re-establishes** itself in a periodic cycle. You hear a "whop-whop-whop" sound, and the hose vibrates violently in your hand.

This cyclic **build-up of pressure → flow reversal → pressure release → re-acceleration** is precisely what happens inside a centrifugal pump or compressor when it surges. The hose is the delivery pipe, your thumb is the system resistance (throttling valve), and the hose vibration is the mechanical consequence of surging.

### 1.2 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Typical Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Surge Limit** | The minimum stable flow rate below which surging begins | Often given as a percentage of BEP (Best Efficiency Point) flow, e.g., **60–80% of Q_BEP** |
| **Surge Margin** | Safety buffer above the surge limit at design operation | Expressed as $\Delta Q_{surge} \, (\%)$ |
| **BEP** | Best Efficiency Point – the design flow rate for optimal performance | $Q_{BEP}, H_{BEP}$ |
| **Anti-Surge Valve** | A recycle/relief valve that maintains flow above the surge limit | Opening pressure: typically 105–110% of design |
| **Frequency of Surging** | The cyclic rate of pressure pulsation | **0.5 Hz to 5 Hz** (very low frequency) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU Machine Design, remember that surging is fundamentally a **system instability**, not a component failure. Therefore, the design mitigation is also **system-level** (e.g., bypass lines, anti-surge control loops), not just machine-level.

---

## 2. Physical Origin: Why Does Surging Occur?

Surging arises from a **positive feedback loop** between the pump and the piping system. The mechanism can be broken down into a four-stage cycle:

1. **Flow Reduction:** The user (or process) demands less flow than the surge limit, e.g., by closing a discharge valve.
2. **Pressure Build-up:** The impeller continues to deliver energy, but the fluid has nowhere to go. Static pressure at the impeller exit rises sharply.
3. **Flow Reversal:** The high back-pressure exceeds the impeller's delivery head. The fluid column in the discharge pipe decelerates, stops, and is **pushed backward** through the impeller.
4. **Recovery & Repeat:** As backflow relieves the pressure, the impeller regains the ability to pump forward again, and the cycle repeats (often every 0.2 to 2 seconds).

### 2.1 The H-Q Characteristic and the Surge Line

Every centrifugal machine has a **Head vs. Flow (H-Q) curve** that is typically **downward sloping** (head decreases as flow increases). The **system resistance curve** is a **parabola** ($H \propto Q^2$). The operating point is their intersection.

- **Normal Region:** Operating point is on the **positive-slope** portion of the combined (machine + system) curve. Stable.
- **Surge Region:** The system curve intersects the machine curve at a point where the slope of the machine curve is **steeper than the slope of the system curve**. This is the condition for instability, derived from the **Greitzer Stability Criterion** (covered in Section 3).

### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Consequences

If surging is left unchecked, the consequences are severe:

- **Mechanical:** Catastrophic bearing failure, shaft deflection, impeller cracking, coupling damage.
- **Thermal:** Compressors experience rapid cycling of temperature, leading to thermal fatigue of blades.
- **Process:** The entire fluid delivery system experiences damaging pressure transients.
- **Acoustic:** A loud, low-frequency "moaning" or "pumping" noise that is unmistakable to plant operators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application:** In oil & gas, LNG plants, and aerospace (jet engine compressors), anti-surge systems are **mandatory safety systems**. In aerospace, a compressor surge in a jet engine at high altitude can cause **flameout** — a complete loss of thrust.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. The Mathematical Foundation: Greitzer's B Parameter

The most accepted theoretical framework for surge analysis is **Greitzer's lumped-parameter model** (1976). It treats the compressor and the connected duct as a single oscillating system governed by acoustic and fluid inertia effects.

### 1.1 Greitzer's Compressible Flow Model

For a compressor connected to a plenum (receiver) of volume $V$, the governing equations of the lumped system are:

$$
\frac{dp_0}{dt} = \frac{a^2}{V} (Q_C - Q_T)
$$

$$
\frac{dQ_T}{dt} = \frac{A_p}{L}(p_0 - p_s)
$$

Where:
- $p_0$ = Plenum (receiver) pressure
- $p_s$ = Discharge (system) pressure
- $Q_C$ = Compressor flow
- $Q_T$ = Throttle (system) flow
- $a$ = Speed of sound in the fluid
- $V$ = Plenum volume
- $A_p$ = Pipe cross-sectional area
- $L$ = Effective length of the discharge duct

### 1.2 The Dimensionless Surge Predictor: B-Parameter

Greitzer defined a **dimensionless parameter B** that predicts the susceptibility of a system to surge:

$$
B = \frac{U}{2a} \sqrt{\frac{V}{A_p L}}
$$

Where $U$ is the impeller tip speed. The **surge criterion** is:

$$
B < B_{critical} \approx 0.7
$$

- If **B > 0.7** (small plenum, short duct, high speed): The system is **rotating stall-dominated**.
- If **B < 0.7** (large plenum, long duct, low speed): The system is **surge-dominated**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU:** While the B-parameter is an advanced concept, students should at minimum be able to **state the condition for surge onset** and explain that the surge limit is reached when the **slope of the H-Q curve equals the slope of the system curve**.

## 2. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pump / Compressor Head (general) | $H = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\rho g}$ | meters (m) of fluid column |
| 2 | System Resistance Curve (friction-dominated) | $H_{sys} = K \cdot Q^2$ | $K$ = resistance coefficient |
| 3 | Operating Point (steady state) | $H_{pump}(Q) = H_{sys}(Q)$ | Graphical or numerical solution |
| 4 | Surge Condition (slope criterion) | $\left\vert \dfrac{dH_{pump}}{dQ} \right\vert > \left\vert \dfrac{dH_{sys}}{dQ} \right\vert$ | At the operating point |
| 5 | Greitzer B-Parameter | $B = \dfrac{U}{2a}\sqrt{\dfrac{V}{A_p L}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Surge Stability Criterion | $B < 0.7$ → Surge regime | Lumped parameter model |
| 7 | Specific Speed (for surge-related design) | $N_s = N\sqrt{Q}/H^{3/4}$ | Revolutions per minute (rpm) |
| 8 | Surge Margin (percentage) | $SM = \dfrac{Q_{op} - Q_{surge}}{Q_{op}} \times 100$ | Typical safe value: **> 10%** |
| 9 | Anti-Surge Recycle Ratio | $R = \dfrac{Q_{recycle}}{Q_{discharge}}$ | Often 0.1 to 0.3 in design |
| 10 | Surge Cycle Frequency (empirical) | $f_{surge} \approx \dfrac{a}{2\pi L}$ | Low frequency, 0.5 to 5 Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for Tables:** Throughout this sheet, the absolute value and magnitude bars are rendered using `\vert` (e.g., $\vert dH/dQ \vert$). This prevents markdown table syntax conflicts. Students writing answer sheets should write "absolute value of dH/dQ" in words to avoid handwriting ambiguity.

## 3. Anti-Surging Design Strategies (KTU High-Yield)

In Machine Design, you may be asked to **suggest methods to prevent surge**. The standard mitigations are:

1. **Bypass (Recycle) Line:** A pipe from the discharge back to the suction, with an **anti-surge valve** that opens when flow drops below the surge limit. This artificially increases the flow through the machine without increasing the user's demand.
2. **Variable Speed Drive (VSD):** Reducing the impeller speed shifts the entire H-Q curve downward, lowering the surge limit. This is the most energy-efficient method.
3. **Inlet Guide Vane (IGV) Throttling:** Closing the IGVs reduces the flow capacity of the impeller, matching it to the new operating point and avoiding the surge zone.
4. **Surge Tank / Plenum Sizing:** Increasing the volume $V$ of the receiver changes the B-parameter. **Larger $V$ lowers B**, but the relationship is non-linear; design must follow Greitzer's analysis.
5. **Operating Procedure:** Operators are trained to **never throttle the discharge** to a flow below the marked surge line on the performance curve.

### 3.1 Why This Matters in KTU Board Exams

A common KTU 2024 question form is:

> *"A centrifugal pump is to deliver water at a head of 30 m. The proposed operating point is found to be on the unstable portion of the H-Q curve. Suggest two design modifications to ensure stable operation."* **[7 Marks]**

The expected answer should reference the **slope criterion**, the **surge limit**, and propose at least one **system-level** mitigation (e.g., bypass line or VSD).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 1. Derivation 1: The Slope Criterion for Surge Onset

We derive the condition under which a small perturbation in flow rate grows unstably, leading to surge.

### 1.1 System Setup

Consider a centrifugal pump delivering fluid to a piping system. The system is at a steady operating point $(Q_0, H_0)$ where:

$$
H_{pump}(Q_0) = H_{sys}(Q_0)
$$

A small disturbance $\delta Q$ occurs. We linearize the system around the operating point.

### 1.2 Linearized Governing Equation

The instantaneous head mismatch acts as the restoring (or destabilizing) force. After linearization of the unsteady momentum equation for the fluid in the pipe, the dynamic equation for the flow perturbation is:

$$
L \rho A_p \frac{d^2 (\delta Q)}{dt^2} = A_p \left[ H_{pump}(Q_0 + \delta Q) - H_{sys}(Q_0 + \delta Q) \right] \cdot \rho g
$$

Dividing by $A_p \rho g$ and expanding to first order in $\delta Q$:

$$
\frac{L}{g A_p} \frac{d^2 (\delta Q)}{dt^2} = \left. \frac{dH_{pump}}{dQ} \right\vert_{Q_0} \delta Q - \left. \frac{dH_{sys}}{dQ} \right\vert_{Q_0} \delta Q
$$

### 1.3 Condition for Stability

Defining the slope difference:

$$
K = \left. \frac{dH_{pump}}{dQ} \right\vert_{Q_0} - \left. \frac{dH_{sys}}{dQ} \right\vert_{Q_0}
$$

The equation becomes the classical Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) form:

$$
\frac{d^2 (\delta Q)}{dt^2} + \frac{g A_p}{L} K \cdot \delta Q = 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on notation:** In the slope difference term, the vertical bars are used to denote evaluation *at the operating point* $Q_0$. They are **not** absolute value bars; they signify a conditional evaluation, written in shorthand for the KTU board exam as "dH/dQ evaluated at $Q_0$".

For SHM-type behavior (oscillatory, bounded), we need the coefficient of $\delta Q$ to be **positive**. If it is **negative**, the solution is exponential — i.e., the perturbation **grows**, and surge occurs.

**Stable Condition (No Surge):**

$$
K > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left. \frac{dH_{pump}}{dQ} \right\vert_{Q_0} > \left. \frac{dH_{sys}}{dQ} \right\vert_{Q_0}
$$

> In simple language: *the system's demand for head must rise faster with flow than the pump's ability to provide it.*

**Unstable Condition (Surge):**

$$
\left. \frac{dH_{pump}}{dQ} \right\vert_{Q_0} < \left. \frac{dH_{sys}}{dQ} \right\vert_{Q_0}
$$

> This is the **surge criterion**. Geometrically, the system curve is "steeper" than the pump curve at the operating point.

### 1.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A centrifugal pump has the H-Q relation $H_p = 40 - 0.5Q^2$ (m, with Q in L/s). The system resistance is $H_s = 0.8Q^2$. Determine the surge limit and the surge margin.

**Step 1:** Find the steady operating point by setting $H_p = H_s$:

$$
40 - 0.5 Q^2 = 0.8 Q^2
$$

$$
40 = 1.3 Q^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q^2 = 30.77 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_0 = 5.55 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the slopes at $Q_0$:

$$
\frac{dH_p}{dQ} = -Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{at } Q_0: \, \frac{dH_p}{dQ} = -5.55 \text{ m/(L/s)}
$$

$$
\frac{dH_s}{dQ} = 1.6 Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{at } Q_0: \, \frac{dH_s}{dQ} = 8.88 \text{ m/(L/s)}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the surge criterion. We need to find the flow at which the magnitudes of the slopes are equal (the **surge limit** $Q_{surge}$):

$$
Q_{surge} = 1.6 \, Q_{surge} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{no finite solution!}
$$

> This means the system curve is **steeper than the pump curve at every operating point**. The pump is fundamentally unstable in this system — a classic KTU-style design flaw.

**Step 4:** Surge Margin (hypothetical, assuming a modified system $H_s = 2.0 Q^2$):

$$
Q_0 = \sqrt{40/2.5} = 4.0 \text{ L/s}
$$

$$
\left\vert \frac{dH_p}{dQ} \right\vert = 4.0; \quad \left\vert \frac{dH_s}{dQ} \right\vert = 8.0
$$

Still unstable. We must reduce system resistance. Let $H_s = 0.5 Q^2$:

$$
40 - 0.5 Q^2 = 0.5 Q^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_0 = \sqrt{40} = 6.32 \text{ L/s}
$$

$$
\left\vert \frac{dH_p}{dQ} \right\vert = 6.32; \quad \left\vert \frac{dH_s}{dQ} \right\vert = 3.16
$$

**Now stable** (pump slope magnitude > system slope magnitude). Surge limit would be where $\vert dH_p/dQ \vert = \vert dH_s/dQ \vert$:

$$
Q_{surge} = 0.5 \times 2 Q_{surge} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_{surge} = 0 \text{ (trivially stable)}.
$$

This simple example shows that the **shape of the system curve** — and not the pump alone — determines surge.

## 2. Derivation 2: Surge Cycle Frequency

The low-frequency oscillation of surge has a characteristic period. From the linearized SHM equation:

$$
\omega_{surge} = \sqrt{\frac{g A_p}{L} \cdot K}
$$

For a typical installation near the surge limit, $K$ is small, giving the low surge frequency:

$$
f_{surge} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{g A_p K}{L}}
$$

For air at standard conditions with $L = 10$ m and $A_p = 0.01$ m², the surge frequency is on the order of **0.5 to 2 Hz** — a slow, throbbing oscillation that is easy to identify acoustically in a plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical implication:** The very low frequency of surge (often below 5 Hz) means that it is **not** a high-frequency vibration. It is a slow pumping motion. This is why anti-surge control systems use **slow response PID loops**, not fast vibration trip systems.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 1. Block Diagram: The Surging Feedback Loop

The following block diagram illustrates the system-level closed-loop interaction between the pump, the piping system, and the surge phenomenon.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Impeller Rotates and Delivers Energy] --> B[Fluid Pressure Rises in Discharge Pipe]
    B --> C{Pressure vs. System Demand}
    C -- Pressure within system capacity --> D[Stable Flow - Normal Operation]
    C -- Pressure exceeds system capacity --> E[Flow Reversal through Impeller]
    E --> F[Pressure Drops Suddenly]
    F --> G[Impeller Regains Pumping Capability]
    G --> A
    D --> H[Operator Throttles Discharge Valve]
    H --> I{Flow Above Surge Limit?}
    I -- Yes --> D
    I -- No --> B
```

> **How to read this diagram:** The top loop (A → B → C → E → F → G → A) is the **self-excited surge cycle**. The bottom loop (D → H → I) shows the **operator-induced path** that drives the system into surge. This is a KTU-style schematic suitable for a 7-mark question on "Explain the mechanism of surging with a block diagram."

## 2. Performance Curve Schematic: Stable vs. Surge Region

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stable_Region
        A1[Operating Point: High Flow]
        A2[Operating Point: BEP - Best Efficiency]
    end
    subgraph Unstable_Surge_Region
        A3[Operating Point: Below Surge Limit]
        A4[SURGE ZONE]
    end
    PumpCurve[Pump H-Q Curve - Downward Slope] --> A1
    PumpCurve --> A2
    PumpCurve --> A3
    PumpCurve --> A4
    SysCurve1[System Curve 1 - Low Resistance] --> A1
    SysCurve2[System Curve 2 - Medium Resistance] --> A2
    SysCurve3[System Curve 3 - High Resistance] --> A3
    SysCurve4[System Curve 4 - Very High Resistance] --> A4
```

> **Interpretation:** As the system resistance (throttling) increases, the operating point migrates to the left. When it crosses the **surge line**, the slope condition is violated and the system becomes unstable.

## 3. Anti-Surge System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compressor Discharge] --> B[Flow Transmitter FT]
    A --> C[Pressure Transmitter PT]
    B --> D{Anti-Surge Controller PID}
    C --> D
    D --> E[Anti-Surge Valve ASV]
    E --> F[Recycle to Suction]
    D --> G[Alarm and Trip Logic]
    G --> H[Compressor Shutdown on Severe Surge]
    A --> I[Process Discharge to User]
```

> **Engineering Note:** This is the standard ISA-84 / IEC 61511 compliant architecture for safety-integrated surge protection in hydrocarbon and chemical plants. The **flow transmitter** is the primary sensor (not pressure), because surge is fundamentally a flow phenomenon.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define surging in a centrifugal pump. What is the surge limit?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **Surging** is a self-excited, large-amplitude axial oscillation of the working fluid that occurs in a centrifugal pump or compressor when the operating flow rate falls below a critical minimum value. It is characterized by periodic flow reversal, severe pressure pulsations, and mechanical vibration. **[2 marks]**
>
> The **surge limit** is the minimum flow rate at which the machine can operate stably. Below this limit, the system H-Q curve is steeper than the pump H-Q curve, leading to instability. It is typically 60–80% of the Best Efficiency Point (BEP) flow. **[1 mark]**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: State two design methods used to prevent surging in centrifugal compressors.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> 1. **Anti-Surge Recycle Line (Bypass):** A pipe with an automatic control valve is provided from the discharge to the suction. When the flow drops near the surge limit, the valve opens and recycles fluid to maintain flow above the surge limit. **[1.5 marks]**
>
> 2. **Variable Speed Drive (VSD):** Reducing the impeller speed shifts the entire H-Q curve downward, effectively lowering the surge limit and allowing stable operation at reduced throughput without flow reversal. **[1.5 marks]**

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question has two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**A centrifugal water pump has the following characteristics at design speed:**
- $H_p = 50 - 1.2 Q^2$ (H in meters, Q in L/s)
- System curve: $H_s = 0.6 Q^2$

**(a)** Determine the operating point. State whether the operation is stable. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** If the system resistance changes such that $H_s = 1.5 Q^2$, find the new operating point and determine if the system will surge. Suggest one design modification. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) Solution:**

Setting $H_p = H_s$:

$$
50 - 1.2 Q^2 = 0.6 Q^2
$$

$$
50 = 1.8 Q^2
$$

$$
Q_0 = \sqrt{50/1.8} = 5.27 \text{ L/s}
$$

Head at operating point:

$$
H_0 = 0.6 \times (5.27)^2 = 16.67 \text{ m}
$$

Slope check:

$$
\frac{dH_p}{dQ} = -2.4 Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{at } Q_0: \, -13.25 \text{ m/(L/s)}
$$

$$
\frac{dH_s}{dQ} = 1.2 Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{at } Q_0: \, 6.32 \text{ m/(L/s)}
$$

Magnitude of pump slope (13.25) **>** system slope (6.32). **Operation is STABLE.** ✅

**Part (b) Solution:**

New operating point with $H_s = 1.5 Q^2$:

$$
50 - 1.2 Q^2 = 1.5 Q^2
$$

$$
50 = 2.7 Q^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_0' = 4.30 \text{ L/s}
$$

$$
H_0' = 1.5 \times (4.30)^2 = 27.75 \text{ m}
$$

Slope check at new operating point:

$$
\left\vert \frac{dH_p}{dQ} \right\vert = 2.4 \times 4.30 = 10.32
$$

$$
\left\vert \frac{dH_s}{dQ} \right\vert = 3.0 \times 4.30 = 12.90
$$

Pump slope (10.32) **<** system slope (12.90). **The system will SURGE.** ⚠️

**Design Modification:** Install an **anti-surge bypass line** with an automatic control valve from the pump discharge back to the suction. When the flow drops below the surge limit (Q ≈ 4.30 L/s), the valve opens to maintain a minimum flow through the impeller, preventing the flow reversal that causes surge.

---

### Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the mechanism of surging in a centrifugal compressor. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Define Greitzer's B-parameter. What is its significance in determining whether a system will experience surge or rotating stall? **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) Solution:**

Surging is a self-excited oscillation in a turbomachine. The mechanism is as follows (block diagram steps):

1. **Energy Input:** The impeller rotates at design speed and continuously adds energy to the fluid in the form of head (pressure).
2. **Flow Demand Mismatch:** The user throttles the discharge valve, demanding less flow than the surge limit. The impeller has nowhere to send the fluid.
3. **Pressure Build-up:** Pressure in the discharge pipe and plenum rises rapidly. This pressure wave travels back toward the impeller.
4. **Flow Reversal:** The back-pressure exceeds the impeller's delivery head. The fluid column in the pipe decelerates, stops, and reverses direction. Fluid flows backward through the impeller.
5. **Pressure Release:** The reverse flow relieves the back-pressure. Pressure at the impeller exit drops to a low value.
6. **Recovery:** The impeller regains its pumping ability and begins to drive fluid forward again, restarting the cycle.
7. **Self-Excited Oscillation:** This entire cycle repeats at a low frequency (0.5–5 Hz) without any external forcing, producing a characteristic throbbing noise and severe vibration.

**[Award full 7 marks for: mechanism explanation (4) + block diagram representation (3). A well-labeled block diagram showing the feedback loop is essential.]**

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Greitzer's B-Parameter:**

$$
B = \frac{U}{2a} \sqrt{\frac{V}{A_p L}}
$$

Where:
- $U$ = Impeller tip speed
- $a$ = Speed of sound in the fluid
- $V$ = Volume of the plenum / receiver
- $A_p$ = Cross-sectional area of the discharge pipe
- $L$ = Effective length of the discharge duct

**Significance:**

The B-parameter is a **dimensionless stability index** that classifies the dominant instability mode in a turbomachine system:

- **B < 0.7:** The system is in the **surge-dominated regime**. The low-frequency, large-amplitude axial oscillation (surge) is the primary concern. **The system will likely experience surge.** *Mitigation: anti-surge valve, recycle loop, VSD.*
- **B > 0.7:** The system is in the **rotating stall-dominated regime**. Localized flow breakdown on the impeller blades is the primary concern, not surge. *Mitigation: casing treatment, IGV design.*

**Design Implication:** The B-parameter is used by turbomachine designers to **size the plenum volume** and **duct geometry** during the system design phase to avoid surge-prone configurations. **[7 marks]**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Surging Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Surging" with "Cavitation":** This is the **#1 error** in KTU answers. Cavitation is a *localized* phenomenon at the impeller eye due to low pressure (vapor formation). Surging is a *system-wide* flow oscillation. Examiners deduct **2 to 3 marks** for this confusion.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Slope Criterion:** Many students answer "what is surge" but **fail to state the mathematical condition** $\vert dH_p/dQ \vert < \vert dH_s/dQ \vert$. This omission costs **2 marks** in Part B questions.
>
> 3. **Confusing the Surge Limit with the Shut-off Head:** Shut-off head ($Q = 0$) is the maximum head at zero flow — it is *not* the surge limit. The surge limit is at a **finite, non-zero flow**.
>
> 4. **Not Drawing a Block Diagram:** In a 7-mark "explain mechanism" question, a textual answer without a labeled block diagram typically receives only **4 of 7 marks**. Always include a diagram.
>
> 5. **Units Mismatch in Numericals:** Mixing $Q$ in L/s with $Q^2$ in (L/s)² and $H$ in meters is acceptable, but you **must declare the units** in the first line of the solution. Failing to do so is a **0.5 mark** deduction.
>
> 6. **Ignoring Real-World Context:** A top-band KTU answer always mentions *one* practical application (jet engine compressor, LNG plant, etc.) to show engineering maturity. This is worth a **bonus 0.5 to 1 mark** in examiners' discretion.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Surging**

- ⚡ **Core definition:** Self-excited, large-amplitude axial flow oscillation in a turbomachine at low flow rates.
- ⚡ **Surge limit:** Minimum stable flow rate; operation below it leads to surge.
- ⚡ **Slope criterion (THE key equation):** $\left\vert dH_p / dQ \right\vert < \left\vert dH_s / dQ \right\vert$ → **SURGE**.
- ⚡ **Greitzer B-parameter:** $B = (U/2a)\sqrt{V/(A_p L)}$; if **B < 0.7** → surge-dominated regime.
- ⚡ **Surge frequency:** Very low (**0.5–5 Hz**) — a slow, throbbing oscillation.
- ⚡ **Distinguishing feature:** Surging is a **system-level** instability (pump + piping), not a component defect.
- ⚡ **Anti-surge design methods:** (1) Bypass/recycle line, (2) VSD, (3) Inlet guide vane throttling, (4) Surge tank sizing, (5) Operator discipline.
- ⚡ **Cavitation ≠ Surging:** Cavitation is local at the impeller eye; surge is system-wide flow reversal.
- ⚡ **Slope of pump H-Q curve:** Typically **negative** (head drops as flow rises).
- ⚡ **Slope of system H-Q curve:** Always **positive** (resistance rises with flow).
- ⚡ **Surge occurs when pump is "too steep" for the system** — wait, no: when system is steeper than pump, the operating point becomes unstable.
- ⚡ **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** is the safest operating point; surge limit is typically to the **left** of BEP.
- ⚡ **For KTU numericals:** Always start by finding the operating point (set $H_p = H_s$), then check the slope criterion.
- ⚡ **Block diagram is mandatory** for 7-mark "explain mechanism" questions.
- ⚡ **Real-world impact:** Surge can cause flameout in jet engines, bearing failure in pumps, and process shutdowns in refineries.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Surging is a high-yield, low-difficulty topic. A well-drawn block diagram, the slope criterion, and a clear mention of Greitzer's B-parameter can earn you **full marks** in any 7 or 14-mark question. Do not overcomplicate it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Critical Frequency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Critical Frequency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Critical Frequency** (also called **Critical Speed** or **Whirling Speed**) is the rotational speed of a shaft at which the **lateral (transverse) natural frequency of vibration** of the shaft coincides with the **frequency of the disturbing force** (which is the rotational frequency itself). At this speed, resonance occurs in the transverse plane, causing the shaft to undergo **whirling** — large-amplitude lateral vibrations that can lead to catastrophic failure.

Mathematically, the **critical angular frequency** $\omega_c$ of a shaft is the speed at which the shaft's transverse vibration natural frequency equals the rotational angular velocity $\omega$. The corresponding **critical speed in rpm** is:

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\omega_c = 9.55\,\omega_c \;\;\text{rpm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Critical frequency is treated as a transverse vibration problem in machine design. The shaft is modelled as a continuous/vibrating beam carrying disc masses, and the condition for resonance in the lateral direction is what defines the "critical" speed. Operating speeds are designed to be sufficiently far (typically ±15–20 %) from all critical frequencies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Swinging Rope Trick"

Imagine you are twirling a rope with a small ball tied at its end in a vertical circle (like tying a stone to a string and swinging it over your head).

- When you twirl it **slowly**, the ball traces a small, stable circle — this is the *sub-critical* regime.
- As you gradually **increase** the spinning speed, the rope stays tight and the motion is steady.
- At **one particular speed**, the rope suddenly whips violently, the stone flies outward, and the rope begins to coil like a corkscrew — this is **whirling** or **resonance**. This is the *critical frequency*.
- If you spin it even faster, the motion stabilises again into a small tight circle — the *super-critical* regime.

The shaft–rotor system behaves identically: the rotating mass wants to fly outward due to **centrifugal force**, and when this matches the *elastic restoring force* of the bending shaft, you get unbounded lateral oscillation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in this Module:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Critical speed relationship: $\omega_c = \sqrt{g/y_{\text{static}}}$ for a simple single-disc shaft.
> - $y_{\text{static}}$ is the static deflection of the shaft at the disc location caused by the weight of the disc.

## 1.3 Why Critical Frequency Matters in Machine Design

A machine designer must ensure that the **operating speed** of every rotating shaft (turbine rotors, motor shafts, pump shafts, compressor rotors, propeller shafts, gear shafts) does **not** coincide with any of its transverse critical frequencies. Running at or near a critical speed results in:

- Excessive lateral vibrations
- Fatigue failure of the shaft
- Bearing damage
- Noise and loss of precision
- Catastrophic disintegration in extreme cases

Hence, computing the critical frequency is a **mandatory step** in the design of high-speed shafts.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of Critical Frequency

When a rotor of mass $m$ rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ about the geometric axis of a flexible shaft, the **centrifugal force** on the rotor is:

$$F_c = m\,\omega^2\,r$$

where $r$ is the radial displacement of the rotor's centre of mass from the rotation axis.

The **elastic restoring force** of the shaft (acting like a spring of stiffness $k$) tries to bring the rotor back toward the axis:

$$F_r = k\,r$$

**Resonance** (and thus the *critical frequency*) occurs when the centrifugal force equals the elastic restoring force for **any non-zero $r$**:

$$m\,\omega_c^2\,r = k\,r \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \text{ rad/s}}$$

Equivalently, since static deflection $y = mg/k$, we have $k/m = g/y$:

$$\boxed{\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{y}} \;\;\text{and}\;\; N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{y}} \text{ rpm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Shaft acts as a "spring in the radial direction":** The same bending stiffness that resists static deflection also resists dynamic (centrifugal) displacement. Hence critical speed is a *direct* function of static deflection.

## 2.2 Critical Speed of a Single-Rotor Shaft (Simply Supported)

For a simply supported uniform shaft of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, carrying a single disc of mass $m$ at the centre, the static deflection is:

$$y = \dfrac{m g L^3}{48\,EI}$$

Substituting into the critical speed expression:

$$N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{48\,EI\,g}{m g L^3}} = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{48\,EI}{m L^3}}$$

This is the **fundamental (first-mode) critical speed** of a simply supported shaft with a central load.

## 2.3 Multi-Rotor Systems — Dunkerley's Method

For a shaft carrying several discs, the **effective critical speed is lower** than that obtained by considering any single disc alone (because each disc adds its own deflection). Dunkerley's equation provides the combined effect:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_1^2} + \dfrac{1}{\omega_2^2} + \dfrac{1}{\omega_3^2} + \cdots + \dfrac{1}{\omega_n^2}}$$

where $\omega_i$ is the critical speed calculated considering **only the $i$-th disc** (with the deflection contribution of that disc alone).

This is a **conservative (lower-bound) estimate** and is the standard KTU-recommended method for multi-disc shaft systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dunkerley's assumption:** Each disc's deflection contribution is independent and small; the method gives the *first* (lowest) critical speed. It neglects disc-to-disc interaction (cross-coupling), but is highly accurate for design because the first critical is the only one of practical interest near operating speed.

## 2.4 Critical Frequency vs. Operating Speed — Design Zones

| Zone | Operating Speed $N$ | Behaviour | Design Action |
|------|-------------------|-----------|---------------|
| Sub-critical | $N < 0.8\,N_c$ | Stable, low vibration | Safe to operate |
| **Forbidden band** | $0.8\,N_c \le N \le 1.2\,N_c$ | Resonance risk | **Avoid** at all costs |
| Super-critical | $N > 1.2\,N_c$ | Stable again (stiff shaft behaviour) | Safe if dynamically balanced |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\omega_c = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Critical angular frequency from stiffness & mass | rad/s |
| 2 | $\omega_c = \sqrt{g/y}$ | Critical frequency from static deflection $y$ | rad/s |
| 3 | $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{g/y} = 9.55\sqrt{g/y}$ | Critical speed in rpm | rpm |
| 4 | $y = \dfrac{m g L^3}{48 EI}$ | Static deflection at centre of simply supported beam | m |
| 5 | $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{48 EI}{m L^3}}$ | Critical speed of simply supported shaft with central disc | rpm |
| 6 | $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{y_1+y_2+\cdots}}$ | Combined critical speed (generalised) | rpm |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \sum \dfrac{1}{\omega_i^2}$ | **Dunkerley's equation** for $n$ discs | rad/s |
| 8 | $f_c = \dfrac{\omega_c}{2\pi}$ | Critical frequency in Hz | Hz |
| 9 | $y_i = \dfrac{m_i g\,a_i^2\,b_i^2}{3 EI\,L}$ | Deflection of simply supported beam at point load $a_i$ from one support, $b_i$ from the other | m |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Critical speed calculations are essential in the design of:
> - Steam and gas turbine rotors
> - Centrifugal pumps and compressors
> - Turbo-generator shafts
> - High-speed motor shafts
> - Propeller and helicopter drive shafts
> - Spindles of machine tools
>
> In all these, designers ensure that the **first critical speed is at least 1.2–1.4 times the maximum operating speed** (a "stiff shaft" design) **or** the operating speed exceeds 1.4 times the first critical (a "flexible shaft" design, used in some modern turbine rotors).

## 2.6 Practical "Rayleigh's Quotient" Check

A quick first estimate of critical speed is given by **Rayleigh's method**, which equates the maximum kinetic energy to the maximum potential energy of the system:

$$\omega_c^2 = \dfrac{g\,\sum m_i\,y_i}{\sum m_i\,y_i^2}$$

where $y_i$ is the static deflection at the location of mass $m_i$. This is often more accurate than simple sum-of-deflections and is sometimes given as an alternative to Dunkerley's method in KTU problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Frequency from Equating Centrifugal and Elastic Forces

**Step 1 — Identify the system.**
A rotor of mass $m$ is mounted on a flexible shaft at a location where the lateral stiffness of the shaft is $k$ (N/m).

**Step 2 — Write the centrifugal force on the rotor when its centre is displaced by $r$ from the geometric axis.**

$$F_c = m\,\omega^2\,r \quad \text{(acts radially outward)}$$

**Step 3 — Write the elastic restoring force exerted by the deflected shaft.**

$$F_r = k\,r \quad \text{(acts radially inward, toward the axis)}$$

**Step 4 — For stable whirling, both forces must balance (the shaft can support the rotor at some offset $r$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m\,\omega^2\,r &= k\,r \\
m\,\omega^2 &= k \quad \text{(since } r \neq 0\text{)} \\
\omega_c &= \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to critical speed in rpm using $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{2\pi N_c}{60} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \\
N_c &= \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Use the relation $k = m g / y$ (from $y = mg/k$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{mg/y}{m}} = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{y}} \\
   &= 9.549\,\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{y}} \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **single-degree-of-freedom** critical speed formula. It forms the foundation of all subsequent derivations.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Speed of a Simply Supported Shaft with a Central Disc

**Step 1 — Static deflection of a simply supported beam of length $L$ with a central point load $W = mg$:**

$$y = \dfrac{W L^3}{48\,EI} = \dfrac{m g L^3}{48\,EI}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into $N_c = (60/2\pi)\sqrt{g/y}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{m g L^3 / (48 EI)}} \\
    &= \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{48 EI}{m L^3}} \\
    &= \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\cdot\dfrac{1}{L^{3/2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{48\,EI}{m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical constant check:**
$\dfrac{60}{2\pi} \approx 9.5493$ and $\sqrt{48} \approx 6.9282$, so the combined numerical coefficient is:

$$9.5493 \times 6.9282 \approx 66.16$$

Thus an equivalent compact form often used in machine design texts is:

$$N_c \approx \dfrac{66.16}{L^{3/2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{m}} \;\;\text{rpm}$$

with $L$ in metres, $EI$ in N·m², and $m$ in kg.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dunkerley's Equation for Multi-Disc Shafts

**Step 1 — Consider a shaft carrying $n$ discs of masses $m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n$.** Let $\omega_c$ be the combined critical speed. If the shaft whirls in some mode, the deflection at disc $i$ is $y_i$.

**Step 2 — The equation of motion for the entire system in vector form (Rayleigh's energy method) is:**

$$\omega_c^2 = \dfrac{g\,\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i y_i^2}$$

**Step 3 — Suppose each disc alone (with all others removed) would give a critical speed $\omega_i$ with its own deflection $y_i^{*}$.** From the single-disc formula:

$$\omega_i^2 = \dfrac{g}{y_i^{*}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad y_i^{*} = \dfrac{g}{\omega_i^2}$$

**Step 4 — Dunkerley's key assumption: the actual deflection at disc $i$ in the combined system equals the *sum* of the deflections that each disc alone would have produced:**

$$y_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} y_{ij}^{*} \approx y_{ii}^{*} \quad \text{(dominant term)}$$

For a *lower bound*, one takes $y_i \approx y_{ii}^{*} = g/\omega_i^2$, leading to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_c^2 &\approx \dfrac{g\,\sum m_i \cdot (g/\omega_i^2)}{\sum m_i \cdot (g/\omega_i^2)^2} = \dfrac{g^2\,\sum (m_i/\omega_i^2)}{g^2\,\sum m_i/\omega_i^4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Dunkerley further simplified this by assuming equal-weight discs in the *same deflected shape* as the first-mode approximation, which yields the elegant result:**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{\omega_i^2}}$$

This is the **Dunkerley equation** used in KTU examinations.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model Solution Pattern)

**Problem:** A shaft of length $L = 1.2$ m, simply supported at the ends, carries two discs:
- Disc 1: $m_1 = 20$ kg at the centre ($a_1 = 0.6$ m from each support)
- Disc 2: $m_2 = 15$ kg at $a_2 = 0.4$ m from the left support ($b_2 = 0.8$ m)

The shaft has $E = 2.1 \times 10^{11}$ N/m² and the second moment of area $I = 5 \times 10^{-8}$ m$^4$ (diameter ≈ 45 mm). Find the critical speed using Dunkerley's method. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1 — Compute $EI$:**

$$EI = (2.1 \times 10^{11})(5 \times 10^{-8}) = 1.05 \times 10^{4} \text{ N·m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the static deflection at Disc 1 due to $m_1$ alone (formula for simply supported beam with point load):**

$$y_{11} = \dfrac{m_1 g a_1^2 b_1^2}{3 EI L}$$

With $a_1 = b_1 = 0.6$ m, $L = 1.2$ m:

$$y_{11} = \dfrac{20 \times 9.81 \times (0.6)^2 \times (0.6)^2}{3 \times 1.05 \times 10^4 \times 1.2}$$

$$y_{11} = \dfrac{20 \times 9.81 \times 0.36 \times 0.36}{3.78 \times 10^4} = \dfrac{25.42}{3.78 \times 10^4}$$

$$y_{11} = 6.726 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.6726 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the critical speed considering Disc 1 alone:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_1 &= \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{y_{11}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9.81}{6.726 \times 10^{-4}}} \\
        &= \sqrt{14588} = 120.78 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$N_1 = 9.549 \times 120.78 = 1153 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the static deflection at Disc 2 due to $m_2$ alone, with $a_2 = 0.4$ m, $b_2 = 0.8$ m, $L = 1.2$ m:**

$$y_{22} = \dfrac{15 \times 9.81 \times (0.4)^2 \times (0.8)^2}{3 \times 1.05 \times 10^4 \times 1.2}$$

$$y_{22} = \dfrac{15 \times 9.81 \times 0.16 \times 0.64}{3.78 \times 10^4} = \dfrac{15.07}{3.78 \times 10^4}$$

$$y_{22} = 3.987 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.3987 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Critical speed considering Disc 2 alone:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_2 &= \sqrt{\dfrac{9.81}{3.987 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{24610} = 156.87 \text{ rad/s} \\
N_2 &= 9.549 \times 156.87 = 1498 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Dunkerley's equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{N_c^2} &= \dfrac{1}{N_1^2} + \dfrac{1}{N_2^2} \\
                &= \dfrac{1}{1153^2} + \dfrac{1}{1498^2} \\
                &= \dfrac{1}{1.3294 \times 10^6} + \dfrac{1}{2.244 \times 10^6} \\
                &= 7.523 \times 10^{-7} + 4.456 \times 10^{-7} \\
                &= 1.198 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$N_c^2 = \dfrac{1}{1.198 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.348 \times 10^{5}$$

$$\boxed{N_c = 913.7 \text{ rpm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The combined critical speed ($914$ rpm) is **lower** than either single-disc critical speed ($1153$ and $1498$ rpm). This is the expected behaviour of Dunkerley's result — adding masses *reduces* the first critical speed.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Formula to Use?"

| Situation | Formula to apply |
|-----------|------------------|
| Single disc, location given, $E, I, L, m$ known | $N_c = (60/2\pi)\sqrt{48 EI / m L^3}$ (if at centre) or general $N_c = 9.55\sqrt{g/y}$ |
| Single disc, static deflection $y$ given | $N_c = 9.55\sqrt{g/y}$ |
| Two or more discs on simply supported shaft | Dunkerley's equation $\sum 1/\omega_i^2$ |
| Multiple discs, energy-based exact solution | Rayleigh's quotient $\omega_c^2 = g\sum m_i y_i / \sum m_i y_i^2$ |
| Operating speed check | Ensure $N \notin [0.8\,N_c, \,1.2\,N_c]$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic Block Diagram — Critical Frequency Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Shaft-Disc System Data] --> B{Number of Discs?}
    B -->|n = 1| C[Compute Static Deflection y at disc location]
    C --> D[Apply Nc = 9.55 * sqrt g over y]
    D --> E[Obtain Nc in rpm]
    B -->|n greater than 1| F[Compute y_ii for each disc i]
    F --> G[Compute wi = sqrt g over y_ii for each disc]
    G --> H[Apply Dunkerley: 1 over wc² = sum 1 over wi²]
    H --> I[Obtain wc and Nc]
    E --> J{Operating Speed N in 0.8 to 1.2 times Nc ?}
    I --> J
    J -->|Yes| K[REDESIGN: change L, D, or material]
    J -->|No| L[Design SAFE: proceed with shaft]
    K --> A
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Single vs. Multi-Rotor Critical Speed Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SingleRotor[Single Rotor Critical Speed]
        S1[Shaft Bending Stiffness k] --> S2[omega c = sqrt k over m]
        M1[Rotor Mass m] --> S2
        S2 --> S3[Nc in rpm]
    end

    subgraph MultiRotor[Multi Rotor via Dunkerley]
        D1[Disc 1 only: omega 1] --> DC[Dunkerley Summation]
        D2[Disc 2 only: omega 2] --> DC
        D3[Disc n only: omega n] --> DC
        DC --> DN[1 over omega c squared = sum 1 over omega i squared]
    end

    subgraph DesignGate[Design Verification Gate]
        G1[Compute Nc] --> G2{Is N outside 0.8 Nc to 1.2 Nc}
        G2 -->|Yes| G3[Approve Design]
        G2 -->|No| G4[Reject: Modify Geometry or Material]
    end
```

## 4.3 Schematic Representation of "Whirling" — Lateral Vibration Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shaft axis of rotation is vertical dashed line] --> B[Rotor centre of mass is OFFSET by r due to unbalance]
    B --> C[Centrifugal force m omega squared r acts outward]
    C --> D[Elastic restoring force k r acts inward]
    D --> E{Forces in balance ?}
    E -->|Yes, at any omega| F[Stable whirling at that radius]
    E -->|No, for small r| G[Resonance: r grows without bound]
    G --> H[This omega is the CRITICAL FREQUENCY omega c]
```

## 4.4 Visual Summary of the Three Sub/Super/Critical Zones

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z1[Sub Critical: N less than 0.8 Nc] -->|Smooth operation| Z2[Approaching critical: rising amplitude]
    Z2 -->|FORBIDDEN ZONE: 0.8 Nc to 1.2 Nc| Z3[Resonance / Whirling - DANGEROUS]
    Z3 -->|Stabilises again| Z4[Super Critical: N greater than 1.2 Nc]
    Z4 -->|Shaft behaves rigid| Z5[Stable operation possible]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid note:** These diagrams show the *functional logic* of critical frequency calculation and the design decision flow, as specified by the KTU-V10 fallback rule for non-physical drawings. They map the interaction between stiffness, mass, deflection, and operating speed.
