This slip corresponds to an angular measure (slip angle): $$\phi = \frac{s \cdot r_1 \cdot \theta_1}{r_1} = s \cdot \theta_1$$ For engineering approximations with $s \ll 1$: $$\phi = s \cdot \pi \quad \text{(radians, since } \theta_1 \approx \pi \text{ for open belt)}$$ **Modified Contact Angles:** $$\theta_1' = \theta_1 - \phi \quad \text{(driving pulley loses contact)}$$ $$\theta_2' = \theta_2 + \phi \quad \text{(driven pulley gains contact)}$$ **Modified Belt Friction Equation:** $$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta_1'} = e^{\mu(\theta_1 - \phi)}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a)** A flat belt drive has the following specifications: - Driving pulley diameter: 400 mm - Driven pulley diameter: 800 mm - Speed of driving pulley: 240 rpm - Slip: 2% - Coefficient of friction: 0.28 Calculate: 1. The power transmitted if the tension ratio is based on the modified contact angle 2. The percentage reduction in power due to slip **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the relationship between slip coefficient **s** and the difference in tensions $(T_1 - T_2)$ for a flat belt drive operating with elastic deformation. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** **Given:** - $d_1 = 400$ mm = 0.4 m - $d_2 = 800$ mm = 0.8 m - $N_1 = 240$ rpm - $s = 0.02$ - $\mu = 0.28$ **Step 1 — Linear belt velocity:** $$v = \frac{\pi d_1 N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.4 \times 240}{60}$$ $$\begin{aligned} v &= \frac{\pi \times 96}{60} = \frac{301.59}{60} \\ v &= 5.0265 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Angles of lap (open belt drive):** Since $d_2 = 2 d_1$ (2:1 ratio), and assuming $C \gg (d_2 - d_1)$: $$\theta_1 \approx \pi - 2\alpha \approx \pi \text{ rad (smaller pulley)}$$ For simplicity, take $\theta_1 = \pi$ rad and $\theta_2 = \pi$ rad. **Step 3 — Slip angle:** $$\phi = s \cdot \pi = 0.02 \times \pi = 0.0628 \text{ rad}$$ **Step 4 — Modified angle of contact (on smaller pulley):** $$\theta_1' = \theta_1 - \phi = \pi - 0.0628 = 3.0788 \text{ rad}$$ **Step 5 — Tension ratio with modified angle:** $$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta_1'} = e^{0.28 \times 3.0788} = e^{0.8621}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \frac{T_1}{T_2} &= 2.368 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Assume $T_2 = 1000$ N (reference):** $$T_1 = 2.368 \times 1000 = 2368 \text{ N}$$ **Step 7 — Power transmitted:** $$P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v = (2368 - 1000) \times 5.0265$$ $$\begin{aligned} P &= 1368 \times 5.0265 = 6876.3 \text{ W} \approx 6.88 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 8 — Power reduction comparison:** Without slip: $P_{\text{ideal}} = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$ with $\theta_1' = \pi$ $$\frac{T_1}{T_2}\bigg|_{\text{no slip}} = e^{0.28\pi} = e^{0.8796} = 2.410$$ Power without slip (same $T_2$): $$P_{\text{no slip}} = (2410 - 1000) \times 5.0265 = 1410 \times 5.0265 = 7087.4 \text{ W}$$ Percentage reduction: $$\%\text{Reduction} = \frac{7087.4 - 6876.3}{7087.4} \times 100$$ $$\begin{aligned} \%\text{Reduction} &= \frac{211.1}{7087.4} \times 100 \approx 2.98\% \end{aligned}$$ --- **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Derivation of Slip Coefficient from Tensions:** **Assumptions:** 1. The belt material is linearly elastic (Hooke's Law applicable). 2. The coefficient of friction $\mu$ is constant over the entire contact arc. 3. Centrifugal effects on the belt are neglected (low-speed assumption). 4. The slip is small, allowing linearization of $\ln(1+x) \approx x$. **Derivation:** Consider a belt element moving from the slack side to the tight side around the driving pulley. **Step 1:** As the element traverses the contact arc, its tension changes from $T_2$ to $T_1$. The corresponding change in length is: $$dL = \frac{(T_1 - T_2) \cdot dl}{E \cdot A}$$ where $dl$ is the original element length. **Step 2:** The relative velocity change (slip) across the element: $$\frac{dv}{v} = \frac{T}{E \cdot A} \cdot d\theta \cdot \frac{r}{\text{(contact path length)}}$$ After integrating over the full contact arc: $$\int_{v_2}^{v_1} \frac{dv}{v} = \frac{1}{E \cdot A}\int_{T_2}^{T_1} dT$$ **Step 3:** This yields: $$\ln\left(\frac{v_1}{v_2}\right) = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{E \cdot A}$$ **Step 4:** For small slip, linearize: $$\frac{v_1 - v_2}{v_1} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{E \cdot A}$$ **Step 5:** By definition of slip fraction: $$\begin{aligned} s &= \frac{v_1 - v_2}{v_1} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{E \cdot A} \end{aligned}$$ This establishes the direct proportionality between slip and the tension difference, mediated by the belt's elastic stiffness $E \cdot A$. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Errors in Slip Problems — Examiner Insights** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m:** When $d_1 = 250$ mm is used directly in $v = \pi d_1 N_1 / 60$, the velocity comes out as **mm/s instead of m/s**. Always convert: $d(\text{m}) = d(\text{mm}) / 1000$. Loss: **1–2 marks**. > > 2. **Inverting velocity ratio:** Students frequently write $N_1/N_2 = d_1/d_2$ instead of $N_2/N_1 = d_1/d_2$. The convention is **driven/driving = driving diameter/driven diameter**. Loss: **1 mark**. > > 3. **Ignoring the $(1-s)$ factor:** A common error is writing the ideal velocity ratio for the actual case. KTU explicitly tests whether students remember the slip correction. Loss: **2 marks**. > > 4. **Confusing angle of slip φ with the angle of lap θ:** Some students interchange these. Recall: $\theta$ is the geometric wrap angle (depends on pulley diameters), while $\phi$ is the small angular slip arc (depends on $s$). > > 5. **Using radians vs degrees inconsistency:** $e^{\mu\theta}$ requires $\theta$ in **radians**. Mixing units (e.g., $\theta = 180°$ instead of $\pi$ rad) leads to massive computational errors. Loss: **2–3 marks**. > > 6. **Neglecting to show derivation steps:** In 7-mark derivations, only stating the final formula without intermediate steps leads to **partial marking only**. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Slip of the Belts** **Core Concepts:** - ☐ Slip = relative motion between belt and pulley due to insufficient friction - ☐ Two types: belt-pulley slip and pulley-pulley slip - ☐ Caused by elasticity of belt, inadequate friction, improper tensioning - ☐ Standard slip range: 1%–5% (use s as a decimal fraction: 0.01 to 0.05) **Key Formulas (Must Memorize):** - ☐ Velocity ratio (no slip): $N_2/N_1 = d_1/d_2$ - ☐ Velocity ratio (with slip): $N_2/N_1 = (d_1/d_2)(1-s)$ - ☐ Slip coefficient: $s = (T_1 - T_2)/(EA)$ - ☐ Belt velocity: $v = \pi d N / 60$ - ☐ Slip angle: $\phi = s\pi$ (engineering approximation) - ☐ Modified angles: $\theta_1' = \theta_1 - \phi$, $\theta_2' = \theta_2 + \phi$ - ☐ Modified tension ratio: $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu(\theta_1 - \phi)}$ - ☐ Power: $P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$ **Engineering Insights:** - ☐ Slip **reduces** the velocity ratio's driven speed - ☐ Slip **reduces** the effective tension ratio - ☐ Slip **reduces** power transmission capacity - ☐ Centrifugal tension is a separate effect — do not confuse with slip - ☐ Creep is closely related but originates from elasticity rather than friction limit **Unit Conversions to Remember:** - ☐ 1 mm = 0.001 m - ☐ 1 rpm × π × d(m) / 60 = v(m/s) - ☐ Angles in radians for exponential calculations **Examination Tips:** - ☐ Always show the slip correction explicitly when asked for "actual" values - ☐ State assumptions clearly in derivations (linear elasticity, constant μ, etc.) - ☐ Draw a simple belt drive schematic for theoretical questions - ☐ Cross-check velocity ratio direction: driven speed is always lower than ideal when slip > 0

topics.title: Creep
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Creep

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Creep** is defined as the **time-dependent, continuous, and permanent (plastic) deformation of a solid material under a sustained load (constant stress) at a temperature that is typically greater than approximately 0.4 times its absolute melting temperature (T > 0.4 T<sub>m</sub>, in Kelvin).**

In the context of **PCMET602 – Machine Design**, creep is treated as a critical design-limiting failure mechanism for components that operate at **elevated temperatures** for prolonged durations, such as:

- Boiler tubes and steam piping
- Gas turbine blades and rotors
- Pressure vessels operating above 350 °C
- Diesel engine exhaust manifolds
- Rocket nozzle throats

The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes that at **elevated homologous temperatures (T/T<sub>m</sub> > 0.4)**, the conventional yield-strength-based design philosophy becomes **invalid** because the material continues to deform even when the applied stress is **below its short-time yield strength**.

### 1.2 Standard Physical Metrics Used by KTU Examiners

- **Homologous Temperature:** T<sub>h</sub> = T / T<sub>m</sub> (dimensionless ratio)
- **Creep Rate (ε̇):** Strain accumulated per unit time, typically expressed in **% per 1000 hours** or **s<sup>-1</sup>**
- **Stress-Rupture Strength (S<sub>r</sub>):** The stress that causes rupture at a specified temperature in a specified time (commonly 10,000 hours or 100,000 hours)
- **Larson-Miller Parameter (LMP or P<sub>LM</sub>):** Combines time and temperature into a single parameter for extrapolation

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Candle on a Sunny Windowsill"

Imagine placing a **soft wax candle** vertically on a windowsill on a hot afternoon.

- On a **cool day**, the candle holds its shape indefinitely under its own weight.
- On a **hot day (above some critical "softening" temperature)**, the candle slowly **slumps, sags, and shortens** over hours — even though the load (its own weight, i.e., a stress below its room-temperature strength) has **not changed**.
- The deformation is **time-dependent** — it does not happen instantly; it accumulates gradually.

The candle is the engineering component, the sun is the elevated temperature, and the sag is **creep strain**. The crucial insight: the candle never broke suddenly (no fracture), it just progressively changed shape until it became useless. **This is exactly what happens in a creeping turbine blade** — it elongates, contacts the casing, and the machine fails — not by sudden fracture, but by **progressive geometric distortion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Creep is NOT a fracture phenomenon — it is a **progressive shape change**. Machine components are usually considered failed by creep when the strain exceeds a tolerable limit (typically **0.1 % to 1 %** depending on service) OR when rupture occurs.

---

## 1.4 Historical / Contextual Note for KTU Viva

The systematic study of creep became a central concern of mechanical design during the rapid expansion of **steam power plants in the 1920s–1930s**, when high-pressure boiler tubes began failing after thousands of hours of service — well below their short-time tensile strength. Designers learned that **time + temperature + stress** must be treated as a coupled problem, leading to the modern **stress-rupture** and **creep-rate** design methodology that the KTU Module 4 syllabus now demands.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Classic Creep Curve (Strain vs. Time)

When a constant stress (σ) is applied at a constant elevated temperature, the typical engineering strain ε plotted against time t produces a **three-stage creep curve**, which is mandatory to memorize for any KTU 14-mark derivation question.

### Stage I — Primary (Transient / Decelerating) Creep
- Creep rate **decreases** with time.
- Material undergoes **strain hardening** due to dislocation entanglement.
- The work hardening rate exceeds the thermal recovery (softening) rate.
- **Dislocation glide** is dominant.

### Stage II — Secondary (Steady-State) Creep
- Creep rate is **constant and minimum** — this is the **longest, most important** stage.
- A dynamic balance is achieved: **strain hardening = thermal recovery (recovery annealing)**.
- This minimum rate, denoted **ε̇<sub>s</sub>** or **ε̇<sub>min</sub>**, is the value used in design.
- Dominated by **dislocation climb + glide**, governed by **self-diffusion** of atoms.

### Stage III — Tertiary (Accelerating) Creep
- Creep rate **increases rapidly** until rupture (t<sub>r</sub>).
- Internal damage accumulates: **grain boundary cavities (creep voids), micro-cracks, necking**.
- **Cavitation** at grain boundaries perpendicular to stress direction is the chief mechanism.
- Failure (rupture) occurs at the end of Stage III.

---

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Creep Rate (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Factor | Effect on Creep Rate |
|---|---|
| **↑ Temperature** | Exponentially **increases** creep rate (Arrhenius behaviour) |
| **↑ Applied Stress** | Power-law **increases** creep rate |
| **↑ Grain Size** | **Decreases** creep rate (fewer grain-boundary diffusion paths) |
| **↑ Melting Point of Material** | **Decreases** creep rate (higher T<sub>m</sub>) |
| **Presence of Alloying Elements (Mo, W, V, Cr, Co)** | **Decreases** creep rate (solid-solution + precipitation strengthening) |
| **Precipitates (carbides, intermetallics, γ′)** | **Decreases** creep rate (obstruct dislocation motion) |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — All Key Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **core toolkit** for solving any KTU Module-4 creep problem. Memorize the variable definitions and the *boundary of applicability* of each equation.

| # | Equation | Variables & Units | Application / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ε̇<sub>s</sub> = A · σ<sup>n</sup> · exp(-Q / RT)** | ε̇<sub>s</sub> = steady-state creep rate (s⁻¹); A = constant; σ = stress (MPa); n = stress exponent (3–8 metals, 1–2 Nabarro-Herring, 2–3 Coble); Q = activation energy (J/mol); R = 8.314 J/mol·K; T = absolute temperature (K) | **Norton's Power Law + Arrhenius term** — Master equation |
| 2 | **ε̇<sub>s</sub> = A · σ<sup>n</sup>** (when T is constant) | Same as above without Arrhenius | Used when temperature is fixed; KTU often gives T constant |
| 3 | **log ε̇<sub>s</sub> = log A + n·log σ** | log base 10 | Gives a **straight line** on log-log plot; **n = slope** |
| 4 | **P<sub>LM</sub> = T · [log(t<sub>r</sub>) + C]** | P<sub>LM</sub> = Larson-Miller parameter; t<sub>r</sub> = rupture time (hours); C ≈ 20 (constant, sometimes 22) | Used to **extrapolate** short-lab-data to long service life |
| 5 | **log σ = a − b · P<sub>LM</sub>** | a, b are material constants | Master rupture-strength curve |
| 6 | **B₀ = σ · t<sub>r</sub><sup>m</sup> · exp(Q / RT)** | B₀ = constant; m ≈ –0.778 for LMP linkage | Alternative rupture-life formula |
| 7 | **t<sub>r</sub> = B · σ<sup>−n</sup> · exp(Q / RT)** | B = material constant | Stress-rupture life equation |
| 8 | **% Creep Strain in t hours ≈ ε̇<sub>s</sub> · t · 100** | Linear approximation valid in Stage II | Quick design check |
| 9 | **Strain-Hardening Rate:** h = dσ/dε | h and ε at constant ε̇ | Used in constitutive models |
| 10 | **Effective Stress (Soderberg):** σ<sub>e</sub> = √(3·J₂) | J₂ = 2nd deviatoric stress invariant | For multiaxial creep |

> **Standard Reference Constants Used in KTU Problems:**
> - R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - C (Larson-Miller) ≈ **20**
> - Typical creep stress exponent n ≈ **3 to 8** for metals (≈ 5 is most common)

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The design philosophy against creep governs the choice of material and allowable stress in:

1. **Power Generation:** Boiler superheater tubes, reheater tubes, headers, steam chests. Designers limit life to **100,000 hours (≈ 11.4 years)** of service.
2. **Aerospace:** Turbine blades (Ni-superalloys with γ′ precipitates), combustion chambers, rocket thrust chambers.
3. **Petrochemical:** Hydrocracker reactor coils, reformer tubes operating at 700–900 °C.
4. **IC Engines:** Exhaust valves, turbocharger housings, exhaust manifolds.

The KTU Module 4 design questions typically ask students to either:
(a) determine the **steady-state creep rate** for a given component, or
(b) determine the **rupture life** using the Larson-Miller parameter.

Both applications depend on **mastering Norton's Power Law** and the **Arrhenius thermal activation** term.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Norton's Power Law (Stage-II Steady-State Creep)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to derive the steady-state creep rate equation from first principles of **dislocation climb theory**. Below is the exhaustive, board-exam-grade derivation.

### Step 1: Phenomenological Assumption (Empirical Foundation)

For most engineering metals at high homologous temperature, experimental observation shows that steady-state creep rate obeys a **power-law dependence on stress**:

$$
\varepsilon_{s}^{\cdot} \propto \sigma^{n}
$$

A proportionality constant, A*, is introduced to capture material behaviour:

$$
\varepsilon_{s}^{\cdot} = A^{*} \cdot \sigma^{n}
$$

**Reasoning:** Dislocation motion (which controls creep) increases with applied stress; the exponent n reflects how rapidly dislocations multiply and move past obstacles as stress rises.

### Step 2: Introducing Thermal Activation (Arrhenius Term)

At elevated temperature, atomic diffusion controls the rate of dislocation climb (the bypass mechanism). Diffusion processes follow the **Arrhenius rate law**:

$$
D = D_{0} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{R \cdot T}\right)
$$

Here, **D** is the self-diffusion coefficient (m²/s), **D₀** is the pre-exponential factor, **Q** is the activation energy for self-diffusion (J/mol), **R** is the universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**, and **T** is absolute temperature in Kelvin.

**Reasoning:** Only those atoms with energy ≥ Q can jump across vacancies; as T increases, exponentially more atoms qualify, accelerating climb and therefore creep.

### Step 3: Coupling the Two Effects

Because climb is the rate-limiting step, the proportionality constant A* is itself **proportional to the diffusion coefficient** D:

$$
A^{*} = A \cdot D = A \cdot D_{0} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{R \cdot T}\right)
$$

Substituting back into the power law:

$$
\varepsilon_{s}^{\cdot} = A \cdot D_{0} \cdot \sigma^{n} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{R \cdot T}\right)
$$

The constants A and D₀ are merged into a single material constant **A** to give the canonical Norton-Bailey equation:

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon_{s}^{\cdot} = A \cdot \sigma^{n} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{R \cdot T}\right)\;}
$$

**Reasoning Recap:** Stress controls the *number* of mobile dislocations (power law), while temperature controls *how fast* the climb process operates (exponential/Arrhenius). The two are independent physical mechanisms multiplied together.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Larson-Miller Parameter (LMP)

The Larson-Miller Parameter is used to extrapolate short-duration lab data to long service life. Here is its derivation.

### Step 1: Start from the Rupture-Life Equation

Empirical rupture-life data fits the form:

$$
t_{r} = B \cdot \sigma^{-n} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{Q}{R \cdot T}\right)
$$

where **t<sub>r</sub>** is the time to rupture (hours), **B** is a material constant, **σ** is applied stress, and other symbols are as defined earlier.

### Step 2: Take Natural Logarithm

$$
\ln t_{r} = \ln B - n \cdot \ln \sigma + \frac{Q}{R \cdot T}
$$

### Step 3: Convert to Base-10 Logarithm (KTU Standard)

Using ln x = 2.3026 log x, and collecting terms:

$$
\log t_{r} = \log B - n \cdot \log \sigma + \frac{Q}{2.3026 \cdot R \cdot T}
$$

### Step 4: Group into the LMP Form

Define the Larson-Miller parameter **P<sub>LM</sub>** as:

$$
P_{LM} = T \cdot \left(\log t_{r} + C\right)
$$

with the constant C chosen to absorb the (Q / 2.3026·R) ratio. Empirically, **C ≈ 20** for most metallic materials.

Solving the previous equation for σ gives the **master rupture-strength curve**:

$$
\log \sigma = a - b \cdot P_{LM}
$$

where **a** and **b** are material constants determined from experimental data.

$$
\boxed{\;P_{LM} = T \left[ \log t_{r} + C \right] \quad \text{with} \quad C \approx 20\;}
$$

**Practical Implication:** Once P<sub>LM</sub> is known from a short lab test (e.g., 1000 hours at 900 K), the rupture life at any *other* service temperature can be **directly computed** without running the experiment for decades — this is the cornerstone of high-temperature design.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Pattern

**Problem:** A steel component operates at **T = 800 K** under sustained stress **σ = 60 MPa**. The Norton-Bailey constants are **A = 4 × 10⁻⁵ MPa⁻ⁿ·h⁻¹**, **n = 4**, and **Q = 200 kJ/mol**. Calculate the steady-state creep rate in s⁻¹.

> **Given:**
> - T = 800 K
> - σ = 60 MPa
> - A = 4 × 10⁻⁵ (MPa⁻ⁿ · h⁻¹)
> - n = 4
> - Q = 200,000 J/mol
> - R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)

> **Find:** ε̇<sub>s</sub> in s⁻¹

**Step 1: Compute the Arrhenius exponential factor**

$$
\frac{Q}{R \cdot T} = \frac{200{,}000}{8.314 \times 800} = \frac{200{,}000}{6{,}651.2} = 30.07
$$

**Step 2: Compute exp(-Q/RT)**

$$
\exp(-30.07) = 9.04 \times 10^{-14}
$$

**Step 3: Compute σⁿ**

$$
\sigma^{4} = 60^{4} = 12{,}960{,}000 = 1.296 \times 10^{7} \;\; \text{MPa}^{4}
$$

**Step 4: Compute ε̇<sub>s</sub> in h⁻¹**

$$
\varepsilon_{s}^{\cdot} = (4 \times 10^{-5}) \times (1.296 \times 10^{7}) \times (9.04 \times 10^{-14})
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{s}^{\cdot} = 4 \times 1.296 \times 9.04 \times 10^{-5+7-14}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{s}^{\cdot} = 46.86 \times 10^{-12} \;\; \text{h}^{-1} = 4.686 \times 10^{-11} \;\; \text{h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to s⁻¹** (1 h = 3600 s)

$$
\varepsilon_{s}^{\cdot} = \frac{4.686 \times 10^{-11}}{3600} = 1.30 \times 10^{-14} \;\; \text{s}^{-1}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A strain rate of 1.3 × 10⁻¹⁴ /s means in **one year (3.15 × 10⁷ s)**, the strain is approximately **4.1 × 10⁻⁷ = 0.000041 %** — *negligible in one year*. However, in **10 years**, it accumulates to ≈ 0.00041 %, and the design strain limit (e.g., 0.2 %) is reached in **~488 years**. The component is safe if it is replaced within the planned service interval.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classic Three-Stage Creep Curve (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["APPLY STRESS AT ELEVATED T"] --> B["INSTANTANEOUS ELASTIC STRAIN"]
    B --> C["STAGE I PRIMARY CREEP DECELERATING"]
    C --> D["STAGE II SECONDARY CREEP STEADY STATE MINIMUM RATE"]
    D --> E["STAGE III TERTIARY CREEP ACCELERATING"]
    E --> F["RUPTURE AT TIME TR"]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style E fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style F fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Yellow (B):** Elastic strain happens instantaneously on load application.
- **Orange (C):** Stage I — rate decreases due to strain hardening.
- **Green (D):** Stage II — the *design-defining* stage; rate is minimum and constant.
- **Light Red (E):** Stage III — rate accelerates due to internal damage (voids, necking).
- **Deep Red (F):** Final rupture point (t<sub>r</sub>).

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of Creep Deformation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["EXTERNAL SUSTAINED STRESS"] --> B["DISLOCATION GLIDE"]
    A --> C["DISLOCATION CLIMB"]
    B --> D["STRAIN HARDENING"]
    C --> E["THERMAL RECOVERY"]
    D --> F{"STAGE II BALANCE?"}
    E --> F
    F -->|BALANCED| G["STEADY STATE CREEP MINIMUM RATE"]
    F -->|IMBALANCE - RECOVERY WINS| H["STAGE III TERTIARY"]
    H --> I["GRAIN BOUNDARY VOIDS"]
    H --> J["NECKING"]
    I --> K["RUPTURE"]
    J --> K
    G --> L["DESIGN USES STAGE II RATE"]
    style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style B fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ff6f00
    style C fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ff6f00
    style D fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e
    style E fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c
    style F fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c
    style G fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style H fill:#ffab91,stroke:#bf360c
    style I fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c
    style J fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c
    style K fill:#e57373,stroke:#b71c1c
    style L fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#311b92
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This functional block diagram shows how stress drives **two parallel micromechanical processes** (glide and climb). Strain hardening from glide and thermal recovery from climb reach equilibrium in Stage II. When recovery begins to dominate (or voids form), Stage III begins, leading inevitably to rupture. The **Stage II rate is the only rate used in KTU design calculations.**

---

## 4.3 Graphical Representation: log ε̇ vs. log σ at Constant T

```
log ε̇
  |                          *
  |                       *
  |                    *
  |                 *
  |              *
  |           *
  |        *
  |     *
  |  *
  |*_________________________ log σ

      Slope of this straight line = n  (Norton stress exponent)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The KTU examiner frequently asks *"How would you determine the constants A and n experimentally?"* The answer is exactly this: **run multiple creep tests at constant T but varying σ**, plot log ε̇<sub>s</sub> vs. log σ, the **slope = n** and the **intercept = log A**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define creep. Under what condition of temperature is creep considered a design criterion?**

> **Model Answer:**
> Creep is the **time-dependent, permanent deformation of a material under a constant sustained load at elevated temperature**. It is considered a design criterion when the operating temperature exceeds approximately **0.4 T<sub>m</sub>** (where T<sub>m</sub> is the absolute melting temperature). At this homologous temperature, atomic diffusion processes (dislocation climb, grain boundary sliding) become significant, allowing continuous deformation even at stresses well below the short-time yield strength. **[3 marks]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Sketch and label the three stages of the creep curve. Which stage is used for design?**

> **Model Answer:**
> The creep curve plots strain (ε) versus time (t) and shows three stages:
> - **Stage I (Primary/Transient):** Decelerating creep rate due to strain hardening.
> - **Stage II (Secondary/Steady-State):** Constant, minimum creep rate — strain hardening and thermal recovery are in balance.
> - **Stage III (Tertiary):** Accelerating creep rate leading to rupture; internal voids and necking dominate.
>
> **Stage II** (the steady-state region) is used for design because it represents the **longest, most predictable** creep rate and governs the useful service life of the component. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]

**A pressure vessel operates at T = 900 K under a sustained stress of 50 MPa. The Norton-Bailey constants for the steel are A = 2 × 10⁻⁶ MPa⁻ⁿ · h⁻¹, n = 5, and Q = 180 kJ/mol. The design strain limit is 0.2 % over the planned 100,000-hour service life.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the steady-state creep rate (ε̇<sub>s</sub>) in s⁻¹ and the percentage creep strain accumulated in 100,000 hours. Comment on whether the design is safe.**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 — Compute Q/(R·T):**
>
> $$
> \frac{Q}{R \cdot T} = \frac{180{,}000}{8.314 \times 900} = \frac{180{,}000}{7{,}482.6} = 24.057
> $$
>
> **Step 2 — Compute exp(-Q/RT):**
>
> $$
> \exp(-24.057) = 3.587 \times 10^{-11}
> $$
>
> **Step 3 — Compute σⁿ:**
>
> $$
> 50^{5} = 312{,}500{,}000 = 3.125 \times 10^{8} \;\; \text{MPa}^{5}
> $$
>
> **Step 4 — Compute ε̇<sub>s</sub> in h⁻¹:**
>
> $$
> \varepsilon_{s}^{\cdot} = (2 \times 10^{-6}) \times (3.125 \times 10^{8}) \times (3.587 \times 10^{-11})
> $$
>
> $$
> \varepsilon_{s}^{\cdot} = 2 \times 3.125 \times 3.587 \times 10^{-6+8-11}
> $$
>
> $$
> \varepsilon_{s}^{\cdot} = 22.42 \times 10^{-9} = 2.242 \times 10^{-8} \;\; \text{h}^{-1}
> $$
>
> **Step 5 — Convert to s⁻¹ (divide by 3600):**
>
> $$
> \varepsilon_{s}^{\cdot} = \frac{2.242 \times 10^{-8}}{3600} = 6.23 \times 10^{-12} \;\; \text{s}^{-1}
> $$
>
> **Step 6 — Strain in 100,000 hours:**
>
> $$
> \varepsilon = \varepsilon_{s}^{\cdot} \times t = 2.242 \times 10^{-8} \times 10^{5} = 2.242 \times 10^{-3} = 0.2242 \%
> $$
>
> **Step 7 — Comparison with design limit:**
>
> Computed strain = **0.224 %** > Limit = **0.2 %**
>
> **Conclusion:** The design **exceeds the strain limit** by ~12 %. The component is **NOT SAFE**. Recommend: reduce stress, lower operating temperature, or select a more creep-resistant alloy. **[7 marks]**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Using the Larson-Miller Parameter with C = 20, calculate the rupture life of the same component if the same stress is maintained but the temperature is increased to 950 K. The same material constants apply (use B from the original equation such that t<sub>r</sub> = 100,000 h at 900 K).**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 — Compute P<sub>LM</sub> at the known condition:**
>
> $$
> P_{LM} = T \cdot [\log t_{r} + C] = 900 \times [\log(100{,}000) + 20]
> $$
>
> $$
> \log(100{,}000) = 5
> $$
>
> $$
> P_{LM} = 900 \times [5 + 20] = 900 \times 25 = 22{,}500
> $$
>
> **Step 2 — Equate P<sub>LM</sub> at the new temperature and solve for t<sub>r</sub>:**
>
> $$
> 22{,}500 = 950 \times [\log t_{r} + 20]
> $$
>
> $$
> \frac{22{,}500}{950} = \log t_{r} + 20
> $$
>
> $$
> 23.684 = \log t_{r} + 20
> $$
>
> $$
> \log t_{r} = 3.684
> $$
>
> **Step 3 — Solve for t<sub>r</sub>:**
>
> $$
> t_{r} = 10^{3.684} = 4{,}830 \;\; \text{hours}
> $$
>
> **Step 4 — Interpretation:**
>
> A mere 50 K (≈ 5.5 %) increase in temperature **reduces the rupture life from 100,000 h to ≈ 4,830 h** — a factor of ~20. This demonstrates the extreme sensitivity of creep life to temperature. **[7 marks]**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**A nickel-based superalloy turbine blade is subjected to a sustained centrifugal stress of σ = 200 MPa at T = 1100 K. Laboratory tests on the alloy yield the following data:**

| Test | Temperature (K) | Stress (MPa) | Steady-state creep rate ε̇<sub>s</sub> (s⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1000 | 200 | 1.0 × 10⁻⁹ |
| 2 | 1100 | 200 | 1.0 × 10⁻⁷ |

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Determine the activation energy Q (in kJ/mol) for creep of this alloy. Comment on the magnitude.**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> At constant stress σ = 200 MPa, Norton's equation reduces to:
>
> $$
> \varepsilon_{s}^{\cdot} = K \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{R \cdot T}\right)
> $$
>
> where K = A · σⁿ is constant at fixed σ.
>
> **Step 1 — Write the ratio of the two test conditions:**
>
> $$
> \frac{\varepsilon_{s,2}^{\cdot}}{\varepsilon_{s,1}^{\cdot}} = \frac{\exp(-Q/(R \cdot T_2))}{\exp(-Q/(R \cdot T_1))} = \exp\!\left[\frac{Q}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]
> $$
>
> **Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**
>
> $$
> \frac{10^{-7}}{10^{-9}} = 100 = \exp\!\left[\frac{Q}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{1000} - \frac{1}{1100}\right)\right]
> $$
>
> $$
> \frac{1}{1000} - \frac{1}{1100} = \frac{1100 - 1000}{1{,}100{,}000} = \frac{100}{1{,}100{,}000} = 9.0909 \times 10^{-5}
> $$
>
> **Step 3 — Take ln of both sides:**
>
> $$
> \ln(100) = 4.6052 = \frac{Q}{8.314} \times 9.0909 \times 10^{-5}
> $$
>
> **Step 4 — Solve for Q:**
>
> $$
> Q = \frac{4.6052 \times 8.314}{9.0909 \times 10^{-5}}
> $$
>
> $$
> Q = \frac{38.297}{9.0909 \times 10^{-5}} = 421{,}267 \;\; \text{J/mol} \approx 421 \;\; \text{kJ/mol}
> $$
>
> **Step 5 — Comment:**
>
> An activation energy of ~421 kJ/mol is **typical of creep in nickel-based superalloys** (reported range 350–500 kJ/mol). It corresponds closely to the activation energy for **self-diffusion in nickel** (~285–300 kJ/mol) plus the additional barrier for **dislocation climb through γ′ precipitates**. This high value confirms the alloy's excellent creep resistance. **[7 marks]**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**If a design life of 25,000 hours is required at T = 1100 K, what is the maximum allowable stress for the blade? Use the Larson-Miller Parameter with C = 20, given that the master rupture curve for this alloy is log σ = 2.5 − 0.00008 · P<sub>LM</sub>.**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 — Compute P<sub>LM</sub>:**
>
> $$
> P_{LM} = T \cdot [\log t_{r} + C] = 1100 \times [\log(25{,}000) + 20]
> $$
>
> $$
> \log(25{,}000) = \log(2.5 \times 10^{4}) = 4 + 0.3979 = 4.3979
> $$
>
> $$
> P_{LM} = 1100 \times [4.3979 + 20] = 1100 \times 24.3979 = 26{,}837.7
> $$
>
> **Step 2 — Substitute into master rupture curve:**
>
> $$
> \log \sigma = 2.5 - 0.00008 \times 26{,}837.7
> $$
>
> $$
> \log \sigma = 2.5 - 2.1470 = 0.3530
> $$
>
> **Step 3 — Solve for σ:**
>
> $$
> \sigma = 10^{0.3530} = 2.254 \;\; (\text{units of σ as in the master curve})
> $$
>
> **Step 4 — Interpretation:**
>
> The maximum allowable stress is **≈ 2.25 (in the units used in the master curve, e.g., 10² MPa = 225 MPa if σ is in units of 10² MPa, OR equivalently, the stress-rupture strength S<sub>r</sub> = 225 MPa)**. Designers must use a **safety factor (typically 1.5–2)** on this value, giving an allowable working stress of ~110–150 MPa.
>
> The blade centrifugal stress must be **redesigned** (e.g., by reducing blade mass, using single-crystal blades, adding internal cooling channels, or selecting a more advanced superalloy with γ′ volume fraction > 60 %). **[7 marks]**

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls in Creep Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — KTU problems often mix **hours** and **seconds**. ε̇ must be reported in **the units requested**. Mixing hr⁻¹ and s⁻¹ without conversion is the **#1 source of marks lost**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Arrhenius factor** — At constant T, students sometimes write only **ε̇ = Aσⁿ**, omitting the exponential. If T varies or is given as a parameter, **always include exp(-Q/RT)**.
>
> 3. **Confusing log base** — Norton's plot uses **log σ** (base 10) but the derivation step often uses **ln**. Ensure consistency.
>
> 4. **Misapplication of LMP** — The Larson-Miller Parameter assumes **the same material and stress mechanism**. It cannot be used to extrapolate across different alloys.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the design strain criterion** — Many students calculate ε̇<sub>s</sub> correctly but **fail to convert it into total strain over service life** and **compare with allowable strain** (typically 0.1 % to 1 %).
>
> 6. **Sign error in (1/T₁ − 1/T₂)** — Always derive the ratio carefully; reversing T₁ and T₂ inverts the sign of the argument of exp.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Creep**
>
> - Creep is **time-dependent permanent deformation** at **T > 0.4 T<sub>m</sub>** under **sustained load**.
> - Three stages: **Primary (decelerating) → Secondary (steady, design-defining) → Tertiary (accelerating to rupture)**.
> - Stage II is **longest** and **used in design**.
> - **Master equation:** ε̇<sub>s</sub> = **A · σⁿ · exp(-Q/RT)** — Norton-Bailey + Arrhenius.
> - **n** = stress exponent (3–8 for metals); determined from **log ε̇ vs. log σ** slope.
> - **Q** = activation energy for creep (≈ self-diffusion Q); determined from **ln ε̇ vs. 1/T** slope.
> - **Larson-Miller Parameter:** P<sub>LM</sub> = **T[log t<sub>r</sub> + 20]**; allows extrapolation of short-lab data to long life.
> - **Master rupture curve:** log σ = **a − b · P<sub>LM</sub>**.
> - Creep resistance ↑ with: **higher melting point, larger grain size, alloying (Mo, W, V, Cr), precipitates (carbides, γ′), single-crystal microstructure**.
> - Creep resistance ↓ with: **higher temperature, higher stress, finer grain size, pure single-phase metals**.
> - **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**, **C ≈ 20**, **1 year ≈ 8760 h**, **100,000 h ≈ 11.4 years** (typical design life).
> - **Typical allowable creep strain** in design: **0.1 % to 1 %** depending on service.
> - Failure by creep is **NOT sudden fracture**; it is **progressive dimensional change** leading to either loss of function (e.g., blade-casing rub) or eventual rupture in Stage III.
> - Two typical KTU problem types: **(i) Compute ε̇<sub>s</sub> and accumulated strain over life**, or **(ii) Compute allowable stress / rupture life via LMP**.
> - **Always check unit consistency** between **h** and **s**, between **MPa** and **Pa** (1 MPa = 10⁶ Pa), and between **log** and **ln**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centrifugal Tension
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Centrifugal Tension

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Centrifugal Tension** is the additional tension induced in a belt, rope, or chain while it is in motion over a pulley, caused by the **centrifugal force** acting on the moving mass of the flexible power transmission element. When the belt rotates continuously around the pulleys, each infinitesimal mass element of the belt experiences an outward radial force. This force pulls the belt away from the pulley surface and manifests itself as a uniform internal tension throughout the belt span — known as **Centrifugal Tension ($T_c$)**.

In formal KTU Machine Design terminology:

> **Centrifugal Tension ($T_c$):** The radial outward pull per unit mass of belt, acting continuously around the pulley due to its rotational velocity, producing a uniform tensile stress equal to $m \cdot v^2$ (where $m$ = mass per unit length of belt in **kg/m** and $v$ = belt velocity in **m/s**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine swinging a stone tied to a string in a circular path over your head.

- The stone pulls **outward** on your hand — that outward pull is essentially the **centrifugal effect**.
- Now replace the stone-string with a **belt wrapped around a spinning pulley**. The belt has mass, and that mass continuously wants to fly outward.
- This outward "flying tendency" creates an additional internal **stretching force** inside the belt itself — this is the **Centrifugal Tension**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuitive Truth:** Centrifugal Tension exists on **BOTH** the tight side and the slack side equally. It does NOT transmit any power; it only **stretches the belt** and **reduces the effective gripping force** between belt and pulley.

## 1.3 Where It Appears in Real Engineering

| Application | Flexible Element Affected |
|---|---|
| Flat Belt Drives | Leather / Rubber / Synthetic Belts |
| V-Belt Drives | V-shaped rubber belts |
| Chain Drives | Roller chains in bicycles, motorbikes |
| Wire Rope Drives | Cranes, elevators, hoists |
| Conveyor Systems | Continuous belt conveyors |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Belt Mass per unit length ($m$): typically **0.1 – 1.5 kg/m** for industrial flat belts
- Belt Velocity ($v$): usually **5 – 30 m/s** for commercial drives
- Centrifugal Tension ($T_c$): in **Newtons (N)**
- Maximum Safe Centrifugal Tension (Design Limit): $T_c \le \dfrac{T_1}{3}$ (classical Shigley/Black design rule)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Whenever a problem on flat belt or V-belt design is given, the first mandatory check is whether **centrifugal tension is included** in the maximum tension equation. Always use $T_{1} = (T_1)' + T_c$ and not $T_1 = (T_1)'$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does Centrifugal Tension Occur? — The Physics

Consider an elemental arc of belt of length **$ds$** on a pulley of radius **$R$**, subtending an angle **$d\theta$** at the center.

- Length of element: $ds = R \cdot d\theta$
- Mass of element: $dm = m \cdot ds = m \cdot R \cdot d\theta$
- The element moves with tangential velocity **$v$** around the pulley.
- To keep this element moving in a circular path, a **centripetal force** is required, directed radially **inward**.
- The belt tension elements ($T$ and $T + dT$) at the two ends of the arc must provide this inward force.
- By Newton's third law, the belt experiences an equal and opposite **outward centrifugal force**.

## 2.2 Derivation of the Governing Equation (Conceptual Flow)

**Step 1:** Consider an elemental arc $ds = R \cdot d\theta$ on the pulley.

**Step 2:** Mass of the elemental arc is $dm = m \cdot R \cdot d\theta$, where **m** is the mass per unit length of the belt.

**Step 3:** This mass rotates in a circular path of radius $R$ at tangential velocity $v$. The required centripetal force is:

$$dF_c = \dfrac{dm \cdot v^2}{R} = \dfrac{m \cdot R \cdot d\theta \cdot v^2}{R} = m \cdot v^2 \cdot d\theta$$

**Step 4:** The radial component of the tensions ($T$) acting at the two ends of the arc must balance this centrifugal force:

$$2T \cdot \sin\left(\dfrac{d\theta}{2}\right) = m \cdot v^2 \cdot d\theta$$

**Step 5:** For very small angles, $\sin(d\theta/2) \approx d\theta/2$:

$$2T \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{2} = m \cdot v^2 \cdot d\theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = m \cdot v^2$$

**Step 6:** This tension is **uniform throughout the belt** because both $m$ and $v$ are constant along the belt length.

## 2.3 Centrifugal Tension in V-Belts

For V-belts, an additional consideration arises — the **centrifugal force also reduces the effective wedging angle** between the belt and the groove. The standard practice is to compute $T_c$ for V-belts using the same formula, but using the **mass of the belt alone** (not the entire belt cross-section per unit length):

$$T_c = m_b \cdot v^2$$

where $m_b$ = mass per unit length of the V-belt (kg/m).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize for KTU Exam**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_c = m \cdot v^2$ | Centrifugal tension (basic) | N |
| 2 | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60}$ | Belt linear velocity from pulley diameter and RPM | m/s |
| 3 | $T_1 = T_c + (T_1)'$ | Maximum (tight side) tension = centrifugal + effective tight tension | N |
| 4 | $T_2 = T_c + (T_2)'$ | Slack side tension = centrifugal + effective slack tension | N |
| 5 | $\dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \theta \csc \alpha}$ | Belt friction / Eytelwein relation (corrected for $T_c$) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \theta}$ | Same relation for flat belts ($\alpha = 90°, \csc\alpha = 1$) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $(T_1)' - (T_2)' = P$ | Effective tension ratio (power equation, where $P$ is in N·m/s = W) | N |
| 8 | $T_c \le \dfrac{T_1}{3}$ | Maximum allowable centrifugal tension (Shigley's design rule) | N |
| 9 | $m = \rho \cdot A$ | Mass per unit length = density × cross-sectional area | kg/m |
| 10 | $P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$ | Power transmitted by belt | W |

> **Quick Notation Key:** $(T_1)'$ and $(T_2)'$ are the *effective* tensions responsible for transmitting power (these alone produce torque transmission). $T_1$ and $T_2$ are the *total* tensions including centrifugal effects.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design Safety:** Knowing $T_c$ helps engineers pre-tension belts correctly. A belt with too little $T_c$ will slip at high speeds; too much $T_c$ means wasted energy and reduced belt life.
- **Speed Limits:** Centrifugal tension is the primary reason belt drives have an **upper operating speed** (~30 m/s for flat belts, ~25 m/s for V-belts).
- **Idler Pulleys and Tensioners:** Designers often pre-stretch belts to counteract the constant radial pull of $T_c$.
- **Failure Diagnosis:** Excessive $T_c$ → belt overheating, glaze formation on pulleys, premature stretching.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Derivation of Centrifugal Tension

We derive $T_c$ rigorously from first principles. Consider a belt passing over a pulley of radius $R$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$. Let the belt's mass per unit length be $m$ kg/m and its linear velocity be $v$ m/s.

**Free-Body Diagram Setup:**

```
        T + dT
         \
          \  (radial inward)
           \___
               \
        T       \   <-- elemental arc ds = R·dθ
       /         \
      /  (radial inward)
     /
   T
```

**Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Take an elemental arc of belt on the pulley of length:

$$ds = R \cdot d\theta$$

where $d\theta$ is the angle subtended at the pulley center.

**Step 2:** The mass of this elemental arc:

$$dm = m \cdot ds = m \cdot R \cdot d\theta$$

**Step 3:** The tangential velocity of the belt equals the linear velocity of the pulley surface:

$$v = \omega \cdot R$$

**Step 4:** This element of mass $dm$ is rotating in a circular path of radius $R$. The required centripetal force is:

$$dF_c = \dfrac{dm \cdot v^2}{R}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $dm = m \cdot R \cdot d\theta$:

$$dF_c = \dfrac{(m \cdot R \cdot d\theta) \cdot v^2}{R} = m \cdot v^2 \cdot d\theta$$

**Step 6:** This centripetal force is supplied by the radial components of the tension forces $T$ and $(T + dT)$ at the ends of the arc. For the radial direction (pointing inward toward the pulley center):

$$2T \cdot \sin\left(\dfrac{d\theta}{2}\right) = dF_c = m \cdot v^2 \cdot d\theta$$

**Step 7:** Apply the small-angle approximation. For infinitesimally small $d\theta$:

$$\sin\left(\dfrac{d\theta}{2}\right) \approx \dfrac{d\theta}{2}$$

**Step 8:** Substitute into the force equation:

$$2T \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{2} = m \cdot v^2 \cdot d\theta$$

$$T \cdot d\theta = m \cdot v^2 \cdot d\theta$$

**Step 9:** Cancel $d\theta$ from both sides (since $d\theta \neq 0$):

$$\boxed{T = m \cdot v^2}$$

This tension $T$ is **independent of $R$** and is **uniform throughout the belt** because both $m$ and $v$ are constants for a given operating condition.

## 3.2 Modification of the Power Transmission Equation

**The Key Correction:**

In the *static* (no centrifugal) analysis, the maximum tension is:

$$T_1^{static} = T_2^{static} \cdot e^{\mu \theta}$$

But with centrifugal tension included, the **effective** tensions that transmit power are:

$$(T_1)' = T_1 - T_c \quad \text{and} \quad (T_2)' = T_2 - T_c$$

The relationship between these effective tensions is governed by friction:

$$\dfrac{(T_1)'}{(T_2)'} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

The **power transmitted** depends only on the effective tensions:

$$P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v = \left[(T_1)' - (T_2)'\right] \cdot v$$

**Important Conclusion:** The centrifugal tension does **not contribute** to power transmission. It only adds a parasitic radial pull that reduces the usable grip.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem Statement:**

A flat belt transmits **10 kW** of power at a belt speed of **15 m/s**. The belt has a mass per unit length of **0.6 kg/m** and runs over a pulley of angle of wrap **$\theta = 180° = \pi$ radians**. The coefficient of friction between belt and pulley is **$\mu = 0.3$**.

Find:
1. The centrifugal tension in the belt.
2. The maximum (tight side) tension $T_1$.
3. The slack side tension $T_2$.
4. Verify if the design satisfies the safe centrifugal tension criterion.

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Power, $P = 10,000$ W
- Belt velocity, $v = 15$ m/s
- Mass per unit length, $m = 0.6$ kg/m
- Angle of wrap, $\theta = \pi$ rad
- Coefficient of friction, $\mu = 0.3$

**Step 1: Compute Centrifugal Tension**

$$T_c = m \cdot v^2 = 0.6 \times (15)^2$$

$$T_c = 0.6 \times 225 = 135 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Compute Effective Tension Difference (from Power Equation)**

$$P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$$

$$T_1 - T_2 = \dfrac{P}{v} = \dfrac{10,000}{15} = 666.67 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Friction Relation (with $T_c$ correction)**

$$\dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

$$e^{\mu \theta} = e^{0.3 \times \pi} = e^{0.9425} \approx 2.566$$

Let $T_2 - T_c = x$. Then $T_1 - T_c = 2.566 \cdot x$.

From Step 2: $T_1 - T_2 = 666.67$ N

$$(T_1 - T_c) - (T_2 - T_c) = 666.67$$

$$2.566x - x = 666.67 \Rightarrow 1.566x = 666.67$$

$$x = \dfrac{666.67}{1.566} \approx 425.7 \text{ N}$$

So:
$$T_2 = T_c + x = 135 + 425.7 = 560.7 \text{ N}$$

$$T_1 = T_c + 2.566x = 135 + 2.566 \times 425.7 = 135 + 1092.4 = 1227.4 \text{ N}$$

**Verification using direct equation:**

$$T_1 - T_2 = 1227.4 - 560.7 = 666.7 \text{ N} \approx 666.67 \text{ N} \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Check the Safe Centrifugal Tension Criterion**

$$\text{Safe limit: } T_c \le \dfrac{T_1}{3}$$

$$\dfrac{T_1}{3} = \dfrac{1227.4}{3} = 409.1 \text{ N}$$

Since $T_c = 135 \text{ N} < 409.1 \text{ N}$, the design **satisfies** the safe criterion. ✓

**Analytical Matrix Summary:**

| Quantity | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Centrifugal Tension ($T_c$) | **135** | N |
| Slack Side Tension ($T_2$) | **560.7** | N |
| Tight Side Tension ($T_1$) | **1227.4** | N |
| Effective Tight Tension $(T_1)'$ | 1092.4 | N |
| Effective Slack Tension $(T_2)'$ | 425.7 | N |
| Power Transmitted | 10000 | W |
| Safe Limit Check ($T_1/3$) | 409.1 N > 135 N | Safe ✓ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Effect of Centrifugal Tension on Belt Drive

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pulley Driver] -->|Rotational Input| B[Belt Moving at Velocity v]
    B --> C[Mass Element dm experiences Centripetal Force]
    C --> D[Centrifugal Reaction Acts Outward]
    D --> E[Induces Uniform Tension Tc = m.v² Throughout Belt]
    E --> F{Where Does Tc Exist?}
    F -->|Tight Side Span| G[T1 = Tc + T1' effective]
    F -->|Slack Side Span| H[T2 = Tc + T2' effective]
    F -->|Both sides equal| I[Tc is constant — no power contribution]
    G --> J[Effective tension T1' - T2' transmits Power]
    H --> J
    I --> K[Net Result: Belt grip reduced]
    J --> L[Pulley Driven]
    K --> M[Design Check: Tc ≤ T1 / 3]
    M -->|Pass| N[Safe Design]
    M -->|Fail| O[Reduce v or use lighter belt]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Centrifugal Tension Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inputs: Power P, Belt Velocity v, Mass per unit length m, Angle theta, CoF mu] --> B[Calculate Tc = m × v²]
    A --> C[Calculate Tension Difference T1 minus T2 = P / v]
    B --> D[Apply Friction Relation: T1 minus Tc / T2 minus Tc = e raised to mu theta]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Solve for T1 and T2 simultaneously]
    E --> F[Compute Effective Tensions T1' and T2']
    E --> G[Verify Tc ≤ T1 / 3]
    F --> H[Compute Belt Width / Cross-section]
    G -->|Pass| I[Final Design OK]
    G -->|Fail| J[Iterate: Reduce v or Change Belt Material]
```

## 4.3 Force Direction Schematic (Text-Based Block Diagram)

```
        INCOMING BELT
              │
              │ ↓ direction of motion
              │
              ●───────► T (tension pulls outward at both ends)
             ╱
            ╱   ↗ T (radial outward, balanced by centrifugal effect)
           ╱   ↗
      ┌───┴───┴───┐
      │  PULLEY   │  ← pulley surface
      │  radius R │     rotates with tangential velocity v
      └───────────┘
             ▲
             │
      dF = dm × v² / R  (centripetal force, pointing inward)
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define centrifugal tension in a belt drive. Why does it occur, and how does it affect the power transmission capacity of the belt?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Centrifugal tension is the additional uniform tension induced in a belt when it moves over a pulley, caused by the centrifugal force acting on the belt's mass due to its rotational motion around the pulley.

**Cause:** As the belt rotates around the pulley at velocity $v$, each mass element $dm$ of the belt experiences a centrifugal force pulling it radially outward. This is balanced by an internal tension in the belt.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$T_c = m \cdot v^2$$

where $m$ = mass per unit length (kg/m) and $v$ = belt velocity (m/s).

**Effect on Power Transmission:** Centrifugal tension does **not contribute** to power transmission. It only adds a parasitic radial pull that **reduces the effective gripping pressure** between the belt and the pulley. The effective tensions are reduced by $T_c$, and hence the maximum transmittable power decreases. The effective power equation becomes:

$$P = (T_1 - T_c) - (T_2 - T_c) \cdot v = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for cause, 1 mark for effect.

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: State the condition for maximum power transmission in a flat belt drive. How does centrifugal tension modify this condition?**

**Model Answer:**

**Without Centrifugal Tension:** Maximum power is transmitted when the tight-side tension is approximately 3 times the slack-side tension, i.e., $T_1 \approx 3T_2$ (for the standard case $e^{\mu\theta} = 3$, requiring $\mu\theta = \ln 3 \approx 1.1$).

**With Centrifugal Tension:** The condition is modified because the effective tension ratio is:

$$\dfrac{(T_1)'}{(T_2)'} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

Maximum power occurs when:

$$T_c = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{3}$$

**Modification:** The presence of centrifugal tension **reduces the maximum power** the belt can transmit because a portion of the tight-side tension is "consumed" as parasitic radial load rather than effective grip.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question B-A.1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

A flat belt drive transmits **20 kW** of power from a motor running at **1440 RPM** to a driven pulley of diameter **400 mm**. The belt has a mass per unit length of **1.2 kg/m**, width **120 mm**, and thickness **10 mm**. The coefficient of friction between belt and pulley is **0.25**, and the angle of wrap is **180°**. The allowable tensile stress for the belt material is **3 MPa**.

**Required:**

**(a)** Calculate the centrifugal tension and verify the safe centrifugal tension criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the maximum tension ($T_1$) and minimum tension ($T_2$) in the belt, and determine the required number of belts if a single belt is insufficient. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:**
- $P = 20,000$ W
- $N = 1440$ RPM
- $d = 400$ mm = 0.4 m
- $m = 1.2$ kg/m
- $b = 120$ mm = 0.12 m
- $t = 10$ mm = 0.01 m
- $\mu = 0.25$
- $\theta = \pi$ rad = 180°
- $\sigma_{allow} = 3$ MPa = $3 \times 10^6$ N/m²

**Step 1: Belt Velocity**

$$v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.4 \times 1440}{60}$$

$$v = \dfrac{1809.56}{60} = 30.16 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Centrifugal Tension**

$$T_c = m \cdot v^2 = 1.2 \times (30.16)^2$$

$$T_c = 1.2 \times 909.6 = 1091.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Safe Centrifugal Criterion Check**

The maximum allowable $T_c$ would depend on $T_1$. We will verify this after computing $T_1$ in part (b).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 4: Tension Difference from Power Equation**

$$T_1 - T_2 = \dfrac{P}{v} = \dfrac{20,000}{30.16} = 663.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Friction Relation (with $T_c$)**

$$\dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu\theta} = e^{0.25 \times \pi} = e^{0.7854}$$

$$e^{0.7854} \approx 2.193$$

Let $T_2 - T_c = x$. Then $T_1 - T_c = 2.193x$.

From Step 4: $T_1 - T_2 = 663.1$ N

$$(2.193x) - x = 663.1 \Rightarrow 1.193x = 663.1$$

$$x = 555.8 \text{ N}$$

So:
$$T_2 = T_c + x = 1091.5 + 555.8 = 1647.3 \text{ N}$$

$$T_1 = T_c + 2.193x = 1091.5 + 2.193 \times 555.8 = 1091.5 + 1218.9 = 2310.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6: Verify Safe $T_c$ Criterion**

$$\dfrac{T_1}{3} = \dfrac{2310.4}{3} = 770.1 \text{ N}$$

But $T_c = 1091.5$ N > 770.1 N → **Design FAILS the safe criterion!** ⚠️

**Step 7: Calculate the Maximum Allowable Belt Velocity**

For safe operation, $T_c \le T_1/3$. Approximating $T_1 \approx T_c + (T_1 - T_2) \cdot e^{\mu\theta}/(e^{\mu\theta}-1)$, we iterate to find $v_{max}$.

Let $T_c = m \cdot v^2 = 1.2 v^2$

For safe operation: $T_c \le T_1/3$, equivalently $T_1 \ge 3T_c$.

Assume $T_1 \approx 3T_c$. Then $T_1 - T_2 \approx 2T_c - 0 = 2T_c$ approximately.

From power: $2T_c \cdot v = 20000$, so $T_c = 10000/v$.

Also $T_c = 1.2 v^2$. Setting equal:

$$1.2 v^2 = \dfrac{10,000}{v} \Rightarrow v^3 = 8333.3 \Rightarrow v \approx 20.2 \text{ m/s}$$

So **reduce pulley diameter or speed** to bring belt velocity below 20.2 m/s.

**Step 8: Number of Belts**

Cross-sectional area of belt: $A = b \cdot t = 0.12 \times 0.01 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m²

Maximum tension capacity of single belt: $T_{max,single} = \sigma_{allow} \cdot A = 3 \times 10^6 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} = 3600$ N

Since $T_1 = 2310.4$ N < 3600 N, **a single belt is sufficient** in terms of strength.

**Final Answer:**
- $T_c = 1091.5$ N
- $T_1 = 2310.4$ N
- $T_2 = 1647.3$ N
- Safe $T_c$ criterion FAILS → reduce belt velocity to $\le 20$ m/s
- Number of belts required: **1** (strength-wise), but speed must be reduced.

---

### Question B-A.2 — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

A V-belt drive is to transmit **15 kW** at a pulley speed of **1500 RPM**. The pulley diameter is **250 mm**. The V-belt has a mass per unit length of **0.8 kg/m** and the groove angle is **38°**. The coefficient of friction is **0.2** and the angle of wrap is **160°**. Take the allowable tensile stress for the V-belt as **2.5 MPa**, and the cross-sectional area of the V-belt as **$A = 750$ mm²**.

**Required:**

**(a)** Calculate the centrifugal tension in the V-belt. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the maximum tension ($T_1$) and verify the design against the safe centrifugal tension criterion. Find the number of V-belts required if necessary. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:**
- $P = 15,000$ W
- $N = 1500$ RPM
- $d = 250$ mm = 0.25 m
- $m = 0.8$ kg/m
- $\alpha = 38°/2 = 19°$ (half groove angle)
- $\mu = 0.2$
- $\theta = 160° = 160\pi/180 = 2.793$ rad
- $\sigma_{allow} = 2.5$ MPa
- $A = 750$ mm² = $7.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m²

**Step 1: Belt Velocity**

$$v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.25 \times 1500}{60} = \dfrac{1178.1}{60} = 19.635 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Centrifugal Tension in V-Belt**

$$T_c = m \cdot v^2 = 0.8 \times (19.635)^2$$

$$T_c = 0.8 \times 385.5 = 308.4 \text{ N}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 3: Tension Difference (Power Equation)**

$$T_1 - T_2 = \dfrac{P}{v} = \dfrac{15,000}{19.635} = 763.9 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Friction Relation for V-Belt**

For V-belts, the effective coefficient is $\mu' = \mu / \sin\alpha$.

$$\sin(19°) = 0.3256$$

$$\mu' = \dfrac{0.2}{0.3256} = 0.6142$$

$$e^{\mu'\theta} = e^{0.6142 \times 2.793} = e^{1.715} = 5.557$$

**Step 5: Solve the System**

$$\dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu'\theta} = 5.557$$

Let $T_2 - T_c = x$. Then $T_1 - T_c = 5.557x$.

From Step 3:
$$5.557x - x = 763.9 \Rightarrow 4.557x = 763.9$$

$$x = 167.6 \text{ N}$$

So:
$$T_2 = T_c + x = 308.4 + 167.6 = 476.0 \text{ N}$$

$$T_1 = T_c + 5.557x = 308.4 + 5.557 \times 167.6 = 308.4 + 931.4 = 1239.8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6: Verify Safe $T_c$ Criterion**

$$\dfrac{T_1}{3} = \dfrac{1239.8}{3} = 413.3 \text{ N}$$

Since $T_c = 308.4$ N < 413.3 N → **Design PASSES** the safe criterion. ✓

**Step 7: Number of V-Belts**

Maximum tension capacity of single belt: $T_{max,single} = \sigma_{allow} \cdot A$

$$T_{max,single} = 2.5 \times 10^6 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-4} = 1875 \text{ N}$$

Since $T_1 = 1239.8$ N < 1875 N → **Single V-belt is sufficient**.

**Final Answer:**
- $T_c = 308.4$ N
- $T_1 = 1239.8$ N
- $T_2 = 476.0$ N
- Safe criterion: PASSED
- Number of belts: **1**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students commonly lose marks on these types of questions due to the following mistakes:
>
> 1. **Using the wrong friction relation:** Students often write $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu\theta}$ **without subtracting $T_c$**. With centrifugal tension, the correct form is $\dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu\theta}$ (for flat belts) or $e^{\mu\theta/\sin\alpha}$ (for V-belts). **Lost marks: 2-3 per question.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to verify the safe $T_c$ criterion** ($T_c \le T_1/3$). This is a mandatory check and is worth **1 mark** in most KTU problems.
>
> 3. **Confusing $T_1$ with $(T_1)'$**: Effective tension $(T_1)'$ is the tension responsible for power transmission, while $T_1$ is the total tension including centrifugal effect. The belt strength must be checked against $T_1$, **not** $(T_1)'$.
>
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Forgetting to convert mm to m or RPM to rad/s in velocity calculation leads to wrong $T_c$. Always convert before substituting.
>
> 5. **Not stating "Single belt sufficient" or "n belts required":** Always conclude with the number of belts — examiners look for this explicit conclusion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Centrifugal Tension**

- **Definition:** $T_c = m \cdot v^2$ — uniform additional tension due to belt's mass moving in a circular path.

- **Independent of:** Pulley radius $R$ (cancels out in derivation).

- **Exists equally** on **both tight and slack sides** — same value throughout.

- **Does NOT transmit power** — only reduces effective grip.

- **Effective tensions:** $(T_1)' = T_1 - T_c$ and $(T_2)' = T_2 - T_c$ — these alone transmit power.

- **Friction relation (corrected):** $\dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu\theta}$ (flat belt) or $e^{\mu\theta\csc\alpha}$ (V-belt).

- **Safe Design Criterion (Shigley):** $T_c \le \dfrac{T_1}{3}$. If violated, reduce belt velocity $v$ or use a lighter belt (smaller $m$).

- **Velocity formula:** $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60}$ (SI: m/s with $d$ in m, $N$ in RPM).

- **Mass per unit length:** $m = \rho \cdot A$ (for solid belt) or $m_{V-belt}$ (from manufacturer data).

- **Typical safe operating velocity:** 5 – 25 m/s for flat belts; up to 30 m/s for V-belts.

- **Power equation remains:** $P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$ — centrifugal tension cancels out in the power difference.

- **Maximum power condition (with $T_c$):** Occurs when $T_c \approx \dfrac{(T_1)' - (T_2)'}{3}$ — a shift from the classical $T_1 = 3T_2$ rule.

- **Always compute $T_c$ FIRST** in any belt design problem, then solve the system of equations using $P/v$ and the friction relation.

- **Conclusion format in KTU answer:** State $T_c$, $T_1$, $T_2$, verify safe criterion, conclude number of belts.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of V-Belt Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of V-Belt Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **V-Belt Drive** is a friction-based power transmission system in which a flexible belt with a trapezoidal cross-section (V-shape) runs in matched V-grooved pulleys. The wedging action of the belt into the pulley groove generates higher frictional grip than a flat belt, allowing greater power transmission in a compact space. It is the most widely used belt drive in industrial machinery (machine tools, compressors, conveyors, automotive auxiliaries).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The design of a V-belt drive involves the selection of a standard belt section, determining the number of belts, and verifying the design using IS/ISO standard power ratings and correction factors.

## 1.2 Standard V-Belt Cross-Sections

The Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 2494) and ISO 4184 classify V-belts into standard sections:

| Section | Top Width $b$ (mm) | Depth $h$ (mm) | Groove Angle $2\alpha$ (°) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **A** | 13 | 8 | 34 |
| **B** | 17 | 11 | 34 |
| **C** | 22 | 14 | 34 |
| **D** | 32 | 19 | 34 |
| **E** | 38 | 23 | 34 |

For the smallest standard section (SPA, SPZ), the groove angle is **32°**; for the largest section (SPC), it becomes **40°**. As the pulley diameter increases, the groove angle is enlarged to compensate for the deformation of the belt under bending.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a V-Belt like a car tire wedged into a pothole:**

Imagine you press a flat plank against a rotating drum — it slips easily because the normal force is just your push. Now imagine wedging a **V-shaped rubber strip** into a matching groove — the harder you pull, the more the groove walls squeeze the rubber against itself (the **wedging effect** or **self-energizing wedge action**). This multiplies the friction force automatically. That is exactly how a V-belt works: the side walls of the groove apply normal pressure on the slanted sides of the belt, and friction on **two faces** instead of one generates 2-3 times more grip than a flat belt.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Examples:**
> - The serpentine belt driving the alternator, water pump, and AC compressor in a car.
> - The drive belt on a lathe spindle connecting the motor to the headstock.
> - Industrial fans and centrifugal pumps in process plants.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Definitions)

- **Angle of Wrap (θ):** The angle (in radians) over which the belt remains in contact with the pulley.
- **Centrifugal Tension (T_c):** The radial pull on the belt due to its mass moving in a circular path; it reduces effective tension.
- **Effective Tension:** $(T_1 - T_c)$, i.e., the slack-side and tight-side tensions after removing centrifugal effects.
- **Rated Power (P_r):** The maximum power a single belt can transmit at a given speed and small-pulley diameter, as catalogued by the manufacturer.
- **Correction Factor (K):** A multiplier (<1) that reduces the rated power to account for arc of contact, belt length, and service conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Physical Constants Used:**
> - Coefficient of friction for V-belt on cast iron pulley: $\mu = 0.25$ to $0.30$
> - Density of rubber-impregnated fabric belt: $\rho = 1000$ to $1200\ \text{kg/m}^3$
> - Mass per unit length $m$ is given by manufacturer data (typically **0.06 to 0.45 kg/m** depending on section).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — How Power is Transmitted

A V-belt transmits power by **friction** between its two slanted sides and the matching pulley walls. The mechanics can be broken into the following logical sequence:

1. **Belt Tensioning:** The belt has a tight side ($T_1$) and slack side ($T_2$).
2. **Wedging Effect:** When belt tensions pull on the wedge, the resultant force pushes the belt outward against the groove walls. The normal reaction $N$ on each side becomes $N = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot \sin\alpha / 2$ per side.
3. **Friction Force:** Total friction = $2\mu N = \mu(T_1 - T_2)\sin\alpha$. Substituting gives the **belt friction equation**:
4. **Centrifugal Tension:** As the belt moves at speed $v$ around the pulley, its own weight generates a centrifugal pull $T_c$ that reduces the effective grip.
5. **Power Equation:** Power transmitted $P = (T_1 - T_2)\cdot v$ in watts (with $v$ in m/s and tensions in N).

## 2.2 Design Procedure — Logical Flow

The KTU-recommended step-by-step design flow:

1. **Identify Input Data:** Driven machine power, speeds of driving and driven pulleys, service conditions, desired center distance.
2. **Calculate Belt Speed:** $v = \pi d_1 n_1 / 60$
3. **Select Belt Section:** Choose section A/B/C/D/E from catalogue charts based on speed × power.
4. **Determine Small Pulley Diameter:** From the section chart, pick the smallest recommended $d_1$.
5. **Calculate Large Pulley Diameter:** $d_2 = d_1 \times (n_1/n_2)$
6. **Compute Belt Length & Center Distance:** Use the open-belt length formula.
7. **Compute Angle of Wrap on Smaller Pulley:** $\theta_s = \pi - 2\alpha$ where $\sin\alpha = (r_1 - r_2)/C$.
8. **Compute Centrifugal Tension:** $T_c = m v^2$.
9. **Calculate Tensions using Friction Equation.**
10. **Determine Power per Belt:** $P_1 = (T_1 - T_2)v$.
11. **Apply Correction Factors:** $K_\theta$ (arc of contact), $K_L$ (belt length).
12. **Calculate Number of Belts:** $n_b = P_d / (P_r \cdot K_\theta \cdot K_L)$ (rounded up).
13. **Verify Initial Tension and Shaft Loading.**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Part B question.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Belt velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_1 \cdot n_1}{60}$ | m/s | $d_1$ in m, $n_1$ in rpm |
| 2 | Centrifugal tension | $T_c = m \cdot v^2$ | N | $m$ in kg/m |
| 3 | Slip ratio (velocity) | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot n}{60}(1 - s)$ | m/s | $s \approx 0.01$ to $0.02$ |
| 4 | Belt friction equation | $\dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu\theta / \sin\alpha}$ | dimensionless | $\theta$ in radians |
| 5 | Power per belt | $P = (T_1 - T_2)\cdot v$ | W | Tension in N |
| 6 | Angle of wrap (small) | $\theta_s = \pi - 2\alpha$ | rad | Open belt |
| 7 | Angle of wrap (large) | $\theta_L = \pi + 2\alpha$ | rad | Open belt |
| 8 | Sin α (open belt) | $\sin\alpha = \dfrac{r_1 - r_2}{C}$ | — | $r_2 > r_1$ |
| 9 | Belt length (open) | $L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(d_1+d_2) + \dfrac{(d_2 - d_1)^2}{4C}$ | m | Standard open-belt formula |
| 10 | Angle factor | $K_\theta = 1.05\left(1 - e^{-\theta_s/\pi}\right)$ | — | Empirical |
| 11 | Length factor | $K_L$ from catalogue | — | Depends on section |
| 12 | Number of belts | $n_b = \dfrac{P_d}{P_r \cdot K_\theta \cdot K_L}$ | — | Round up to integer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Belt Length Equation — Derivation Logic:** The total belt length = (two straight runs) + (arc on small pulley) + (arc on large pulley). For an open belt drive with center distance $C$, the angle of lap on the smaller pulley is $\pi - 2\alpha$ and on the larger is $\pi + 2\alpha$. Summing these arcs and the straight runs (each of length $\sqrt{C^2 - (r_1-r_2)^2}$) yields the standard formula shown above.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why V-Belts are preferred over Flat Belts in industry:**
> - 2 to 3 times higher power transmission capacity at the same belt width.
> - Self-energizing wedge action — grip increases automatically with load.
> - Can transmit power over shorter center distances with high velocity ratios.
> - Easier alignment and replacement (matched standard sections).
>
> **Where they are NOT preferred:** Very long-distance power transmission (use flat belts), exact timing applications (use timing/synchronous belts), or environments with oil/chemicals (use chain drives).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Belt Friction Equation for a V-Belt

We consider an elemental belt arc subtending angle $d\theta$ at the pulley center. Let the tensions on the two sides of the element be $T$ and $T + dT$, and the normal reaction on each V-groove side wall be $dN$.

**Step 1 — Force balance in the tangential direction:**
The friction force $\mu \, dN$ on each of the two slanted sides opposes motion.

$$
\mu \, dN + \mu \, dN = dT
$$

$$
2\mu \, dN = dT \quad \text{(Equation 1)}
$$

**Step 2 — Force balance in the radial direction:**
The two normal reactions $dN$ have a radial inward component each equal to $dN \cdot \sin\alpha$ (since each wall is inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal). The tension $T$ pulling on either side contributes a radial outward component $T \cdot \sin(d\theta/2) \approx T \, d\theta/2$ on each side.

$$
T \sin\!\left(\dfrac{d\theta}{2}\right) + \bigl(T + dT\bigr)\sin\!\left(\dfrac{d\theta}{2}\right) = 2 \, dN \cdot \sin\alpha
$$

For small $d\theta$, $\sin(d\theta/2) \approx d\theta/2$. Also, $dT \cdot d\theta$ is negligible.

$$
T \, d\theta = 2 \, dN \cdot \sin\alpha
$$

$$
dN = \dfrac{T \, d\theta}{2 \sin\alpha} \quad \text{(Equation 2)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Equation 2 into Equation 1:**

$$
2\mu \cdot \dfrac{T \, d\theta}{2 \sin\alpha} = dT
$$

$$
\dfrac{dT}{T} = \dfrac{\mu}{\sin\alpha} \, d\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate between the tight-side tension $T_1$ (at $\theta = 0$) and slack-side tension $T_2$ (at $\theta = \theta_s$):**

$$
\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \dfrac{dT}{T} = \dfrac{\mu}{\sin\alpha}\int_{0}^{\theta_s} d\theta
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \dfrac{\mu \, \theta_s}{\sin\alpha}
$$

$$
\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta_s / \sin\alpha}
$$

**Step 5 — Account for Centrifugal Tension:**

When the belt moves at speed $v$, centrifugal tension $T_c$ acts equally on both tight and slack sides. Subtracting $T_c$ from each side gives the **effective tension ratio**:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \theta_s / \sin\alpha}}
$$

This is the governing equation that distinguishes V-belt analysis from flat-belt analysis (in a flat belt, $\sin\alpha = 1$, recovering the classical equation).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Sample KTU-Style Design Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (Typical 14-mark KTU style):**
>
> Design a V-belt drive to transmit **10 kW** at **1440 rpm** driving a compressor running at **720 rpm**. The center distance should be approximately **1000 mm**. The drive operates 16 hours/day under moderate shock conditions. Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.25$, groove angle $2\alpha = 34°$, belt mass $m = 0.3$ kg/m.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Belt Velocity and Pulley Sizes:**
Assuming a small pulley diameter of $d_1 = 125$ mm (typical for a 10 kW drive):

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi \, d_1 \, n_1}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.125 \times 1440}{60} = 9.425\ \text{m/s}
$$

Speed ratio: $i = 1440/720 = 2.0$

$$
d_2 = i \cdot d_1 = 2.0 \times 125 = 250\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Belt Length (Open Belt, $C = 1000$ mm):**

$$
L = 2(1000) + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(125 + 250) + \dfrac{(250 - 125)^2}{4 \times 1000}
$$

$$
L = 2000 + 588.97 + 3.906 = 2592.9\ \text{mm} \approx 2593\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Angle of Wrap on Smaller Pulley:**

$$
\sin\alpha = \dfrac{r_1 - r_2}{C} = \dfrac{62.5 - 125}{1000} = \dfrac{-62.5}{1000}
$$

Note: $r_2 > r_1$ here, so $\sin\alpha$ must use $|r_2 - r_1|$, and $\alpha$ is taken positive:

$$
\sin\alpha = \dfrac{125 - 62.5}{1000} = 0.0625 \implies \alpha = 3.584°
$$

$$
\theta_s = \pi - 2\alpha = 180° - 7.17° = 172.83° = 3.017\ \text{rad}
$$

**Step 4 — Centrifugal Tension:**

$$
T_c = m \, v^2 = 0.3 \times (9.425)^2 = 0.3 \times 88.83 = 26.65\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Tension Ratio:**

With $\mu = 0.25$, $\theta_s = 3.017$ rad, $\sin\alpha = 0.2874$ (using half groove angle for section B: $2\alpha = 34°$, so $\alpha = 17°$, $\sin 17° = 0.2924$):

Wait — careful! The $\alpha$ in the friction equation is **half the groove angle**, not the belt-angle-of-tilt. Let me redo:

Half groove angle $\alpha = 17°$, $\sin 17° = 0.2924$.

$$
\dfrac{\mu \, \theta_s}{\sin\alpha} = \dfrac{0.25 \times 3.017}{0.2924} = 2.580
$$

$$
e^{2.580} = 13.19
$$

So:
$$
\dfrac{T_1 - 26.65}{T_2 - 26.65} = 13.19
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Tension in Belt:**
For a section B belt, maximum allowable tension $T_1 = 450$ N (from catalogue).

$$
T_2 - 26.65 = \dfrac{450 - 26.65}{13.19} = \dfrac{423.35}{13.19} = 32.10\ \text{N}
$$

$$
T_2 = 58.75\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 7 — Power per Belt:**

$$
P_{\text{belt}} = (T_1 - T_2)\cdot v = (450 - 58.75)\times 9.425 = 3685\ \text{W} \approx 3.69\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 8 — Apply Correction Factors:**
- $K_\theta = 1.05\left(1 - e^{-172.83/180}\right) = 1.05(1 - e^{-0.96}) = 1.05(1 - 0.383) = 1.05 \times 0.617 = 0.648$

Actually, the standard empirical formula commonly used:

$$
K_\theta = 1.05\left(1 - e^{-\theta_s/\pi}\right) = 1.05\left(1 - e^{-3.017/\pi}\right) = 1.05\left(1 - e^{-0.961}\right) = 1.05(1 - 0.382) = 0.648
$$

Wait, the correct form gives values near 1.0 for $\theta = 180°$. A more accurate empirical form is:

$$
K_\theta = 0.7 + 0.3\left(\dfrac{\theta_s}{\pi}\right)
$$

For $\theta_s = 172.83° = 3.017$ rad:

$$
K_\theta = 0.7 + 0.3\left(\dfrac{3.017}{\pi}\right) = 0.7 + 0.288 = 0.988
$$

- $K_L = 0.95$ (from catalogue for belt length 2593 mm in section B).
- $K_s = 0.9$ (service factor for moderate shock).

**Step 9 — Number of Belts:**

$$
n_b = \dfrac{P_d \cdot K_s}{P_r \cdot K_\theta \cdot K_L} = \dfrac{10 \times 0.9}{3.69 \times 0.988 \times 0.95} = \dfrac{9.0}{3.464} = 2.6
$$

$$
\boxed{n_b = 3\ \text{belts (round up)}}
$$

**Step 10 — Initial Tension:**

$$
T_0 = \dfrac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + T_c = \dfrac{450 + 58.75}{2} + 26.65 = 281.0\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 11 — Shaft Load (for bearing design check):**

$$
F_{\text{shaft}} = T_1 + T_2 + 2T_c = 450 + 58.75 + 53.30 = 562.05\ \text{N}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The final design requires:**
> - 3 belts of section **B**
> - Pulley diameters: $d_1 = 125$ mm, $d_2 = 250$ mm
> - Center distance $C = 1000$ mm (adjustable by ±25 mm to match standard belt length)
> - Initial tension $T_0 = 281$ N per belt
> - Shaft load $F = 562$ N
