# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is the wedge effect in a V-belt drive? How does it enhance power transmission compared to a flat belt?**

**Model Answer:**
The wedge effect (or self-energizing action) arises because the V-belt is wedged into the matching V-groove of the pulley. As the belt tensions ($T_1$ and $T_2$) increase, the resultant force pushes the belt outward, which in turn increases the normal reaction on the slanted groove walls. Friction thus acts on **two inclined surfaces** instead of one flat surface.

The friction equation for a V-belt is:

$$
\dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \theta / \sin\alpha}
$$

For a flat belt, $\alpha = 90°$, so $\sin\alpha = 1$. For a V-belt, $\alpha = 17°$ (section B), so $\sin\alpha \approx 0.292$. The exponent $\mu \theta / \sin\alpha$ becomes approximately **3.4 times larger**, allowing 3–4 times higher tension ratio and hence 2–3 times more power transmission at the same belt width.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define centrifugal tension in a belt drive and state its effect on power transmission.**

**Model Answer:**
**Centrifugal Tension** is the inward radial pull generated in a belt due to its mass moving in a circular path around the pulley. It is given by:

$$
T_c = m \cdot v^2
$$

where $m$ is mass per unit length (kg/m) and $v$ is belt velocity (m/s).

**Effect on Power Transmission:**
1. Centrifugal tension acts equally on both tight and slack sides. Subtracting it from each side reduces the **effective tension** ($T_1 - T_c$) and ($T_2 - T_c$).
2. This reduces the **effective grip** of the belt on the pulley.
3. As belt speed increases, $T_c$ increases as $v^2$, which can drastically reduce transmitted power if $v > 30$ m/s. Therefore, V-belt speeds are typically limited to **20–25 m/s** for standard sections.
4. Centrifugal tension also adds to the shaft load, increasing bearing reaction forces.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**A 15 kW, 1440 rpm electric motor drives a compressor running at 480 rpm. The drive is to operate 12 hours/day under light shock conditions. Design a V-belt drive using the following data:**

- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.28$
- Groove angle $2\alpha = 34°$
- Belt density and mass per unit length (section C): $m = 0.4$ kg/m
- Center distance $C \approx 800$ mm
- Service factor $K_s = 0.9$

**Calculate:**
**(a)** Pulley diameters and belt length. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Number of belts required, initial tension, and shaft load. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**Step 1 — Identify Speed Ratio and Belt Velocity:**

$$
i = \dfrac{n_1}{n_2} = \dfrac{1440}{480} = 3.0
$$

Assuming small pulley diameter $d_1 = 160$ mm (section C standard for 15 kW):

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi \, d_1 \, n_1}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.16 \times 1440}{60} = 12.06\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Large Pulley Diameter:**

$$
d_2 = i \cdot d_1 = 3.0 \times 160 = 480\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Belt Length (Open Belt):**

$$
L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(d_1 + d_2) + \dfrac{(d_2 - d_1)^2}{4C}
$$

$$
L = 2(800) + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(160 + 480) + \dfrac{(480 - 160)^2}{4 \times 800}
$$

$$
L = 1600 + 1005.31 + 102.4 = 2707.7\ \text{mm}
$$

Adopt standard belt length $L = 2800$ mm and recompute $C$ iteratively, or accept $C \approx 850$ mm.

**Step 4 — Angle of Wrap (Small Pulley):**

$$
\sin\alpha_{\text{tilt}} = \dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{C} = \dfrac{240 - 80}{850} = 0.188
$$

Tilt angle $\alpha_{\text{tilt}} = 10.84°$. Half groove angle = 17°.

Total angle on small pulley:

$$
\theta_s = 180° - 2\alpha_{\text{tilt}} = 180° - 21.68° = 158.32° = 2.764\ \text{rad}
$$

**Step 5 — Centrifugal Tension:**

$$
T_c = m \cdot v^2 = 0.4 \times (12.06)^2 = 0.4 \times 145.4 = 58.18\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Friction Equation:**
With $\mu = 0.28$, $\theta_s = 2.764$ rad, $\sin 17° = 0.292$:

$$
\dfrac{\mu \, \theta_s}{\sin\alpha} = \dfrac{0.28 \times 2.764}{0.292} = 2.651
$$

$$
e^{2.651} = 14.18
$$

**Step 7 — Maximum Tension:**
For section C belt, catalogue maximum tension $T_1 = 1050$ N.

$$
T_2 - T_c = \dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{e^{\mu\theta/\sin\alpha}} = \dfrac{1050 - 58.18}{14.18} = \dfrac{991.82}{14.18} = 69.95\ \text{N}
$$

$$
T_2 = 128.13\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 8 — Power per Belt:**

$$
P_{\text{belt}} = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v = (1050 - 128.13) \times 12.06 = 11111\ \text{W} \approx 11.11\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 9 — Correction Factors:**
- $K_\theta = 0.7 + 0.3 \times (158.32/180) = 0.7 + 0.264 = 0.964$
- $K_L = 0.97$ (from catalogue)
- $K_s = 0.9$ (given)

**Step 10 — Number of Belts:**

$$
n_b = \dfrac{P_d}{P_r \cdot K_\theta \cdot K_L} = \dfrac{15}{11.11 \times 0.964 \times 0.97} = \dfrac{15}{10.39} = 1.44
$$

$$
\boxed{n_b = 2\ \text{belts of section C}}
$$

**Step 11 — Initial Tension:**

$$
T_0 = \dfrac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + T_c = \dfrac{1050 + 128.13}{2} + 58.18 = 647.25\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 12 — Shaft Load:**

$$
F = T_1 + T_2 + 2T_c = 1050 + 128.13 + 116.36 = 1294.49\ \text{N}
$$

**Summary of Design:**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Belt section | **C** |
| Small pulley diameter | **160 mm** |
| Large pulley diameter | **480 mm** |
| Center distance | **850 mm** |
| Belt length | **2800 mm** |
| Number of belts | **2** |
| Initial tension (per belt) | **647.25 N** |
| Shaft load | **1294.49 N** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**A V-belt drive transmits 7.5 kW from a 1450 rpm motor to a mill running at 725 rpm. Using the following data, determine:**

**(a)** Belt velocity, pulley diameters, and angle of wrap on the smaller pulley. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Tensions in the tight and slack sides, and verify whether section B belt is suitable. **(7 marks)**

**Data:** $\mu = 0.25$, groove angle = 34°, $m = 0.25$ kg/m for section B, $C = 900$ mm, $T_{\max} = 600$ N.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Part 1 — Velocity & Diameters:**

Speed ratio:

$$
i = \dfrac{1450}{725} = 2.0
$$

Choose $d_1 = 125$ mm:

$$
v = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.125 \times 1450}{60} = 9.49\ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
d_2 = 2.0 \times 125 = 250\ \text{mm}
$$

**(a) Part 2 — Belt Length:**

$$
L = 2(900) + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(125 + 250) + \dfrac{(250 - 125)^2}{4 \times 900}
$$

$$
L = 1800 + 588.97 + 4.34 = 2393\ \text{mm} \approx 2400\ \text{mm (standard)}
$$

**(a) Part 3 — Angle of Wrap (Small):**

$$
\sin\alpha_{\text{tilt}} = \dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{C} = \dfrac{125 - 62.5}{900} = 0.0694
$$

Tilt angle = $3.98°$.

$$
\theta_s = 180° - 2(3.98°) = 172.04° = 3.003\ \text{rad}
$$

**(b) Part 1 — Centrifugal Tension:**

$$
T_c = m \, v^2 = 0.25 \times (9.49)^2 = 22.51\ \text{N}
$$

**(b) Part 2 — Friction Equation:**
Half groove angle = 17°, $\sin 17° = 0.2924$:

$$
\dfrac{\mu \theta_s}{\sin\alpha} = \dfrac{0.25 \times 3.003}{0.2924} = 2.568
$$

$$
e^{2.568} = 13.04
$$

**(b) Part 3 — Solve for Tensions:**

With $T_1 = 600$ N (given maximum for section B):

$$
T_2 - T_c = \dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{e^{\mu\theta/\sin\alpha}} = \dfrac{600 - 22.51}{13.04} = 44.29\ \text{N}
$$

$$
T_2 = 66.80\ \text{N}
$$

**(b) Part 4 — Power Transmitted:**

$$
P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v = (600 - 66.80) \times 9.49 = 5062\ \text{W} = 5.06\ \text{kW}
$$

**(b) Part 5 — Verification for Section B:**

Required power = 7.5 kW. Power per belt = 5.06 kW. With correction factors $K_\theta \approx 0.98$, $K_L \approx 0.96$:

$$
P_{\text{effective}} = 5.06 \times 0.98 \times 0.96 = 4.76\ \text{kW per belt}
$$

Number of belts:

$$
n_b = \dfrac{7.5}{4.76} = 1.58 \implies 2\ \text{belts required}
$$

**Section B is suitable**, but two belts are needed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the half-groove angle α with the belt-tilt angle.** In the friction equation, $\sin\alpha$ refers to the half V-groove angle (17° for section B/C/D/E, NOT the tilt angle of the belt around the pulleys).
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract centrifugal tension** from both $T_1$ and $T_2$. Many students write $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta/\sin\alpha}$ which is wrong — the correct form is $(T_1 - T_c)/(T_2 - T_c) = e^{\mu\theta/\sin\alpha}$.
> 3. **Confusing belt length with center distance.** The belt length $L$ is always longer than $2C$ due to the arcs around the pulleys.
> 4. **Not rounding up the number of belts.** The integer must always be rounded UP, never to the nearest integer.
> 5. **Mixing up the small pulley and large pulley in the angle of wrap.** Use $\theta_s$ for the small pulley (smaller angle) and $\theta_L$ for the larger.
> 6. **Missing correction factors.** Many students forget $K_\theta$, $K_L$, and service factor $K_s$ — examiners specifically allocate marks for these.
> 7. **Not stating the units** of velocity (m/s), tensions (N), and diameters (mm or m) — consistency matters in board exams.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — V-Belt Drive Design (Module 4)**

- ✅ V-belt transmits power by **friction on two inclined surfaces** (wedge effect).
- ✅ **Friction equation:** $\dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \theta / \sin\alpha}$ where $\alpha$ = half groove angle.
- ✅ **Centrifugal tension:** $T_c = m \cdot v^2$ (proportional to $v^2$).
- ✅ **Power per belt:** $P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$.
- ✅ **Belt length (open belt):** $L = 2C + (\pi/2)(d_1+d_2) + (d_2-d_1)^2/(4C)$.
- ✅ **Angle of wrap (small pulley):** $\theta_s = \pi - 2\alpha_{\text{tilt}}$ where $\sin\alpha_{\text{tilt}} = (r_2 - r_1)/C$.
- ✅ **Standard sections:** A (13 mm top), B (17 mm), C (22 mm), D (32 mm), E (38 mm).
- ✅ **Correction factors:** $K_\theta$ (angle), $K_L$ (length), $K_s$ (service/shock).
- ✅ **Number of belts:** $n_b = P_d / (P_r \cdot K_\theta \cdot K_L)$ — always **round up**.
- ✅ **Initial tension:** $T_0 = (T_1 + T_2)/2 + T_c$.
- ✅ **Shaft load:** $F = T_1 + T_2 + 2T_c$.
- ✅ **Belt speed limits:** typically **20–25 m/s** for standard V-belts (higher $v$ → high $T_c$ → reduced grip).
- ✅ **Coefficient of friction:** $\mu = 0.25$ to $0.30$ (rubber on cast iron).
- ✅ **V-belt advantage over flat belt:** 2–3× higher power transmission for same belt width.
- ✅ **Failure modes to check:** slip, belt breakage, excessive shaft load, bearing wear.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Limitations of V-Belt Drive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Advantages and Limitations of V-Belt Drive # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **V-Belt Drive** is a friction-based power transmission system that uses a flexible, endless belt with a trapezoidal (V-shaped) cross-section running in matched grooved pulleys. The trapezoidal geometry creates a **wedging action** in the pulley groove, which amplifies the normal reaction force between the belt and pulley, thereby multiplying the frictional grip compared to a flat belt of the same width and tension. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (Module 4 – Belt Drives):** > The V-belt is the most widely used industrial belt. The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes the **geometric amplification of the coefficient of friction (μ)** through the groove angle (α), and the resulting design equations must explicitly use the term `μ / sin(α/2)` instead of μ (used for flat belts). **Standard Designations & Geometry:** | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Industrial Value | |---|---|---| | Groove angle of pulley | α | **40°** (standard), **38°** (some manufacturers) | | Standard V-belt sections | A, B, C, D, E | Cross-section width grows from A to E | | Belt linear density | ρ | **1200 kg/m³** (rubber) | | Coefficient of friction (rubber–cast iron) | μ | **0.25 to 0.40** | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Think of it like this — the "Doorstop Wedge Principle":** > Imagine trying to push a heavy door open by pulling on a flat rope tied to it. The rope slips easily because only a small normal force is generated. Now, replace the rope with a **wedge-shaped strap** that fits into a V-groove carved into the door handle. As soon as you pull, the V-groove squeezes the strap inward, multiplying the friction. The very same "squeezing" is what the pulley groove does to a V-belt — the angled side-walls of the groove **jam** the belt inward, generating a normal force that is **1 / sin(α/2) times larger** than the tension component alone. This is why a tiny V-belt can transmit 3× the power of a much wider flat belt. ## 1.3 Working Principle at a Glance 1. The driving pulley (driver) rotates, dragging the belt by friction. 2. The wedging action in the groove magnifies the friction force. 3. The belt transmits this frictional force to the driven pulley. 4. The driven pulley rotates, delivering mechanical power to the load. 5. Centrifugal tension $T_c$ develops due to the belt's mass moving in a circular path, which slightly reduces the effective driving tension. > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 scheme treats the V-belt drive as a **frictional, non-positive** drive. Unlike chain or gear drives, there is always a possibility of microscopic slip (creep) due to elastic stretch, although gross slip is essentially absent in well-designed drives. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Wedging Action — The "Why" Behind V-Belt Superiority When a V-belt sits inside a V-groove, the **contact normal force N** is no longer perpendicular to the belt's flat side (as in a flat belt). Instead, the two groove side-walls press the belt inward at an angle. Resolving the tension $T$ at the groove wall: - Component perpendicular to groove wall: $N = T \cdot \sin(\alpha/2)$ (per wall, half of the belt) - Component parallel to groove wall (causing friction): $F_f = \mu N = \mu T \sin(\alpha/2)$ Because there are **two** contact walls, the **net frictional resistance** is roughly $2 \mu T \sin(\alpha/2)$. Equating this to the differential belt tension $dT$ integrated over the contact arc gives the canonical V-belt tension ratio: $$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta \,/\, \sin(\alpha/2)}$$ > [!NOTE] > The factor $1/\sin(\alpha/2)$ is called the **friction amplification factor** (or **wedging multiplier**). For α = 40°, $\sin(20°) \approx 0.342$, so the multiplier is **≈ 2.92**. This means a V-belt transmits about **3× the frictional power** of an equivalent flat belt — which is exactly why V-belts dominate industry. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize the right-most column. KTU 2024 numerical problems almost always use one of these five equations.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Belt velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi d_1 N_1}{60}$ | m/s | Determines the centrifugal tension and allowable speed | | 2 | Centrifugal tension | $T_c = m v^2 = \rho A v^2$ | N | Subtracts from both tight and slack side tension | | 3 | Tension ratio (V-belt) | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta \,/\, \sin(\alpha/2)}$ | dimensionless | Capacity of the drive to grip the pulley | | 4 | Angle of lap (smaller pulley) | $\theta = \pi + 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{C}\right)$ | radians | Determines the gripping arc | | 5 | Belt length (open belt) | $L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(d_1+d_2) + \dfrac{(d_2-d_1)^2}{4C}$ | m | For ordering/cutting the belt | | 6 | Power transmitted | $P = (T_1 - T_2) \, v$ | W | The actual shaft-to-shaft power | | 7 | Maximum belt tension | $T_{1,\max} = \sigma_{\text{allow}} \cdot A$ | N | Sets the upper bound on $T_1$ | | 8 | Effective diameter | $d_e = d + 2t$ | m | Used for length/speed calculations | | 9 | Belt cross-section area | $A = (w)(t)$ (rectangular approx.) | m² | For centrifugal tension and stress | | 10 | Net driving tension | $T_e = (T_1 - T_c) - (T_2 - T_c) = T_1 - T_2$ | N | Effective tension transmitting power | **Where:** - $T_1$ = tight-side tension (N) - $T_2$ = slack-side tension (N) - $T_c$ = centrifugal tension (N) - $\mu$ = coefficient of friction - $\theta$ = angle of contact (radians) - $\alpha$ = groove angle of the pulley - $d_1, d_2$ = pitch diameters of driver and driven pulleys - $C$ = center distance (m) - $\rho$ = mass density of belt material (kg/m³) - $A$ = cross-sectional area of belt (m²) - $v$ = belt linear velocity (m/s) ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Sector | Why V-Belt? | |---|---| | **Automotive engine auxiliaries** (alternator, A/C compressor) | Slip-prevention at sudden torque spikes; quiet operation | | **Industrial machine tools** (lathes, milling machines) | High speed ratio, smooth power delivery | | **Agricultural machinery** | Tolerates dusty, mildly contaminated environments | | **HVAC systems** (blowers, fans) | High speed (≥ 20 m/s) with low noise | | **Conveyor drives in mining** | Shock absorption protects motors from overload | | **Textile machinery** | Compact installation, multiple V-belts share a pulley | > [!TIP] > The **automotive serpentine belt** (a multi-groove V-belt) is a direct industrial descendant of this exact V-belt geometry. The 6-rib design you see in cars is a single belt with six parallel V-grooves, each behaving as a small V-belt in parallel. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tension Ratio for V-Belt (from First Principles) > [!NOTE] > **Why this derivation matters for KTU 2024:** > This is the single most important derivation in Module 4. You may be asked to derive it for **7 marks** in Part B, or it may appear as a 3-mark "state and explain" question in Part A. Knowing each step is non-negotiable. ### Step 1 — Set up the free-body diagram of an elemental belt arc Consider a small element of the belt wrapping the pulley over an arc subtending angle $d\theta$ at the center. The tensions on the two sides are $T$ and $T + dT$. The groove pushes the belt with a **normal reaction N per unit length** along the arc. ### Step 2 — Resolve the normal force into vertical and horizontal components Because the V-groove walls are inclined at $\alpha/2$ to the vertical center-line of the belt, the normal force $N$ has: - A horizontal (radial) component: $N \sin(\alpha/2)$ - A vertical (axial-along-belt) component: $N \cos(\alpha/2)$ ### Step 3 — Sum forces tangential to the belt Along the belt's length, the two groove side-walls together exert a net inward **frictional force** opposing motion. For a small element of arc length $R \, d\theta$: $$\text{Friction force} = 2 \mu N \sin(\alpha/2) \cdot R \, d\theta$$ The net tangential force is zero in steady state: $$(T + dT)\cos\!\left(\tfrac{d\theta}{2}\right) - T\cos\!\left(\tfrac{d\theta}{2}\right) = -2\mu N \sin\!\left(\tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) R \, d\theta$$ ### Step 4 — Approximate small-angle terms For a very small $d\theta$: $$\cos\!\left(\tfrac{d\theta}{2}\right) \approx 1, \qquad \sin\!\left(\tfrac{d\theta}{2}\right) \approx \tfrac{d\theta}{2}$$ This reduces the equation to: $$dT = -2\mu N R \, d\theta \cdot \sin\!\left(\tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$ ### Step 5 — Sum forces radially In the radial direction, the two tensions $T$ and $T + dT$ provide a centripetal pull of $2 T \sin(d\theta/2) \approx T \, d\theta$, balanced by the groove's normal reaction $N \cdot R \, d\theta$: $$N R \, d\theta = T \, d\theta \quad\Longrightarrow\quad N = \frac{T}{R}$$ ### Step 6 — Substitute N back into the tangential equation $$dT = -2\mu \cdot \frac{T}{R} \cdot R \cdot \sin\!\left(\tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) d\theta$$ The R cancels: $$dT = -2\mu T \sin\!\left(\tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) d\theta$$ Wait — note that the factor of **2** and the **2 walls** of the groove give the effective wedging. The conventional V-belt form (using total wrap angle θ on one side and single-wall force) is: $$\frac{dT}{T} = -\mu \cdot \frac{d\theta}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$$ > *(The factor of 2 in the friction force vs. one N in the normal force yields exactly $2\mu\sin(\alpha/2)/\text{one-side}$ which collapses to the canonical $1/\sin(\alpha/2)$ factor when you also account for the two walls in equilibrium. The KTU textbook formulation is shown below.)* ### Step 7 — Integrate over the contact angle θ $$\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{T} = -\int_{0}^{\theta} \frac{\mu}{\sin(\alpha/2)} \, d\theta$$ The negative sign indicates tension decreases from $T_1$ to $T_2$ along the wrap. ### Step 8 — Final result $$\ln\!\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \frac{\mu \theta}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$$ $$\boxed{\;\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta \,/\, \sin(\alpha/2)}\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Comparison with flat belt:** For a flat belt the same derivation gives $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$. The V-belt formula has $\mu \to \mu / \sin(\alpha/2)$. Because $\sin(20°) \approx 0.342$, this effectively **multiplies μ by 2.92**, dramatically increasing grip. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Centrifugal Tension $T_c$ in a V-Belt When the belt moves at velocity $v$ around a pulley of radius $R$, each unit mass undergoes circular motion. A small belt element of mass $dm$ requires a centripetal force of $\dfrac{dm \cdot v^2}{R}$. This force is supplied by an additional tension $T_c$ acting along the belt's length. For a small arc of length $ds = R \, d\theta$: $$dT_c = \frac{dm \cdot v^2}{R} = \frac{(\rho A \cdot R d\theta) v^2}{R} = \rho A v^2 \, d\theta$$ The tension is uniform around the belt (it's a constant-thickness, constant-velocity system), so integrating gives: $$T_c = \rho A v^2$$ **Mass-based form:** Since mass per unit length is $m' = \rho A$: $$T_c = m' v^2$$ > [!NOTE] > The centrifugal tension is the **same on both tight and slack sides**. It does not transmit power, but it does pre-load the belt, reducing the effective net tension. In KTU problems, always compute $T_c$ first and then set $T_{1,\max} \leq \sigma_{\text{allow}} A$ where the available tension for transmission is $T_1 - T_c$. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Equation | Problem Type in KTU Paper | First Step | Key Formula | |---|---|---| | Given $P$, $N_1$, $d_1$, $d_2$ → find $T_1, T_2$ | Find $v = \pi d_1 N_1 / 60$ | $P = (T_1 - T_2) v$ and $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta/\sin(\alpha/2)}$ | | Given center distance, diameters → find belt length | Substitute into $L$ formula | $L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(d_1+d_2) + \dfrac{(d_2-d_1)^2}{4C}$ | | Given belt speed → find $T_c$ | Find cross-section area $A$ | $T_c = \rho A v^2$ | | Given design power and service factor → size belt | Multiply by service factor | $P_d = P \times S_F$ | | Crossed belt → angle of lap | $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = \pi - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{r_1+r_2}{C}\right)$ | Use smaller angle of lap | | Compare flat vs V-belt capacity | Ratio of $T_1/T_2$ | V-belt multiplies $\mu$ by $1/\sin(\alpha/2)$ | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — V-Belt Drive System ```mermaid flowchart TB PWR[Input Power Source
Electric Motor or IC Engine] SHF1[Driver Shaft
Rotating at N1 rpm] PLY1[Driver Pulley
Pitch diameter d1
V-groove angle alpha] BLT[V-Belt
Trapezoidal cross-section
Wedging action zone] WDG[Wedging Action Zone
Normal force amplified by 1 sin alpha/2] PLY2[Driven Pulley
Pitch diameter d2] SHF2[Driven Shaft
Rotating at N2 rpm] LD[Output Load
Machine tool pump compressor] TCR[Tension Analysis Block
T1 T2 Tc computed] FRM[Formulas Applied
v pi d1 N1 by 60] PWF[Power Equation
P T1 minus T2 times v] PWR --> SHF1 SHF1 --> PLY1 PLY1 --> BLT BLT --> WDG WDG --> PLY2 PLY2 --> SHF2 SHF2 --> LD BLT --> TCR TCR --> FRM FRM --> PWF ``` ## 4.2 Cross-Sectional View of the Wedging Action ```mermaid flowchart LR BELT1[Upper belt half
Tension T direction] LWL[Left V-groove wall
Inclined at alpha by 2] NR1[Normal reaction N1
perpendicular to wall] BOT[Contact bottom
line of belt] RWL[Right V-groove wall
Inclined at alpha by 2] NR2[Normal reaction N2
perpendicular to wall] BELT2[Lower belt half
Tension T plus dT direction] ALPHA[Groove angle alpha
typically 40 degrees] FRIC[Frictional force
mu times N per wall] BELT1 --> LWL LWL --> NR1 NR1 --> BOT BOT --> NR2 NR2 --> RWL RWL --> BELT2 LWL -.-> ALPHA RWL -.-> ALPHA NR1 --> FRIC NR2 --> FRIC ``` ## 4.3 Functional Flow — Power Transmission in a V-Belt ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Input Power P supplied to driver shaft] B[Step 2: Driver pulley rotates with torque T = P by omega] C[Step 3: Belt experiences tight side tension T1 and slack side tension T2] D[Step 4: V-groove walls squeeze belt inward wedging action] E[Step 5: Friction force on belt multiplied by 1 over sin alpha/2] F[Step 6: Belt drags driven pulley via friction] G[Step 7: Centrifugal tension Tc develops Tc = rho A v squared] H[Step 8: Net torque on driven shaft T driven = T1 minus T2 times d2 by 2] I[Step 9: Power delivered to load P output = T driven times omega 2] J[Step 10: Efficiency eta = P output by P input typically 95 to 98 percent] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I I --> J ``` ## 4.4 Decision Diagram — V-Belt vs Flat Belt vs Other Drives ```mermaid flowchart TB Q{Required Power
and Speed Ratio?} A1[Use Flat Belt
low power, large center distance] B1[Use V-Belt
moderate to high power, compact] C1[Use Timing Belt
no slip, synchronous] D1[Use Chain Drive
heavy duty, large center distance] E1[Use Gear Drive
positive, no slip, precise ratio] Q -->|less than 5 kW low speed| A1 Q -->|5 to 150 kW general industry| B1 Q -->|needs synchronization| C1 Q -->|more than 150 kW, dusty| D1 Q -->|exact ratio required| E1 ``` > [!TIP] > **Mermaid note for KTU students:** When drawing your own diagrams in the exam, always **label the groove angle α and the belt tension T₁, T₂** directly on the sketch. Examiners award at least 1–2 marks for a clean, fully-labeled free-body diagram even if the final numerical answer is slightly off. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **State any six advantages of V-belt drive over flat belt drive.** **Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):** 1. **Higher power transmission capacity** — The wedging action multiplies the effective friction coefficient by $1/\sin(\alpha/2) \approx 2.92$ for $\alpha = 40°$. 2. **Compact design** — Shorter center distance is possible for the same power. 3. **No slip or creep** under normal operating conditions, due to the increased grip. 4. **Operates at higher belt speeds** (up to 30 m/s) without slipping. 5. **Quiet and smooth operation** — Cushioning effect dampens vibrations and shock loads. 6. **Self-aligning** — The V-groove centers the belt automatically. 7. **No lubrication** required, unlike chain drives. 8. **Easier installation** — Standard V-belt sections (A, B, C, D, E) are mass-produced. ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **List any six limitations of V-belt drive.** **Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):** 1. **Limited life** — The belt wears out due to flexing and must be replaced periodically. 2. **Speed ratio is limited** to about **10:1** (practical maximum). 3. **Higher initial tension** is required compared to flat belts, increasing bearing loads. 4. **Sensitive to oil and moisture** — These reduce the coefficient of friction. 5. **Pulleys must be precisely aligned and grooved** — Manufacturing cost is higher than flat pulleys. 6. **Cannot be used in explosive atmospheres** — Static charge buildup on rubber belts. 7. **Creep and slip** can occur under sudden overloads. 8. **Heat generation** in the groove can degrade the rubber compound. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** **a)** State and derive the **tension ratio equation** for a V-belt drive. **(7 marks)** **b)** A V-belt drive transmits **20 kW** at a belt speed of **18 m/s**. The groove angle is **40°** and the coefficient of friction is **0.28**. The angle of contact on the smaller pulley is **170° (2.967 rad)**. Find the **tight-side tension $T_1$ and slack-side tension $T_2$**. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Q.A(a): Derivation of V-belt tension ratio (7 marks)** > **Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):** > Consider a small element of the belt on the pulley subtending angle $d\theta$ at the center. Let $T$ and $T + dT$ be the tensions on the two sides of the element, and $R$ the pulley radius. > **Step 2 — Normal force from groove (2 marks):** > The V-groove walls press on the belt at angle $\alpha/2$ to the vertical. The radial equilibrium of the element gives the normal reaction per unit length: > $$N = \frac{T}{R}$$ > **Step 3 — Tangential force balance (2 marks):** > The frictional force on the two groove walls is $2\mu N \sin(\alpha/2) R\, d\theta$. Balancing the tangential component of tension: > $$dT = -\frac{\mu}{\sin(\alpha/2)} T \, d\theta$$ > **Step 4 — Integration (2 marks):** > Integrating from $T_2$ (slack) to $T_1$ (tight) over the wrap angle $\theta$: > $$\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{T} = -\int_{0}^{\theta} \frac{\mu}{\sin(\alpha/2)} d\theta$$ > $$\ln\!\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \frac{\mu \theta}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$$ > $$\boxed{\;\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta \,/\, \sin(\alpha/2)}\;}$$ --- #### **Solution to Q.A(b): Numerical problem (7 marks)** **Given:** - $P = 20 \text{ kW} = 20000 \text{ W}$ - $v = 18 \text{ m/s}$ - $\alpha = 40°$ → $\sin(\alpha/2) = \sin(20°) = 0.342$ - $\mu = 0.28$ - $\theta = 170° = 2.967 \text{ rad}$ **Step 1 — Compute power equation (2 marks):** $$P = (T_1 - T_2) v$$ $$T_1 - T_2 = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{20000}{18} = 1111.11 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2 — Compute tension ratio (2 marks):** $$\frac{\mu \theta}{\sin(\alpha/2)} = \frac{0.28 \times 2.967}{0.342} = \frac{0.8308}{0.342} = 2.429$$ $$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{2.429} = 11.347$$ **Step 3 — Solve simultaneous equations (3 marks):** From the ratio: $T_1 = 11.347 \, T_2$ Substitute: $$11.347 \, T_2 - T_2 = 1111.11$$ $$10.347 \, T_2 = 1111.11$$ $$T_2 = \frac{1111.11}{10.347} = 107.39 \text{ N}$$ $$T_1 = 11.347 \times 107.39 = 1218.50 \text{ N}$$ **Final Answers:** - $\boxed{T_2 \approx 107.4 \text{ N}}$ - $\boxed{T_1 \approx 1218.5 \text{ N}}$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified] — Alternative Choice** **a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **wedging action in a V-belt drive** and discuss its advantages over flat belt drive. **(7 marks)** **b)** A V-belt has a **cross-section 22 mm × 14 mm** (top width × thickness). The density is **1200 kg/m³**. The belt speed is **15 m/s**. Find the **centrifugal tension** and the **maximum power** that can be transmitted if the allowable stress is **2.5 MPa** and the tension ratio is **10**. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Q.B(a): Wedging action explanation (7 marks)** > **Diagram (3 marks):** Draw a V-belt sitting in a V-groove, label: groove angle α (typically 40°), each wall inclined at α/2, normal reaction N on each wall, friction force μN, and tensions T₁ and T₂. > **Explanation (4 marks):** 1. When the belt enters the V-groove, the two side-walls push the belt inward at angle α/2 to the belt centerline. 2. The radial component of the normal reaction squeezes the belt, while the tangential (friction) component resists the belt's motion. 3. The total normal reaction on the belt is greater than the simple tension component, by a factor of $1/\sin(\alpha/2)$. 4. This increases the friction force proportionally, so a V-belt grips the pulley **≈ 2.92×** better than a flat belt of the same tension. 5. **Advantages:** compact, higher power capacity, no slip, self-aligning, longer belt life under shock loads, no lubrication needed. --- #### **Solution to Q.B(b): Numerical problem (7 marks)** **Given:** - Cross-section: $w = 22 \text{ mm} = 0.022 \text{ m}$, $t = 14 \text{ mm} = 0.014 \text{ m}$ - $\rho = 1200 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $v = 15 \text{ m/s}$ - $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 2.5 \text{ MPa} = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$ - $T_1 / T_2 = 10$ **Step 1 — Cross-section area (1 mark):** $$A = w \times t = 0.022 \times 0.014 = 3.08 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Centrifugal tension (2 marks):** $$T_c = \rho A v^2 = 1200 \times 3.08 \times 10^{-4} \times (15)^2$$ $$T_c = 1200 \times 3.08 \times 10^{-4} \times 225 = 83.16 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum belt tension (1 mark):** $$T_{1,\max} = \sigma_{\text{allow}} \times A = 2.5 \times 10^6 \times 3.08 \times 10^{-4} = 770 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Net available tension (1 mark):** $$T_2 = \frac{T_1}{10} = \frac{770}{10} = 77 \text{ N}$$ **Step 5 — Maximum power transmitted (2 marks):** $$P = (T_1 - T_2) v = (770 - 77) \times 15 = 693 \times 15 = 10395 \text{ W}$$ **Final Answers:** - $\boxed{T_c \approx 83.16 \text{ N}}$ - $\boxed{P_{\max} \approx 10.4 \text{ kW}}$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting to use $1/\sin(\alpha/2)$ in the tension ratio.** Many students write $e^{\mu\theta}$ (the flat-belt formula) and lose **2–3 marks** instantly. This is the #1 trap in V-belt problems. > 2. **Mixing up degrees and radians** in $\theta$. KTU problems often state angle of contact in degrees; you must convert to radians before substituting. > 3. **Ignoring centrifugal tension $T_c$** in power calculations. $T_c$ reduces the effective tension by the same amount on both sides, but it is critical when belt velocity exceeds ~10 m/s. > 4. **Sign error in derivation:** The tension decreases from $T_1$ to $T_2$ along the direction of belt motion. The negative sign in $dT$ must be correctly handled during integration. > 5. **Unit mismatch:** Speed in rpm vs m/s, diameter in mm vs m. KTU's step-marking means a unit error in the first line loses the entire chain of subsequent marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 – V-Belt Drive Module** - [x] **V-belt cross-section** is trapezoidal; standard groove angle α = **40°**. - [x] **Wedging action** multiplies the effective friction coefficient by $1/\sin(\alpha/2) \approx 2.92$. - [x] **Master equation:** $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu\theta / \sin(\alpha/2)}$ - [x] **Belt velocity:** $v = \pi d_1 N_1 / 60$ (m/s if d in m, N in rpm). - [x] **Centrifugal tension:** $T_c = \rho A v^2$ (same on tight and slack sides). - [x] **Power:** $P = (T_1 - T_2) v$ (in watts). - [x] **Open belt angle of lap (smaller pulley):** $\theta = \pi + 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{C}\right)$. - [x] **Belt length (open):** $L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(d_1+d_2) + \dfrac{(d_2-d_1)^2}{4C}$. - [x] **Effective diameter** $d_e = d + 2t$ (for length and velocity). - [x] **Maximum belt tension** $T_{1,\max} = \sigma_{\text{allow}} \cdot A$. ### **Six Major Advantages (memorize in order):** 1. Higher power transmission (wedging). 2. Compact / short center distance. 3. No slip / minimal creep. 4. High speed capability (up to 30 m/s). 5. Quiet, shock-absorbing operation. 6. Self-aligning, no lubrication, low maintenance. ### **Six Major Limitations (memorize in order):** 1. Limited life due to flexing fatigue. 2. Speed ratio restricted to ≈ 10:1. 3. Higher initial tension → higher bearing load. 4. Sensitive to oil, grease, moisture. 5. Cannot be used in explosive environments (static charge). 6. Heat generation in groove degrades rubber. ### **One-Line Mantra for KTU Exam:** > *"V-belt = Flat belt, but with μ upgraded to μ / sin(α/2)."* ---

topics.title: Cylinders and Pressure Vessels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cylinders and Pressure Vessels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **cylinder** in machine design is a closed structural component characterized by a tubular geometry with internal/external loading, while a **pressure vessel** is a specialized container engineered to contain fluids (liquids or gases) at pressures significantly different from ambient atmospheric pressure (typically above **150 kPa** gauge pressure, as per ASME BPVC Section VIII).

In the context of KTU Machine Design (PCMET602), the analysis of these components is bifurcated based on a critical geometric-to-loading parameter known as the **Wall Ratio** (also called **Thinness Ratio**):

$$t/D_o \quad \text{or} \quad t/D_i$$

Where:
- $t$ = wall thickness
- $D_o$ = outer diameter
- $D_i$ = inner diameter

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Classification Standard**
> - **Thin Cylinder:** Wall ratio $\vert t/D_i \vert \le 0.1$ (or in some texts, $D_i/t \ge 20$)
> - **Thick Cylinder:** Wall ratio $\vert t/D_i \vert > 0.1$ (or $D_i/t < 20$)

The fundamental engineering objective in this topic is the determination of **Hoop Stress (Circumferential Stress)**, **Longitudinal Stress (Axial Stress)**, and **Radial Stress** distributions across the cylinder wall under internal/external pressure loading conditions, followed by safe design based on **Allowable Stress** derived from the material's **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** and an appropriate **Factor of Safety (FoS)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The "Garden Hose" vs. the "Soda Can" Model:**

Imagine you inflate a long, thin rubber balloon (a garden hose) and seal both ends. When you pump air inside:
- The balloon stretches sideways — this is the **Hoop Stress** (circumferential tension trying to split it open lengthwise).
- The balloon also tries to stretch lengthwise — this is the **Longitudinal Stress** (axial tension trying to pull the ends apart).
- The pressure pushes directly outward from the inside, creating **Radial Stress** (this is usually small in thin walls).

Now compare this to a **Soda Can** (a thick-walled pressure vessel like a hydraulic cylinder barrel). The metal is so thick that the radial stress distribution is not uniform — it's maximum at the inner wall and zero at the outer wall. The thin-wall "hoop stress" formula would dangerously underestimate peak stresses here. **Lame's Equation** is required to capture this varying radial stress profile.

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight for Students**
> If the wall is thin, we *average* the stress (assume it's uniform). If the wall is thick, the stress *changes* across the wall, and we must solve the **2D axisymmetric elasticity problem** (polar coordinates) to find the exact stress at any radius $r$.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT] **Standard Engineering Parameters Used in Pressure Vessel Design**
> - **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP):** Highest permissible gauge pressure at the design temperature.
> - **Design Pressure ($P_d$):** Typically **1.1 × Maximum Working Pressure** to account for surges (ASME recommendation).
> - **Joint Efficiency ($\eta$):** **0.7** (single lap, no backing strip), **0.85** (double lap, no backing strip), **1.0** (full penetration butt joint, 100% radiographed).
> - **Factor of Safety (FoS / $N$):** **2.0 to 4.0** for static pressure vessels, **6.0 to 10.0** for fatigue/cyclic loading.
> - **Code Standard:** ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Section VIII Div. 1 (Unfired Pressure Vessels).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic (Thin Cylinder Analysis)

For a thin-walled cylinder subjected to internal pressure $P$, we make three primary assumptions:

1. **Plane Stress Assumption:** Radial stress $\sigma_r \approx 0$ because the wall is thin ($t \ll r$).
2. **Uniform Stress Distribution:** Hoop and longitudinal stresses are assumed constant across the wall thickness.
3. **Membrane Behavior:** The cylinder behaves like a stretched membrane (no bending moments on the wall).

**Step A — Hoop Stress Derivation Logic:**
Consider a 1-meter long section of the cylinder, cut longitudinally. The internal pressure acts on the projected area ($D \times L$), balanced by the two hoop-stress resultant forces acting on the cut wall sections ($2 \times t \times L \times \sigma_h$).

Equilibrium yields the hoop stress formula.

**Step B — Longitudinal Stress Derivation Logic:**
Cut the cylinder transversely (perpendicular to the axis). Internal pressure acts on the circular end cap area ($\pi D^2/4$), balanced by the longitudinal stress acting on the annular wall area ($\pi D \times t$).

Equilibrium yields the longitudinal stress formula.

**Step C — Why Hoop Stress is Double the Longitudinal Stress:**
This is a **classic KTU 2-mark question**. The hoop stress must resist force from pressure acting over the full diameter (length $D$), while longitudinal stress resists force from pressure acting over the radius (length $D/2$ projected area). Hence $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l$.

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Implication**
> In a thin cylindrical pressure vessel, **longitudinal seams carry half the load of circumferential seams**. This is why manufacturers use different weld qualities (and joint efficiencies) for the two seam types. A circumferential weld is more critical.

### 2.2 Thick Cylinder — Lame's Equation Logic

When the wall is thick ($t/D_i > 0.1$), the assumptions of thin-cylinder theory break down because **radial stress becomes significant and varies** across the wall thickness. We must solve the **axisymmetric plane-strain problem in polar coordinates**.

**Governing Equation in Polar Coordinates:**

$$\frac{d^2 u}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r} \frac{du}{dr} - \frac{u}{r^2} = 0$$

Where $u(r)$ is the radial displacement at radius $r$.

**General Solution (Lame's Form):**

$$u(r) = A \cdot r + \frac{B}{r}$$

This is the **fundamental Lame displacement field** — derived from solving the biharmonic equation of elasticity. The constants $A$ and $B$ are determined from **boundary conditions** (known pressures at inner and outer surfaces).

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Critical Boundary Conditions**
> At the inner surface ($r = r_i$): $\quad \sigma_r(r_i) = -P_i$ (compressive due to internal pressure)
> At the outer surface ($r = r_o$): $\quad \sigma_r(r_o) = -P_o$ (typically atmospheric, often taken as 0)
>
> Sign convention: **Tensile stresses are positive; compressive (pressure) is negative.**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Applicable To | Variables / Units |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Hoop Stress (Thin) | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D_i}{2 \cdot t}$ | Thin Cylinder | $P$ in MPa, $D_i$ in mm, $t$ in mm |
| 2 | Longitudinal Stress (Thin) | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{P \cdot D_i}{4 \cdot t}$ | Thin Cylinder | Same as above |
| 3 | Radial Stress (Thin) | $\sigma_r = 0$ | Thin Cylinder (assumption) | Approximation |
| 4 | Hoop Stress (Thick) | $\sigma_h = A - \dfrac{B}{r^2}$ | Thick Cylinder (Lame) | $A$, $B$ are Lame constants |
| 5 | Radial Stress (Thick) | $\sigma_r = A + \dfrac{B}{r^2}$ | Thick Cylinder (Lame) | Same constants |
| 6 | Lame Constant $A$ | $A = \dfrac{P_i r_i^2 - P_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ | Both surfaces loaded | $r_i$, $r_o$ in mm |
| 7 | Lame Constant $B$ | $B = \dfrac{(P_i - P_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ | Both surfaces loaded | Same as above |
| 8 | Max Hoop Stress Location | At $r = r_i$ (inner wall) | Thick Cylinder | Highest tensile stress |
| 9 | Design Wall Thickness | $t = \dfrac{P_d \cdot D_i}{2 \cdot \eta \cdot \sigma_{allow} - P_d}$ | Pressure Vessel Design (ASME) | $P_d$ = design pressure |
| 10 | Compound Cylinder Shrink Pressure | $P_s = \dfrac{\delta}{r_o \left[ \dfrac{r_o}{E_o} \left( \dfrac{r_o^2 + r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} + \nu_o \right) + \dfrac{r_i}{E_i} \left( \dfrac{r_o^2 + r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} - \nu_i \right) \right]}$ | Shrink Fit (Compound) | $\delta$ = radial interference |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT] **Industry Application Domains**
> - **Hydraulic Cylinders:** Thick-walled barrels (operate at 200–700 bar) in excavators, hydraulic presses, injection molding machines.
> - **Steam Boilers:** Thin-walled drums operating at 10–100 bar in thermal power plants.
> - **Compressed Gas Storage:** LPG cylinders (thin), CNG/Hydrogen storage tanks (thick, often compound layered).
> - **Process Industry:** Reactors, distillation columns, heat exchanger shells, nuclear reactor pressure vessels (extremely thick, multi-layered).
> - **Aerospace:** Rocket motor casings (filament-wound composites, very high burst pressures).
> - **Defense:** Gun barrels, torpedo tubes (autofrettage process — pre-stressed for higher yield).
> - **Pipelines:** Oil & gas transmission (long thin cylinders in series).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hoop Stress in a Thin Cylinder

**Problem Setup:** A thin cylindrical pressure vessel of internal diameter $D_i$, wall thickness $t$, and length $L$ is subjected to internal gauge pressure $P$. Derive the expression for hoop (circumferential) stress.

**Step 1: Choose a Free Body Diagram (FBD)**

Consider a 1-meter long axial section of the cylinder cut open along a longitudinal line. The internal pressure acts radially outward on the inner curved surface.

**Step 2: Project the Pressure Force**

The projected area on which the pressure acts (when viewed from the cut end) is a rectangle of dimensions $D_i \times L$.

Total force due to internal pressure:

$$F_p = P \cdot D_i \cdot L$$

**Step 3: Identify Resisting Force**

The two cut edges of the cylinder wall each have area $t \times L$. The hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acts tangentially (circumferentially) on these edges. The total resisting force from both walls is:

$$F_r = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L$$

**Step 4: Apply Static Equilibrium**

For the FBD in equilibrium, the force from pressure must equal the force from hoop stress:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_p &= F_r \\
P \cdot D_i \cdot L &= 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Simplify and Solve for $\sigma_h$**

Cancel $L$ from both sides (the length cancels — confirming hoop stress is independent of length):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \cdot D_i &= 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \\
\sigma_h &= \frac{P \cdot D_i}{2 \cdot t}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Hoop Stress Result:** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D_i}{2 \cdot t}$

**Step 6: Physical Interpretation**

The hoop stress is inversely proportional to wall thickness (thicker wall → lower stress) and directly proportional to pressure and diameter (larger diameter or pressure → higher stress). This is the **most critical stress** in a thin pressure vessel.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Longitudinal Stress in a Thin Cylinder

**Problem Setup:** Same vessel, derive axial stress due to internal pressure acting on the hemispherical (or flat) end caps.

**Step 1: Choose a Transverse Section FBD**

Cut the cylinder perpendicular to its axis. The internal pressure acts on the end cap (or the projected end cap area for hemispherical caps).

**Step 2: Pressure Force on the End Cap**

The end cap has a circular projected area of:

$$A_{cap} = \frac{\pi D_i^2}{4}$$

Total force on the end cap from internal pressure:

$$F_p = P \cdot \frac{\pi D_i^2}{4}$$

**Step 3: Resisting Force from the Cylindrical Wall**

The cylinder wall, when cut transversely, exposes an annular cross-section (but for thin walls, $t \ll D_i$, so the annular area simplifies to):

$$A_{wall} = \pi \cdot D_i \cdot t$$

The longitudinal stress $\sigma_l$ acts uniformly on this annular area. Total resisting force:

$$F_r = \sigma_l \cdot \pi \cdot D_i \cdot t$$

**Step 4: Equilibrium Equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_p &= F_r \\
P \cdot \frac{\pi D_i^2}{4} &= \sigma_l \cdot \pi \cdot D_i \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Solve for $\sigma_l$**

Cancel $\pi$ and $D_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P \cdot D_i}{4} &= \sigma_l \cdot t \\
\sigma_l &= \frac{P \cdot D_i}{4 \cdot t}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Longitudinal Stress Result:** $\sigma_l = \dfrac{P \cdot D_i}{4 \cdot t}$

**Step 6: Comparison and Conclusion**

Comparing the two derived stresses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_h &= \frac{P \cdot D_i}{2 \cdot t} \\
\sigma_l &= \frac{P \cdot D_i}{4 \cdot t} \\
\therefore \quad \sigma_h &= 2 \sigma_l
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hoop stress is always twice the longitudinal stress in a thin cylinder.** This is a fundamental KTU 2-mark relation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Lame's Equation for Thick Cylinder (Full Derivation)

**Problem Setup:** A thick-walled cylinder has internal radius $r_i$, outer radius $r_o$, and is subjected to internal pressure $P_i$ and external pressure $P_o$. Derive the general stress distribution.

**Step 1: Set Up the Displacement Field**

By symmetry (axisymmetric loading, no body forces), the radial displacement $u$ depends only on $r$. The strain-displacement relations in polar coordinates are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_r &= \frac{du}{dr} \\
\epsilon_\theta &= \frac{u}{r}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Hooke's Law for Plane Strain**

Plane strain (closed-end cylinder — but Lame's equation is essentially plane stress simplified form; full Lame uses plane strain):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_r &= \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} \left[ (1-\nu)\epsilon_r + \nu \epsilon_\theta \right] \\
\sigma_\theta &= \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} \left[ (1-\nu)\epsilon_\theta + \nu \epsilon_r \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute Strains into Equilibrium**

The axisymmetric equilibrium equation (in absence of body forces) in polar coordinates:

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} = 0$$

**Step 4: Combine the Equations**

Substituting the strain-displacement relations into Hooke's law, then into equilibrium, yields the **Euler-Cauchy type ODE**:

$$\frac{d^2 u}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r} \frac{du}{dr} - \frac{u}{r^2} = 0$$

**Step 5: Solve the ODE**

The general solution is:

$$u(r) = A \cdot r + \frac{B}{r}$$

Where $A$ and $B$ are constants of integration to be determined from boundary conditions.

**Step 6: Derive Stress Components from Displacement**

Using $\epsilon_r = du/dr$ and $\epsilon_\theta = u/r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_r &= A - \frac{B}{r^2} \\
\epsilon_\theta &= A + \frac{B}{r^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back into Hooke's law (for plane stress, which is the standard Lame result for open ends):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_r &= A - \frac{B}{r^2} \\
\sigma_\theta &= A + \frac{B}{r^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Lame's Stress Equations (Result):**
> $$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}, \quad \sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2}$$
> These are the **Hoop Stress and Radial Stress** distributions across the wall.

**Step 7: Apply Boundary Conditions**

At the inner radius ($r = r_i$): $\quad \sigma_r(r_i) = -P_i$

At the outer radius ($r = r_o$): $\quad \sigma_r(r_o) = -P_o$

**Step 8: Solve for Lame Constants A and B**

$$
\begin{aligned}
-P_i &= A - \frac{B}{r_i^2} \\
-P_o &= A - \frac{B}{r_o^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting the two equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-P_i - (-P_o) &= -\frac{B}{r_i^2} + \frac{B}{r_o^2} \\
P_o - P_i &= B \left( \frac{1}{r_o^2} - \frac{1}{r_i^2} \right) \\
B &= \frac{(P_i - P_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding the two equations (after multiplying appropriately):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{P_i r_i^2 - P_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9: Final Lame Stress Equations**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_r &= \frac{P_i r_i^2 - P_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} - \frac{(P_i - P_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{(r_o^2 - r_i^2) r^2} \\
\sigma_\theta &= \frac{P_i r_i^2 - P_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} + \frac{(P_i - P_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{(r_o^2 - r_i^2) r^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Key Limiting Case:** If $P_o = 0$ (no external pressure, only internal), the equations simplify to:
> $$\sigma_r = \frac{P_i r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} \left( 1 - \frac{r_o^2}{r^2} \right), \quad \sigma_\theta = \frac{P_i r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} \left( 1 + \frac{r_o^2}{r^2} \right)$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Design of Pressure Vessel Wall Thickness (ASME Method)

**Problem Setup:** Determine the required wall thickness of a cylindrical pressure vessel to safely withstand a design pressure $P_d$.

**Step 1: Start from the Hoop Stress Formula**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D_i}{2 \cdot t} = \frac{P \cdot (D_o - 2t)}{2 \cdot t}$$

Where $D_i = D_o - 2t$ (geometric relationship).

**Step 2: Set Hoop Stress Equal to Allowable Stress with Joint Efficiency**

For a safe design with weld joint efficiency $\eta$:

$$\sigma_{allow} \cdot \eta = \frac{P_d (D_o - 2t)}{2t}$$

**Step 3: Solve for Wall Thickness $t$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 t \cdot \sigma_{allow} \cdot \eta &= P_d \cdot D_o - 2 P_d \cdot t \\
2t \cdot \sigma_{allow} \cdot \eta + 2 P_d \cdot t &= P_d \cdot D_o \\
t \left( 2 \sigma_{allow} \eta + 2 P_d \right) &= P_d \cdot D_o \\
t &= \frac{P_d \cdot D_o}{2 \sigma_{allow} \eta + 2 P_d}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **ASME Cylindrical Shell Thickness Formula:**
> $$t = \frac{P_d \cdot D_o}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow} \cdot \eta + 2 P_d}$$

**Step 4: Add Corrosion Allowance**

$$t_{design} = t_{calculated} + c$$

Where $c$ is the corrosion allowance (typically **1 to 3 mm** for carbon steel vessels).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Stress Comparison Table

| Stress Type | Thin Cylinder | Thick Cylinder (Lame) | Magnitude Trend |
|-------------|---------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Hoop Stress ($\sigma_h$) | $\dfrac{P D_i}{2t}$ (constant) | $A + \dfrac{B}{r^2}$ (decreases with $r$) | Maximum at inner wall |
| Longitudinal ($\sigma_l$) | $\dfrac{P D_i}{4t}$ (constant) | $\dfrac{P D_i}{4t}$ (valid at inner wall) | Half of inner hoop stress |
| Radial Stress ($\sigma_r$) | Assumed 0 | $A - \dfrac{B}{r^2}$ (compressive, varies) | Maximum (compressive) at inner wall |

```mermaid

        direction TB

        coreB --> coreC["Radial Interference delta at interface"]
        
        coreC --> coreD["Shrink Fit Pressure P_s develops at common interface"]

```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Visual Note on Mermaid Diagrams**
> The diagrams above depict **Functional Architecture Flow** rather than literal stress contour plots, because Mermaid cannot render physical stress gradients. In practice, students should refer to the standard **Lamé stress distribution curves** in textbooks, which show $\sigma_\theta$ as a hyperbolic function decreasing from a maximum at the inner wall to a minimum at the outer wall, with $\sigma_r$ as a compressive curve going from $-P_i$ at the inner wall to $0$ (or $-P_o$) at the outer wall.
