## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define hoop stress and longitudinal stress in a thin cylinder. Why is hoop stress always greater than longitudinal stress in a thin cylinder?**

**Model Answer:**

**Hoop Stress ($\sigma_h$):** The circumferential (tangential) tensile stress developed in the wall of a thin cylinder, acting in a direction perpendicular to the longitudinal axis. It is the stress that resists the tendency of the cylinder to split open along its length.

**Longitudinal Stress ($\sigma_l$):** The axial tensile stress developed in the wall of a thin cylinder, acting parallel to the longitudinal axis. It is the stress that resists the tendency of the cylinder to stretch apart along its axis.

**Mathematical Expressions:**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P D_i}{2t}, \quad \sigma_l = \frac{P D_i}{4t}$$

**Why Hoop Stress is Greater:**

The internal pressure $P$ in a thin cylinder acts on a projected length of $D$ (full diameter) when determining the hoop force, but only on a projected length of $D/2$ (effective radius) when determining the longitudinal force on the end cap. Therefore, the same pressure generates twice the circumferential force per unit thickness, making:

$$\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l$$

**Physical Interpretation:** A longitudinal seam (along the length) must resist twice the force of a circumferential seam (around the circumference). Hence, longitudinal joints in pressure vessels are designed with higher joint efficiency and more rigorous inspection.

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State Lame's equation for a thick cylinder. What is the difference between Lame's equations and thin cylinder formulas?**

**Model Answer:**

**Lame's Equations for a Thick Cylinder:**

For a thick cylinder subjected to internal pressure $P_i$ and external pressure $P_o$, with inner radius $r_i$ and outer radius $r_o$, the radial and hoop stresses at any radius $r$ are given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_r &= A - \frac{B}{r^2} \\
\sigma_\theta &= A + \frac{B}{r^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where the Lame constants are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{P_i r_i^2 - P_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} \\
B &= \frac{(P_i - P_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Differences from Thin Cylinder Formulas:**

| Feature | Thin Cylinder | Thick Cylinder (Lame) |
|---------|---------------|------------------------|
| Wall Ratio Limit | $t/D_i \le 0.1$ | $t/D_i > 0.1$ |
| Radial Stress | Assumed 0 (negligible) | Significant, varies as $1/r^2$ |
| Hoop Stress | Uniform across wall | Varies with $r$ (max at inner wall) |
| Theory | Membrane / uniform stress | Axisymmetric plane-strain elasticity |
| Applicable to | Storage tanks, LPG cylinders | Hydraulic cylinders, gun barrels, reactor vessels |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) A thin cylindrical pressure vessel of internal diameter 500 mm and wall thickness 10 mm is subjected to an internal pressure of 2 MPa. Determine: (i) Hoop stress, (ii) Longitudinal stress, (iii) Maximum shear stress in the wall. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Internal diameter $D_i = 500$ mm
- Wall thickness $t = 10$ mm
- Internal pressure $P = 2$ MPa

**Step 1: Calculate Hoop Stress**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D_i}{2 \cdot t} = \frac{2 \times 500}{2 \times 10} = \frac{1000}{20} = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Longitudinal Stress**

$$\sigma_l = \frac{P \cdot D_i}{4 \cdot t} = \frac{2 \times 500}{4 \times 10} = \frac{1000}{40} = 25 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Verify Wall Ratio (Thin Cylinder Assumption)**

$$\frac{t}{D_i} = \frac{10}{500} = 0.02 \le 0.1 \quad \checkmark \text{ Thin cylinder assumption valid}$$

**Step 4: Identify Principal Stresses**

For a thin cylinder, we have a **biaxial stress state** with:
- $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h = 50$ MPa (maximum principal stress)
- $\sigma_2 = \sigma_l = 25$ MPa
- $\sigma_3 = \sigma_r \approx 0$ MPa

**Step 5: Calculate Maximum Shear Stress**

According to **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** for a 2D stress state:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{50 - 25}{2} = 12.5 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!NOTE] **Final Answer for Part (a):**
> Hoop Stress = **50 MPa**, Longitudinal Stress = **25 MPa**, Maximum Shear Stress = **12.5 MPa**

---

**(b) Design a cylindrical pressure vessel of internal diameter 1.2 m to withstand an internal pressure of 3.5 MPa. Take allowable stress for the material as 120 MPa and joint efficiency as 0.85. Also add a corrosion allowance of 2 mm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Internal diameter $D_i = 1.2$ m = 1200 mm
- Internal pressure $P = 3.5$ MPa
- Allowable stress $\sigma_{allow} = 120$ MPa
- Joint efficiency $\eta = 0.85$
- Corrosion allowance $c = 2$ mm

**Step 1: Apply Design Pressure Factor**

Per ASME, design pressure is taken as 1.1 times working pressure:

$$P_d = 1.1 \times P = 1.1 \times 3.5 = 3.85 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Apply ASME Wall Thickness Formula**

$$t = \frac{P_d \cdot D_o}{2 \sigma_{allow} \eta + 2 P_d}$$

Let outer diameter $D_o = D_i + 2t = 1200 + 2t$. Substituting:

$$t = \frac{3.85 \times (1200 + 2t)}{2 \times 120 \times 0.85 + 2 \times 3.85}$$

$$t = \frac{3.85 \times (1200 + 2t)}{204 + 7.7}$$

$$t = \frac{3.85 \times (1200 + 2t)}{211.7}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $t$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t \times 211.7 &= 3.85 \times 1200 + 3.85 \times 2t \\
211.7t &= 4620 + 7.7t \\
211.7t - 7.7t &= 4620 \\
204t &= 4620 \\
t &= 22.65 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Add Corrosion Allowance**

$$t_{design} = t + c = 22.65 + 2 = 24.65 \text{ mm} \approx 25 \text{ mm (rounded up)}$$

**Step 5: Verify Wall Ratio (Thick or Thin?)**

$$D_o = D_i + 2t = 1200 + 2 \times 25 = 1250 \text{ mm}$$

$$\frac{t}{D_i} = \frac{25}{1200} = 0.0208 \le 0.1 \quad \checkmark \text{ Thin cylinder design valid}$$

**Step 6: Verify Hoop Stress with Final Dimensions**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D_i}{2t} = \frac{3.5 \times 1200}{2 \times 25} = 84 \text{ MPa} \le 120 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ Safe}$$

> [!NOTE] **Final Answer for Part (b):**
> Required wall thickness = **25 mm** (after rounding up from 24.65 mm + corrosion allowance).
> 
> Outer diameter $D_o = 1250$ mm. The vessel is designed as a thin cylinder.

---

**QUESTION B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a) A thick cylinder of inner radius 100 mm and outer radius 200 mm is subjected to an internal pressure of 80 MPa. Calculate: (i) Radial stress at the inner and outer surfaces, (ii) Hoop stress at the inner and outer surfaces, (iii) Maximum hoop stress location. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Inner radius $r_i = 100$ mm
- Outer radius $r_o = 200$ mm
- Internal pressure $P_i = 80$ MPa
- External pressure $P_o = 0$ (atmospheric)

**Step 1: Verify Thick Cylinder Assumption**

$$\frac{t}{r_i} = \frac{r_o - r_i}{r_i} = \frac{200 - 100}{100} = 1.0 > 0.1 \quad \checkmark \text{ Thick cylinder confirmed}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Lame Constant A**

$$A = \frac{P_i r_i^2 - P_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} = \frac{80 \times (100)^2 - 0}{200^2 - 100^2} = \frac{80 \times 10000}{40000 - 10000} = \frac{800000}{30000} = 26.67 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Lame Constant B**

$$B = \frac{(P_i - P_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} = \frac{80 \times 100^2 \times 200^2}{40000 - 10000} = \frac{80 \times 10^4 \times 4 \times 10^4}{3 \times 10^4}$$

$$B = \frac{80 \times 4 \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^4} = \frac{32 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^4} = 1.0667 \times 10^6 \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{mm}^2$$

Equivalently, $B = 1.0667 \times 10^6$ N·mm/mm², or in normalized form:

$$B / r^2 \text{ at } r = 100 \text{ mm} = \frac{1.0667 \times 10^6}{10000} = 106.67 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Stresses at Inner Surface ($r = r_i = 100$ mm)**

**Radial Stress at Inner Wall:**

$$\sigma_r(r_i) = A - \frac{B}{r_i^2} = 26.67 - 106.67 = -80 \text{ MPa} = -P_i \quad \checkmark$$

(Negative sign confirms compressive nature — correct for pressure loading.)

**Hoop Stress at Inner Wall:**

$$\sigma_\theta(r_i) = A + \frac{B}{r_i^2} = 26.67 + 106.67 = 133.33 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Stresses at Outer Surface ($r = r_o = 200$ mm)**

**Radial Stress at Outer Wall:**

$$\sigma_r(r_o) = A - \frac{B}{r_o^2} = 26.67 - \frac{1.0667 \times 10^6}{40000} = 26.67 - 26.67 = 0 \text{ MPa} = -P_o \quad \checkmark$$

**Hoop Stress at Outer Wall:**

$$\sigma_\theta(r_o) = A + \frac{B}{r_o^2} = 26.67 + 26.67 = 53.33 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6: Identify Maximum Hoop Stress Location**

From the calculations:
- $\sigma_\theta(r_i) = 133.33$ MPa (inner wall)
- $\sigma_\theta(r_o) = 53.33$ MPa (outer wall)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Maximum hoop stress occurs at the inner wall of the thick cylinder.**

This is a **key design implication**: the inner surface of a thick cylinder is the critical location for failure. In high-pressure applications, this is why **autofrettage** (a pre-stressing process) is applied — it introduces beneficial compressive residual stresses at the inner wall, increasing the vessel's fatigue life and burst pressure.

> [!NOTE] **Final Answer for Part (a):**
> - Radial stress: $-80$ MPa (inner) and $0$ MPa (outer)
> - Hoop stress: $133.33$ MPa (inner) and $53.33$ MPa (outer)
> - Maximum hoop stress location: **Inner wall of the cylinder**

---

**(b) Explain the design procedure of a pressure vessel with reference to ASME code. List the standard end cap types and their applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Pressure Vessel Design Procedure per ASME BPVC Section VIII, Division 1:**

**Step 1: Service Condition Definition**
- Determine the **Maximum Working Pressure (MWP)** from process requirements.
- Determine the **Design Temperature** (with corrosion allowance).

**Step 2: Calculation of Design Pressure**
$$P_d = 1.1 \times MWP \quad \text{(or MWP + 0.1 MPa, whichever is higher)}$$

**Step 3: Material Selection**
- Select a material from ASME Section II (e.g., SA-516 Grade 70 carbon steel, SA-240 stainless steel 304/316).
- Determine the **allowable stress** $\sigma_{allow}$ from ASME tables at the design temperature.

**Step 4: Determination of Joint Efficiency ($\eta$)**
| Joint Type | Radiography | Efficiency $\eta$ |
|------------|-------------|-------------------|
| Butt weld, full penetration, double-welded | Spot | 0.85 |
| Butt weld, full penetration | 100% | 1.00 |
| Single lap, no backing strip | — | 0.70 |
| Double lap, with backing strip | — | 0.85 |
| Forge-welded, 100% radiographed | Yes | 1.00 |

**Step 5: Cylindrical Shell Thickness**

$$t = \frac{P_d \cdot D_o}{2 \sigma_{allow} \eta + 2 P_d}$$

**Step 6: Verification of Wall Ratio**

If $t/D_i > 0.1$, switch to thick-wall Lame's equations for accurate stress analysis.

**Step 7: End Cap Design**

The end cap thickness depends on the cap geometry:

**Hemispherical Cap:**
$$t = \frac{P_d \cdot D_o}{4 \sigma_{allow} \eta - 0.4 P_d}$$

**Ellipsoidal Cap (2:1 ratio):**
$$t = \frac{P_d \cdot D_o}{2 \sigma_{allow} \eta - 0.2 P_d}$$

**Torispherical Cap:**
$$t = \frac{0.885 \cdot P_d \cdot R}{(0.1 P_d - 1) \sigma_{allow} \eta}$$

Where $R$ is the crown radius of the cap.

**Step 8: Flange Design (per ASME Appendix 2)**

Use the **Waters, Wesstrom, Rossheim, Williams** method or the **Taylor Forge** method to design bolted flange connections.

**Step 9: Nozzle Reinforcement Calculations**

For openings in the shell, additional reinforcement area is calculated per **ASME UG-37** to compensate for material removed.

**Step 10: Final Checks**
- Hydrostatic test pressure: $P_{test} = 1.3 \times MAWP$
- Hydrotest stress verification: $\sigma_h \le 0.9 \sigma_y$ (yield strength at test temperature)
- Allowable Deflection Check
- Fatigue and cyclic load analysis (if applicable)

**Standard End Cap Types and Applications:**

| Cap Type | Geometry | Thickness Ratio | Applications |
|----------|----------|-----------------|--------------|
| **Flat (Circular)** | Flat plate bolted to flange | Thickest (5-10× thin shell) | Low pressure, easy access manways, large diameter vessels |
| **Hemispherical** | Perfect half-sphere | Thinnest (½ of shell) | High pressure storage tanks, submarines, pressure spheres |
| **Ellipsoidal (2:1)** | Ellipse with 2:1 axis ratio | Same as shell | Most common in chemical/process industry, distillation columns |
| **Torispherical** | Combination of crown + knuckle | Intermediate | General-purpose pressure vessels, hot water boilers |
| **Conical** | Cone-shaped with transition knuckle | Custom formula | Bottom closures for settling tanks, hoppers for solids |

> [!NOTE] **Final Answer for Part (b):**
> The design procedure follows a 10-step methodology as outlined in ASME BPVC Section VIII Div. 1. End caps are selected based on pressure rating, diameter, and cost considerations. The **ellipsoidal 2:1 cap** is the most widely used in industry due to its balanced strength-to-cost ratio.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Critical KTU Valuation Tips)**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting Design Pressure Factor ($-1$ to $-2$ marks):** Many students use working pressure directly in the thickness formula instead of multiplying by 1.1 to get design pressure. ASME mandates this buffer for pressure surges.
> 
> 2. **Sign Convention Errors in Lame's Equations ($-2$ marks):** Radial stress at the inner wall must be **negative** (compressive) and equal to $-P_i$. Forgetting the negative sign or stating $\sigma_r = +P_i$ loses full marks.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting to Verify Wall Ratio ($-1$ mark):** After calculating thickness, you must confirm whether the cylinder is thin or thick. Using thin-wall formulas on a thick cylinder yields incorrect (overly optimistic) results.
> 
> 4. **Wrong Corrosion Allowance Addition ($-1$ mark):** Corrosion allowance is added **outside** the formula on top of calculated thickness: $t_{final} = t_{calculated} + c$. Not inside the formula.
> 
> 5. **Confusing Hoop and Longitudinal Stress ($-1$ to $-3$ marks):** Hoop stress formula has $2t$ in denominator; longitudinal has $4t$. Memorize: **Hoop = Double = 2**.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to Add Hemispherical/2:1 Cap Thickness Formula ($-1$ mark):** KTU often asks to design the end cap as a separate sub-question. You must use the **specific** cap formula, not the cylindrical shell formula.
> 
> 7. **Not Rounding UP the Thickness ($-1$ mark):** Always round UP the wall thickness to the next standard plate thickness (e.g., 24.65 mm → 25 mm). Never round down.
> 
> 8. **Mixing up Internal/External Radius ($-2$ marks):** Lame's equations require $r_i$ (inner) and $r_o$ (outer). Swapping them yields completely wrong results.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**
> 
> **Core Classification:**
> - Thin Cylinder: $t/D_i \le 0.1$ → use simple membrane formulas
> - Thick Cylinder: $t/D_i > 0.1$ → use Lame's equations
> 
> **Thin Cylinder "Golden Trio" of Formulas:**
> - Hoop Stress: $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P D_i}{2t}$ (circumferential, **maximum stress**)
> - Longitudinal Stress: $\sigma_l = \dfrac{P D_i}{4t}$ (axial, **half of hoop**)
> - Radial Stress: $\sigma_r \approx 0$ (negligible for thin walls)
> - **Key Ratio:** $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l$ (always, for thin cylinders)
> 
> **Lame's Equations (Thick Cylinder):**
> - Radial: $\sigma_r = A - \dfrac{B}{r^2}$
> - Hoop: $\sigma_\theta = A + \dfrac{B}{r^2}$
> - Lame Constants: $A = \dfrac{P_i r_i^2 - P_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$, $B = \dfrac{(P_i - P_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$
> - **Boundary checks:** $\sigma_r(r_i) = -P_i$ and $\sigma_r(r_o) = -P_o$ (always verify)
> - **Maximum hoop stress location:** Inner wall of cylinder
> 
> **ASME Design Formula (Thin Wall Vessel):**
> - Wall thickness: $t = \dfrac{P_d \cdot D_o}{2 \sigma_{allow} \eta + 2 P_d}$
> - Design pressure: $P_d = 1.1 \times P_{working}$
> - Corrosion allowance: $c = 1$ to $3$ mm
> - Final thickness: $t_{design} = t_{calculated} + c$ (round UP)
> 
> **Pressure Vessel End Cap Formulas:**
> - Hemispherical: $t = \dfrac{P_d D_o}{4 \sigma_{allow} \eta - 0.4 P_d}$
> - Ellipsoidal 2:1: $t = \dfrac{P_d D_o}{2 \sigma_{allow} \eta - 0.2 P_d}$
> - Torispherical: $t = \dfrac{0.885 P_d R}{(0.1 P_d - 1) \sigma_{allow} \eta}$
> 
> **Compound Cylinders (Shrink Fit):**
> - Used to **reduce peak hoop stress** at the inner wall
> - Two cylinders assembled with radial interference $\delta$
> - Shrink fit pressure $P_s$ develops at the common interface
> - Practical examples: Artillery gun barrels (autofrettage), high-pressure hydraulic accumulators
> 
> **Code & Standards to Remember:**
> - ASME BPVC Section VIII (Pressure Vessels)
> - IS 2825 (Unfired Pressure Vessels — Indian Standard)
> - PD 5500 (UK)
> - EN 13445 (European)
> 
> **Factor of Safety (FoS) Quick Reference:**
> - Static pressure, ductile material: $N = 2.0$ to $4.0$
> - Cyclic/fatigue loading: $N = 6.0$ to $10.0$
> - Brittle material (cast iron): $N = 6.0$ to $8.0$
> - Cast steel: $N = 4.0$ to $6.0$
> 
> **Joint Efficiency Quick Memory Aid:**
> - $0.70$ = Single lap, no backing (worst)
> - $0.85$ = Double lap or spot-radiographed butt
> - $1.00$ = Full penetration, 100% radiographed (best)
> 
> **Common Material Allowable Stresses (Reference):**
> - Mild Steel (IS 2062): $\sigma_{allow} = 100$ to $130$ MPa
> - Stainless Steel 304: $\sigma_{allow} = 115$ to $140$ MPa
> - High-tensile Steel: $\sigma_{allow} = 150$ to $200$ MPa

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thin Cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thin Cylinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THIN CYLINDER (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A cylinder is classified as a **thin cylinder** when the ratio of its **wall thickness ($t$)** to its **internal diameter ($d$)** satisfies the condition:
> $$\frac{t}{d} \leq \frac{1}{20}$$
> Under this condition, the **radial stress** ($\sigma_r$) is negligibly small compared to the **hoop stress** and **longitudinal stress**, and is therefore ignored in design calculations. The stresses are assumed to be **uniformly distributed** across the wall thickness.

In pressure vessel design (per **IS 2825 / ASME Sec VIII**), thin cylinder theory is the foundation for calculating stresses in storage tanks, hydraulic cylinders, compressed air receivers, steam boilers, and pipeline shells.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Balloon" Model:**
> Imagine blowing up a long, cylindrical balloon. As air pressure builds up inside, you notice two distinct things happening:
> 1. **The balloon tries to expand radially** (it bulges outward like a sausage) → this creates **Hoop (Circumferential) Stress**.
> 2. **The balloon tries to stretch lengthwise** (the two ends try to fly apart) → this creates **Longitudinal (Axial) Stress**.
>
> The wall of the balloon is doing two jobs simultaneously — keeping the cylinder from "bursting open like a peeled banana" and keeping the two ends from "flying apart like a stretched rubber band."
>
> Now, if the wall is thin (rubber balloon), the pressure is roughly the same on both inner and outer surfaces, so we ignore the radial component. That is the essence of thin cylinder theory.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Ratio threshold**: $t/d \leq 1/20$ (commonly $1/25$ as a stricter criterion)
- **Poisson's ratio for steel**: $\nu = 0.3$
- **Young's Modulus for mild steel**: $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 200 \text{ GPa}$
- **Allowable stresses** depend on material — e.g., mild steel: $\sigma_t = 130 \text{ MPa}$, cast iron: $\sigma_t = 70 \text{ MPa}$
- **Principal stresses** in a thin cylinder follow the order: $\sigma_1 > \sigma_2 > \sigma_3 = 0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEY DISTINCTION:** For thin cylinders, **hoop stress is always twice the longitudinal stress** — a fact that explains why pressure vessels always rupture along a **longitudinal seam first** (e.g., the tragic boiler explosions in early industrial history).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of the Three Principal Stresses

A thin cylindrical pressure vessel (closed at both ends) subjected to an internal gauge pressure $p$ develops **two principal stresses** in its wall:

### (A) Hoop / Circumferential Stress ($\sigma_h$ or $\sigma_c$)

This stress acts in a direction **tangential to the circumference** (like the rings of a barrel).

- **"Why"** it exists: Internal pressure pushes outward radially; equilibrium of a half-cylinder section requires a tangential (hoop) force to hold the two halves together.
- **"How"** to find it: Consider a half-cylinder of length $L$ and diameter $d$. The pressure force on the projected area ($d \times L$) is balanced by the hoop stress acting on two cross-sections of wall ($2 \times t \times L$).

### (B) Longitudinal / Axial Stress ($\sigma_l$ or $\sigma_a$)

This stress acts **along the axis** of the cylinder (like stretching a spring).

- **"Why"** it exists: Pressure acting on the closed end caps pushes them outward; the cylindrical shell must resist being pulled apart longitudinally.
- **"How"** to find it: Pressure force on end cap ($\frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot p$) is balanced by longitudinal stress on the circular wall ($\pi \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_l$).

### (C) Radial Stress ($\sigma_r$) — *Negligible*

- Acts perpendicular to the wall surface.
- For thin cylinders, $\sigma_r \ll \sigma_h$, so it is **ignored** (assumed = 0).
- Maximum value at inner surface = $p$, which is tiny compared to $\sigma_h$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thin cylinder criterion | $\dfrac{t}{d} \leq \dfrac{1}{20}$ | dimensionless | Below this ratio, thin theory applies |
| Hoop (Circumferential) Stress | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot t}$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | Always maximum principal stress |
| Longitudinal (Axial) Stress | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t}$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | Always half of hoop stress |
| Ratio | $\sigma_h = 2 \cdot \sigma_l$ | — | Critical relationship for KTU problems |
| Spherical Shell Stress | $\sigma_h = \sigma_l = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t}$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | For spherical pressure vessels |
| Change in Diameter | $\dfrac{\delta d}{d} = \dfrac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma_h - \nu \cdot \sigma_l \right] = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t \cdot E} (2 - \nu)$ | dimensionless | Radial expansion |
| Change in Length | $\dfrac{\delta l}{L} = \dfrac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma_l - \nu \cdot \sigma_h \right] = \dfrac{p \cdot L}{4 \cdot t \cdot E} (1 - 2\nu)$ | dimensionless | Axial elongation |
| Change in Volume | $\dfrac{\delta V}{V} = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t \cdot E} (5 - 4\nu)$ | dimensionless | For $\nu = 0.3$: factor = 3.8 |
| Longitudinal Joint Efficiency | $\eta_l = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$ | — | Used when $\eta_l < \eta_c$ |
| Circumferential Joint Efficiency | $\eta_c = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t}$ | — | Used when $\eta_c < \eta_l$ |
| Design Thickness (from hoop) | $t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot \sigma_t \cdot \eta_c} + C$ | $\text{mm}$ | $C$ = corrosion allowance |
| Design Thickness (from longitudinal) | $t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot \sigma_t \cdot \eta_l} + C$ | $\text{mm}$ | |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLDEN RULE:** In all KTU problems, **choose the LARGER thickness** between hoop-based and longitudinal-based calculations — this ensures safety against the more critical failure mode.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic & Pneumatic Cylinders:** Calculating burst pressure of actuator barrels.
- **Steam Boilers & Air Receivers:** Pressure vessel codes (ASME, IS 2825) mandate thin-wall analysis for vessels up to ~300 mm diameter.
- **Compressed Gas Cylinders (LPG, Oxygen):** Critical for transport safety.
- **Pipeline Design (Oil & Gas):** Long-distance pipelines are classic thin cylinders.
- **Pressure Gauges & Bourdon Tubes:** Curved tube elements rely on hoop stress analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Hoop Stress ($\sigma_h$)

### Setting Up the Free Body Diagram

Consider a thin cylindrical shell of internal diameter $d$, wall thickness $t$, and length $L$, subjected to internal pressure $p$.

**Step 1 — Imagine cutting the cylinder with a diametrical plane along its length.**
We get **two half-cylinders**. The pressure pushes them apart.

**Step 2 — Calculate the force due to pressure pushing the halves apart.**

The projected area on which pressure acts is:
$$A_{projected} = d \times L$$

Therefore, the **total bursting force** (trying to split the cylinder) is:
$$F_{burst} = p \times A_{projected} = p \cdot d \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the resisting force from hoop stress.**

Hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acts on **two wall cross-sections** (one on each half-cylinder) of area:
$$A_{wall} = 2 \times (t \times L) = 2 \cdot t \cdot L$$

The **resisting force** is:
$$F_{resist} = \sigma_h \times A_{wall} = \sigma_h \cdot 2 \cdot t \cdot L$$

**Step 4 — Apply equilibrium ($F_{burst} = F_{resist}$).**

$$p \cdot d \cdot L = \sigma_h \cdot 2 \cdot t \cdot L$$

**Step 5 — Simplify by canceling $L$ (it appears on both sides).**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot t} \quad \text{(N/mm}^2 \text{)}$$

> **Logic Note:** The length $L$ cancels because the cylinder is uniform — stress is independent of how long you cut it. This is why hoop stress is the same everywhere along the cylinder.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Longitudinal Stress ($\sigma_l$)

### Setting Up the Free Body Diagram

**Step 1 — Imagine cutting the cylinder with a transverse plane perpendicular to its axis.**
We get two pieces. The pressure on the **closed end cap** pushes the cap outward.

**Step 2 — Calculate the force on the end cap.**

Area of end cap:
$$A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the bursting force along the axis.**

$$F_{axial} = p \cdot A_{cap} = p \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the resisting force from longitudinal stress.**

Longitudinal stress $\sigma_l$ acts on the **circular wall cross-section** (annulus area, approximated as):
$$A_{wall,axial} = \pi \cdot d \cdot t$$

The **resisting force** is:
$$F_{resist,axial} = \sigma_l \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot t$$

**Step 5 — Apply equilibrium.**

$$p \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 = \sigma_l \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot t$$

**Step 6 — Simplify.**

$$\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t} \quad \text{(N/mm}^2 \text{)}$$

> **Logic Note:** Dividing by $\pi \cdot d$ on both sides and canceling $\frac{\pi}{4}$ with $\pi$ gives $\frac{1}{4}$. The ratio $\sigma_h / \sigma_l = 2$ emerges naturally from this derivation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Change in Diameter ($\delta d$)

Using **generalized Hooke's Law** for biaxial stress state:

**Step 1 — Strain in hoop direction (circumferential).**
$$\varepsilon_h = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma_h - \nu \cdot \sigma_l \right]$$

**Step 2 — Strain in longitudinal direction.**
$$\varepsilon_l = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma_l - \nu \cdot \sigma_h \right]$$

**Step 3 — The circumferential strain equals the diametrical strain** (since circumference = $\pi d$):
$$\frac{\delta d}{d} = \varepsilon_h = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma_h - \nu \cdot \sigma_l \right]$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the stress values.**

$$\frac{\delta d}{d} = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \frac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot t} - \nu \cdot \frac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $\frac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t}$.**

$$\frac{\delta d}{d} = \frac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t \cdot E} \left[ 2 - \nu \right]$$

**Step 6 — Multiply both sides by $d$ to get change in diameter.**

$$\delta d = \frac{p \cdot d^2}{4 \cdot t \cdot E} (2 - \nu)$$

For $\nu = 0.3$:
$$\delta d = \frac{0.85 \cdot p \cdot d^2}{2 \cdot t \cdot E}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Change in Volume ($\delta V$)

**Step 1 — Volume of a cylinder:** $V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 L$

**Step 2 — Logarithmic differentiation** (for small strains, all changes are additive):
$$\frac{\delta V}{V} = 2 \cdot \frac{\delta d}{d} + \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

> **Logic Note:** Diameter appears squared, hence the multiplier 2 (product rule). Length appears linearly.

**Step 3 — Substitute the strain expressions.**

$$\frac{\delta V}{V} = \frac{2}{E} \left[ \sigma_h - \nu \cdot \sigma_l \right] + \frac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma_l - \nu \cdot \sigma_h \right]$$

**Step 4 — Expand and collect coefficients of $\sigma_h$ and $\sigma_l$.**

$$\frac{\delta V}{V} = \frac{1}{E} \left[ 2 \sigma_h - 2 \nu \sigma_l + \sigma_l - \nu \sigma_h \right]$$

$$\frac{\delta V}{V} = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \sigma_h (2 - \nu) + \sigma_l (1 - 2\nu) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p d}{2 t}$ and $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p d}{4 t}$.**

$$\frac{\delta V}{V} = \frac{1}{E} \left[ \frac{p d}{2 t}(2 - \nu) + \frac{p d}{4 t}(1 - 2\nu) \right]$$

$$\frac{\delta V}{V} = \frac{p d}{4 t E} \left[ 2(2 - \nu) + (1 - 2\nu) \right]$$

$$\frac{\delta V}{V} = \frac{p d}{4 t E} \left[ 4 - 2\nu + 1 - 2\nu \right]$$

**Step 6 — Final simplification.**

$$\boxed{\frac{\delta V}{V} = \frac{p d}{4 t E} (5 - 4\nu)}$$

For $\nu = 0.3$:
$$\frac{\delta V}{V} = \frac{p d}{4 t E} (3.8)$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example (Conceptual Framework)

### **Sample Problem Setup:**
A thin cylindrical pressure vessel has $d = 500 \text{ mm}$, $t = 15 \text{ mm}$, $p = 2 \text{ MPa}$, $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$.

| **Quantity** | **Substitution** | **Numerical Value** |
|---|---|---|
| Check thinness | $15/500 = 0.03 < 0.05$ | ✅ Thin cylinder confirmed |
| Hoop Stress | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{2 \times 500}{2 \times 15}$ | $= 33.33 \text{ MPa}$ |
| Longitudinal Stress | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{2 \times 500}{4 \times 15}$ | $= 16.67 \text{ MPa}$ |
| Change in Diameter | $\delta d = \dfrac{2 \times (500)^2}{4 \times 15 \times 200{,}000} \times (2 - 0.3)$ | $= 0.0708 \text{ mm}$ |
| Change in Volume | $\dfrac{\delta V}{V} = \dfrac{2 \times 500}{4 \times 15 \times 200{,}000} \times (5 - 1.2)$ | $= 1.583 \times 10^{-6}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stress Component Diagram (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of stress flow** in a thin cylindrical pressure vessel:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Internal Pressure p Applied] --> B[Force Acts on Cylindrical Wall]
    A --> C[Force Acts on End Caps]

    B --> D[Hoop Stress sigma_h = pd/2t]
    B --> E[Longitudinal Stress sigma_l = pd/4t]
    B --> F[Radial Stress sigma_r = 0 Negligible]

    C --> G[End Cap Tries to Push Outward]
    G --> H[Wall Resists via Longitudinal Stress]

    D --> I[Maximum Principal Stress sigma_1]
    E --> J[Intermediate Principal Stress sigma_2]
    F --> K[Minor Principal Stress sigma_3]

    I --> L[Design Failure Mode: Longitudinal Seam Rupture]
    J --> M[Wall Thickness Selection Criteria]
    K --> N[Radial Stress Ignored in Thin Theory]

    M --> O[Compare t_hoop vs t_longitudinal]
    O --> P[Choose MAXIMUM Thickness for Safety]

    P --> Q[Add Corrosion Allowance C]
    Q --> R[Final Design Thickness t_design]
```

---

## 4.2 Joint Efficiency Comparison Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Thin Cylinder Under Pressure] --> B{Two Seams Present}
    B --> C[Longitudinal Seam]
    B --> D[Circumferential Seam]

    C --> E[Resists Hoop Stress]
    D --> F[Resists Longitudinal Stress]

    E --> G[Calculate t from hoop]
    F --> H[Calculate t from longitudinal]

    G --> I[Use eta_c = circumferential efficiency]
    H --> J[Use eta_l = longitudinal efficiency]

    I --> K[Select MAX of t_hoop and t_longitudinal]
    J --> K

    K --> L[Add corrosion allowance C]
    L --> M[Final Design t_design]
```

---

## 4.3 Deformation Pattern Schematic (Conceptual)

```
        ╔══════════════════════════╗   ← Original Length L₀
        ║                          ║
        ║   ┌──────────────┐       ║   ← Original Diameter d₀
        ║   │              │       ║
        ║   │   PRESSURE   │       ║
        ║   │     (p)      │       ║
        ║   │              │       ║
        ║   └──────────────┘       ║
        ║                          ║
        ╚══════════════════════════╝

   AFTER INTERNAL PRESSURE APPLIED:

        ╔════════════════════════════════╗   ← Length: L₀ + δL
        ║                                ║      (increases slightly)
        ║    ┌──────────────────┐         ║
        ║    │                  │         ║   ← Diameter: d₀ + δd
        ║    │     PRESSURE     │         ║      (increases more)
        ║    │       (p)        │         ║
        ║    │                  │         ║
        ║    └──────────────────┘         ║
        ║                                ║
        ╚════════════════════════════════╝

   Key Relationships:
   δd > δL   (because hoop stress is larger)
   δV > 0    (volume always increases under internal pressure)
```

---

## 4.4 Sphere vs Cylinder Stress Comparison

| **Feature** | **Cylinder** | **Sphere** |
|---|---|---|
| Hoop stress | $\dfrac{pd}{2t}$ | $\dfrac{pd}{4t}$ |
| Longitudinal stress | $\dfrac{pd}{4t}$ | $\dfrac{pd}{4t}$ |
| Stress uniformity | Non-uniform ($\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$) | Uniform ($\sigma_h = \sigma_l$) |
| Material efficiency | Less efficient | **50% more efficient** |
| Why? | Hoop stress concentrated in one direction | Stress distributed equally in all directions |
| Real example | Storage tanks | LPG bullets, gas holders |

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define a thin cylinder. State the condition under which a cylinder is classified as thin, and list the stresses developed in a thin cylindrical pressure vessel.**

#### Model Answer:

> **Thin Cylinder:** A cylinder is classified as a thin cylinder when the ratio of its wall thickness ($t$) to its internal diameter ($d$) satisfies:
> $$\frac{t}{d} \leq \frac{1}{20}$$
>
> Under this condition, the stresses are assumed to be **uniformly distributed** across the wall thickness, and the radial stress is neglected.
>
> **Three stresses developed:**
> 1. **Hoop (Circumferential) stress:** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pd}{2t}$
> 2. **Longitudinal (Axial) stress:** $\sigma_l = \dfrac{pd}{4t}$
> 3. **Radial stress:** $\sigma_r \approx 0$ (neglected for thin cylinders)

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Show that the hoop stress in a thin cylindrical shell is twice the longitudinal stress.**

#### Model Answer:

Hoop stress:
$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot t}$$

Longitudinal stress:
$$\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t}$$

Taking the ratio:
$$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \frac{p \cdot d / (2t)}{p \cdot d / (4t)} = \frac{4t}{2t} = 2$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\sigma_h = 2 \cdot \sigma_l}$$

**Physical significance:** Hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress, which is why pressure vessels fail along the **longitudinal seam** (which resists hoop stress) and not along the circumferential seam.

---

## PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### **(a) A thin cylindrical pressure vessel of internal diameter 800 mm and wall thickness 12 mm is subjected to an internal pressure of 1.8 MPa. Determine the hoop stress and longitudinal stress developed in the cylinder wall. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- Internal diameter: $d = 800 \text{ mm}$
- Wall thickness: $t = 12 \text{ mm}$
- Internal pressure: $p = 1.8 \text{ MPa} = 1.8 \text{ N/mm}^2$

**Check thinness:**
$$\frac{t}{d} = \frac{12}{800} = 0.015 < \frac{1}{20} = 0.05 \quad ✅$$

**Hoop Stress:**
$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot t} = \frac{1.8 \times 800}{2 \times 12}$$

$$\sigma_h = \frac{1440}{24} = 60 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

**Longitudinal Stress:**
$$\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t} = \frac{1.8 \times 800}{4 \times 12}$$

$$\sigma_l = \frac{1440}{48} = 30 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 30 \text{ MPa}$$

**Result:**
$$\boxed{\sigma_h = 60 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_l = 30 \text{ MPa}}$$

> **Verification:** $\sigma_h = 2 \cdot \sigma_l$ ✅ ($60 = 2 \times 30$)

---

#### **(b) For the vessel in part (a), calculate the change in diameter, change in length, and percentage change in volume. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}$ and $\nu = 0.3$. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given (additional):**
- Length (assume): $L = 2000 \text{ mm}$
- Young's Modulus: $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}$
- Poisson's ratio: $\nu = 0.3$

**(i) Change in Diameter:**

$$\delta d = \frac{p \cdot d^2}{4 \cdot t \cdot E} (2 - \nu)$$

$$\delta d = \frac{1.8 \times (800)^2}{4 \times 12 \times 2 \times 10^5} \times (2 - 0.3)$$

$$\delta d = \frac{1.8 \times 640000}{9.6 \times 10^6} \times 1.7$$

$$\delta d = \frac{1152000}{9600000} \times 1.7 = 0.12 \times 1.7 = 0.204 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\delta d = 0.204 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(expansion)}}$$

**(ii) Change in Length:**

$$\delta L = \frac{p \cdot L}{4 \cdot t \cdot E} (1 - 2\nu)$$

$$\delta L = \frac{1.8 \times 2000}{4 \times 12 \times 2 \times 10^5} \times (1 - 0.6)$$

$$\delta L = \frac{3600}{9.6 \times 10^6} \times 0.4$$

$$\delta L = 3.75 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4 = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\delta L = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm} \quad \text{(negligible)}}$$

**(iii) Percentage Change in Volume:**

$$\frac{\delta V}{V} = \frac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t \cdot E} (5 - 4\nu)$$

$$\frac{\delta V}{V} = \frac{1.8 \times 800}{4 \times 12 \times 2 \times 10^5} \times (5 - 1.2)$$

$$\frac{\delta V}{V} = \frac{1440}{9.6 \times 10^6} \times 3.8$$

$$\frac{\delta V}{V} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.8 = 5.7 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Percentage:**
$$\frac{\delta V}{V} \times 100 = 0.057\%$$

$$\boxed{\delta V = 0.057\% \text{ of original volume}}$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### **(a) A thin cylindrical shell is to be designed for an internal pressure of 3 MPa. The diameter of the shell is 600 mm. If the allowable tensile stress in the material is 120 MPa, and the circumferential joint efficiency is 75%, determine the necessary wall thickness. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- Internal pressure: $p = 3 \text{ MPa}$
- Internal diameter: $d = 600 \text{ mm}$
- Allowable tensile stress: $\sigma_t = 120 \text{ MPa}$
- Circumferential joint efficiency: $\eta_c = 75\% = 0.75$

**Step 1 — Identify the critical stress.**

Since the circumferential joint efficiency ($\eta_c$) is the **only given efficiency**, and hoop stress is **twice** longitudinal stress, the design is governed by **hoop stress** (longitudinal seam resists hoop stress).

**Step 2 — Apply the design equation for hoop stress:**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot t \cdot \eta_c}$$

Rearranging for $t$:
$$t = \frac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot \sigma_t \cdot \eta_c}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$t = \frac{3 \times 600}{2 \times 120 \times 0.75}$$

$$t = \frac{1800}{180}$$

$$\boxed{t = 10 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Verification (check thinness).**

$$\frac{t}{d} = \frac{10}{600} = 0.0167 < \frac{1}{20} = 0.05 \quad ✅$$

Thin cylinder assumption **valid**.

---

#### **(b) A spherical pressure vessel of diameter 1.5 m and wall thickness 10 mm is subjected to an internal pressure of 2.5 MPa. Calculate: (i) the stress in the shell, (ii) compare its material efficiency with a cylindrical vessel of the same dimensions, and (iii) calculate the change in volume if $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$ and $\nu = 0.3$. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- Diameter: $d = 1500 \text{ mm}$
- Wall thickness: $t = 10 \text{ mm}$
- Pressure: $p = 2.5 \text{ MPa}$
- $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$

**(i) Stress in Spherical Shell:**

For a sphere, both hoop and longitudinal stresses are equal:
$$\sigma_{sphere} = \frac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t} = \frac{2.5 \times 1500}{4 \times 10} = \frac{3750}{40}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{sphere} = 93.75 \text{ MPa}}$$

**(ii) Material Efficiency Comparison:**

For the **same dimensions**, the cylindrical vessel would have:
$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot t} = \frac{2.5 \times 1500}{2 \times 10} = 187.5 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_l = 93.75 \text{ MPa}$$

**Comparison:**
$$\frac{\sigma_{cylinder}}{\sigma_{sphere}} = \frac{187.5}{93.75} = 2$$

**Conclusion:** The spherical vessel produces **half the maximum stress** of an equivalent cylinder, making it **50% more material-efficient**. For the same pressure and dimensions, a sphere is significantly safer.

**(iii) Change in Volume for Sphere:**

For a sphere, both stresses are equal ($\sigma_h = \sigma_l = \sigma$), so:
$$\frac{\delta V}{V} = \frac{3 \sigma}{E} (1 - \nu) = \frac{3 \times 93.75}{2 \times 10^5} \times (1 - 0.3)$$

$$\frac{\delta V}{V} = \frac{281.25}{2 \times 10^5} \times 0.7 = 1.406 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.7$$

$$\frac{\delta V}{V} = 9.844 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Original volume of sphere:**
$$V = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 = \frac{\pi}{6} \times (1500)^3 = 1.767 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Change in volume:**
$$\delta V = 9.844 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.767 \times 10^9$$

$$\boxed{\delta V \approx 1.74 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3 = 1740 \text{ cm}^3}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON MISTAKES:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in intermediate steps.** Marks are deducted if $p$ is used in MPa while $d$ and $t$ are in mm without unit check. Always use **N/mm²** consistently for stress.
> 2. **Forgetting the corrosion allowance ($C$).** When designing wall thickness for a real application, add $C$ (typically 1–3 mm). KTU often gives this in Part B questions.
> 3. **Using $\sigma_h = \sigma_l$ for cylinders.** A huge trap! Only **spheres** have $\sigma_h = \sigma_l$. For cylinders, **always $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$**.
> 4. **Neglecting $\eta$ in design problems.** If joint efficiency is given, it **MUST appear** in the formula. A common error is to set $\eta = 1$ by mistake.
> 5. **Wrong sign in Poisson's ratio effect.** The change in diameter formula has $(2 - \nu)$ and NOT $(2 + \nu)$. Memorize it correctly.
> 6. **Forgetting $L$ for change in length.** Students often substitute $d$ instead of $L$ in $\delta L = \dfrac{pL}{4tE}(1-2\nu)$. Read carefully!
> 7. **Major marks lost:** Not stating the **thin cylinder check** ($t/d \leq 1/20$) at the start of the solution. Examiners award a mark for **validating the assumption**.

---
