# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**State Lame's equations for a thick cylinder and define all the terms involved.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The radial and circumferential (hoop) stresses in a thick-walled cylinder under internal and external pressure are given by:

$$ \sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^{2}} $$

$$ \sigma_{\theta} = A + \frac{B}{r^{2}} $$

where:
- $A$ and $B$ = **Lamé's constants**, evaluated from boundary conditions.
- $r$ = radial distance from the cylinder axis.
- $\sigma_r$ = radial stress.
- $\sigma_{\theta}$ = circumferential (hoop) stress.

The values of $A$ and $B$ for internal pressure $p_i$ and external pressure $p_o$ with inner radius $a$ and outer radius $b$ are:

$$ A = \frac{p_i a^{2} - p_o b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}, \qquad B = \frac{(p_i - p_o) a^{2} b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}} $$

> **Marking scheme:** Equations (1 mark) + constant definitions (1 mark) + boundary-condition interpretation (1 mark).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**List any six assumptions made in the derivation of Lame's equations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. The material is **homogeneous and isotropic**.
2. The material obeys **Hooke's law** (linearly elastic).
3. The cylinder is **initially stress-free**.
4. Loading is **axisymmetric** and uniform along the length.
5. **Plane sections remain plane** (plane stress / plane strain).
6. The cylinder is **infinitely long**, so end effects are neglected.
7. The wall thickness is **uniform**.
8. Pressure is **uniformly distributed** over the inner and outer surfaces.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** A thick cylinder has an internal diameter of **200 mm** and an external diameter of **400 mm**. It is subjected to an internal pressure of **60 N/mm²**. Determine:
1. The Lamé constants $A$ and $B$.
2. The hoop stress and radial stress at the inner and outer surfaces.
3. The maximum hoop stress and the radial stress at the inner surface. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** If the same cylinder is also subjected to an external pressure of **20 N/mm²** in addition to the internal pressure, find the new hoop stresses at the inner and outer surfaces and the radial stress at both surfaces. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Given:**
- $a = 100$ mm (inner radius), $b = 200$ mm (outer radius)
- $p_i = 60$ N/mm², $p_o = 0$

**1) Lamé Constants:**

$$ A = \frac{p_i a^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}} = \frac{60 \times 100^{2}}{200^{2} - 100^{2}} = \frac{60 \times 10000}{40000 - 10000} = \frac{600000}{30000} = 20 \text{ N/mm}^{2} $$

$$ B = \frac{p_i a^{2} b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}} = \frac{60 \times 100^{2} \times 200^{2}}{30000} = \frac{60 \times 10^{4} \times 4 \times 10^{4}}{3 \times 10^{4}} = 80 \times 10^{5} / 30000 = 8 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}^{0} $$

Wait — re-evaluating cleanly:

$$ B = \frac{p_i \, a^{2} b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}} = \frac{60 \times 10000 \times 40000}{30000} = \frac{60 \times 4 \times 10^{8}}{3 \times 10^{4}} = 8 \times 10^{5} \text{ N·mm}^{2} $$

So $B = 8 \times 10^{5}$ N·mm² (units of stress × length²). When divided by $r^{2}$, units become stress.

**2) Stresses at the Inner Surface ($r = a = 100$ mm):**

$$ \sigma_r(a) = A - \frac{B}{a^{2}} = 20 - \frac{8 \times 10^{5}}{10000} = 20 - 80 = -60 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \quad (\text{compressive, equals } -p_i) $$

$$ \sigma_{\theta}(a) = A + \frac{B}{a^{2}} = 20 + 80 = 100 \text{ N/mm}^{2} $$

**3) Stresses at the Outer Surface ($r = b = 200$ mm):**

$$ \sigma_r(b) = A - \frac{B}{b^{2}} = 20 - \frac{8 \times 10^{5}}{40000} = 20 - 20 = 0 \text{ N/mm}^{2} $$

$$ \sigma_{\theta}(b) = A + \frac{B}{b^{2}} = 20 + 20 = 40 \text{ N/mm}^{2} $$

**4) Maximum Hoop Stress:** Occurs at the inner surface:

$$ \sigma_{\theta,\max} = \frac{p_i (a^{2} + b^{2})}{b^{2} - a^{2}} = \frac{60 \times (10000 + 40000)}{30000} = \frac{60 \times 50000}{30000} = 100 \text{ N/mm}^{2} $$

**Maximum shear stress at the inner surface:**

$$ \tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_{\theta} - \sigma_r}{2} = \frac{100 - (-60)}{2} = 80 \text{ N/mm}^{2} $$

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Given:** $p_i = 60$ N/mm², $p_o = 20$ N/mm², $a = 100$ mm, $b = 200$ mm.

**New Lamé Constants:**

$$ A = \frac{p_i a^{2} - p_o b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}} = \frac{60 \times 10000 - 20 \times 40000}{30000} = \frac{600000 - 800000}{30000} = \frac{-200000}{30000} = -6.67 \text{ N/mm}^{2} $$

$$ B = \frac{(p_i - p_o) a^{2} b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}} = \frac{(60 - 20) \times 10000 \times 40000}{30000} = \frac{40 \times 4 \times 10^{8}}{3 \times 10^{4}} = 5.333 \times 10^{5} \text{ N·mm}^{2} $$

**Stresses at Inner Surface ($r = 100$ mm):**

$$ \sigma_r(a) = -6.67 - \frac{5.333 \times 10^{5}}{10000} = -6.67 - 53.33 = -60 \text{ N/mm}^{2} $$

$$ \sigma_{\theta}(a) = -6.67 + 53.33 = 46.67 \text{ N/mm}^{2} $$

**Stresses at Outer Surface ($r = 200$ mm):**

$$ \sigma_r(b) = -6.67 - \frac{5.333 \times 10^{5}}{40000} = -6.67 - 13.33 = -20 \text{ N/mm}^{2} $$

$$ \sigma_{\theta}(b) = -6.67 + 13.33 = 6.67 \text{ N/mm}^{2} $$

**Observation:** The hoop stress has dropped from 100 N/mm² to 46.67 N/mm² at the inner surface — a **53% reduction**. This is the engineering rationale behind **autofrettage and compound cylinder design**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Derive Lame's equations for the stresses in a thick cylinder subjected to internal pressure only. State clearly all the assumptions used. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A closed-end cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of **300 mm** and a wall thickness of **30 mm**. The internal pressure is **12 N/mm²**. Calculate:
1. Hoop stress, longitudinal stress, and maximum shear stress.
2. The change in internal diameter if $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm² and $\nu = 0.3$. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

Refer to the complete derivation in **Section 3 of these notes**. A condensed board-style version follows:

1. State the equilibrium equation in polar coordinates:
$$ \frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_{\theta}}{r} = 0 $$
2. State compatibility condition yielding $\sigma_r + \sigma_{\theta} = 2A$ and $\sigma_{\theta} - \sigma_r = 2B/r^{2}$.
3. Solve to obtain:
$$ \sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^{2}}, \qquad \sigma_{\theta} = A + \frac{B}{r^{2}} $$
4. Apply boundary conditions $\sigma_r(a) = -p_i$ and $\sigma_r(b) = 0$ to find $A = p_i a^{2}/(b^{2}-a^{2})$ and $B = p_i a^{2} b^{2}/(b^{2}-a^{2})$.
5. List assumptions (homogeneous, isotropic, axisymmetric, plane stress/strain, Hookean, no initial stress, etc.).

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Given:**
- Inner diameter = 300 mm ⇒ $a = 150$ mm
- Wall thickness = 30 mm ⇒ $b = 180$ mm
- $p_i = 12$ N/mm², $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.3$

**1) Lamé Constants:**

$$ A = \frac{p_i a^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}} = \frac{12 \times 150^{2}}{180^{2} - 150^{2}} = \frac{12 \times 22500}{32400 - 22500} = \frac{270000}{9900} = 27.27 \text{ N/mm}^{2} $$

$$ B = \frac{p_i a^{2} b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}} = \frac{12 \times 22500 \times 32400}{9900} = \frac{12 \times 7.29 \times 10^{8}}{9900} = 8.836 \times 10^{5} \text{ N·mm}^{2} $$

**Hoop stress at inner surface (maximum):**

$$ \sigma_{\theta,\max} = \frac{p_i (a^{2} + b^{2})}{b^{2} - a^{2}} = \frac{12 \times (22500 + 32400)}{9900} = \frac{12 \times 54900}{9900} = 66.55 \text{ N/mm}^{2} $$

**Hoop stress at outer surface (minimum):**

$$ \sigma_{\theta,\min} = \frac{2 p_i a^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}} = \frac{2 \times 12 \times 22500}{9900} = 54.55 \text{ N/mm}^{2} $$

**Longitudinal stress (closed ends):**

$$ \sigma_z = A = 27.27 \text{ N/mm}^{2} $$

**Maximum shear stress (at inner surface):**

$$ \tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_{\theta,\max} - \sigma_r(a)}{2} = \frac{66.55 - (-12)}{2} = 39.27 \text{ N/mm}^{2} $$

**2) Change in Internal Diameter:**

Radial displacement at $r = a$:

$$ u(a) = \frac{1}{E}\left[(1-\nu) A a + (1+\nu) \frac{B}{a}\right] $$

$$ u(a) = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{5}}\left[0.7 \times 27.27 \times 150 + 1.3 \times \frac{8.836 \times 10^{5}}{150}\right] $$

$$ u(a) = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{5}}\left[2863.4 + 7658.1\right] = \frac{10521.5}{2 \times 10^{5}} = 0.0526 \text{ mm} $$

Change in diameter:

$$ \Delta d = 2 \times u(a) = 0.1052 \text{ mm} $$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where KTU students lose 2–5 marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention mix-up** — Mixing tensile-positive and compressive-positive conventions within the same problem. Always declare your convention at the start.
> 2. **Forgetting the longitudinal stress** — Closed-end cylinders have $\sigma_z = A$. KTU deducts **2 full marks** if $\sigma_z$ is omitted in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Incorrect $B$ calculation** — Many students confuse $B = (p_i - p_o) a^2 b^2 / (b^2 - a^2)$ with $B = p_i a^2 b^2 / (b^2 - a^2)$ when $p_o \neq 0$. Re-derive every time.
> 4. **Unit slip in $B$** — $B$ has units of N·mm² (not N/mm²). It only becomes stress when divided by $r^2$. **Always show the units**.
> 5. **Maximum hoop stress location** — Maximum hoop stress is at the **inner surface**, not the outer. The radial stress is **most negative** at the inner surface.
> 6. **Thin vs Thick classification** — If $t \le d/20$, use the thin-wall formula and explicitly state the classification. KTU awards only **half marks** if classification is missing.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Lame's Equation (Module 4)**

- **Lamé's Equations:**
$$ \sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^{2}}, \qquad \sigma_{\theta} = A + \frac{B}{r^{2}} $$
- **General Lame Constants** (with $p_i$ and $p_o$):
$$ A = \frac{p_i a^{2} - p_o b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}, \qquad B = \frac{(p_i - p_o) a^{2} b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}} $$
- **Only internal pressure ($p_o = 0$):** $A = p_i a^2 / (b^2 - a^2)$, $B = p_i a^2 b^2 / (b^2 - a^2)$.
- **Only external pressure ($p_i = 0$):** Swap roles of $a$ and $b$ with appropriate sign changes.
- **Maximum hoop stress** (internal pressurisation) occurs at the **inner radius**.
- **Maximum shear stress** in the wall = $(\sigma_\theta - \sigma_r)/2$ at the inner surface.
- **Longitudinal stress (closed ends)** $\sigma_z = A$ — useful cross-check.
- **Thick wall criterion:** $t > d/20$ (or $d_i/t < 20$).
- **Radial displacement** formula needed for compound cylinder (shrink-fit) problems.
- **Failure theories** commonly applied: Maximum Principal Stress, Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca), and Distortion Energy (von Mises).
- **Common exam pattern:** (i) Derivation, (ii) Stress calculation at inner & outer surfaces, (iii) Identify max stress, (iv) Compute FOS or change in dimensions.
- **Sign convention:** Always declare it. **Most KTU textbooks follow tensile-positive convention.**
- **Plane stress vs plane strain:** Plane stress uses $E$ and $\nu$ directly; plane strain uses effective moduli $E/(1-\nu^2)$ and $\nu/(1-\nu)$.
- **Practical design uses:** IS 2825 (Unfired Pressure Vessels), ASME Section VIII Division 1 & 2.
- **Hooke's Law in 2D polar form** must be memorised:
$$ \varepsilon_r = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_r - \nu \sigma_\theta), \qquad \varepsilon_\theta = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_\theta - \nu \sigma_r) $$
- **Boundary condition sign:** Internal pressure produces **compressive radial stress** at the inner wall ($\sigma_r(a) = -p_i$). KTU examiners are strict about this.
- **A and B have different units:** $A$ is stress (N/mm²), $B$ is stress × length² (N).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clavarino's and Birnie's Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Clavarino's and Birnie's Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the design of **compound (built-up) cylinders**, two thin-walled or thick-walled cylinders are assembled together by **shrink fitting** — the outer cylinder is heated, slipped over the inner cylinder, and then cooled. The original difference in their mating radii is called the **radial interference (δ)**. This interference generates a **contact pressure (p)** at the interface even before any external load is applied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clavarino's Equation** is the *compatibility equation* that relates the **radial interference (δ)** to the resulting **shrink-fit contact pressure (p)** at the common interface, considering the elastic deformations of both cylinders.
>
> **Birnie's Equation** gives the **final tangential (hoop) stresses** in the inner and outer cylinders at the interface when the compound cylinder is simultaneously subjected to **internal pressure (pᵢ)** and the **shrink-fit pressure (p)**.

These two equations, used along with **Lamé's equations**, complete the rigorous thick-cylinder analysis demanded in KTU 2024 Scheme Machine Design (Module 4).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a tight metal **ring (outer cylinder)** onto a slightly larger **shaft (inner cylinder)**. Even before you apply any twisting or pushing force, the ring grips the shaft tightly because the ring had to be stretched (expanded) and the shaft compressed (squeezed in) during assembly.

- The amount by which the shaft was originally *larger* than the hole in the ring = **radial interference (δ)**.
- The pressure that develops at the metal-to-metal contact = **shrink-fit pressure (p)**.
- **Clavarino's Equation** tells you: *"Given the interference δ, what contact pressure p will the materials develop?"* (Like asking how hard you need to push a cork into a bottle based on the cork's diameter overshoot.)
- **Birnie's Equation** then tells you: *"Now, when high-pressure fluid pᵢ is also pumped inside, what is the actual hoop stress in the shaft and in the ring at the interface?"* (Like asking how the stresses redistribute once fluid pushes the inner wall outward.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are these equations needed?**
> - A plain Lamé analysis ignores the **residual assembly stresses**.
> - In real KTU-board problems on gun barrels, hydraulic cylinders, and shrink-fit hubs (gears, pulleys, railway wheels), **Clavarino + Birnie + Lamé** form the trinity of equations.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Geometric Parameters

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| a | Inner radius of inner cylinder | mm |
| b | Common interface radius (nominal) | mm |
| c | Outer radius of outer cylinder | mm |
| δ | Radial interference | mm |
| p | Shrink-fit contact pressure | N/mm² (MPa) |
| pᵢ | Applied internal pressure | N/mm² (MPa) |
| pₒ | Applied external pressure | N/mm² (MPa) |
| E₁, E₂ | Young's moduli of inner and outer cylinder | N/mm² |
| ν₁, ν₂ | Poisson's ratios | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lamé's Equations — The Foundation

For a thick cylinder subjected to internal pressure pᵢ and external pressure pₒ, Lamé's equations give:

$$\sigma_t = \frac{p_i \cdot a^2 - p_o \cdot b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{(p_i - p_o) \cdot a^2 \cdot b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\,r^2}$$

$$\sigma_r = \frac{p_i \cdot a^2 - p_o \cdot b^2}{b^2 - a^2} - \frac{(p_i - p_o) \cdot a^2 \cdot b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\,r^2}$$

These form the **building block** for both Clavarino's and Birnie's equations.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**
The compound cylinder has three radii: a, b, c. The interface is at radius b.

**Step 2 — Decide which cylinder sees which pressure.**
- *Inner cylinder*: Internal pressure = pᵢ (applied), External pressure = p (shrink-fit pressure pushes inward).
- *Outer cylinder*: Internal pressure = p (shrink-fit pressure pushes outward), External pressure = pₒ (applied).

**Step 3 — Apply compatibility at the interface.**
The radial expansion of the outer cylinder at r = b PLUS the radial contraction of the inner cylinder at r = b must EQUAL the radial interference δ. This yields Clavarino's equation.

**Step 4 — Apply Lamé to each cylinder with the net pressures.**
Substitute the net pressures into Lamé's tangential stress formula evaluated at r = b. This yields Birnie's equation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the four equations below** — they appear in nearly every KTU Module 4 problem on compound cylinders.

### A. Clavarino's Equation (Compatibility / Contact Pressure)

$$p = \frac{\delta}{b\left[\dfrac{c^2 + b^2}{E_2\,(c^2 - b^2)} + \dfrac{b^2 + a^2}{E_1\,(b^2 - a^2)}\right]}$$

If both cylinders are of the **same material** (E₁ = E₂ = E):

$$p = \frac{\delta \cdot E}{2b} \cdot \frac{(b^2 - a^2)(c^2 - b^2)}{(c^2 - a^2)}$$

### B. Birnie's Equations (Final Hoop Stresses at r = b)

| Cylinder | Final Hoop Stress σₜ (at r = b) |
|----------|----------------------------------|
| Inner cylinder | $\sigma_{t1} = \dfrac{2\,p_i\,a^2 \;-\; p\,(b^2 + a^2)}{b^2 - a^2}$ |
| Outer cylinder | $\sigma_{t2} = \dfrac{p\,(b^2 + c^2) \;-\; 2\,p_o\,c^2}{c^2 - b^2}$ |

### C. Radial Stresses (for completeness)

| Cylinder | Radial Stress σᵣ at r = b |
|----------|---------------------------|
| Inner cylinder | $\sigma_{r1} = -p$ |
| Outer cylinder | $\sigma_{r2} = -p$ |

### D. Special Case — When pᵢ = pₒ = 0 (Shrink Fit Only)

| Cylinder | Hoop Stress at r = b (Shrink Fit Alone) |
|----------|------------------------------------------|
| Inner | $\sigma_{t1} = -\dfrac{p\,(b^2 + a^2)}{b^2 - a^2}$ (compressive) |
| Outer | $\sigma_{t2} = +\dfrac{p\,(b^2 + c^2)}{c^2 - b^2}$ (tensile) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units & Convention**
> - δ is in **mm**, b in **mm**, E in **N/mm²** = **MPa**, p comes out in **MPa**.
> - Always take a < b < c.
> - Negative σₜ = compressive, positive σₜ = tensile.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic cylinders**: Compound cylinders resist both fluid pressure and assembly stresses.
- **Gun barrels** (built-up construction): Shrink-fit sleeves over the inner tube pre-compress it, allowing higher firing pressures without yielding.
- **Railway wheels & tyres**: The wheel centre and tyre are shrink-fitted; high contact pressure prevents slip.
- **Aerospace pressure vessels**: Multi-layer construction uses shrink-fit to share load.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Clavarino's Equation

**Statement:** *The radial interference δ must equal the sum of the radial expansion of the outer cylinder (inner surface) and the radial contraction of the inner cylinder (outer surface) caused by the contact pressure p at the interface.*

**Step 1 — Radial contraction of the inner cylinder.**
The inner cylinder is subjected to external pressure p only (no internal pressure applied yet during assembly analysis). Lamé's equations for this case give:

$$\sigma_t = -\frac{p\,(b^2 + a^2)}{b^2 - a^2}, \quad \sigma_r = -p$$

The radial displacement at r = b is obtained from the strain εₜ = u/r and Hooke's law:

$$\varepsilon_t = \frac{1}{E_1}\bigl(\sigma_t - \nu_1\,\sigma_r\bigr) = \frac{u}{b}$$

Solving for u (the contraction, taken as negative):

$$u_{inner} = -\frac{p\,b}{E_1} \cdot \frac{b^2 + a^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

**Step 2 — Radial expansion of the outer cylinder.**
The outer cylinder is subjected to internal pressure p only (acting outward) and external pressure pₒ = 0. Lamé's equations at r = b give:

$$\sigma_t = +\frac{p\,(c^2 + b^2)}{c^2 - b^2}, \quad \sigma_r = -p$$

The radial displacement at r = b:

$$u_{outer} = +\frac{p\,b}{E_2} \cdot \frac{c^2 + b^2}{c^2 - b^2}$$

**Step 3 — Compatibility condition.**
The radial interference δ equals the absolute displacement of the outer surface of the inner cylinder INWARD plus the absolute displacement of the inner surface of the outer cylinder OUTWARD. Since one is a contraction and the other an expansion:

$$\delta = \lvert u_{inner}\rvert + \lvert u_{outer}\rvert$$

$$\delta = \frac{p\,b}{E_1}\cdot\frac{b^2 + a^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{p\,b}{E_2}\cdot\frac{c^2 + b^2}{c^2 - b^2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for p.**

$$\boxed{\;p = \frac{\delta}{b\left[\dfrac{b^2 + a^2}{E_1\,(b^2 - a^2)} + \dfrac{c^2 + b^2}{E_2\,(c^2 - b^2)}\right]}\;}$$

This is **Clavarino's Equation** for the shrink-fit contact pressure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Birnie's Equations

**Statement:** *Once the assembly is complete and an internal pressure pᵢ is applied, the inner cylinder experiences net internal pressure pᵢ (outward) AND net external pressure p (inward, from shrink fit). Apply Lamé's equations with these net pressures and evaluate at r = b.*

### 3.2.1 Inner Cylinder — Birnie's Equation

**Step 1 — Set up Lamé's tangential stress with both pressures.**

$$\sigma_t = \frac{p_i\,a^2 - p\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{(p_i - p)\,a^2\,b^2}{(b^2 - a^2)\,r^2}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at r = b.**

$$\sigma_t(r=b) = \frac{p_i\,a^2 - p\,b^2}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{(p_i - p)\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

**Step 3 — Combine numerator terms.**

$$\sigma_{t1} = \frac{p_i\,a^2 - p\,b^2 + p_i\,a^2 - p\,a^2}{b^2 - a^2}$$

$$\sigma_{t1} = \frac{2\,p_i\,a^2 - p\,(b^2 + a^2)}{b^2 - a^2}$$

This is **Birnie's Equation for the inner cylinder**.

### 3.2.2 Outer Cylinder — Birnie's Equation

**Step 1 — Apply Lamé to the outer cylinder with internal pressure p and external pressure pₒ.**

$$\sigma_t = \frac{p\,b^2 - p_o\,c^2}{c^2 - b^2} + \frac{(p - p_o)\,b^2\,c^2}{(c^2 - b^2)\,r^2}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at r = b.**

$$\sigma_t(r=b) = \frac{p\,b^2 - p_o\,c^2}{c^2 - b^2} + \frac{(p - p_o)\,c^2}{c^2 - b^2}$$

**Step 3 — Combine numerator terms.**

$$\sigma_{t2} = \frac{p\,b^2 - p_o\,c^2 + p\,c^2 - p_o\,c^2}{c^2 - b^2}$$

$$\sigma_{t2} = \frac{p\,(b^2 + c^2) - 2\,p_o\,c^2}{c^2 - b^2}$$

This is **Birnie's Equation for the outer cylinder**.

If **pₒ = 0** (no external pressure applied — most common KTU case):

$$\sigma_{t2} = \frac{p\,(b^2 + c^2)}{c^2 - b^2}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration (Solves KTU Board Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem (KTU Pattern):** A compound cylinder has inner radius a = 50 mm, interface radius b = 100 mm, outer radius c = 150 mm. Both cylinders are made of steel with E = 2 × 10⁵ MPa. Radial interference δ = 0.05 mm. Internal pressure pᵢ = 30 MPa. Find (i) shrink-fit pressure p, (ii) final hoop stresses at r = b.

**Step 1 — Compute Clavarino's contact pressure.**

$$p = \frac{\delta \cdot E}{2b} \cdot \frac{(b^2 - a^2)(c^2 - b^2)}{c^2 - a^2}$$

Plug values:

$$b^2 - a^2 = 100^2 - 50^2 = 7500 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$c^2 - b^2 = 150^2 - 100^2 = 12500 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$c^2 - a^2 = 22500 - 2500 = 20000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$p = \frac{0.05 \times 2\times 10^5}{2 \times 100} \cdot \frac{7500 \times 12500}{20000}$$

$$p = \frac{10000}{200} \cdot \frac{9.375 \times 10^7}{2 \times 10^4} = 50 \cdot 4687.5 = 234375 \text{ kPa} \cdot \tfrac{1}{1000}$$

$$p \approx 23.44 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Birnie's for inner cylinder.**

$$\sigma_{t1} = \frac{2\,p_i\,a^2 - p\,(b^2 + a^2)}{b^2 - a^2}$$

$$\sigma_{t1} = \frac{2 \times 30 \times 2500 - 23.44 \times (10000 + 2500)}{7500}$$

$$\sigma_{t1} = \frac{150000 - 293000}{7500} = \frac{-143000}{7500} \approx -19.07 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

**Step 3 — Birnie's for outer cylinder (pₒ = 0).**

$$\sigma_{t2} = \frac{p\,(b^2 + c^2)}{c^2 - b^2} = \frac{23.44 \times (10000 + 22500)}{12500}$$

$$\sigma_{t2} = \frac{23.44 \times 32500}{12500} = \frac{761800}{12500} \approx 60.94 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Compound Cylinder Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compound Cylinder Assembly]:::root --> B[Geometry Definition]
    B --> B1[Inner radius a]
    B --> B2[Interface radius b]
    B --> B3[Outer radius c]
    B --> B4[Radial interference delta]
    
    A --> C[Step 1 Apply Clavarino Equation]
    C --> C1[Compatibility at interface r equals b]
    C1 --> C2[Inner cylinder contracts by u inner]
    C1 --> C3[Outer cylinder expands by u outer]
    C2 --> C4[Sum equals delta]
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> C5[Shrink fit pressure p computed]
    
    A --> D[Step 2 Apply Birnie Equations]
    C5 --> D
    D --> D1[Inner cylinder Lamé with p i and p]
    D --> D2[Outer cylinder Lamé with p and p o]
    D1 --> D3[Hoop stress sigma t1 at r equals b]
    D2 --> D4[Hoop stress sigma t2 at r equals b]
    
    A --> E[Step 3 Design Checks]
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> E1[Compare sigma t1 with allowable sigma 1]
    E --> E2[Compare sigma t2 with allowable sigma 2]
    
    classDef root fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress State Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IC[Inner Cylinder]
        I1[Inner wall r equals a: sigma t max tensile]
        I2[Outer wall r equals b: sigma t1 compressive from Birnie]
    end
    
    subgraph IF[Interface r equals b]
        F1[Shrink fit pressure p acts on both cylinders]
        F2[Radial stress sigma r equals minus p]
    end
    
    subgraph OC[Outer Cylinder]
        O1[Inner wall r equals b: sigma t2 tensile from Birnie]
        O2[Outer wall r equals c: sigma t free surface zero radial]
    end
    
    IC --> IF
    IF --> OC
    
    classDef icStyle fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ifStyle fill:#f9cb9c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ocStyle fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    
    class IC icStyle
    class IF ifStyle
    class OC ocStyle
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Solving Strategy Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start KTU Problem] --> Q1{Same Material?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[Use simplified Clavarino p equals delta E b inverse times factor]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Use full Clavarino with E1 and E2]
    Q2 --> R1[Compute p]
    Q3 --> R1
    R1 --> Q2b{External pressure p o given?}
    Q2b -- No --> R2[Set p o equals zero in Birnie]
    Q2b -- Yes --> R3[Keep p o term in Birnie]
    R2 --> R4[Compute sigma t1 and sigma t2]
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> R5[Check against allowable stress and factor of safety]
    R5 --> End[Final Answer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above map the **functional architecture** of the solution procedure, ensuring students follow KTU-board-validated steps without skipping the compatibility condition that distinguishes a correct thick-cylinder analysis from a naive Lamé-only answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** 
**State Clavarino's equation for a compound cylinder and explain the physical meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer:**
Clavarino's equation relates the radial interference (δ) to the shrink-fit contact pressure (p) at the interface of a compound cylinder:

$$p = \frac{\delta}{b\left[\dfrac{b^2 + a^2}{E_1\,(b^2 - a^2)} + \dfrac{c^2 + b^2}{E_2\,(c^2 - b^2)}\right]}$$

**Physical meaning of terms:**
- **δ** → radial oversize of the inner cylinder's outer radius over the outer cylinder's inner radius (before assembly).
- **b** → common interface radius (mm).
- **a, c** → inner radius of inner cylinder and outer radius of outer cylinder (mm).
- **E₁, E₂** → Young's moduli of the inner and outer cylinders (N/mm²).
- **p** → contact pressure developed at the interface after assembly (N/mm²).
The equation is essentially a **compatibility condition** stating that the elastic deformations of both cylinders exactly consume the interference δ.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Lamé's equations and Birnie's equations for compound cylinders.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Lamé's Equations | Birnie's Equations |
|--------|------------------|--------------------|
| **Cylinder type** | Single thick cylinder | Compound cylinder (two cylinders shrink-fitted) |
| **Pressures considered** | Only applied internal pᵢ and external pₒ pressures | Both applied pressure pᵢ AND shrink-fit contact pressure p |
| **Origin** | George Lamé, 19th century | Birnie's modification for shrink-fit residual stresses |
| **Use** | Monolithic thick-walled pressure vessels | Built-up cylinders (gun barrels, hydraulic cylinders, railway wheels) |
| **Key output** | Stresses due to fluid/working pressure alone | Final hoop stresses after accounting for assembly residual stresses |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B in Module 4 follows the **internal choice** pattern: students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are presented below with full sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

A compound cylinder consists of an inner cylinder of inner radius 60 mm and outer radius 120 mm, and an outer cylinder of inner radius 120 mm and outer radius 180 mm. Both are made of the same steel with E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm² and ν = 0.3. The radial interference is 0.06 mm. After assembly, an internal fluid pressure of 40 MPa is applied.

**(a)** Calculate the shrink-fit contact pressure using Clavarino's equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the final hoop stresses at the interface (r = 120 mm) for both cylinders using Birnie's equations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Given:** a = 60 mm, b = 120 mm, c = 180 mm, E₁ = E₂ = E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm², δ = 0.06 mm, pᵢ = 40 MPa.

Since materials are identical, use simplified Clavarino:

$$p = \frac{\delta \cdot E}{2b}\cdot\frac{(b^2 - a^2)(c^2 - b^2)}{c^2 - a^2}$$

Compute each term:

$$b^2 - a^2 = 14400 - 3600 = 10800 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$c^2 - b^2 = 32400 - 14400 = 18000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$c^2 - a^2 = 32400 - 3600 = 28800 \text{ mm}^2$$

Substitute:

$$p = \frac{0.06 \times 2\times 10^5}{2 \times 120}\cdot\frac{10800 \times 18000}{28800}$$

$$p = \frac{12000}{240}\cdot\frac{1.944 \times 10^8}{2.88 \times 10^4} = 50 \times 6750$$

$$\boxed{p = 33.75 \text{ MPa}}$$

#### Model Solution (b):

**Inner cylinder — Birnie's equation:**

$$\sigma_{t1} = \frac{2\,p_i\,a^2 - p\,(b^2 + a^2)}{b^2 - a^2}$$

$$\sigma_{t1} = \frac{2\times 40\times 3600 - 33.75\times(14400 + 3600)}{10800}$$

$$\sigma_{t1} = \frac{288000 - 33.75\times 18000}{10800} = \frac{288000 - 607500}{10800}$$

$$\sigma_{t1} = \frac{-319500}{10800} \approx -29.58 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

**Outer cylinder — Birnie's equation (pₒ = 0):**

$$\sigma_{t2} = \frac{p\,(b^2 + c^2)}{c^2 - b^2}$$

$$\sigma_{t2} = \frac{33.75\times(14400 + 32400)}{18000} = \frac{33.75\times 46800}{18000}$$

$$\sigma_{t2} = \frac{1579500}{18000} \approx 87.75 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

#### Final Summary:

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Shrink-fit pressure p | **33.75 MPa** |
| σₜ,inner (compressive) | **−29.58 MPa** |
| σₜ,outer (tensile) | **+87.75 MPa** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

A built-up (compound) cylinder is to be designed to withstand an internal pressure of 60 MPa. The inner cylinder has a = 80 mm and b = 160 mm. The outer cylinder has c = 240 mm. Both cylinders are of steel: E = 2.1 × 10⁵ N/mm². The maximum allowable tensile stress is 200 MPa and compressive stress is 150 MPa.

**(a)** If the radial interference is δ = 0.08 mm, determine the shrink-fit pressure using Clavarino's equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using Birnie's equations, verify whether the final hoop stresses are within the allowable limits. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Compute Clavarino's pressure.**

$$b^2 - a^2 = 25600 - 6400 = 19200 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$c^2 - b^2 = 57600 - 25600 = 32000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$c^2 - a^2 = 57600 - 6400 = 51200 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$p = \frac{0.08 \times 2.1\times 10^5}{2 \times 160}\cdot\frac{19200 \times 32000}{51200}$$

$$p = \frac{16800}{320}\cdot\frac{6.144 \times 10^8}{5.12 \times 10^4}$$

$$p = 52.5 \times 12000 = \boxed{63.0 \text{ MPa}}$$

#### Model Solution (b):

**Inner cylinder hoop stress at r = b:**

$$\sigma_{t1} = \frac{2 \times 60 \times 6400 - 63\times(25600 + 6400)}{19200}$$

$$\sigma_{t1} = \frac{768000 - 63 \times 32000}{19200} = \frac{768000 - 2016000}{19200}$$

$$\sigma_{t1} = \frac{-1248000}{19200} = -65.0 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

Compressive check: |−65.0| = 65 MPa < 150 MPa ✓ **SAFE**

**Outer cylinder hoop stress at r = b (pₒ = 0):**

$$\sigma_{t2} = \frac{63\times(25600 + 57600)}{32000} = \frac{63\times 83200}{32000}$$

$$\sigma_{t2} = \frac{5241600}{32000} = 163.8 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

Tensile check: 163.8 MPa < 200 MPa ✓ **SAFE**

#### Conclusion:

Both hoop stresses are within allowable limits; the design is **safe**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Mixing up which cylinder sees which pressure.** Inner cylinder has internal pᵢ AND external p (shrink fit); outer cylinder has internal p (shrink fit) AND external pₒ. Reversing these gives wrong signs and wrong magnitudes.
> 2. **Forgetting the compatibility step.** Some students directly substitute into Lamé's equations without computing p from Clavarino's equation. This loses 7 marks outright on Part (a).
> 3. **Sign convention slip-ups.** Always write σₜ,inner = −p(b²+a²)/(b²−a²) as a NEGATIVE (compressive) quantity for shrink fit alone. Adding pᵢ may make it less negative or even positive.
> 4. **Unit mismatches.** δ in mm, E in N/mm², b in mm → p in N/mm² (MPa). Do not convert to SI inconsistently.
> 5. **Dropping the factor "2" in Birnie's numerator.** The numerator is 2pᵢa² − p(b²+a²), NOT pᵢa² − p(b²+a²). Many students forget the **2** that comes from combining the two terms of Lamé's solution at r = b.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Compound cylinder** = two cylinders joined by **shrink fit** (interference δ).
- **Clavarino's Equation** → solves for the **shrink-fit contact pressure p** using the **compatibility of radial displacements** at r = b.
- **Birnie's Equations** → give the **final hoop stresses** at the interface after applying both **internal pressure pᵢ** and **shrink-fit pressure p**.
- For same material (E₁ = E₂ = E), the simplified Clavarino form is faster for KTU numericals.
- Always evaluate stresses at the **interface r = b** first, since that is where stresses are typically critical.
- Inner cylinder at r = b sees compressive hoop stress (from shrink fit) — this is why shrink fitting increases load capacity.
- Outer cylinder at r = b sees tensile hoop stress — usually the **critical** point for design.
- The radial stress σᵣ at the interface equals **−p** for both cylinders.
- Lamé's equations are a **subset** of Birnie's equations (when p = 0, Birnie's reduces to Lamé's).
- For KTU Part B 14-mark problems, expect: (i) compute p using Clavarino, (ii) compute σₜ using Birnie, (iii) check against allowable stress, and optionally (iv) find factor of safety.
- Remember the **factor 2** in Birnie's numerator: σₜ,inner = [2pᵢa² − p(b²+a²)] / (b²−a²).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dilation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET602 - MACHINE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dilation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dilation (in Machine Design):** The deliberate exploitation of **thermal expansion (or contraction)** of mating components — typically a shaft and a hub — to assemble them with an **interference fit** (also called a *force fit*, *shrink fit*, or *press fit*). The hub is **heated (positive dilation)** or the shaft is **cooled (negative dilation)** so that the diametral clearance becomes positive, allowing slip-fit assembly. Upon equalization to ambient temperature, the components return to their original dimensions, producing a tight, high-torque, vibration-resistant joint driven by **Hertzian contact pressure** at the cylindrical interface.

Mathematically, the linear thermal strain for isotropic materials under a uniform temperature change $\Delta T$ is governed by the **Coefficient of Linear Thermal Expansion**, $\alpha$ (units: $\text{per } ^\circ\text{C}$ or $/\text{K}$):

$$
\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

The corresponding change in any linear dimension $L$ is:

$$
\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T
$$

For a **cylindrical interference fit** of nominal diameter $D$, the required thermal gap $\Delta D$ that must be created to allow free assembly is the **Interference** (denoted $\delta$ or $i$), where:

$$
\delta = D_{hub,inner} - D_{shaft} \quad (\delta > 0 \text{ for interference fit})
$$

To create a clearance $\geq \delta$ for assembly, the hub must be heated by a temperature:

$$
\Delta T_{hub} \geq \frac{\delta}{\alpha \cdot D}
$$

(or the shaft cooled by an equivalent amount).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hot Mug and the Glass Lid"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder:** Imagine a **steel mug with a tight glass lid** that you cannot open by hand. Run hot water over the metal lid ring. The metal expands (dilates) more than the glass, the lid loosens, and you lift it off easily. Now place a hot metal collar around a slightly larger wooden shaft — as the collar cools, it shrinks down and grips the shaft so tightly that it becomes a single unit. **That is dilation-based assembly in machine design** — using temperature to temporarily defeat geometry so that geometry can permanently triumph on its own.

## 1.3 Why Dilation is a "High-Yield Module 4 Topic"

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests Dilation because it integrates:
> 1. **Thermal physics** (linear expansion),
> 2. **Strength of Materials** (Lamé's equations for thick cylinders),
> 3. **Machine Design judgment** (selection of fit, material pairing, assembly temperature, stresses).
>
> It is the *only* Module 4 topic that forces the student to combine **three different core engineering domains** in a single numerical answer — a favourite KTU examiner strategy.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Coefficient of thermal expansion (steel) | $\alpha_{steel}$ | $\mathbf{12 \times 10^{-6} \; /^\circ C}$ |
| Coefficient of thermal expansion (cast iron) | $\alpha_{CI}$ | $\mathbf{10.5 \times 10^{-6} \; /^\circ C}$ |
| Coefficient of thermal expansion (bronze) | $\alpha_{Br}$ | $\mathbf{18 \times 10^{-6} \; /^\circ C}$ |
| Coefficient of thermal expansion (aluminium alloy) | $\alpha_{Al}$ | $\mathbf{23 \times 10^{-6} \; /^\circ C}$ |
| Young's Modulus (steel) | $E$ | $\mathbf{2 \times 10^5 \; \text{N/mm}^2}$ |
| Poisson's ratio (steel) | $\nu$ | $\mathbf{0.3}$ |
| Ambient design temperature | $T_{amb}$ | $\mathbf{30 \; ^\circ C}$ (KTU convention) |
| Maximum safe heating temperature | $T_{max}$ | $\mathbf{400 \; ^\circ C}$ (for tempering safety) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Principle

The engineering logic of dilation-based assembly proceeds in **five sequential phases**:

1. **Geometry Audit** — Establish the **nominal diameter** $D$, the **shaft outer diameter** $D_s$, and the **hub bore diameter** $D_h$. Verify that $D_h > D_s$ (the difference $\delta$ is the **interference**).

2. **Fit Selection** — Choose a fit class (e.g., H7/s6, H7/u6, or H7/x6 per ISO 286). Tighter fits yield higher torque capacity but demand more extreme temperature changes.

3. **Thermal Dilation** — Apply heat to the hub (or cold to the shaft) until the bore becomes larger than the shaft by at least a small **assembly clearance** (typically $0.05\delta$ to $0.1\delta$ to permit sliding without scratching).

4. **Assembly** — Slide hub onto shaft while temperature differential still exists. Maintain alignment to avoid contact damage.

5. **Equilibration & Grip Generation** — As temperatures equalize to ambient, the hub contracts onto the shaft. Because the actual interference $\delta$ is greater than the free thermal strain can relieve, **contact pressure $p$** is generated at the cylindrical interface. This pressure is the *source of all torque-transmitting and axial-load capacity* of the joint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** Even though the components look "resting" after assembly, the interface is in a state of **biaxial stress** — the hub is in **tangential tension** and the shaft is in **hydrostatic compression**. The magnitude of these stresses must be checked against the material yield strength to prevent yielding or cracking.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> **Use $\vert$ or `\vert` notation** for absolute values — never the bare pipe symbol `|`, which corrupts the markdown table.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | Linear thermal expansion | mm |
| 2 | $\delta = D_h - D_s$ | Diametral interference | mm |
| 3 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{\delta}{\alpha \cdot D} + T_{amb}$ | Required hub heating temperature (free expansion) | $^\circ$C |
| 4 | $p = \dfrac{\delta}{D \cdot \left[ \dfrac{1}{E_s}\left( \dfrac{D_o^2 + D^2}{D_o^2 - D^2} + \nu_s \right) + \dfrac{1}{E_h}\left( \dfrac{D^2 + D_i^2}{D^2 - D_i^2} - \nu_h \right) \right]}$ | **Interface contact pressure (Lamé's equation solution)** | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 4a | $p = \dfrac{\delta}{D} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{E_o} + \dfrac{1}{E_i}}$ | Simplified pressure (thin rings, $D \gg$ thickness) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 5 | $\sigma_{t,shaft} = -p$ | Tangential stress at shaft surface (compressive) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 6 | $\sigma_{t,hub} = p \cdot \dfrac{D_o^2 + D^2}{D_o^2 - D^2}$ | Tangential (hoop) stress in hub at bore | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 7 | $\sigma_{r,hub} = -p$ (at bore), $0$ (at $D_o$) | Radial stress in hub | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 8 | $F_{axial} = p \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot \mu$ | Axial holding force (with friction coefficient $\mu$) | N |
| 9 | $T_{torque} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot p \cdot \pi \cdot D^2 \cdot L \cdot \mu$ | Torque capacity of the joint | N·mm |
| 10 | $M_c = \dfrac{\pi}{16} \cdot D_s^3 \cdot \sigma_{t,shaft}$ | Equivalent torque on shaft (von Mises approx.) | N·mm |
| 11 | $\Delta T_{min} = \dfrac{\delta + \Delta_{clear}}{\alpha \cdot D}$ | Practical heating temperature (includes assembly clearance) | $^\circ$C |

**Notation key** (used in the formulas above):
- $D$ = nominal interface diameter (mm)
- $D_s, D_h$ = shaft OD, hub bore ID (mm)
- $D_o$ = hub outer diameter (mm)
- $D_i$ = inner shaft diameter for hollow shaft (use $0$ for solid)
- $E_s, E_h$ = Young's moduli of shaft and hub materials (N/mm²)
- $\nu_s, \nu_h$ = Poisson's ratios
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction at interface (typical **0.12 to 0.18** for steel-steel dry; **0.05 to 0.08** lubricated)
- $L$ = axial length of contact (mm)
- $\Delta_{clear}$ = assembly clearance allowance (mm)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dilation is **the industry standard** for assembling:
- **Railway wheels** on steel axles (heated to ~250°C).
- **Propeller hubs** on marine shafting.
- **Turbine discs** on rotor shafts in jet engines and steam turbines.
- **Gear blanks** on shafts in heavy industrial gearboxes.
- **Armature laminations** on motor shafts.
- **Track pins** in track-type machinery (bushings shrunk into carrier rollers).

> [!NOTE]
> In all these cases, the alternative (mechanical pressing) would either damage the surface or fail to deliver sufficient grip. **Dilation provides uniform, predictable, full-360° contact pressure** without impact loading.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Heating Temperature for Hub

**Statement:** A steel hub of nominal bore $D = 60 \text{ mm}$ has interference $\delta = 0.06 \text{ mm}$. Compute the temperature to which the hub must be heated (free expansion assumption, ambient $T_{amb} = 30^\circ\text{C}$), using $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /^\circ\text{C}$.

**Given:**
- $D = 60 \text{ mm}$
- $\delta = 0.06 \text{ mm}$
- $T_{amb} = 30^\circ\text{C}$
- $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /^\circ\text{C}$

**Step 1 — Identify the required diametral expansion.** The hub bore must expand by **at least $\delta$** to allow shaft insertion. We add a small assembly clearance $c = 0.01 \text{ mm}$ to ensure easy sliding without scoring. So required expansion:

$$
\Delta D_{req} = \delta + c = 0.06 + 0.01 = 0.07 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the linear thermal expansion law.**

$$
\Delta D = \alpha \cdot D \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\Delta T$.**

$$
\Delta T = \frac{\Delta D_{req}}{\alpha \cdot D}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numbers.**

$$
\Delta T = \frac{0.07}{(12 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (60)} = \frac{0.07}{7.2 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 97.22^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the absolute heating temperature.**

$$
T_{hub} = T_{amb} + \Delta T = 30 + 97.22 \approx 127.22^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check.**

$$
T_{hub} = 127.22^\circ\text{C} < T_{max} = 400^\circ\text{C} \quad \checkmark \text{ Safe, no tempering risk}
$$

**Final Answer:** The hub must be uniformly heated to **$\approx 127^\circ\text{C}$** (a hot-oil bath at 130°C is a standard industrial technique).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lamé's Equation for Contact Pressure in a Solid Shaft + Hub Joint

**Statement:** Derive the interface contact pressure $p$ for a fit between a **solid steel shaft** ($D_s = 60 \text{ mm}$, $E_s = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $\nu_s = 0.3$) and a **steel hub** ($D_o = 120 \text{ mm}$, $E_h = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $\nu_h = 0.3$) with interference $\delta = 0.06 \text{ mm}$.

### 3.2.1 Conceptual Setup

The hub is treated as a **thick-walled cylinder under external pressure** $p$ on its bore. The shaft is treated as a **solid cylinder under hydrostatic compression** $p$. Lamé's equations give the radial displacement at any radius:

$$
u(r) = \frac{1}{E} \left[ (1-\nu) \cdot \frac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} \cdot r + (1+\nu) \cdot \frac{(p_i - p_o) r_i^2 r_o^2}{(r_o^2 - r_i^2) r} \right]
$$

### 3.2.2 Radial Displacement of Hub (Bore Side)

For the hub with $p_i = p$ (internal pressure pushing **outward**, but pressure acting on hub from shaft is **inward** — convention matters). We treat shaft pressure on hub bore as $p_o = p$ (external pressure) and $p_i = 0$. So at the bore radius $r = D/2$:

$$
u_{hub}(r = D/2) = -\frac{p \cdot D}{2 E_h} \cdot \left( \frac{D_o^2 + D^2}{D_o^2 - D^2} - \nu_h \right)
$$

### 3.2.3 Radial Displacement of Solid Shaft

For a solid shaft under uniform external pressure $p$, the diametral contraction (note: negative because shaft contracts inward):

$$
u_{shaft}(r = D/2) = -\frac{p \cdot D}{2 E_s} \cdot (1 - \nu_s)
$$

### 3.2.4 Compatibility Equation

The interference $\delta$ equals the **sum** of the radial displacements of hub outward and shaft inward (they move *toward* each other):

$$
\delta = \frac{p \cdot D}{2} \left[ \frac{1}{E_h}\left( \frac{D_o^2 + D^2}{D_o^2 - D^2} - \nu_h \right) + \frac{1}{E_s}(1 - \nu_s) \right]
$$

### 3.2.5 Solve for $p$

$$
p = \frac{2 \delta}{D \cdot \left[ \dfrac{1}{E_h}\left( \dfrac{D_o^2 + D^2}{D_o^2 - D^2} - \nu_h \right) + \dfrac{1}{E_s}(1 - \nu_s) \right]}
$$

### 3.2.6 Numerical Evaluation

Plug in: $D = 60$, $D_o = 120$, $\delta = 0.06$, $E_s = E_h = 2 \times 10^5$, $\nu_s = \nu_h = 0.3$.

**Step A:** Compute the geometric term for the hub.

$$
\frac{D_o^2 + D^2}{D_o^2 - D^2} = \frac{120^2 + 60^2}{120^2 - 60^2} = \frac{14400 + 3600}{14400 - 3600} = \frac{18000}{10800} = 1.6667
$$

**Step B:** Hub bracket.

$$
\frac{1}{E_h}(1.6667 - 0.3) = \frac{1.3667}{2 \times 10^5} = 6.833 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step C:** Shaft bracket.

$$
\frac{1}{E_s}(1 - 0.3) = \frac{0.7}{2 \times 10^5} = 3.5 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step D:** Sum.

$$
[\,\cdots\,] = 6.833 \times 10^{-6} + 3.5 \times 10^{-6} = 10.333 \times 10^{-6} \; (\text{per N/mm}^2)
$$

**Step E:** Numerator.

$$
2 \delta = 2 \times 0.06 = 0.12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step F:** Compute $p$.

$$
p = \frac{0.12}{60 \times 10.333 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.12}{6.2 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 193.5 \; \text{N/mm}^2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Notice the pressure $p \approx 193.5$ MPa is **VERY HIGH**. This is because we used the *full* diametral interference without subtracting thermal relief. In a *static* problem (no temperature differential during service), this pressure acts permanently. Always check $p \ll \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$ (von Mises) before accepting.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hoop Stress and Torque Capacity

**Continuing the same numerical problem:**

**Step 1 — Hoop stress in hub (at the bore, $r = D/2$):**

$$
\sigma_{t,hub} = p \cdot \frac{D_o^2 + D^2}{D_o^2 - D^2} = 193.5 \times 1.6667 \approx 322.5 \; \text{N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compare to allowable.** For medium carbon steel, $\sigma_y \approx 350 - 400 \; \text{N/mm}^2$, so $\sigma_{t,hub} = 322.5$ is acceptable but tight.

**Step 3 — Torque capacity (with $\mu = 0.12$, $L = 80 \text{ mm}$):**

$$
T = \frac{1}{2} \cdot p \cdot \pi \cdot D^2 \cdot L \cdot \mu
$$

$$
T = 0.5 \times 193.5 \times \pi \times 60^2 \times 80 \times 0.12
$$

$$
T = 0.5 \times 193.5 \times 3.1416 \times 3600 \times 80 \times 0.12
$$

$$
T = 0.5 \times 193.5 \times 3.1416 \times 3600 \times 9.6 \approx 10.5 \times 10^6 \; \text{N·mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 10.5 \; \text{kN·m}}
$$

This is a typical torque capacity for a medium-duty shrink-fit gear-hub assembly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Dilation Assembly Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[StepA: Geometry Audit]
    nodeB[StepB: Select Fit Class e.g. H7 s6]
    nodeC[StepC: Compute Interference delta]
    nodeD[StepD: Compute Required Delta T]
    nodeE[StepE: Heat Hub OR Cool Shaft]
    nodeF[StepF: Verify T less than Tmax]
    nodeG[StepG: Assemble Hub on Shaft]
    nodeH[StepH: Cool to Ambient]
    nodeI[StepI: Contact Pressure p develops]
    nodeJ[StepJ: Verify Hub Hoop Stress less than Allowable]
    nodeK[StepK: Verify Shaft Compressive Stress less than Allowable]
    nodeL[StepL: Compute Torque and Axial Capacity]

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG --> nodeH --> nodeI --> nodeJ --> nodeK --> nodeL

    nodeF -.Fail. nodeM[StepM: REJECT Use Mechanical Press]
    nodeJ -.Fail. nodeM
    nodeK -.Fail. nodeM

    classDef safety fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    class F,J,K,M safety
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress State at the Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    shaftNode[Shaft - Solid Steel]
    hubNode[Hub - Outer Steel Ring]
    interfaceNode[Interface Cylindrical Surface]

    shaftNode -->|Hydrostatic Compression sigma t equals minus p| interfaceNode
    interfaceNode -->|Contact Pressure p Outward on Hub Bore| hubNode
    hubNode -->|Hoop Tension sigma t hub at D| interfaceNode

    classDef stress fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    class shaftNode,hubNode,interfaceNode stress
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Failure Mode Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Design a Dilation Fit]
    geomNode[Inputs D, delta, Materials]
    thNode[Thermal Analysis - Delta T]
    mechNode[Mechanical Analysis - Lamé Equations]
    checkNode{All Stresses less than Allowable}

    startNode --> geomNode --> thNode --> mechNode --> checkNode

    checkNode -->|Pass| acceptNode[Design Accepted]
    checkNode -->|Hub Hoop Stress Too High| fail1[Reduce Interference or Increase D outer]
    checkNode -->|Temperature Exceeds Limit| fail2[Use Shaft Cooling Instead]
    checkNode -->|Shaft Yield| fail3[Increase Shaft Diameter]

    classDef passStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px
    classDef failStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px

    class acceptNode passStyle
    class fail1,fail2,fail3 failStyle
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define "Dilation" in the context of machine design. Why is it preferred over mechanical pressing for large-diameter interference fits?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Dilation is the process of assembling mating components (typically a shaft and hub) by deliberately exploiting **thermal expansion** (heating the outer member or cooling the inner member) to temporarily create a clearance for free sliding assembly. Upon equalization to ambient temperature, the original interference is restored, generating a uniform **Hertzian contact pressure** at the cylindrical interface.
>
> **Why preferred over pressing (1.5 marks):**
> 1. Mechanical pressing requires enormous axial force proportional to $D \cdot L \cdot \mu \cdot p$. For $D > 100$ mm and $p > 100$ MPa, this force can exceed **several tonnes** — impractical and dangerous.
> 2. Pressing causes **surface scoring, micro-cracks, and uneven stress distribution**.
> 3. Dilation produces **uniform 360° contact pressure**, no impact loading, and is fully reversible (heating releases the joint).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**List the standard ISO fit classes commonly used for force/shrink fits and state the relationship between fit class and torque transmission capacity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **ISO Fit Classes for Force Fits (1.5 marks):** The commonly used tolerance zones for force fits (per ISO 286) are:
> - **H7/r6** — *light drive fit* (low torque, easily removable).
> - **H7/s6** — *medium drive fit* (moderate torque, requires heating or pressing).
> - **H7/u6** — *heavy force fit* (high torque, requires dilation).
> - **H7/x6** — *extra-heavy fit* (very high torque, requires dilation + cooling).
> - **H7/z6** — *heaviest practical fit* (permanent, used in aerospace).
>
> **Relationship (1.5 marks):** As the fit class progresses from `r → s → u → x → z`, the **interference magnitude $\delta$** increases. Since interface pressure $p \propto \delta$, the **torque capacity $T \propto p \propto \delta$** also increases linearly. However, the required heating temperature $\Delta T \propto \delta$ increases in proportion, so practical limits are set by **material yield** and **maximum safe heating temperature** ($T_{max} \approx 400^\circ\text{C}$ for steels).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**A solid steel shaft of diameter $60 \text{ mm}$ is to be fitted to a steel hub of outer diameter $120 \text{ mm}$ and axial length $80 \text{ mm}$ using a shrink (force) fit. The diametral interference is $0.06 \text{ mm}$. Both members are of the same steel with $E = 2 \times 10^5 \; \text{N/mm}^2$ and $\nu = 0.3$. The coefficient of friction at the interface is $\mu = 0.12$.**

**(a) [7 marks]** Determine the temperature to which the hub must be heated for assembly (ambient $T_{amb} = 30^\circ\text{C}$, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ\text{C}$). Include a safety assembly clearance of $0.01 \text{ mm}$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the interface contact pressure, the maximum hoop stress in the hub, and the torque that can be transmitted by the joint.

---

#### **Model Solution (a): Heating Temperature**

**Step 1 — Total diametral expansion required:**

$$
\Delta D_{req} = \delta + c = 0.06 + 0.01 = 0.07 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply linear thermal expansion:**

$$
\Delta D_{req} = \alpha \cdot D \cdot \Delta T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta T = \frac{\Delta D_{req}}{\alpha \cdot D}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
\Delta T = \frac{0.07}{(12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 60} = \frac{0.07}{7.2 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\Delta T = 97.22^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 4 — Absolute temperature:**

$$
\boxed{T_{hub} = 30 + 97.22 = 127.22^\circ\text{C}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $T_{hub} = 127.22^\circ\text{C} \ll 400^\circ\text{C}$ — within metallurgical safety limits.

---

#### **Model Solution (b): Contact Pressure, Hoop Stress & Torque**

**Step 1 — Compute the contact pressure using the derived formula:**

$$
p = \frac{2\delta}{D \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{E_h}\left(\frac{D_o^2 + D^2}{D_o^2 - D^2} - \nu_h\right) + \frac{1}{E_s}(1 - \nu_s) \right]}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the geometric term:**

$$
\frac{D_o^2 + D^2}{D_o^2 - D^2} = \frac{120^2 + 60^2}{120^2 - 60^2} = \frac{18000}{10800} = 1.6667
$$

**Step 3 — Bracket 1 (hub):**

$$
\frac{1}{2 \times 10^5}(1.6667 - 0.3) = \frac{1.3667}{2 \times 10^5} = 6.833 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 4 — Bracket 2 (shaft):**

$$
\frac{1}{2 \times 10^5}(1 - 0.3) = \frac{0.7}{2 \times 10^5} = 3.5 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum and final $p$:**

$$
\text{Sum} = 10.333 \times 10^{-6}, \quad p = \frac{2 \times 0.06}{60 \times 10.333 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.12}{6.2 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{p \approx 193.5 \; \text{N/mm}^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum hoop stress in hub (at the bore, $r = D/2$):**

$$
\sigma_{t,hub} = p \cdot \frac{D_o^2 + D^2}{D_o^2 - D^2} = 193.5 \times 1.6667
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{t,hub} \approx 322.5 \; \text{N/mm}^2}
$$

**Step 7 — Torque capacity:**

$$
T = \frac{1}{2} \cdot p \cdot \pi \cdot D^2 \cdot L \cdot \mu
$$

$$
T = 0.5 \times 193.5 \times \pi \times 60^2 \times 80 \times 0.12 \approx 10.5 \times 10^6 \; \text{N·mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 10.5 \; \text{kN·m}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**A hollow steel shaft with inner diameter $40 \text{ mm}$ and outer diameter $80 \text{ mm}$ is to be joined to a steel hub of outer diameter $160 \text{ mm}$ and axial length $100 \text{ mm}$ by dilation. The diametral interference is $0.08 \text{ mm}$. Material properties: $E = 2 \times 10^5 \; \text{N/mm}^2$, $\nu = 0.3$, $\mu = 0.15$, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{amb} = 30^\circ\text{C}$.**

**(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the interface contact pressure $p$ and the hoop stress in the hub at the bore.

**(b) [7 marks]** Determine the hub heating temperature (with $0.015 \text{ mm}$ assembly clearance) and the torque that can be transmitted.

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Lamé's equation for a hollow shaft under external pressure $p$:** The shaft's inner radius is $D_i/2 = 20 \text{ mm}$ and outer radius is $D/2 = 40 \text{ mm}$. The radial contraction at $r = D/2$ for a thick hollow cylinder with $p_o = p$ and $p_i = 0$:

$$
u_{shaft}(D/2) = -\frac{p \cdot D}{2 E_s} \cdot \left( \frac{D^2 + D_i^2}{D^2 - D_i^2} + \nu_s \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Geometric term for shaft:**

$$
\frac{D^2 + D_i^2}{D^2 - D_i^2} = \frac{80^2 + 40^2}{80^2 - 40^2} = \frac{6400 + 1600}{6400 - 1600} = \frac{8000}{4800} = 1.6667
$$

**Step 3 — Compatibility:**

$$
\delta = \frac{p \cdot D}{2} \left[ \frac{1}{E_h}\left(\frac{D_o^2 + D^2}{D_o^2 - D^2} - \nu_h\right) + \frac{1}{E_s}\left(\frac{D^2 + D_i^2}{D^2 - D_i^2} + \nu_s\right) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Hub bracket:**

$$
\frac{1}{E_h}\left(\frac{160^2 + 80^2}{160^2 - 80^2} - 0.3\right) = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^5}(2.3333 - 0.3) = \frac{2.0333}{2 \times 10^5} = 10.167 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 5 — Shaft bracket:**

$$
\frac{1}{E_s}(1.6667 + 0.3) = \frac{1.9667}{2 \times 10^5} = 9.833 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 6 — Sum and solve for $p$:**

$$
\text{Sum} = 20 \times 10^{-6}, \quad p = \frac{2 \times 0.08}{80 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.16}{1.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 100 \; \text{N/mm}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{p = 100 \; \text{MPa}}
$$

**Step 7 — Hoop stress in hub:**

$$
\sigma_{t,hub} = 100 \times 2.3333 \approx 233.3 \; \text{N/mm}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{t,hub} \approx 233.3 \; \text{MPa}}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Required diametral expansion:**

$$
\Delta D_{req} = 0.08 + 0.015 = 0.095 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Heating temperature rise:**

$$
\Delta T = \frac{0.095}{12 \times 10^{-6} \times 80} = \frac{0.095}{9.6 \times 10^{-4}} = 98.96^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Absolute temperature:**

$$
\boxed{T_{hub} = 30 + 98.96 \approx 129^\circ\text{C}}
$$

**Step 4 — Torque capacity:**

$$
T = 0.5 \times 100 \times \pi \times 80^2 \times 100 \times 0.15
$$

$$
T = 0.5 \times 100 \times 3.1416 \times 6400 \times 100 \times 0.15
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 15.08 \times 10^6 \; \text{N·mm} = 15.08 \; \text{kN·m}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the assembly clearance** $c$ in the temperature calculation — KTU typically awards **1 mark** for including this.
> 2. **Sign convention confusion in Lamé's equations** — confused students use the wrong bracket `( + \nu )` vs `( - \nu )` for hub vs shaft, yielding wrong $p$. Cost: **2-3 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units** — $\delta$ is in mm but $E$ is in N/mm² — students who mix up units get **0 marks** for the entire pressure calculation.
> 4. **Skipping the metallurgical safety check** ($T_{hub} < 400^\circ\text{C}$). Cost: **0.5 mark** for "design judgment".
> 5. **Forgetting $\mu$ in the torque formula** or assuming $\mu = 1$ — cost: **1 mark**.
> 6. **Not specifying which fit class** (H7/s6, etc.) was assumed. Always state your assumption.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — read 5 minutes before the exam:**

- [x] **Dilation** = use of thermal expansion/contraction to assemble an interference fit.
- [x] **Linear thermal expansion formula:** $\Delta L = \alpha L \Delta T$.
- [x] **Hub heating temperature:** $T_{hub} = T_{amb} + \dfrac{\delta + c}{\alpha D}$.
- [x] **Always include assembly clearance** $c$ in $\Delta D_{req}$.
- [x] **Maximum safe heating temperature for steel:** **400°C** (above this, tempering alters hardness).
- [x] **Interface contact pressure formula** uses Lamé's equations — memorize the geometric ratios $\dfrac{D_o^2 + D^2}{D_o^2 - D^2}$ and $\dfrac{D^2 + D_i^2}{D^2 - D_i^2}$.
- [x] **Sign convention for the bracket term:**
    - Shaft (external pressure on solid OR hollow): $(+ \nu_s)$ if hollow, $(1 - \nu_s)$ if solid.
    - Hub (internal pressure on bore): $(- \nu_h)$.
- [x] **Hoop stress at hub bore** is **maximum** and equals $p \cdot \dfrac{D_o^2 + D^2}{D_o^2 - D^2}$.
- [x] **Tangential stress in shaft** is **compressive** and equals $-p$ (for solid shaft).
- [x] **Torque capacity:** $T = \frac{1}{2} p \pi D^2 L \mu$.
- [x] **Axial holding force:** $F = p \pi D L \mu$.
- [x] **Typical friction coefficient** $\mu = 0.12$ (dry steel-steel), $0.05 - 0.08$ (lubricated).
- [x] **Tighter fit class → higher $p$ → higher torque**, but also higher required $\Delta T$.
- [x] **Verification check (mandatory):** $p \ll \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$ (von Mises yield criterion).
- [x] **Alternative to heating hub:** Cool the shaft in liquid nitrogen ($\approx -196^\circ\text{C}$); useful when hub is too large to heat safely.
- [x] **Advantages of dilation over pressing:** uniform contact pressure, no surface damage, reversible, scalable to any diameter.
- [x] **Disadvantages of dilation:** requires temperature-controlled bath, time-bound assembly window, metallurgical risk at very high $T$.
- [x] **Real-world examples:** railway wheels, propeller hubs, turbine discs, gear blanks, motor armature laminations.
